May 18, 2022, 8:45 PM

I had another minor disagreement with Meifang today over the salt. She added an extra spoonful while simmering the soup; I caught the scent and went over immediately to check. She said, "You can't even taste the difference," and I didn't say much back, but I was thinking—her blood pressure is already at 140/92. I saw the last physical exam report clearly. Just one extra spoonful of salt can cause a five-point spike; it’s not a small matter.

I poured her a glass of water and told her to drink it first. Then I told her I’d be putting together a low-sodium meal plan for her this weekend. She didn’t object; she just gave me a smile—that look of someone who’s grown used to my ways.

While sitting in the workshop, I flipped through the tool log again. I’ve already recorded over thirty items this year, each marked with its purpose and maintenance method. Sometimes I write "Who will use this?" in the margins—a question for myself. Hao is selling real estate in Shenzhen, and Yu is teaching in Guangzhou; neither of them will ever touch these things. I know that.

But I keep recording anyway. While I can still move.

The May physical is coming up. The cardiologist mentioned last time that I need regular follow-ups. I haven't told the family exactly what it's for, just that it's a routine check-up. Meifang asked once, and I said, "Nothing major, just some fluctuations in the data." Honestly, I don't really want to dwell on it myself.

Tonight, I glanced at a WeChat message from Hao. He said the mortgage pressure is a bit heavy this month and asked if I could support him a little more. I transferred five thousand yuan to him without saying much. He replied with a "Thanks, Dad." That was it. I tried to remember the last time he came home—was it three months ago? Or four? I couldn't recall.

I wonder, should I keep doing all this after I retire? Should I keep keeping the accounts? Should I keep polishing the tools? Should I keep managing everything in this house?

I have to get everything done that needs doing, while I still can.

I have to hurry.

ranked_items.content:
April 5, 2022. Night.

I’ve been sitting in the office for a long time. Wang Gang’s quality inspection report is still spinning in my head.

The formatting was off again when he handed it in this morning—the classification standards in the header didn't match last year’s specifications. It’s such a basic thing to get wrong. I couldn't help myself; I snapped at him. He tried to explain, but I cut him off. No excuses. Professionalism is about an obsession with detail.

His face changed the moment I said it.

Looking back now, my tone was harsh. Harsher than usual. He’s actually a decent employee. In the past, I would have pointed out these small mistakes, but never like that. Why today...?

My fingers have been tapping on the desk for a long while. Rapidly. I didn't even realize I was doing it.

What am I thinking? Was his report really that outrageous? Or... was I just taking it out on him?

It’s been like this lately. I’m becoming more demanding with my subordinates and more micro-managing with my wife. This morning, I was looking at her blood pressure logs again; a single high reading and I started feeling restless. I spent another whole night in the tool shed, numbering all the precision tools and documenting their uses. I’m terrified that one day I’ll suddenly... lose the ability to move, and these things will become completely useless because no one knows how to handle them.

Two years until retirement. Two years.

I know what I’m trying to do. I’m trying to control things. Control the work, the standards, everything at home—even the aging of my own body. It’s as if by managing every tiny detail, I can prove I’m still useful, that I can still... what?

Wang Gang has to come in for work tomorrow. I need to figure out how to handle this. Not for his sake, but because... I don’t want to become this kind of person. I don’t want to use harshness to mask my own fear.

But I don’t know how to change.

How much longer can I do this? How much longer can I keep things under control like this?

ranked_items.content:
# Office Memo

**April 5, 2022, 2:20 PM**

---

My fingers are tapping the desk again. I can’t stop.

I was too harsh with Wang Gang just now. His suggestion regarding the inspection process was actually fine; why did I have to insist on pointing out his lack of precision? His expression changed—I saw it—but I didn't apologize. I just kept talking about my standards.

Why is it always like this?

During the afternoon meeting, I lowered the third-quarter defect rate target again. From 0.8 to 0.6. No one dared to object, but I saw Li Qiang’s pen tear through the paper while he was taking notes. He’s afraid. Someone else in my department will quit. I know it.

But I can’t control myself.

The physical exam report is still in the drawer. Blood pressure 139/92. The doctor said I need to be careful. I’m monitoring Li Meifang’s salt intake, yet I—never mind.

I wiped down the tools in the workshop again today. Screwdrivers, calipers, micrometers. Every single one is recorded in the logbook. Use, specifications, year of purchase. Chen Hao called to say his mortgage went up again; I listened to him finish and then just said, "Cut back on your spending." He didn't say anything else. It occurred to me that he might have wanted my help, but I didn't ask.

What use are these tools, anyway?

Two years until retirement. What exactly am I planning for?

Li Meifang said she wants braised pork for dinner. I’m already thinking about how to convince her to eat something lighter. She’ll listen to me, as she always does. But the way she looks at me—I’ve noticed it—she’s avoiding my eyes.

When did I become this kind of person?

Fingers still tapping. Tap. Tap. Tap.

I need to write this down. While I still can.

---

*Check tomorrow’s quality inspection standards before leaving. Might need to adjust them one more time.*

**Date:** Monday, March 28, 2022

**Weather:** Overcast

---

## Work Record

**Morning:** Completed the Q1 quality inspection data summary. The defect rate stands at 0.8%, down 0.2% quarter-on-quarter. Coordinated next week’s production capacity plan with the Production Department; order volume remains stable.

**Afternoon:** Audited the operating procedures of the newly hired inspectors and identified three non-compliances, which were corrected on the spot. Proposed a process optimization plan during the departmental meeting regarding batch processing logic for incoming inspections; it is expected to improve efficiency by approximately 12%. My supervisor indicated that it would be included in the upcoming review.

**Evening:** Organized the tool inventory and added some instructional notes for several older tools.

---

**Mood Index:** 3/10

**Stress Level:** 7/10

**Physical Condition:** Normal

I asked a few questions, but the communication didn't seem to achieve the desired effect. He hung up after a few words, claiming he still had client files to process.

After hanging up, I stood in the living room for a while.

ranked_items.content:
April 15, 2022. Evening.

Another entry today. Li Meifang added too much salt to the food at dinner. I tapped my bowl with my chopsticks. Her hand gave a little tremor. I got up, walked to the kitchen, took out my notebook, and wrote: "Added salt at dinner; blood pressure management inadequate."

She said she was afraid.

I didn't comfort her. I told her that data doesn't lie, and that this was for her own good. She kept her head down and didn't say another word.

It’s 7:30 PM now. She’s in the living room watching TV; I’m in the study. My hand is still trembling slightly.

It’s not out of anger. I have to admit, it’s not.

I’m thinking: if I can’t even manage her blood pressure, what else can I manage? Process optimization, quality standards—I can do all of that at work. But at home? She’s been used to my coldness for thirty-five years, and now that I’m using data and records to "care" for her, she’s actually afraid. What does that say?

I flip open my medical report. My blood sugar levels are fluctuating again. The doctor says it’s nothing major, but I know it’s the beginning. My body is weakening; I can feel it. I need to control everything more strictly—diet, routine, data—otherwise, things will spiral out of control.

But I’ve realized I can’t control her. I can’t control time. I can’t control where those tools will eventually end up.

My eldest son is in Shenzhen, my daughter in Guangzhou. Neither of them will learn my craft. I’ve used this notebook to record the purpose of every tool, trying to turn the intangible into words, but halfway through, I stopped and asked myself—what’s the point? Who will read this? Who will care?

I’ll be retiring in two years. What then? No work, no processes to optimize, no data to manage. What will I have left?

She might be crying right now. I can’t hear it, but I know.

Should I go and apologize? But I was right. Her blood pressure *does* need managing. Data *is* objective.

Why does my rationality always hurt her? Why can’t I just say something soft like everyone else?

I keep flipping through the notebook. Page after page of entries. Neat, precise, and utterly cold.

Is this my life?

I put down the pen. My hand is still shaking.

To be continued tomorrow.

ranked_items.content:
It was already past 7 p.m. by the time I got home. My wife was busy in the kitchen; hearing the door, she poked her head out and asked if I’d eaten. I told her I hadn't, and she said the food was still warm.

I set down my briefcase and sat on the sofa. She brought over a bowl of soup. I took two sips and then just sat there, motionless.

As she was clearing the dishes, she asked, "What’s wrong? You seem a bit down today."

I didn't answer directly. After a moment, I finally said, "I didn't play very well today."

"Oh? Did you lose?" she asked.

"I didn't lose. It’s just... my reactions aren't as sharp anymore." I paused. "My chess partner said I was slow today. I felt it myself, too."

She sat down beside me. She didn't say anything, she just stayed there with me.

I spoke again: "Have you noticed anything about me lately?"

"Like what?" she asked.

"Just... feeling a bit tired." I rubbed my temples. "Maybe work has been too intense recently."

Actually, that wasn't what I wanted to say. What I wanted to ask was: Have you noticed me changing? Do you think I'm getting old? But I couldn't bring myself to say those words.

"Then get some rest early," she said. "I'll make you some red bean porridge; it's good for you."

I nodded. She got up and headed for the kitchen. I sat on the sofa, looking at my hands—the knuckles were a bit stiff, the skin becoming loose.

I took out my phone and opened last week’s physical exam report, looking over the data once more. My blood sugar was 0.3 higher than last year, and my blood pressure was fluctuating. I remembered what the doctor said, but I hadn't told my wife.

She was humming a song in the kitchen, her voice soft and gentle. It suddenly occurred to me that her blood pressure had been rising too. We are both changing; we are both aging. The thought made me feel a dull ache.

I turned off the screen, leaned back against the sofa, and closed my eyes.

I still have to go to the office tomorrow. There are still those tools, those records. Time is moving on, and it feels like there is less and less I can do.

# Game Log - May 8, 2022

**Time**: 15:00–16:30  
**Location**: Recreation Room  
**Opponent**: Chess Friend A  

---

## Game Progress

A noticeable pause occurred starting from the 23rd move. I stared at the board for nearly three minutes before making a move. When Friend A asked, "What's wrong?", I told him I’m getting older and my reactions aren't as sharp as they used to be. He remarked that my skill level has always been very stable.

I replied: "It's stable because I’m afraid to change."

We continued playing after that, but I felt a slight heaviness in my chest.

---

## Physical Status Log

- Reaction time has significantly lengthened (usually I move within a minute; today most moves took over two minutes).
- Eyes felt a bit strained; had to adjust my distance to see the board clearly.
- Felt fatigued throughout the afternoon; lower back started aching after sitting for 90 minutes.
- No obvious dizziness or palpitations; blood pressure should be fine.

---

## Miscellaneous

Friends B and C were also there; they didn't say anything. I lost this game.

Need to check last week’s physical exam report when I get home—take another look at that blood sugar reading. My wife said we’ll have a light dinner; her blood pressure is a bit high again.

Will continue logging tomorrow.

**Date: Monday, March 21, 2022**

**Weather: Overcast**

---

**Morning Records:**

8:30 Arrived at the office. Reviewed last Friday’s QC reports and found that three products from Line B had dimensional deviations exceeding the standard by 0.2mm. This is unacceptable. Immediately called a meeting with the relevant personnel; the root cause must be identified today. There is no compromise on quality.

9:15 Discussed process issues with Master Li. His explanation was still too superficial. I pointed out the problem directly—it’s not a machine issue, but a failure to strictly follow operating procedures. This kind of low-level error should not occur in our department. Gave him a clear deadline: an improvement plan must be on my desk by this Friday. No room for negotiation.

10:00 Compiled the first draft of this month’s improvement proposals. I plan to submit four items to my superiors:
- Optimization of calibration cycles for testing equipment
- Upgrading classification standards for raw material warehousing
- Refining the skill assessment system for subordinates
- Preventive maintenance plan for the production lines

These are all necessary. We cannot slack off.

**Midday Notes:**

During lunch, it occurred to me that if we maintain current efficiency levels, we could reduce our defect rate by another 3-5%. But the prerequisite is that everyone must execute the standards to the letter. No one can be the weak link.

**Afternoon Plan:**

- 14:00 On-site production inspection
- 15:30 Monthly summary meeting with the QC team
- 16:30 Organize tool inventory (Need to document the specific uses of those precision tools in my home workshop, just in case—never mind, just get them recorded for now)

**Other:**

Lately, I feel the work pace needs to be tightened further. There can be no laxity. Time passes quickly; what can be done must be done early. Efficiency is paramount.

My wife mentioned yesterday that her blood pressure is a bit high again. Reminded her to take her medicine on time and stop eating so much salty food. These small matters must also be managed properly.

---

**Remarks:**

Focus on the improvement progress of Line B next week. A standard is a standard; there is no room for bargaining.

ranked_items.content:
# May 8, 2022, past 10 PM

Sitting in the study, it’s already very quiet outside. Meifang fell asleep a long while ago; I can hear her light snoring drifting in from the bedroom. Her blood pressure was a bit high again today. I saw the reading she took this afternoon, but I didn't tell her.

What happened at the chess room today keeps spinning in my head.

Midway through the game, I suddenly realized I was staring blankly at the board. My opponent asked what I was thinking about, and I said, "I'm getting older; my reactions aren't as sharp anymore." As soon as the words left my mouth, they felt foreign to me. I wouldn't have said that before. I would have said, "This is an interesting move," or I wouldn't have explained at all and just played the piece.

But today, I was just slow. The calculations in my head felt sluggish. I could feel it.

Player A tried to comfort me, saying my game has always been stable. I heard myself reply: "It’s stable because I’m afraid to change."

I regretted saying it immediately. Not because it wasn't true, but because it was *too* true. I’ve never been that blunt before. Stable... yes, I’ve always chased stability. Forty years, the same factory, the same way of working, the same rhythm of life. No job-hopping, no risks, every step planned out.

That’s not stability. That’s stagnation.

I left the chess room halfway through. That has never happened before. My friends were a bit surprised; I told them I was tired and needed to go home. As I turned to leave, I looked back at that place—the familiar boards, the familiar faces, everything so familiar. My eyes stung a little.

Why is this happening?

The tools in the workshop are still there. All these years, I’ve been wiping them down and organizing them, like I’m looking after something precious. But Hao is in real estate in Shenzhen, and Yu is teaching in Guangzhou. They’ve never asked what those tools are for. Once, I tried to explain the use of a specific drill bit to Hao, but before I could finish, he said, "Dad, I’ve got a client I need to call back."

I’m recording the purpose of every tool in a notebook. Writing them down, one by one. But halfway through, I asked myself—what’s the point? Who is ever going to look at this notebook?

The physical exam report is still sitting in the drawer. The doctor said those figures "require attention." Every two days, I take it out and look at it, staring at the numbers, trying to find some kind of pattern. As if by doing that, I could control something. But the truth is, I can’t control anything. Blood pressure rises, blood sugar fluctuates, reactions slow down. These things are happening, and all I can do is record them.

Meifang’s blood pressure is rising too. I’m monitoring her diet and pushing her to get checkups. But what if one day I can’t move either? In our thirty-five years together, we’ve said too little. Now, even if I want to make up for it, I can't.

How much time is left?

I don't know. But I can feel time accelerating. It’s as if I used to have plenty of time to do something, and now I suddenly realize it’s gone. Retirement in two years, and then what? Empty days, no work, no one wanting anything from me, no one inheriting anything I know.

I sit here, pen in hand, but I don’t know what to write.

Maybe it’s best to just keep working tomorrow. At least when I’m working, I still know what I’m doing.

ranked_items.content:
April 28, 2022, 11:15 PM

I’m sitting in the study. The lights are off. It’s pitch black outside the window.

I’ve been sitting here in the dark for a long time. My hand is pressed against my chest; I can feel my heartbeat. The rhythm is irregular. My breathing is a bit shallow, but I haven't moved. I just sit here, feeling it all.

The physical exam report is still on the desk. Blood pressure 139/88, fasting blood sugar 6.8. There’s a waveform on the ECG that the doctor said needs observation. He used the phrase "requires attention." I heard him loud and clear.

I’m thinking: this is how it begins.

I press my fingers against my pulse and count—76 beats per minute. Within the normal range. But I know it was racing just a moment ago. Why did it speed up? No exercise, nothing special happened. I was just sitting here when I suddenly felt... something I can't put into words.

Hao called today. He said the mortgage pressure is heavy and he might have to pay back a little less this month. I didn't say much; I just noted it down in my ledger. The mortgage, his sales commissions, the fluctuations of Shenzhen’s housing prices. I track all these data points. But what’s the use of tracking? Can I change anything?

The tools in the workshop are still on the shelves. Lately, I’ve been numbering them, writing down their uses and the years they were purchased. Some were bought in 1995, others in 2008. I wonder, if I were suddenly gone, who would know how to use these things? Not Hao. Not Yu. And certainly not Meifang.

They would just become scrap metal. Or be tossed out as junk.

I’ve been sitting in the dark for a long time, without turning on the light.

Tomorrow, I need to schedule a more detailed check-up. Cardiology. I need to know exactly what that abnormal waveform means. I need to establish a long-term monitoring plan. Every month’s data must be recorded so I can see the trends.

I also need to keep supervising Meifang’s blood pressure. She’s started eating salty foods again; I have to remind her once more.

My breathing has settled down a bit now.

But that sense of unease remains.

It’s as if something is slowly coming loose, and I can’t reach out to catch it.

# May 11, Sunny

The physical exam report came back.

Blood pressure: 138/88. Higher than the 133/85 back in 2020. I did the math—systolic pressure is up by 3.7%. I wrote the numbers down on paper and calculated it again. It is indeed 3.7%.

Meifang has been taking blood pressure medication lately, the kind I bought for her. She says she doesn't feel much, just gets dizzy sometimes. I didn't say anything, but I made a mental note—I must urge her to go for another checkup next month. Her blood pressure is higher than mine.

Sitting in the office, I organized the last five years of my physical exam data into a table. 2018, 2020, 2022. The numbers lined up in a row on the paper. Looking at them, a strange question crossed my mind—why am I documenting my own aging with such precision?

During the meeting this afternoon, I proposed a process improvement plan. The new quality inspector hadn't been thorough enough, so I pointed it out in front of everyone. He turned red. I knew he was uncomfortable, but standards are standards; you can't lower requirements just to avoid hurting feelings. That has always been my principle.

But sitting in my office later, it suddenly occurred to me—how much longer can I keep demanding this level of rigor from others?

I wiped down the tools in the workshop once more. That vernier caliper I bought in 1985 is still as accurate as ever. In my notebook, I wrote down its purpose, precision, and range of application. I’ve already documented over thirty items. Hao is busy selling real estate in Shenzhen, and Yu is teaching in Guangzhou. Neither of them will ever use these things.

I asked myself—why keep recording?

But I kept recording anyway. I poked a small hole in the corner of the notebook with the tip of my pen. Not with force, just a gentle poke. I did it several times.

Li Meifang asked me what I wanted for dinner. I said "whatever." She said we’d have something light then. I nodded.

Two years until retirement. The plan hasn't changed. But I feel the plan becoming stricter—every item must be more detailed, more precise, more intolerant of error. It’s as if I’m using more control to fight against something. But it doesn't change anything.

Time is still slipping away.

3.7%.

That’s all.

# Memo - Abnormal Treadmill Heart Rate Data Log

**Date:** June 2, 2022  
**Time:** 10:15 AM  
**Location:** Home Gym

---

## Incident Log

Abnormal heart rate data was detected during this morning's treadmill workout.

**Machine Displayed Data:**
- Heart Rate: 135 bpm
- Duration: 8 minutes
- Speed: 6.5 km/h

**Manual Verification (Pulse Count Method):**
- Pulse count within 15 seconds: 19 beats
- Calculated Heart Rate: 76 bpm
- Variance: 59 bpm

**Exercise stopped immediately. Re-measured after 5 minutes of rest:**
- Machine Display: 118 bpm
- Manual Count: 72 bpm
- Variance: 46 bpm

---

## Analysis of Potential Causes

1. **Poor Sensor Contact** — Most Likely
   - Need to check if the electrode plates are making proper contact.
   - Consider cleaning the sensor surfaces.

2. **Machine Calibration Error** — Requires Investigation
   - Last maintenance: March 2022.
   - Suggest contacting the manufacturer for calibration.

3. **Measurement Error** — Low Probability
   - Manual counting may have a deviation of ±5 bpm.
   - However, a 59 bpm variance is well beyond the normal range.

---

## Follow-up Actions

- [ ] Inspect treadmill sensor contact condition.
- [ ] Record heart rate data comparisons for the coming week.
- [ ] Reference May medical checkup report: Resting heart rate 72 bpm (Normal range).
- [ ] Consult a doctor if abnormalities persist.
- [ ] Schedule a maintenance check for the treadmill.

---

**Notes:** Currently leaning towards a machine malfunction rather than a physical health issue. The data discrepancy is too large and does not align with normal physiological patterns. Will continue to monitor.

# Work Log

**Date:** April 5, 2022  
**Recorded by:** Chen Guilin  
**Department:** Production Quality Management

---

## Morning Summary

At 9:30 AM, a departmental meeting was held to review quality inspection standards. The primary focus was checking the formatting compliance of recently submitted quality inspection reports.

**Issues Identified:** The third batch product inspection report submitted by inspector Wang Gang showed significant formatting non-compliance. Specific issues included:
- Missing mandatory timestamps in the data record columns.
- Vague descriptions in the "Abnormal Items" section, failing to meet the requirements of Article 4.2 of the *Quality Inspection Report Standardization Template v2.1*.
- Incomplete information in the signature fields.

**Actions Taken:**  
I pointed out these issues publicly and emphasized the necessity of standardized formatting. The core of quality management lies in an obsession with detail—this is not a trivial matter; it is a manifestation of professionalism. If we cannot even control basic report formatting, how can we ensure the credibility and traceability of our data?

Wang Gang attempted to explain the reasons, but that is beside the point. Standards are standards; there is no room for negotiation in execution. I have ordered him to resubmit a compliant version by 3:00 PM tomorrow.

---

## Afternoon Schedule

- 14:00-14:30: Coordinate this week's production capacity plan with the Production Department.
- 14:30-15:30: Audit report samples from other inspectors (spot check of 10 reports).
- 16:00: Compile the first draft of this month's process improvement plan, expected to be submitted to superiors next week.

---

## Remarks

The department's defect rate remains at 0.8% this month, continuing to stay below the industry average. However, personnel execution needs further strengthening. Professionalism is non-negotiable.

# MEMO

**Date:** May 18, 2022, 8:45 PM
**Subject:** Li Meifang’s Dietary Management – Implementation of Low-Sodium Diet Plan

---

## Incident Log

During dinner, I noticed Li Meifang adding another spoonful of salt to the braised fish. When asked why, she said, "It needs it for the flavor." I didn't say much at the time, but I immediately thought of last week’s physical exam report—her blood pressure was 139/88, up 5 points from last month.

This is not a trivial matter.

## Data Correlation

- Last month’s BP: 134/83
- This month’s BP: 139/88
- Fluctuation: +5 mmHg (Systolic)
- Likely triggers: Excessive sodium intake + irregular sleep schedule

A spoonful of salt may seem insignificant, but the cumulative effect is clear. Intervention is necessary.

## Action Plan

**Phase 1 (This week):**
- Purchase low-sodium salt to replace the current kitchen salt.
- Develop daily menus to limit sodium intake to ≤ 2000mg.
- Record daily blood pressure readings (once in the morning, once in the evening).

**Phase 2 (Starting next week):**
- Prepare a list of low-sodium recipes (3 rotating sets each for breakfast, lunch, and dinner).
- Reduce the use of pickled foods and sauces.
- Ensure she takes her blood pressure medication on time.

**Monitoring Schedule:**
- Check the seasoning of dishes every day before dinner.
- Record blood pressure data every Monday and Thursday.
- Compare results during next month’s follow-up.

## Points of Concern

The issue with Li Meifang is that she doesn't see this as a problem. She says, "It's just a little salt," but she doesn't understand the cumulative effect. I need her to realize that this isn't me being nitpicky—it's what the data shows.

I’ve already told her that I will help her implement this plan. It’s not about her keeping track; it’s about me taking control. That is the only way to ensure it works.

## Next Steps

A follow-up blood pressure check is scheduled for May 25. If it’s still rising, we’ll need to consider increasing her exercise or adjusting her medication. But first, the diet must be brought under control.

Time is of the essence. We need to manage these details while we still can.

---

**Note:** Remind Li Meifang tomorrow morning about her regular check-up. She always procrastinates, but we can't let it slide this time.

ranked_items.subquestion: Back during that early medical checkup, if you hadn't moved the date from the 25th to the 15th and had waited those extra ten days as originally planned, do you think that sense of anxiety would have eased a little, or would it have made you even more restless?

ranked_items.content:
July 14, 2022, 3:00 PM

Just got off the phone with the hospital. I moved my physical exam from July 25th up to tomorrow, July 15th.

The operator asked why I wanted to move it forward. I told her there was no special reason, just that I’m relatively free right now. That was a lie. I knew I was lying, but I couldn't explain the real reason. Maybe there isn't a reason at all, or maybe there are too many.

*The sooner it’s done, the better.* This sentence keeps looping in my head. It’s as if once I finish the check-up tomorrow and get the report, everything will be fine. But that logic doesn't hold up. I know that perfectly well.

My productivity has been low these past two days. I had a quality review meeting this morning and found myself repeating the same things; the expressions on my subordinates' faces told me they noticed it too. When I got back to the office this afternoon, I was supposed to organize some process documentation, but I sat there for half an hour without typing a single word. My appetite isn't great either; I only ate half of my lunch. Li Meifang asked if I was feeling unwell. I said no, I’m just not hungry.

My fingers have been tapping on the desk all afternoon. *Tap, tap, tap.* The rhythm is fast and restless. I can’t stand the sound of it myself, but I can’t stop. This isn't like me. I can usually control these things.

In the evening, I flipped through that tool logbook in the workshop. Looking at those dense notes, it suddenly felt a bit absurd. What am I recording? Who is going to use these tools? My eldest son is selling his house, and my daughter is teaching. Neither of them needs any of this. So why am I still keeping records?

I caught a glimpse of myself in the mirror. I look a bit pale.

The results will be out tomorrow afternoon. My heart is racing, but I tell myself it’s a normal physiological response. Just nerves. But why am I nervous? I should be feeling confident.

Whatever. Time for bed. Waking up at 6:00 AM tomorrow.
ranked_items.subquestion: Back during that early medical checkup, if you hadn't moved the date from the 25th to the 15th and had waited those extra ten days as originally planned, do you think that sense of anxiety would have eased a little, or would it have made you even more restless?

ranked_items.content:
April 28, 2022, 11:15 PM

I’m sitting in the study. The lights are off. It’s pitch black outside the window.

I’ve been sitting here in the dark for a long time. My hand is pressed against my chest; I can feel my heartbeat. The rhythm is irregular. My breathing is a bit shallow, but I haven't moved. I just sit here, feeling it all.

The physical exam report is still on the desk. Blood pressure 139/88, fasting blood sugar 6.8. There’s a waveform on the ECG that the doctor said needs observation. He used the phrase "requires attention." I heard him loud and clear.

I’m thinking: this is how it begins.

I press my fingers against my pulse and count—76 beats per minute. Within the normal range. But I know it was racing just a moment ago. Why did it speed up? No exercise, nothing special happened. I was just sitting here when I suddenly felt... something I can't put into words.

Hao called today. He said the mortgage pressure is heavy and he might have to pay back a little less this month. I didn't say much; I just noted it down in my ledger. The mortgage, his sales commissions, the fluctuations of Shenzhen’s housing prices. I track all these data points. But what’s the use of tracking? Can I change anything?

The tools in the workshop are still on the shelves. Lately, I’ve been numbering them, writing down their uses and the years they were purchased. Some were bought in 1995, others in 2008. I wonder, if I were suddenly gone, who would know how to use these things? Not Hao. Not Yu. And certainly not Meifang.

They would just become scrap metal. Or be tossed out as junk.

I’ve been sitting in the dark for a long time, without turning on the light.

Tomorrow, I need to schedule a more detailed check-up. Cardiology. I need to know exactly what that abnormal waveform means. I need to establish a long-term monitoring plan. Every month’s data must be recorded so I can see the trends.

I also need to keep supervising Meifang’s blood pressure. She’s started eating salty foods again; I have to remind her once more.

My breathing has settled down a bit now.

But that sense of unease remains.

It’s as if something is slowly coming loose, and I can’t reach out to catch it.

ranked_items.content:
# May 8, 2022, past 10 PM

Sitting in the study, it’s already very quiet outside. Meifang fell asleep a long while ago; I can hear her light snoring drifting in from the bedroom. Her blood pressure was a bit high again today. I saw the reading she took this afternoon, but I didn't tell her.

What happened at the chess room today keeps spinning in my head.

Midway through the game, I suddenly realized I was staring blankly at the board. My opponent asked what I was thinking about, and I said, "I'm getting older; my reactions aren't as sharp anymore." As soon as the words left my mouth, they felt foreign to me. I wouldn't have said that before. I would have said, "This is an interesting move," or I wouldn't have explained at all and just played the piece.

But today, I was just slow. The calculations in my head felt sluggish. I could feel it.

Player A tried to comfort me, saying my game has always been stable. I heard myself reply: "It’s stable because I’m afraid to change."

I regretted saying it immediately. Not because it wasn't true, but because it was *too* true. I’ve never been that blunt before. Stable... yes, I’ve always chased stability. Forty years, the same factory, the same way of working, the same rhythm of life. No job-hopping, no risks, every step planned out.

That’s not stability. That’s stagnation.

I left the chess room halfway through. That has never happened before. My friends were a bit surprised; I told them I was tired and needed to go home. As I turned to leave, I looked back at that place—the familiar boards, the familiar faces, everything so familiar. My eyes stung a little.

Why is this happening?

The tools in the workshop are still there. All these years, I’ve been wiping them down and organizing them, like I’m looking after something precious. But Hao is in real estate in Shenzhen, and Yu is teaching in Guangzhou. They’ve never asked what those tools are for. Once, I tried to explain the use of a specific drill bit to Hao, but before I could finish, he said, "Dad, I’ve got a client I need to call back."

I’m recording the purpose of every tool in a notebook. Writing them down, one by one. But halfway through, I asked myself—what’s the point? Who is ever going to look at this notebook?

The physical exam report is still sitting in the drawer. The doctor said those figures "require attention." Every two days, I take it out and look at it, staring at the numbers, trying to find some kind of pattern. As if by doing that, I could control something. But the truth is, I can’t control anything. Blood pressure rises, blood sugar fluctuates, reactions slow down. These things are happening, and all I can do is record them.

Meifang’s blood pressure is rising too. I’m monitoring her diet and pushing her to get checkups. But what if one day I can’t move either? In our thirty-five years together, we’ve said too little. Now, even if I want to make up for it, I can't.

How much time is left?

I don't know. But I can feel time accelerating. It’s as if I used to have plenty of time to do something, and now I suddenly realize it’s gone. Retirement in two years, and then what? Empty days, no work, no one wanting anything from me, no one inheriting anything I know.

I sit here, pen in hand, but I don’t know what to write.

Maybe it’s best to just keep working tomorrow. At least when I’m working, I still know what I’m doing.

I stared at the board for nearly two minutes before making a move—something that never used to happen. My fingers were resting on the piece, but my mind was elsewhere—thinking about those figures in last week’s physical exam report, thinking about the dizzy spells I’ve been having some mornings.

Started my morning run at 6:30 AM. At the 8-minute mark, the treadmill showed my heart rate suddenly jumping to 128, which didn't feel right. **Date: July 14, 2022**

## I. Preparation for Tomorrow (Morning of July 15)

- [ ] Fast (no food or water) after 21:00 tonight
- [ ] Prepare ID card, medical insurance card, and previous checkup reports
- [ ] Wear loose clothing for easy blood draws and examinations
- [ ] Arrive at the hospital 15 minutes early
- [ ] Bring a notebook to record all doctor's advice
- [ ] Confirm if the Cardiology appointment is booked

---

## II. Checkup Item Checklist (Verify all are covered)

- [ ] Blood pressure measurement
- [ ] ECG (Electrocardiogram)
- [ ] Chest X-ray
- [ ] Blood tests (Blood sugar, lipids, liver and kidney function)
- [ ] Urinalysis
- [ ] Echocardiogram (Priority focus)
- [ ] Cervical spine X-ray

---

## III. Post-Checkup Follow-up Plan (Afternoon of July 15)

**Same-day Afternoon:**
- [ ] Collect preliminary results
- [ ] Record all data and the doctor’s exact words
- [ ] Consult the doctor immediately regarding any abnormal indicators
- [ ] Take photos to save the report sheets

**By July 20:**
- [ ] Collect the full report
- [ ] Compare with last year’s data and list all changes
- [ ] Schedule a Cardiology follow-up (if necessary)
- [ ] Organize data into a spreadsheet for archiving

---

## IV. Potential Outcome Scenarios

**Scenario A - All Normal:**
- Maintain current lifestyle pace
- Regular follow-up (same time next year)
- Maintain current diet and exercise routine

**Scenario B - Minor Abnormalities (e.g., high blood pressure/blood sugar):**
- Adjust diet plan (supervise Li Meifang to implement simultaneously)
- Increase exercise frequency
- Re-examine in 3 months

**Scenario C - Further Treatment Required:**
- Consult a specialist immediately
- Develop a medication plan
- Evaluate the impact on retirement plans
- Notify family (Tentative: do not tell wife and children unless absolutely necessary)

---

## V. Communication Plan with Li Meifang

- [ ] Do not proactively mention results for now (to avoid causing her undue worry)
- [ ] If there are abnormalities, discuss them with her privately later
- [ ] Continue to supervise her diet and regular checkups
- [ ] If the doctor suggests lifestyle changes, implement them under the guise of a "Family Health Plan"

---

## VI. Miscellaneous

- [ ] Organize tool records in the notebook within a week after the checkup (while I still have the energy)
- [ ] Check if the medical budget in the family financial plan needs updating
- [ ] Monitor recent company orders—this will affect the retirement timing decision

---

**Notes:**
I’ll know tomorrow. Instead of speculating here, it’s better to list all variables and handle them one by one. 
At least this way, I can stay in control of the situation.

ranked_items.content:
# June 28, 2022, 10:15 PM

The light in the workshop is still on. Li Meifang has already gone to bed. Her blood pressure has been a bit high lately, and she’s still not really drinking the herbal tea I brewed for her. Forget it, I’ll deal with it tomorrow.

I just flipped through my notebook again. I’ve been documenting the tools since last year, and there are over thirty entries now. For each one, I’ve noted its purpose, specifications, and date of purchase. Some of the handwriting has started to fade, so I traced over it again in pencil.

A question suddenly crossed my mind—if my body starts to fail, what will I have left to do?

This question has been circling in my head for a long time. The May physical exam report is still in the drawer; I haven’t looked at it a second time. I remember all the metrics and indicators the doctor mentioned, but I chose not to dwell on them. And now? I still don't want to think too deeply about it, but it seems I can’t avoid it anymore.

My eldest son is in Shenzhen, preoccupied with his mortgage; my daughter is teaching in Guangzhou. I’ve never asked them if they’re interested in learning any of this. Perhaps they lost interest long ago. Perhaps I shouldn't have expected anything from the start.

I added another question mark under the one already in the notebook. Then, I placed the notebook in the most prominent spot on the workbench. I kept staring at it in the darkness. It’s a bit of a foolish gesture, but I just wanted to look at it. I wanted to see if those questions sitting there meant they truly existed, and truly needed to be resolved.

*While I can still move, I have to hurry.* This phrase keeps popping up now.

I proposed four more process improvement plans at work this year, and all of them were adopted. My subordinates are still afraid of me. A new QC inspector made three mistakes last week that I pointed out in front of everyone; now he’s all submissive whenever he sees me. I know it’s not right, but I can’t help myself. Maybe I’m using this as a way to fight back against something—against time, against powerlessness, against that growing realization: I am losing control over everything.

Li Meifang says I’ve become even quieter lately. She’s right. What else is there to say?

Tomorrow, I’ll continue documenting the tools. There are still seven or eight left to write down.

Must hurry.

ranked_items.content:
# Monday, July 18, 2022, 14:30

It’s been two hours since I got the report.

I’m sitting in my office with the report spread out on the desk. I told everyone "it’s nothing serious," but my fingers keep tracing those marked spots—blood pressure 139/92, fasting blood glucose 6.8. The numbers themselves don’t lie. I know that better than anyone.

I heard everything the doctor said, but I chose to remember only the first half of his sentences. "It’s under decent control." The rest—the parts about "needing to be more cautious" and "regular follow-ups"—I just let slide. That’s not my style.

Meifang was cooking when I got home. She saw the look on my face and asked about it. I didn't give her a straight answer, just said "I'm fine." But I know she can tell. After 35 years, she knows me too well. She tried to come closer, wanting me to talk, but I turned away and went into the study.

Then I suddenly turned back around and said to her, "Starting today, you need to cooperate too. We can't let your blood pressure issues drag on anymore. I’m going to set up a plan for you."

She was startled by my tone. I saw it. But I didn't stop. I need to control something. I need to ensure that something is within my grasp.

She isn't the problem. I know that. But at this moment, I can't face the fact that *I* am the problem.

Data doesn't lie. I’ve lived my whole life by data and standards, and now the data is starting to betray me. Blood pressure, blood sugar, weight—every indicator is slowly crossing the line. I can see the trend. I can predict it. And that is exactly what’s so terrifying.

The two-year retirement plan. I spoke of it with such certainty. But now I’m starting to wonder: if my body continues like this, how many "two years" do I have left?

The tools in the workshop are still there. I wanted to go and wipe them down, as I usually do. But today, I didn't move. I sat in my office, staring at this report, trying to use logic to convince myself that this is all within manageable limits.

But I know that some things are forever beyond my control.

For the first time, I truly felt that.

ranked_items.content:
It was already past 7 p.m. by the time I got home. My wife was busy in the kitchen; hearing the door, she poked her head out and asked if I’d eaten. I told her I hadn't, and she said the food was still warm.

I set down my briefcase and sat on the sofa. She brought over a bowl of soup. I took two sips and then just sat there, motionless.

As she was clearing the dishes, she asked, "What’s wrong? You seem a bit down today."

I didn't answer directly. After a moment, I finally said, "I didn't play very well today."

"Oh? Did you lose?" she asked.

"I didn't lose. It’s just... my reactions aren't as sharp anymore." I paused. "My chess partner said I was slow today. I felt it myself, too."

She sat down beside me. She didn't say anything, she just stayed there with me.

I spoke again: "Have you noticed anything about me lately?"

"Like what?" she asked.

"Just... feeling a bit tired." I rubbed my temples. "Maybe work has been too intense recently."

Actually, that wasn't what I wanted to say. What I wanted to ask was: Have you noticed me changing? Do you think I'm getting old? But I couldn't bring myself to say those words.

"Then get some rest early," she said. "I'll make you some red bean porridge; it's good for you."

I nodded. She got up and headed for the kitchen. I sat on the sofa, looking at my hands—the knuckles were a bit stiff, the skin becoming loose.

I took out my phone and opened last week’s physical exam report, looking over the data once more. My blood sugar was 0.3 higher than last year, and my blood pressure was fluctuating. I remembered what the doctor said, but I hadn't told my wife.

She was humming a song in the kitchen, her voice soft and gentle. It suddenly occurred to me that her blood pressure had been rising too. We are both changing; we are both aging. The thought made me feel a dull ache.

I turned off the screen, leaned back against the sofa, and closed my eyes.

I still have to go to the office tomorrow. There are still those tools, those records. Time is moving on, and it feels like there is less and less I can do.

ranked_items.content:
# May 11, Sunny

The physical exam report came back.

Blood pressure: 138/88. Higher than the 133/85 back in 2020. I did the math—systolic pressure is up by 3.7%. I wrote the numbers down on paper and calculated it again. It is indeed 3.7%.

Meifang has been taking blood pressure medication lately, the kind I bought for her. She says she doesn't feel much, just gets dizzy sometimes. I didn't say anything, but I made a mental note—I must urge her to go for another checkup next month. Her blood pressure is higher than mine.

Sitting in the office, I organized the last five years of my physical exam data into a table. 2018, 2020, 2022. The numbers lined up in a row on the paper. Looking at them, a strange question crossed my mind—why am I documenting my own aging with such precision?

During the meeting this afternoon, I proposed a process improvement plan. The new quality inspector hadn't been thorough enough, so I pointed it out in front of everyone. He turned red. I knew he was uncomfortable, but standards are standards; you can't lower requirements just to avoid hurting feelings. That has always been my principle.

But sitting in my office later, it suddenly occurred to me—how much longer can I keep demanding this level of rigor from others?

I wiped down the tools in the workshop once more. That vernier caliper I bought in 1985 is still as accurate as ever. In my notebook, I wrote down its purpose, precision, and range of application. I’ve already documented over thirty items. Hao is busy selling real estate in Shenzhen, and Yu is teaching in Guangzhou. Neither of them will ever use these things.

I asked myself—why keep recording?

But I kept recording anyway. I poked a small hole in the corner of the notebook with the tip of my pen. Not with force, just a gentle poke. I did it several times.

Li Meifang asked me what I wanted for dinner. I said "whatever." She said we’d have something light then. I nodded.

Two years until retirement. The plan hasn't changed. But I feel the plan becoming stricter—every item must be more detailed, more precise, more intolerant of error. It’s as if I’m using more control to fight against something. But it doesn't change anything.

Time is still slipping away.

3.7%.

That’s all.

Haozi called while I was in the workshop. He said business hasn't been great lately and he’s feeling the pressure. I asked if he’d thought about switching gears, but he said he has to stick with real estate. I didn't push it further.

After hanging up, I just sat there, looking at the tools on the wall. Some were left by my father; others I’ve picked up myself over the years. It took me nearly a month to number them and document what each one is for. I only finished the last page yesterday.

I’ve been wondering what will happen to all this stuff in the end. Haozi has no interest in them, and Yu’er wouldn't know how to use them. No matter how detailed my notes are, it doesn't seem like it’ll change anything.

I have to go back to the hospital for a follow-up tomorrow. The doctor said I need regular monitoring. I didn’t tell you because there wasn’t much to say—it’s just a routine check-up.

How is your blood pressure? Have you been taking your medicine on time lately? I’ll go to the hospital with you this weekend to pick up that test report.

—Chen Guilin

ranked_items.content:
June 15, 2022, 7:30 PM

I lost again at the chess room this afternoon. It wasn't that I lost to Player A; I lost to myself.

Player A said I played very conservatively today. The word he used was "cautious," but I could hear what he really meant—he was saying I’ve changed. I didn’t argue. I just said, "I’m getting older, I have to be more careful," and kept setting up the pieces. He didn’t say anything else.

But I know he saw it. I saw it in myself, too.

I used to dare to take risks, to make bold moves in the mid-game. And now? Now I pause and think for a long time before every single step. It’s not because I’ve become smarter; it’s because I’m afraid—afraid that one wrong move will lead to a point of no return. Afraid of wasting time.

It’s strange. A game only lasts two hours, and losing is just losing a game. But I find myself applying this same logic to everything in my life. I’m stricter with my subordinates at work, I’m more controlling over Meifang’s diet at home, and I even use a magnifying glass to scrutinize the numbers on my medical reports.

Why?

I tell myself this is being responsible. But deep down, I know it’s fear. Fear that time is running out. Fear that something is slipping through my fingers and I won’t be able to catch it in time.

The tools in the workshop are still there. The notebooks are there, too. I’ve recorded about half of it. Hao is in Shenzhen, and Yu is in Guangzhou. Neither of them has ever asked what those tools are for. I haven't brought it up, either.

Sometimes I wonder, living this cautiously—what exactly am I trying to protect?

I have to go back to the chess room tomorrow. I wonder if Player A will notice something else.

Forget it, stop thinking. Time for bed. I haven't finished going through the medical report from May; I’ll give it another careful look tomorrow.

ranked_items.content:
April 15, 2022. Evening.

Another entry today. Li Meifang added too much salt to the food at dinner. I tapped my bowl with my chopsticks. Her hand gave a little tremor. I got up, walked to the kitchen, took out my notebook, and wrote: "Added salt at dinner; blood pressure management inadequate."

She said she was afraid.

I didn't comfort her. I told her that data doesn't lie, and that this was for her own good. She kept her head down and didn't say another word.

It’s 7:30 PM now. She’s in the living room watching TV; I’m in the study. My hand is still trembling slightly.

It’s not out of anger. I have to admit, it’s not.

I’m thinking: if I can’t even manage her blood pressure, what else can I manage? Process optimization, quality standards—I can do all of that at work. But at home? She’s been used to my coldness for thirty-five years, and now that I’m using data and records to "care" for her, she’s actually afraid. What does that say?

I flip open my medical report. My blood sugar levels are fluctuating again. The doctor says it’s nothing major, but I know it’s the beginning. My body is weakening; I can feel it. I need to control everything more strictly—diet, routine, data—otherwise, things will spiral out of control.

But I’ve realized I can’t control her. I can’t control time. I can’t control where those tools will eventually end up.

My eldest son is in Shenzhen, my daughter in Guangzhou. Neither of them will learn my craft. I’ve used this notebook to record the purpose of every tool, trying to turn the intangible into words, but halfway through, I stopped and asked myself—what’s the point? Who will read this? Who will care?

I’ll be retiring in two years. What then? No work, no processes to optimize, no data to manage. What will I have left?

She might be crying right now. I can’t hear it, but I know.

Should I go and apologize? But I was right. Her blood pressure *does* need managing. Data *is* objective.

Why does my rationality always hurt her? Why can’t I just say something soft like everyone else?

I keep flipping through the notebook. Page after page of entries. Neat, precise, and utterly cold.

Is this my life?

I put down the pen. My hand is still shaking.

# MEMO

**Date:** May 18, 2022, 8:45 PM
**Subject:** Li Meifang’s Dietary Management – Implementation of Low-Sodium Diet Plan

---

## Incident Log

During dinner, I noticed Li Meifang adding another spoonful of salt to the braised fish. When asked why, she said, "It needs it for the flavor." I didn't say much at the time, but I immediately thought of last week’s physical exam report—her blood pressure was 139/88, up 5 points from last month.

This is not a trivial matter.

## Data Correlation

- Last month’s BP: 134/83
- This month’s BP: 139/88
- Fluctuation: +5 mmHg (Systolic)
- Likely triggers: Excessive sodium intake + irregular sleep schedule

A spoonful of salt may seem insignificant, but the cumulative effect is clear. Intervention is necessary.

## Action Plan

**Phase 1 (This week):**
- Purchase low-sodium salt to replace the current kitchen salt.
- Develop daily menus to limit sodium intake to ≤ 2000mg.
- Record daily blood pressure readings (once in the morning, once in the evening).

**Phase 2 (Starting next week):**
- Prepare a list of low-sodium recipes (3 rotating sets each for breakfast, lunch, and dinner).
- Reduce the use of pickled foods and sauces.
- Ensure she takes her blood pressure medication on time.

**Monitoring Schedule:**
- Check the seasoning of dishes every day before dinner.
- Record blood pressure data every Monday and Thursday.
- Compare results during next month’s follow-up.

## Points of Concern

The issue with Li Meifang is that she doesn't see this as a problem. She says, "It's just a little salt," but she doesn't understand the cumulative effect. I need her to realize that this isn't me being nitpicky—it's what the data shows.

I’ve already told her that I will help her implement this plan. It’s not about her keeping track; it’s about me taking control. That is the only way to ensure it works.

## Next Steps

A follow-up blood pressure check is scheduled for May 25. If it’s still rising, we’ll need to consider increasing her exercise or adjusting her medication. But first, the diet must be brought under control.

Time is of the essence. We need to manage these details while we still can.

---

**Note:** Remind Li Meifang tomorrow morning about her regular check-up. She always procrastinates, but we can't let it slide this time.

ranked_items.content:
March 20, 2022, 11:47 PM

Sitting in the workshop. My wife is already asleep. The noises downstairs have all quieted down.

I finished page thirty today. Starting from the first page, my handwriting was neat; the purpose, specifications, and purchase date of every tool were recorded clearly. But toward the end, the pen started to shake. I’m not sure when it began—maybe around page twenty-five. The strokes became irregular, and some words looked rushed.

When I got to the entry for the wrench, I stopped for a long time. I bought this wrench in 1998. It’s a Japanese import with a precision of 0.02 mm. I’ve used it for over twenty years, and it shows very little wear. I wanted to record its use, but the pen just hovered there. Then I wrote—"Who will ever use this?"

After writing that sentence, I didn't cross it out. It’s strange. I’m not usually like this. The notebooks on my desk have always been spotless—no scribbles, no traces of hesitation. But this time, looking at that sentence, I just couldn't bring myself to erase it.

Hao is in Shenzhen; Yu is in Guangzhou. Neither of them will ever come to this workshop. Hao is in real estate sales, and Yu teaches. They have their own lives. I never expected them to inherit anything, but sitting here now, looking at these tools, I suddenly realized—these things are about to become useless. Not because they’re broken or lost, but because no one will know how to use them.

This morning, Meifang asked me what I was busy with. I only replied with a grunt. She didn't ask again. She’s used to my silence. After thirty-five years, she’s long known that I’m a man of few words. But when that "mm-hmm" came out today, even I could hear it—it wasn't silence; it was avoidance. I don’t know how to explain to her what I’m doing. I can’t even explain it to myself.

The physical exam report is still in the drawer. My blood sugar is up a bit again. The doctor said I need to be careful. I’m in my fifties now; my body is starting to disobey me. I used to think that as long as I kept things under control and followed the plan, nothing would go wrong. But the body doesn't follow a plan. Neither does time.

I’m a little over a year away from retirement. I’ve been waiting for this day, but now that it’s almost here, I’m starting to feel afraid. Afraid of what I’ll do after I retire. Afraid of being idle. Afraid that these tools will just keep sitting here, unused and misunderstood.

The notebook is still open. "Who will ever use this" is still there on page thirty. Tomorrow, I have to keep writing. There are still many tools left to record. I have to get them all down while I can still move.

But even if I record them, what then?

# Memo - Abnormal Treadmill Heart Rate Data Log

**Date:** June 2, 2022  
**Time:** 10:15 AM  
**Location:** Home Gym

---

## Incident Log

Abnormal heart rate data was detected during this morning's treadmill workout.

**Machine Displayed Data:**
- Heart Rate: 135 bpm
- Duration: 8 minutes
- Speed: 6.5 km/h

**Manual Verification (Pulse Count Method):**
- Pulse count within 15 seconds: 19 beats
- Calculated Heart Rate: 76 bpm
- Variance: 59 bpm

**Exercise stopped immediately. Re-measured after 5 minutes of rest:**
- Machine Display: 118 bpm
- Manual Count: 72 bpm
- Variance: 46 bpm

---

## Analysis of Potential Causes

1. **Poor Sensor Contact** — Most Likely
   - Need to check if the electrode plates are making proper contact.
   - Consider cleaning the sensor surfaces.

2. **Machine Calibration Error** — Requires Investigation
   - Last maintenance: March 2022.
   - Suggest contacting the manufacturer for calibration.

3. **Measurement Error** — Low Probability
   - Manual counting may have a deviation of ±5 bpm.
   - However, a 59 bpm variance is well beyond the normal range.

---

## Follow-up Actions

- [ ] Inspect treadmill sensor contact condition.
- [ ] Record heart rate data comparisons for the coming week.
- [ ] Reference May medical checkup report: Resting heart rate 72 bpm (Normal range).
- [ ] Consult a doctor if abnormalities persist.
- [ ] Schedule a maintenance check for the treadmill.

---

**Notes:** Currently leaning towards a machine malfunction rather than a physical health issue. The data discrepancy is too large and does not align with normal physiological patterns. Will continue to monitor.

ranked_items.content:
# Office Memo

**April 5, 2022, 2:20 PM**

---

My fingers are tapping the desk again. I can’t stop.

I was too harsh with Wang Gang just now. His suggestion regarding the inspection process was actually fine; why did I have to insist on pointing out his lack of precision? His expression changed—I saw it—but I didn't apologize. I just kept talking about my standards.

Why is it always like this?

During the afternoon meeting, I lowered the third-quarter defect rate target again. From 0.8 to 0.6. No one dared to object, but I saw Li Qiang’s pen tear through the paper while he was taking notes. He’s afraid. Someone else in my department will quit. I know it.

But I can’t control myself.

The physical exam report is still in the drawer. Blood pressure 139/92. The doctor said I need to be careful. I’m monitoring Li Meifang’s salt intake, yet I—never mind.

I wiped down the tools in the workshop again today. Screwdrivers, calipers, micrometers. Every single one is recorded in the logbook. Use, specifications, year of purchase. Chen Hao called to say his mortgage went up again; I listened to him finish and then just said, "Cut back on your spending." He didn't say anything else. It occurred to me that he might have wanted my help, but I didn't ask.

What use are these tools, anyway?

Two years until retirement. What exactly am I planning for?

Li Meifang said she wants braised pork for dinner. I’m already thinking about how to convince her to eat something lighter. She’ll listen to me, as she always does. But the way she looks at me—I’ve noticed it—she’s avoiding my eyes.

When did I become this kind of person?

Fingers still tapping. Tap. Tap. Tap.

I need to write this down. While I still can.

---

*Check tomorrow’s quality inspection standards before leaving. Might need to adjust them one more time.*

ranked_items.content:
April 5, 2022. Night.

I’ve been sitting in the office for a long time. Wang Gang’s quality inspection report is still spinning in my head.

The formatting was off again when he handed it in this morning—the classification standards in the header didn't match last year’s specifications. It’s such a basic thing to get wrong. I couldn't help myself; I snapped at him. He tried to explain, but I cut him off. No excuses. Professionalism is about an obsession with detail.

His face changed the moment I said it.

Looking back now, my tone was harsh. Harsher than usual. He’s actually a decent employee. In the past, I would have pointed out these small mistakes, but never like that. Why today...?

My fingers have been tapping on the desk for a long while. Rapidly. I didn't even realize I was doing it.

What am I thinking? Was his report really that outrageous? Or... was I just taking it out on him?

It’s been like this lately. I’m becoming more demanding with my subordinates and more micro-managing with my wife. This morning, I was looking at her blood pressure logs again; a single high reading and I started feeling restless. I spent another whole night in the tool shed, numbering all the precision tools and documenting their uses. I’m terrified that one day I’ll suddenly... lose the ability to move, and these things will become completely useless because no one knows how to handle them.

Two years until retirement. Two years.

I know what I’m trying to do. I’m trying to control things. Control the work, the standards, everything at home—even the aging of my own body. It’s as if by managing every tiny detail, I can prove I’m still useful, that I can still... what?

Wang Gang has to come in for work tomorrow. I need to figure out how to handle this. Not for his sake, but because... I don’t want to become this kind of person. I don’t want to use harshness to mask my own fear.

But I don’t know how to change.

How much longer can I do this? How much longer can I keep things under control like this?

ranked_items.content:
July 10, 2022, 10:47 PM

I pulled out this unused notebook, though I’m not sure why. Maybe it’s because what happened yesterday is still spinning in my head.

I lost my temper yesterday over a spreadsheet formatting issue. Xiao Wang had reversed the order of the data columns, and I pointed it out in front of everyone, using some pretty harsh words. His face went pale instantly. I saw it, but I didn’t stop. Instead, I kept going on and on about how "details determine product quality, and product quality determines the company's survival."

Thinking back now, what was I so angry about? A formatting error. It was that simple.

But it wasn’t, really.

I’ve noticed myself setting stricter standards lately. Stricter inspection processes. Stricter requirements. As if doing this could... could what? I can’t quite put my finger on it. Maybe I’m trying to prove something. Maybe I’m trying to control something.

Li Meifang asked me what was wrong last night, but I didn't answer. She’s long since used to it.

I’ve gone over my May medical report three times now. The doctor said there’s nothing majorly wrong, just that I need to "pay attention." Pay attention to what? Pay attention to time passing. Pay attention to my body changing. Pay attention to how many more years I can keep doing this job.

I reorganized the tools in the workshop again recently. I’m recording the purpose of each one, one by one. Chen Hao won’t use them. Neither will Chen Yu. They have their own lives. I know this, yet I’m still taking notes. Why? While I can still move?

How many years of movement do I have left?

I’m supposed to play chess with my friends next week. I don’t know what to say to them. Lately, when I’m with them, I’ve been pathetically quiet. I was always a man of few words, but now I feel like I can’t even focus on the game. My mind is always elsewhere.

I’m trying to fight something off by exerting more control. I know this. But I can’t stop.

Xiao Wang didn't look at me today. I should say something to him, but I don't know what.

Maybe I’ll delete these words tomorrow. There’s no need to leave this behind.

But for now, I’ll let them stay here.

ranked_items.content:
In the workshop, Chen Guilin sat on a simple wooden stool, a notebook open before him. On the tool rack, the precision instruments glinted coldly under the fluorescent lights. His phone screen lit up—a WeChat message from his wife, Li Meifang: "What do you want for dinner?"

He didn't reply immediately. Instead, he stared at the dense notes in his notebook and the words "Who would use this?" circled in red ink.

Five minutes later, he finally typed:

"Do you think... there's any point in me keeping these records?"

After sending it, he added another line:

"Just thinking about what happens after I retire. These tools will just be sitting there anyway."

He paused for a long time, stopping himself from typing more. His fingers hovered over the keyboard before he finally lowered them.

Then he added one last message:

"I’ve been feeling a bit tired lately. Not the work kind of tired."

---

*Chen Guilin put down his phone and turned back to wiping a precision vernier caliper on the rack. His movements were slow and meticulous, but his eyes were no longer on the tool.*

ranked_items.content:
May 18, 2022, 8:45 PM

I had another minor disagreement with Meifang today over the salt. She added an extra spoonful while simmering the soup; I caught the scent and went over immediately to check. She said, "You can't even taste the difference," and I didn't say much back, but I was thinking—her blood pressure is already at 140/92. I saw the last physical exam report clearly. Just one extra spoonful of salt can cause a five-point spike; it’s not a small matter.

I poured her a glass of water and told her to drink it first. Then I told her I’d be putting together a low-sodium meal plan for her this weekend. She didn’t object; she just gave me a smile—that look of someone who’s grown used to my ways.

While sitting in the workshop, I flipped through the tool log again. I’ve already recorded over thirty items this year, each marked with its purpose and maintenance method. Sometimes I write "Who will use this?" in the margins—a question for myself. Hao is selling real estate in Shenzhen, and Yu is teaching in Guangzhou; neither of them will ever touch these things. I know that.

But I keep recording anyway. While I can still move.

The May physical is coming up. The cardiologist mentioned last time that I need regular follow-ups. I haven't told the family exactly what it's for, just that it's a routine check-up. Meifang asked once, and I said, "Nothing major, just some fluctuations in the data." Honestly, I don't really want to dwell on it myself.

Tonight, I glanced at a WeChat message from Hao. He said the mortgage pressure is a bit heavy this month and asked if I could support him a little more. I transferred five thousand yuan to him without saying much. He replied with a "Thanks, Dad." That was it. I tried to remember the last time he came home—was it three months ago? Or four? I couldn't recall.

I wonder, should I keep doing all this after I retire? Should I keep keeping the accounts? Should I keep polishing the tools? Should I keep managing everything in this house?

I have to get everything done that needs doing, while I still can.

I have to hurry.

ranked_items.content:
**July 10, 2022 Work Log**

**Weather: Sunny  Temperature: 32°C**

**Morning Work Record**

09:15 Quality Control Department weekly meeting. Discovered issues while reviewing yesterday’s production reports.

The periodic inspection sheet submitted by my subordinate, Li, was a mess—he used Chinese capitalized characters for the date column, which violates the company’s unified standards. This is no small matter. I pointed out the error in front of everyone. My tone might have been harsh, but it had to be. Details determine product quality, and product quality determines the company’s survival. This logical chain cannot be allowed to slacken. A formatting error can lead to data misinterpretation; misinterpretation can lead to missed inspections; and a missed inspection means defective products reaching the market. I cannot tolerate a failure in any link of this chain.

Li looked visibly upset at the time. I know he probably thinks I went overboard. But I don’t need him to like me; I need him to understand the importance of standardization.

**Developing New QC Standards**

09:45-11:20 Drafted the new "Detailed Rules for Quality Inspection of Precision Components (Revised Edition)" in my office.

Key adjustments:
- Table formats unified to Arabic numerals and 24-hour clock.
- Added a "Basis for Judgment" column for every inspection item; reference standard numbers must be cited.
- Added an "Auditor Review" step at the inspector’s signature line—a double-confirmation system.
- Data entry deadline moved up to 17:00 daily, leaving 2 hours for data auditing.
- Added a "Deviation Analysis" section to the monthly summary report; reasons and corrective measures for every out-of-spec item must be explained.

None of this is needless bureaucracy. Every single rule points toward the same goal: turning invisible risks into visible data, and turning potential errors into inevitable discoveries.

I plan to hold a departmental meeting next Monday to announce the new standards. Everyone will be given a 3-day transition period, with formal implementation starting July 15.

**Other**

Did not go out for lunch. Stayed in the office to review the draft of the new standards again and refined the wording in two places.

I am meeting with my superior this afternoon to report on this month’s improvement plan. This new set of standards should be adopted.

**Physical Status**

Sleep has been okay lately. No particular discomfort.

My wife mentioned yesterday that her blood pressure has risen again. Reminded her to take her medication on time and sent her a text at noon telling her to use less salt in dinner.

**Remarks**

I feel my demands for work have been increasing lately. It’s not because of new pressure from the company, but rather a feeling that I cannot allow for any slack. I have less than two years until retirement; I want to optimize all processes and set all standards in stone during this time. That way, even after I leave, the system will continue to function properly. This is important.

---

*Recorded by: Chen Guilin*
*Date: 2022-07-10*

ranked_items.content:
# Monday, July 18, 2022, 14:30

It’s been two hours since I got the report.

I’m sitting in my office with the report spread out on the desk. I told everyone "it’s nothing serious," but my fingers keep tracing those marked spots—blood pressure 139/92, fasting blood glucose 6.8. The numbers themselves don’t lie. I know that better than anyone.

I heard everything the doctor said, but I chose to remember only the first half of his sentences. "It’s under decent control." The rest—the parts about "needing to be more cautious" and "regular follow-ups"—I just let slide. That’s not my style.

Meifang was cooking when I got home. She saw the look on my face and asked about it. I didn't give her a straight answer, just said "I'm fine." But I know she can tell. After 35 years, she knows me too well. She tried to come closer, wanting me to talk, but I turned away and went into the study.

Then I suddenly turned back around and said to her, "Starting today, you need to cooperate too. We can't let your blood pressure issues drag on anymore. I’m going to set up a plan for you."

She was startled by my tone. I saw it. But I didn't stop. I need to control something. I need to ensure that something is within my grasp.

She isn't the problem. I know that. But at this moment, I can't face the fact that *I* am the problem.

Data doesn't lie. I’ve lived my whole life by data and standards, and now the data is starting to betray me. Blood pressure, blood sugar, weight—every indicator is slowly crossing the line. I can see the trend. I can predict it. And that is exactly what’s so terrifying.

The two-year retirement plan. I spoke of it with such certainty. But now I’m starting to wonder: if my body continues like this, how many "two years" do I have left?

The tools in the workshop are still there. I wanted to go and wipe them down, as I usually do. But today, I didn't move. I sat in my office, staring at this report, trying to use logic to convince myself that this is all within manageable limits.

But I know that some things are forever beyond my control.

For the first time, I truly felt that.

# May 8, 2022, past 10 PM

Sitting in the study, it’s already very quiet outside. Meifang fell asleep a long while ago; I can hear her light snoring drifting in from the bedroom. Her blood pressure was a bit high again today. I saw the reading she took this afternoon, but I didn't tell her.

What happened at the chess room today keeps spinning in my head.

Midway through the game, I suddenly realized I was staring blankly at the board. My opponent asked what I was thinking about, and I said, "I'm getting older; my reactions aren't as sharp anymore." As soon as the words left my mouth, they felt foreign to me. I wouldn't have said that before. I would have said, "This is an interesting move," or I wouldn't have explained at all and just played the piece.

But today, I was just slow. The calculations in my head felt sluggish. I could feel it.

Player A tried to comfort me, saying my game has always been stable. I heard myself reply: "It’s stable because I’m afraid to change."

I regretted saying it immediately. Not because it wasn't true, but because it was *too* true. I’ve never been that blunt before. Stable... yes, I’ve always chased stability. Forty years, the same factory, the same way of working, the same rhythm of life. No job-hopping, no risks, every step planned out.

That’s not stability. That’s stagnation.

I left the chess room halfway through. That has never happened before. My friends were a bit surprised; I told them I was tired and needed to go home. As I turned to leave, I looked back at that place—the familiar boards, the familiar faces, everything so familiar. My eyes stung a little.

Why is this happening?

The tools in the workshop are still there. All these years, I’ve been wiping them down and organizing them, like I’m looking after something precious. But Hao is in real estate in Shenzhen, and Yu is teaching in Guangzhou. They’ve never asked what those tools are for. Once, I tried to explain the use of a specific drill bit to Hao, but before I could finish, he said, "Dad, I’ve got a client I need to call back."

I’m recording the purpose of every tool in a notebook. Writing them down, one by one. But halfway through, I asked myself—what’s the point? Who is ever going to look at this notebook?

The physical exam report is still sitting in the drawer. The doctor said those figures "require attention." Every two days, I take it out and look at it, staring at the numbers, trying to find some kind of pattern. As if by doing that, I could control something. But the truth is, I can’t control anything. Blood pressure rises, blood sugar fluctuates, reactions slow down. These things are happening, and all I can do is record them.

Meifang’s blood pressure is rising too. I’m monitoring her diet and pushing her to get checkups. But what if one day I can’t move either? In our thirty-five years together, we’ve said too little. Now, even if I want to make up for it, I can't.

How much time is left?

I don't know. But I can feel time accelerating. It’s as if I used to have plenty of time to do something, and now I suddenly realize it’s gone. Retirement in two years, and then what? Empty days, no work, no one wanting anything from me, no one inheriting anything I know.

I sit here, pen in hand, but I don’t know what to write.

Maybe it’s best to just keep working tomorrow. At least when I’m working, I still know what I’m doing.
