ranked_items.content:
# Monday, September 12, 2022 – Sunny

After the morning meeting, I sat in my office, my hands still trembling slightly.

It wasn’t nerves. It was because I had just done something that didn't feel like me.

Li Ming, my subordinate, messed up his data sheet again—the third time this month. By my standards, a low-level error like that should have been called out directly; I should have made him understand exactly what "unacceptable" means. I had even rehearsed the blunt words in my head. But in the conference room, when I saw his tense expression, I paused.

I paused.

What I actually said was: "Let’s look at how we can improve this together." Then I spent ten minutes explaining it to him, instead of my usual three-sentence reprimand.

It’s so strange.

Sitting here now in the corner of my office, I’m trying to understand why I did that. Is it because I’ve been dwelling on the doctor’s words all week? Or is it because I’m starting to realize that I might actually be changing?

The doctor said my body is failing. Pre-metabolic syndrome. Early signs of carotid atherosclerosis. A blood sugar level of 110 mg/dL. These numbers are like nails driven into my brain. I always thought that through precise management—routine, control, supervision—I could maintain everything. But the doctor used data to tell me that I’ve failed.

Maybe that’s why I softened my stance with Li Ming. Maybe it’s because I suddenly realized I can’t control everything. I can’t even control my own body.

So, what right do I have to treat my subordinates that way?

The thought feels foreign to me. It also feels frightening.

Because if I let go of my obsession with detail, if I abandon that relentless management style, then who am I? What was the point of everything I’ve stood for over the last forty years?

On the other hand, I saw a spark in Li Ming’s eyes after I changed my tone. He started listening intently, started asking questions, instead of just hanging his head and taking the criticism. Maybe this change... maybe it isn't entirely a bad thing?

I don’t know.

I’m exhausted right now. These thoughts have drained my energy. The pressure is still there, but it feels different. It’s no longer anger at imperfection; it’s anxiety about time. How much longer? How much time do I have left to change? How much time is left to pass things on?

My wife said yesterday, "I knew you’d have this eventually." She was right. She’s always right. But I didn’t want to admit it.

Now, I have to.

The medicine the doctor prescribed is still in the drawer. I haven't taken it yet.

Tomorrow. I’ll deal with it tomorrow.

ranked_items.content:
It was just after 5:00 AM. Chen Guilin had been sitting in the bedroom for a long time. Outside the window, the sky was still dark, save for the occasional sound of a passing car below. He hadn't slept—or rather, he had given up on trying.

Beside him, Li Meifang rolled over. He could hear her breathing, very faint.

He opened the notes app on his phone. Inside were the figures he’d jotted down yesterday: 110, 140/90, pre-metabolic syndrome, long-term medication. His finger hovered over the screen for a long time before he finally deleted the line: "How much longer can I work?"

He put the phone down, then picked it up again.

Finally, he sent Li Meifang a message on WeChat. She wouldn't be awake yet, but he needed to get these words out.

---

**Chen Guilin**: I’ve been thinking all night about what the doctor said yesterday. It’s not a small thing.

**Chen Guilin**: Long-term medication—he was very clear about that. It means... we might have to change our plans.

**Chen Guilin**: I’m thinking about the retirement timeline. Two years might be too tight. Or rather, I need to run the numbers again.

**Chen Guilin**: And the insurance. Our current coverage might not be enough. I’m going to look into it today.

**Chen Guilin**: The other day you said, "I knew you’d have this sooner or later." When did you start worrying?

**Chen Guilin**: ...Never mind. We’ll talk in the morning. I just keep thinking—all these years, I thought I was managing things well. Turns out...

**Chen Guilin**: Keep sleeping. I’m fine. Just thinking through some of the accounts.

---

He put the phone down and sat for a while longer. The sky was gradually turning gray.

He got up, walked to the living room, and switched on the light in his workshop. His tools were arranged neatly on the shelves. He picked up a pair of vernier calipers and began to wipe them with a soft cloth. His movements were slow and heavy.

He wiped them once, then wiped them again.

September 12, 2022, Evening

Sitting in the workshop, wiping down my bench vise. My movements are slow, rubbing the same spot over and over.

I read this afternoon’s medical report three times. Blood sugar at 110 mg/dL, Stage 2 hypertension, and that term—pre-metabolic syndrome. The doctor spoke calmly, but those numbers are driven into my brain like nails. Early signs of carotid atherosclerosis. Long-term medication.

I don’t understand.

For the past forty years, I’ve never been careless. A regular routine, annual checkups, a controlled diet—I even managed Li Meifang’s blood pressure. I thought this was how you stayed in control. I always believed that precise management could stave off decline. But the doctor told me—it’s not enough.

My wife said something in the kitchen earlier that I’m still turning over in my mind: "I knew you’d have this sooner or later."

She’s right. That makes it harder to swallow.

I agreed to her suggestion that we go to the hospital together. I just said "Okay." She probably didn't notice my hands were shaking.

After dinner, I asked her how to be a better mentor to my subordinates. She looked startled for a moment, then started talking about things at her school. I was listening, but my mind was running a different set of numbers—how much longer? How many working years are left? Those tools, that experience—do I still have time to pass them on?

It’s too late. The thought surfaced suddenly, crystal clear.

I don’t want to admit that I can’t control everything. But I can’t deny that report, either. The doctor told me to pick up my prescription tomorrow. Long-term medication. The phrase feels stuck in my throat.

It’s 7:45 PM. Li Meifang is watching TV. I’m in here, wiping my tools.

I need to make a new plan. But right now, I don’t know where to start.

ranked_items.content:
September 10, 2022, 8:15 PM

I’ve been sitting in the workshop for two hours. I’m wiping down that 1985 vernier caliper, my movements slow. My wife is watching TV in the living room; I can hear the sound, but I don’t want to go out.

110 mg/dL. Stage 2 hypertension. Early signs of carotid atherosclerosis.

The doctor spoke those words calmly, as if reading a product inspection report. But I heard them clearly. Every word felt like a nail being driven in. I’ve always done my checkups, always logged the data, always believed I was in control. Wake up at 6:00 AM, sleep at 10:00 PM, a light diet, regular exercise. For forty years, that’s how I’ve lived.

My wife said, "I knew you’d get this sooner or later."

That sentence stung more than the diagnosis itself. When did she see through me? I thought I’d hidden it well. I thought that through precise management, I could stop aging, stop my body from betraying me. But she knew all along. What does that mean? Does it mean my efforts look like a joke to everyone else?

Long-term medication. The doctor said I need long-term medication.

I don’t know how long "long-term" is. One year? Five? Ten? Until I die? I’ve never taken medicine long-term. I rarely even catch a cold. Now I have to take pills for the rest of my life just to maintain a number—110. The number itself is meaningless, but what it represents matters. It represents my loss of control over my own body.

My wife said she’d go with me to get the prescription filled tomorrow. I didn’t respond, just nodded, and turned to walk into this workshop. I didn’t want her to see my hands shaking.

The tools are still here. Dozens of them. I spent forty years collecting them. Each one represents a stage, a decision, a moment I thought was important. Now I’m wondering, if I collapse, what happens to these things? Chen Hao doesn't understand mechanics. Neither does Chen Yu. Will they be treated as scrap metal?

I donated some tools to the industrial technical school. Was that the right decision? Or was it just a disguised way of settling my estate?

I don’t want to think like this. But I can’t help it.

The doctor said I need to change my lifestyle. Reduce stress. Maintain a routine. I’m already doing those things. Does that mean I haven’t done enough? Or does it mean none of it matters?

My hands are still shaking. I put the caliper down.

I have to get the medicine tomorrow. I need to make a list—drug names, usage, side effects, prices, insurance coverage. I need to turn this into data, into something manageable. That’s the only way I’ll feel at peace.

But I know that some things simply cannot be managed.

# September 15, 2022, 19:30

Sitting in the study, my hands are still shaking a little.

What happened this afternoon keeps looping in my head. Meifang said she needs to go for a follow-up checkup too, her voice laced with worry. I originally wanted to comfort her with data like I always do—tell her I’ve started the medication, the indicators will gradually improve, and everything will be fine as long as we follow the doctor’s plan. But when the words reached my lips, I didn't say them. Instead, I heard myself say, "I’m nervous too."

The moment those words came out, even I was a bit stunned.

I never say things like that in front of her. For forty years, I’ve been the one in control—the accounts, the plans, every matter in the house, big or small. She’s used to my composure, and I’m used to being the one who must remain unshakable. But today, I admitted it. I said, "Let’s go together."

And then, I took her hand.

Thinking back on that gesture now, it still feels strange. My hand holding hers; her hand was very warm, probably because she was nervous too. We just sat there for a while, neither of us speaking. I could feel her looking at me, but I didn't look up.

The doctor’s terminology is still echoing in my mind—metabolic syndrome, atherosclerosis, long-term medication. I always thought that through discipline and control, I could manage my body like a precision machine. But now I realize I have no control at all. The body has its own logic; it doesn't run according to my schedule.

The moment I held her hand, I wasn't thinking about the diagnosis itself, but rather—if I really collapse, what happens to her? All those tools, forty years of experience, the things I haven't had time to organize yet...

I don't know if this counts as weakness. I’ve always hated weakness. But today, it seems I allowed myself a little bit of it.

My hands are still shaking. I went to the workshop and wiped down my tools, from the first to the last, every single one of them with great care. Only while doing that did my hands slowly steady themselves.

Tomorrow, I’ll continue as planned. But something has changed, and I haven't figured out how to face it yet.

Blood pressure 147/92, blood sugar 6.8, and a slight abnormality on the ECG. I hadn't even figured out how to say it, and yet there it was.

A said it was fine, that many people are like this and I just need regular check-ups. His words sounded hollow. I know he meant well, but he doesn't understand at all. No one can. This isn't just about abnormal data.

I’ve been controlling everything. Recording my wife’s blood pressure every day, watching everything she eats, walking 8,000 steps myself, weighing my diet down to the gram, organizing all the data into spreadsheets. I thought that by doing this, I could... I could stop something. But now I realize it’s all futile. Data can’t change anything.

Maybe I should retire early. I startled myself when I heard those words come out of my mouth. But once the thought appeared, I couldn't shake it. If my body is truly starting to fail, how much time do I have left? How much time is left for the things I haven't done yet?

The tools in the workshop, those ideas, and... I haven't even had a real conversation with Hao and Yu. They are in Shenzhen and Guangzhou, while I stay here recording data as if that were a form of love.

When I lost the game, I looked at the black and white pieces on the board and suddenly felt exhausted. Not a physical exhaustion, but something else.

It’s 8:30 PM. I have work tomorrow. Next week is the department’s quality assessment. Everything continues as if nothing has changed. But everything has changed.

I don’t know what to do. Or rather, I know what to do—make a plan, adjust the regimen, control the variables—but this time, I realize there are just too many things beyond my control.

Maybe I should sleep. I have to record the blood pressure data tomorrow.

# MEMORANDUM

**Date: September 11, 2022, 3:30 AM**

---

**To-Do List / Matters to Address:**

**Medical Related**
- [ ] Schedule follow-up appointment with Cardiology (within 3 months) — Ask the doctor about the medication cycle.
- [ ] Consult on long-term medication plan — Specific usage and potential side effects of Atorvastatin and Nifedipine.
- [ ] Blood pressure monitoring log — Buy a reliable monitor; measure every morning and evening, and record in the log.
- [ ] Carotid ultrasound follow-up — Confirm if the "early signs" are progressing.
- [ ] Ask the doctor: Is pre-metabolic syndrome reversible? How long will it take to see improvement?
- [ ] Organize physical exam data — 110 mg/dL blood sugar, Stage 2 hypertension, elevated LDL... keep these numbers clear in mind.

**Lifestyle Adjustments**
- [ ] Diet plan — Reduce salt intake, control fats, and cut out afternoon tea pastries.
- [ ] Exercise regimen — Doctor recommended aerobic exercise, but at what intensity? How many times a week? Clarify before starting.
- [ ] Supervise Li Meifang’s diet and check-ups — Her blood pressure is high too; we can’t both have health issues at the same time.

**Financial / Retirement Planning**
- [ ] Recalculate retirement reserves — If long-term medication is required, how much will medical expenses increase?
- [ ] Check health insurance coverage — Are these specific medications covered?
- [ ] Are life and critical illness insurance sufficient? — Do I need supplemental coverage?
- [ ] Organize balance sheet — Update the latest family financial statement.
- [ ] Consult on pension calculations — If my health affects my ability to work, should I move up my retirement date?
- [ ] Security for the children — Chen Hao’s mortgage pressure, Chen Yu’s marital status... what happens to them if something happens to me?

**Work Related**
- [ ] Streamline department process documentation — Just in case, ensure someone else can take over.
- [ ] Organize records of improvement plans from the past 20 years — This is my professional legacy.
- [ ] Reflect on retirement timing — Can I manage another 2 years? Or should I leave earlier?

**Other**
- [ ] Tool donation to the Industrial Technical School — I’ve been meaning to do this; now it feels more urgent than ever.
- [ ] Organize workshop tool inventory — Which ones are worth keeping, and which can be passed down?

---

**Personal Thoughts:**

How much longer can I work?

How many years are left?

I’ve noted down everything the doctor said. The data doesn't lie. But I still can’t wrap my head around it—how did 40 years of my life lead to this? I kept a regular routine, had periodic check-ups, and ate a bland diet... I thought that would be enough.

Now, I have to take medication for the rest of my life.

I keep thinking about what Li Meifang said: "I knew this would happen to you sooner or later." She was right. I looked down on people who lacked self-discipline, and yet, I couldn't escape this myself.

My fingers are still shaking.

Listing these things out makes me feel a bit better. At least there are still things I can control.

Tomorrow—no, this morning—I need to call the hospital.

ranked_items.content:
September 22, 2022, 8:30 PM

I’m sitting in my workshop, still holding that pair of pliers. I’ve wiped them over and over, but they never seem to get quite clean.

The doctor’s words are still circling in my head. 110, atherosclerosis, long-term medication. The numbers are clear. My whole life, I’ve been best at dealing with clear numbers, but this time, that clarity feels suffocating.

Meifang said something yesterday that I hadn’t been able to put into words: "I knew you’d have this sooner or later." She saw it coming. In the office, I can spot a flaw in the smallest detail, yet I couldn’t see my own body slowly breaking down. Irony.

I pulled out that notebook. Usually, I only record work logs, accounts, and process improvements. Today, for some reason, I started writing something else. I wrote a sentence, crossed it out. Wrote it again.

"If I could do it over, I would teach the younger ones more."

My hand shook a little as I wrote that.

Forty years. Forty years in this factory, from inspector to manager. Those precision tools, those processes, those improvement plans I puzzled out all by myself… suddenly I wonder, what happens to all of it if I collapse? Will my subordinates keep up my standards? Or will everything just fall apart?

But then I thought, maybe I’m wrong. Maybe the value of these things doesn’t depend on me handing them over personally. Maybe it’s already there—in the processes I improved, in the standards I demanded, in the things those young people learned even while they were resenting me.

I wrote another line in the notebook: "Maybe I should hold a technical sharing session before I retire."

As I finished the sentence, the tightness in my chest loosened a bit. Not entirely, but it was there.

It’s not about proving anything. It’s just… if I talk about these things, if I let people see *why* I do what I do instead of just how strict I am, then maybe… maybe there won’t be so much regret.

The doctor said I need to control my diet, get regular check-ups, and take my medicine. I can do that. Following a plan is what I do best.

But this time, there’s something new in the plan. Not just to live longer—though I hope for that, too. But to make these forty years actually mean something.

Meifang is calling me for dinner from downstairs. I didn’t answer right away. I looked again at those sentences in the notebook, the ones I’d revised over and over.

Maybe I’ll propose the idea to my supervisor tomorrow. Maybe I won’t. I don’t know yet.

But at least I wrote it down.

ranked_items.content:
April 28, 2022, 11:15 PM

I’m sitting in the study. The lights are off. It’s pitch black outside the window.

I’ve been sitting here in the dark for a long time. My hand is pressed against my chest; I can feel my heartbeat. The rhythm is irregular. My breathing is a bit shallow, but I haven't moved. I just sit here, feeling it all.

The physical exam report is still on the desk. Blood pressure 139/88, fasting blood sugar 6.8. There’s a waveform on the ECG that the doctor said needs observation. He used the phrase "requires attention." I heard him loud and clear.

I’m thinking: this is how it begins.

I press my fingers against my pulse and count—76 beats per minute. Within the normal range. But I know it was racing just a moment ago. Why did it speed up? No exercise, nothing special happened. I was just sitting here when I suddenly felt... something I can't put into words.

Hao called today. He said the mortgage pressure is heavy and he might have to pay back a little less this month. I didn't say much; I just noted it down in my ledger. The mortgage, his sales commissions, the fluctuations of Shenzhen’s housing prices. I track all these data points. But what’s the use of tracking? Can I change anything?

The tools in the workshop are still on the shelves. Lately, I’ve been numbering them, writing down their uses and the years they were purchased. Some were bought in 1995, others in 2008. I wonder, if I were suddenly gone, who would know how to use these things? Not Hao. Not Yu. And certainly not Meifang.

They would just become scrap metal. Or be tossed out as junk.

I’ve been sitting in the dark for a long time, without turning on the light.

Tomorrow, I need to schedule a more detailed check-up. Cardiology. I need to know exactly what that abnormal waveform means. I need to establish a long-term monitoring plan. Every month’s data must be recorded so I can see the trends.

I also need to keep supervising Meifang’s blood pressure. She’s started eating salty foods again; I have to remind her once more.

My breathing has settled down a bit now.

But that sense of unease remains.

It’s as if something is slowly coming loose, and I can’t reach out to catch it.

ranked_items.content:
September 17, 2022 Afternoon

My hands are still shaking.

It’s been three hours since I left the hospital. I’m sitting in my workshop, and my hands are still shaking. 110 mg/dL. Grade 2 hypertension. Carotid atherosclerosis. The doctor spoke these words with a calm tone, as if he were talking about the weather. But I heard him loud and clear. Every number felt like a nail being driven in.

I tell myself these are just data points. I’ve organized forty years of production reports; I know how to read data. But this time, the data is telling me that my body is failing.

My wife said, "I knew this would happen to you sooner or later." That hurt more than the diagnosis itself. When did she notice? I always thought I had everything under control—diet, routine, checkups. I was even secretly monitoring her blood pressure, terrified something might happen to her. And the result? I’m the one who collapsed first.

It’s ridiculous.

My wife urged me to adjust my pace of life. I told her, "I know. That’s exactly what I’m doing." But what am I doing right now? I’m in the workshop, repeatedly wiping down these tools. I’ve wiped the same screwdriver over a dozen times. My movements are forceful, as if I’m trying to scrub something off. I know it’s absurd. I’m using this to mask my anxiety. But I can’t stop.

The doctor said I’ll need long-term medication. "Long-term." That’s a heavy word.

I’m thinking about those precision tools—the ones I’ve collected for over twenty years. And those forty years of experience—the quality control standards, the process optimizations, the things I’ve never explicitly said but are etched into my brain. If I fall, what happens to all of it? I don’t have time to think about that, because the moment I do, I start to feel afraid.

I don’t want to tell Chen Hao and Chen Yu. Not yet. They’ll ask too many questions, and I don’t have the energy to answer them. Besides, I don’t know what I’d even say. "Your father is sick"? That sounds too dramatic. But if I don’t tell them, they’ll hear it from their mother sooner or later.

I need to make a plan. The doctor gave me a medication list; I’ve already noted it down. Regular follow-ups. Dietary adjustments. And... I don’t know what else. I listened to everything the doctor said, but right now, only one thought is stuck in my head: I’m running out of time.

I originally planned to retire in two years. Now that plan might have to change. Or maybe it can’t be changed at all. I don’t even know how much longer I can keep working.

It’s ironic. I’ve spent my whole life pursuing control. Precise management, clear plans, every step taken by the book. But my body won’t listen. The body has its own logic, and I can’t change it with willpower alone.

I put down the screwdriver. My hands are still shaking.

I have work tomorrow.

September 20, 2022, 10:15 PM

Li Meifang said something tonight that I’m still thinking about.

Over dinner, I brought up work—the new QC inspector made another mistake, and I called him out on it directly. She put down her chopsticks, looked at me, and said, "You know, I knew you’d end up with this sooner or later." I didn't realize what she meant at first. Then she added, "Maybe your problem isn't physical; it’s that you never leave any room for error—not for others, and not for yourself."

I didn't answer. We kept eating, just like usual. But those words have been circling in my head ever since.

The doctor’s words from this afternoon are still ringing in my ears. 110, Grade 2, atherosclerosis. The numbers are clear. I always thought I had everything under control—diet, routine, exercise—but clearly, it wasn't enough. I don’t even know when it stopped being enough.

While I was wiping down my tools in the workshop, I thought that maybe Meifang is right. My attitude toward that new employee seems to be the same as my attitude toward my own body right now—demanding perfection, allowing no flaws. But perfection... I suddenly realized that maybe it doesn't even exist.

I told Meifang that being diligent might not be enough; maybe I need more patience. I was a bit surprised at myself for saying it. It felt strange coming out of my mouth. She just nodded and didn't say anything, but I could tell she was smiling.

I’m lying in bed now; my wife is already asleep. I’m wondering, if I can’t even fully control my own body, then what was the point of everything I’ve taken pride in for the last forty years—the precision management, the process optimizations?

Maybe the point isn't perfection itself, but... I haven't figured it out yet.

I have to go to the hospital tomorrow to pick up my prescription. The doctor said I’ll need long-term medication. I don’t like the word "long-term." But it seems I don't have a choice right now.

ranked_items.content:
September 14, 2022, 10:00 PM

I just gave Hao a call.

I originally intended to ask if he was feeling the pressure of his mortgage or if he was short on cash lately. But the moment the call connected, I suddenly changed my tune. I asked him how he’s been doing and if there’s anything he needs help with.

He paused for a second; I could hear it in his voice. He probably found it strange—I never ask things that way. Usually, I just get straight to the point: work, income, mortgage progress. This time... for some reason, I just wanted to try a different approach.

We spoke for about five minutes. He said work has been okay, just that the recent heat has been a bit draining. I asked him if he’s been eating well and taking regular breaks. He laughed and said, "Dad, why the sudden concern?"

I didn't answer.

Before hanging up, I said, "Remember to let me know if you need anything." After I said it, even I was a bit stunned. Those are words I rarely ever say.

I’m sitting in the study now. The doctor’s diagnosis is still in the drawer. Grade 2 hypertension. Pre-metabolic syndrome. "Long-term medication." These terms have been spinning in my head all day, like a calculator that won't stop.

I always thought that through routine, through control, and through every precise number, I could keep everything held together. But the doctor said that’s not enough. Meifang’s words have also been ringing in my ears—"I knew you’d end up with this sooner or later." It’s as if she saw right through it all along, while I was still lying to myself.

Why did I suddenly want to check on Hao?

Maybe it’s because... I suddenly realized that for some things, time might be running out. The things I’ve always wanted to say but didn't; the areas I’ve always supervised but never truly cared about. For forty years, I’ve wiped down tools one by one in the workshop, established protocols in the office, and managed the accounts at home. But it seems I’ve never truly asked them what they actually need.

I was just controlling.

Now that I can’t even control my own body, I suddenly want to... change something. But change what? I don’t even know.

The thought is unfamiliar, and unsettling.

I have to go to the hospital to pick up my prescription tomorrow.

ranked_items.content:
Sunday, September 18, 2022, 10:47 PM

I haven’t slept well since getting back from the hospital yesterday. I just lay in bed, turning those numbers over and over in my head. 110, Grade 2, atherosclerosis. Meifang was sound asleep; I didn’t disturb her.

I went out this morning. I didn’t tell her where I was going. I bought a blood pressure monitor, a glucose meter, and a new notebook—black cover, high-quality paper. The cashier asked if I was buying them for a family member. I didn’t answer.

After getting home, I spent two hours in the workshop. I organized everything. I wiped all my tools clean. Then, I started recording.

8:15 AM: BP 128/82.
12:30 PM: BP 125/79.
3:45 PM: Blood sugar 5.8.
7:20 PM: BP 132/85.

I downloaded three apps: "BP Manager," "Health Log," and one specifically for tracking diet. The interfaces are all very clear. I entered everything I ate today—plain congee with pickles for breakfast, steamed fish and greens for lunch, tomato and egg noodles for dinner. No oil. No salt.

The doctor said I need long-term medication. But I’m thinking, what if I can keep these numbers within the normal range? If I stick to the logs, stick to the diet, and stick to the exercise—I’m going to walk 8,000 steps a day now, I will do it—then maybe I won’t have to take those pills? Or at least I can delay it?

Meifang asked why I was so busy today. I told her it was nothing, just that I wanted to be more meticulous about managing my health. She gave me a look... that kind of look, as if she already knew what was coming. I didn’t want to look her in the eye.

On the first page of the notebook, I wrote a title: "Health Management Plan: September 2022 – September 2023." Below it are weekly logs and monthly summaries. Everything is clear and orderly.

As long as I persist, as long as I don’t slack off, I can definitely reverse this. What the doctor said... maybe he just saw me at my worst moment. I’m starting now, starting today.

Continue tomorrow. The blood pressure monitor is on the nightstand.

I cannot slack off.

# Health Management Plan - September 2022

**Date of Record:** September 29, 2022  
**Date of Diagnosis:** September 23, 2022  
**Attending Physician:** Cardiologist  

---

## I. Summary of Diagnosis

- Hypertension (Stage II)
- Signs of Left Ventricular Hypertrophy (LVH)
- Long-term medication management required
- Doctor's Recommendation: Immediate commencement of medication + lifestyle intervention

---

## II. Prescribed Medication and Dosage Schedule

| Medication | Dosage | Frequency | Time | Remarks |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Amlodipine | 5mg | Once daily | 07:00 | Take in the morning |
| Valsartan | 80mg | Once daily | 19:00 | Take in the evening |

**Mobile Reminders:** ✓ Set  
**Storage:** Bedroom nightstand; keep in a cool, dry place away from light.

---

## III. Lifestyle Modification Plan

### A. Dietary Adjustments
- [ ] Daily salt intake < 6g (Li Meifang informed)
- [ ] Reduce intake of high-fat foods
- [ ] Increase proportion of vegetables and whole grains
- [ ] Limit pickled foods and processed meats
- [ ] Alcohol cessation (previously an occasional social drinker; now stopped)

### B. Exercise Plan
- Aerobic exercise 3–4 times per week, 30 minutes per session
- Primarily walking (Monday, Wednesday, Friday evenings after 6:00 PM)
- Avoid strenuous exercise and sudden physical exertion
- Continue Go (Weiqi) activities, but be mindful of excessive stress/tension

### C. Weight Management
- Target BMI: Under 24
- Current Weight: To be measured and recorded this week

### D. Stress Management
- Continue playing Go; it remains the most effective way to relax
- Maintain existing work pace; do not take on additional pressure
- Evening professional journal reading time can remain unchanged

---

## IV. Medical Check-up and Follow-up Schedule

| Item | First Follow-up | Subsequent Frequency |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Blood Pressure Monitoring | In 2 weeks (Oct 13) | Every 2 weeks |
| Blood Tests | In 1 month (Oct 29) | Every 3 months |
| ECG | In 3 months (Dec 29) | Every 6 months |
| Echocardiogram | In 6 months (Mar 29, 2023) | Annually |

**Note:** All follow-ups must be booked in advance and documented.

---

## V. Symptom Monitoring Checklist

**Seek immediate medical attention for:**
- Chest pain or discomfort
- Severe dizziness or vertigo
- Shortness of breath
- Blurred vision
- Slurred speech

**Daily Monitoring Items (to be recorded daily):**
- Morning blood pressure (use electronic monitor; record in log)
- Presence of dizziness or headaches
- Sleep quality
- Fatigue levels

---

## VI. Home Blood Pressure Log

**Purchase Plan:** Buy a home electronic blood pressure monitor this weekend (Model TBD)

| Date | Morning BP | Evening BP | Remarks |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Sept 29 | - | - | Awaiting equipment purchase |
| | | | |

---

## VII. Key Information

**Doctor’s Contact:** Saved in phone notes  
**Pharmacy Location:** 1st Floor Pharmacy, hospital near home  
**Emergency Contact:** Li Meifang (Wife)

---

## VIII. Personal Reflections

The doctor said this diagnosis isn't an end, but a new beginning. I believe that by executing this plan with precision, I can keep the situation under control. I have managed workflows and quality standards for 40 years in my career; managing my own health should be well within my capabilities.

The keys are **consistency** and **data**. I need to approach this like my work—every detail must be recorded, and every metric must be tracked.

I haven't told Chen Hao or Chen Yu yet. They have their own pressures right now, and there's no need to add to their burden. Meifang already knows; she has been more cautious than usual these past two days, and I can sense her worry. I need her to see that I have everything under control so she can feel at ease.

Next step: Purchase the blood pressure monitor this weekend; first follow-up on October 13.

July 10, 2022, 10:47 PM

I pulled out this unused notebook, though I’m not sure why. Maybe it’s because what happened yesterday is still spinning in my head.

I lost my temper yesterday over a spreadsheet formatting issue. Xiao Wang had reversed the order of the data columns, and I pointed it out in front of everyone, using some pretty harsh words. His face went pale instantly. I saw it, but I didn’t stop. Instead, I kept going on and on about how "details determine product quality, and product quality determines the company's survival."

Thinking back now, what was I so angry about? A formatting error. It was that simple.

But it wasn’t, really.

I’ve noticed myself setting stricter standards lately. Stricter inspection processes. Stricter requirements. As if doing this could... could what? I can’t quite put my finger on it. Maybe I’m trying to prove something. Maybe I’m trying to control something.

Li Meifang asked me what was wrong last night, but I didn't answer. She’s long since used to it.

I’ve gone over my May medical report three times now. The doctor said there’s nothing majorly wrong, just that I need to "pay attention." Pay attention to what? Pay attention to time passing. Pay attention to my body changing. Pay attention to how many more years I can keep doing this job.

I reorganized the tools in the workshop again recently. I’m recording the purpose of each one, one by one. Chen Hao won’t use them. Neither will Chen Yu. They have their own lives. I know this, yet I’m still taking notes. Why? While I can still move?

How many years of movement do I have left?

I’m supposed to play chess with my friends next week. I don’t know what to say to them. Lately, when I’m with them, I’ve been pathetically quiet. I was always a man of few words, but now I feel like I can’t even focus on the game. My mind is always elsewhere.

I’m trying to fight something off by exerting more control. I know this. But I can’t stop.

Xiao Wang didn't look at me today. I should say something to him, but I don't know what.

Maybe I’ll delete these words tomorrow. There’s no need to leave this behind.

But for now, I’ll let them stay here.

Are you asleep?

I was just in the study tidying up my tools, thinking about what you said. You’re right—I really have changed.

For years, I always felt that being responsible meant keeping everything under control—with you, the kids, and my work. I only realized later that it wasn't responsibility; it was a lack of trust. I didn't trust that you could take care of yourself, or that they could make their own decisions.

It wasn't until that incident with the doctor that I truly understood—there are some things I simply cannot control. Through it all, you were the one who stayed, never once looking down on me.

Thank you for being by my side.

Let’s take things slow, together.

ranked_items.content:
Haozi, how have you been lately?

I’ve been thinking, and I wanted to share a few words with you. This recent health scare has given me a lot to reflect on. The doctor said I need to stay on medication and slow down. With your mom here by my side, I’ve realized that all these years, I’ve been constantly rushing—rushing to keep everything under control, to make everything perfect.

Regarding your mortgage in Shenzhen, I know I used to pester you with questions, almost like I was auditing your books. Looking back, that wasn't right. You’re an adult now, and you have your own plans. I thought I was "helping" you in my own way, but in reality, I was just putting pressure on you.

I’ve only recently come to understand that maybe life doesn't need to be perfectly controlled. In my forty years of work, I always pursued "zero defects," but life isn't an assembly line. There are things you just can't control, and there’s pressure you have to learn to let go of. Otherwise, you’ll end up like me—waiting until your sixties to regret what you’ve missed out on.

I’m not telling you this so you’ll follow in my footsteps. On the contrary, I hope you can live a more relaxed life than I did. A mortgage is just a mortgage, and a job is just a job; don't keep yourself wound so tight. Taking time for family and getting proper rest—those things matter, too.

Your mom says I’ve become quite talkative lately, ha. Maybe I really am changing.

If you’re free this weekend, give me a call. Let’s have a good chat.

— Dad

ranked_items.content:
October 12, 2022, 7:30 PM

Had a phone call with Chen Hao.

In the past, I’d always ask him if the mortgage was a burden, how the orders were coming along, or if his job was stable. I don’t know what came over me today, but the first thing out of my mouth was, "How have you been lately? Is there anything I can help with?" He seemed taken aback; there was a two-second silence before he answered.

He said work is exhausting, that sales is high-pressure, and sometimes he wonders if he’s doing the right thing. He’s lost. He was very candid, and I could hear the fatigue in his voice.

Before, I would have jumped straight in with advice, telling him exactly what he should do. But this time, I just said, "I understand. I’ve been through a stage like that, too."

Even I was a bit surprised by my own words. In forty years, I’ve never spoken to him like that.

He asked me when that was. I told him that when I was his age, I wasn't sure if I was making the right choices either. Back then, I was a quality inspector at the grassroots level, doing the same repetitive work every day, unable to see a way out. What I didn’t tell him was that I just kept going down that same path ever since, never changing course again.

He didn’t respond immediately, but I could sense something had shifted. It wasn't that superior-subordinate dynamic anymore; it felt more like... two people who had walked the same road.

After hanging up, I sat at my desk and looked at that medical report. The doctor said I’ll need long-term medication and a lifestyle change. I’ve been thinking about how to reverse this outcome through stricter management, just like I do at work—precise control, eliminating variables.

But while talking to Chen Hao, it suddenly occurred to me that maybe some things can’t be controlled. Some paths, once walked, are simply walked.

I need to rethink how I interact with them. Not supervising, not guiding. Maybe just... being there?

The thought makes me a little uneasy. I don't know if I still know how to do it.

More tomorrow.

ranked_items.content:
July 5, 2022, 6:45 PM

Had a call with Hao. He said the real estate market has been a bit cold lately, but he’s holding on. I asked him if he’d ever thought about learning something about mechanics—maybe it could be a hobby later on. He laughed and said, "Dad, what are you talking about? I don’t have the brain for that." I didn't push it further.

After hanging up, I sat in the workshop for a long time.

Actually, that wasn't what I wanted to ask. What I wanted to ask was—has he ever thought about what will happen to all these tools, all these things I’ve spent forty years learning? But I couldn't get the words out. Just like I never can.

During our call this afternoon, I told him "everything is under control." Thinking back on that phrase now, it feels a bit ridiculous. What does "under control" even mean? What am I controlling? Hao is in Shenzhen worrying about his mortgage, Yu is busy with work in Guangzhou, and here I am, documenting the uses of these tools as if I’m in a race against time. But time isn't racing me—it doesn't care about me at all.

Li Meifang is downstairs watching TV. I can hear her coughing. It’s time to nag her about going to the hospital to check her blood pressure again. These are things I can control. But what’s the use of controlling them?

The tool records in my notebook are halfway finished. With every entry I write, I wonder—who is going to use this? Hao? No. Yu? Even less likely. So why am I writing all this?

Maybe it’s just to keep myself from being idle. To tell myself there’s still something left to do.

The physical exam report from May is still in the drawer. A few of the indicators fluctuated a bit. The doctor said it’s nothing serious, just needs regular follow-ups. But I keep taking it out, looking at those numbers over and over. As if I could find some hidden meaning in them.

I’ve never known how to talk to them. That’s the truth. It’s been that way since they were little. I thought it would get better as they grew up, but it seems to have gotten worse. Now, I even self-censor before asking a simple question, terrified of saying something that might cause awkwardness.

Maybe I should have started teaching them earlier. But back then, I was busy with work. Now that I want to teach, it’s already too late.

Tomorrow, I’ll continue documenting the tools. There are still over thirty items left to record.

I should do it while I can still move.

**Date: Saturday Morning, October 22, 2022**

Had a phone call with Chen Hao. After hearing about my diagnosis, his first instinct was to ask if I’d been taking my medicine on time and if he should take time off to come back. I didn't expect him to ask that.

Before this, I always felt like there was something standing between us. Our calls were always about work, the house, or whether they were eating well—like going through a checklist. Their answers were always brief. I thought that was just how we were.

But this time was different. When he said, "Dad, you need to take care of yourself," I heard something different in his voice. My own voice choked up a bit; all I could manage was, "Okay, thank you." That’s rare for me.

Li Meifang says the way I’ve shown concern for the kids all these years has been flawed—always supervising, never just being there. I used to disagree with her. But thinking about it now, maybe she’s right.

**A few things I need to figure out:**

1. Have a real talk with Chen Hao and Chen Yu individually before I retire. Not to ask about their careers or lives, but to truly listen to them. Maybe they have things they want to say to me, too.

2. What does "legacy" actually mean? I’ve always wanted to pass down my understanding of work and my obsession with quality to them. But they have their own lives. Maybe I should just record these things for them to reference, rather than expecting them to inherit it all.

3. The collaboration with the technical school—donating tools, doing sharing sessions. I want to do this even more now. Not to prove anything, but because I truly want to leave these things behind.

4. Have a proper talk with Li Meifang next week. She’s been by my side all these years, yet I’ve always been "managing" her. It’s time to change that.

**One more thought:**

Maybe the things I’ve done right over these forty years aren't the process improvements or the defect rate data. Maybe it’s that obsession with detail, that commitment to responsibility, and that care for my family—even if I haven't been great at expressing it. If Chen Hao and Chen Yu can see these things in me, that’s enough.

I don’t need them to become me. I just need them to know that I’ve always been here.

---

*Going to play chess with the guys this afternoon. My game hasn't been great lately; my mind keeps wandering. But maybe that’s okay—I don’t need to win. Just sitting there, being with them, is enough.*

ranked_items.content:
Tuesday afternoon, October 18, 2022

I spent the entire afternoon sitting in my small workshop. I didn’t clean any tools or organize my inventory. I just flipped through my notebooks, page by page.

What was I recording back then? Screwdriver models, wrench specifications, the purchase year and maintenance cycle for every single tool. Everything was neat and tidy, precise to a fault. Looking at these records used to give me a sense of peace—as if I had mastered something.

But today, I’m wondering: what did these tools actually teach me?

It wasn't how to use them. That’s too simple. It was... how to approach a task. With seriousness. Without compromise. Knowing that every detail matters. For forty years, the products I made with these tools had a defect rate 25% lower than the industry average. That wasn't a coincidence. It was because I never allowed myself to slack off.

But—

I wrote that word on a new page, then crossed it out. Wrote it again. Crossed it out again.

But I treated people the same way. My subordinates, my family. I demanded perfection and left no room for error. I thought that was called being responsible. Now, I’m not so sure.

Li Meifang has been humming in the kitchen lately. I haven't heard her hum like that in a long time. She says it’s because I’ve finally "loosened up a bit." I can hear it in her voice—it wasn't a compliment. It was... relief? She feels relieved.

That hurts a little.

I turned to a blank page and drew a timeline with a pencil. From 1982 to now. Forty years. I marked the key years: technician, team leader, department manager. I did well at every stage. Defect rates, process optimizations, the proposals that were adopted. It’s a beautiful report card.

But so what?

Chen Hao is in Shenzhen, under a lot of pressure. I keep track of his mortgage and his performance, but we barely have anything to say to each other. When Chen Yu comes home occasionally, our conversations are like status reports—how’s work, is your marriage stable. I thought I was teaching them about perseverance and quality awareness, but I was never truly there for them.

This is my regret. Perhaps my greatest regret.

Next to the timeline, I wrote: "If I could do it over, what would I do?"

But I know there are no "ifs." I only have the now. And about two years left of work. Maybe in these two years, I should record some things. Not tool lists, not process documents. But... what I was thinking during these forty years. Why I did things the way I did. Young people might need that.

Or rather, I need to do this. To forgive myself.

Meifang is calling me for dinner. I hear her voice drifting up from downstairs—gentle, not rushing me. I close the notebook and stand up.

Maybe I should listen to her more. Really listen, instead of mentally calculating her blood pressure and diet.

Maybe that is what I really need to learn.

ranked_items.content:
September 19, 2022, 10:47 PM

Something strange happened in the office today.

During this afternoon's meeting, Xiao Wang from Quality Control handed in a report with problematic data. Usually, I would have said bluntly, "This is unacceptable. Redo it." But in that moment, I paused. I looked at his nervous expression and a thought suddenly hit me—is he afraid of something, too?

Instead, I said, "Let’s take a look together and see where things went wrong."

There was a moment of silence in the conference room. I could feel everyone looking at me. Xiao Wang’s shoulders relaxed a little.

After the meeting, I wrote in my notebook: "Maybe I should change my approach."

But now, sitting here in my workshop looking at these tools, I’m not sure if this is progress or a retreat.

The doctor’s words are still ringing in my ears: "Long-term medication, regular check-ups." I always thought that as long as I was strict and cautious enough, I could control everything. My work, my health—everything. But clearly, I was wrong. My body doesn't follow my orders. I can’t protect myself through perfect management.

Maybe what my subordinates see isn't my high standards, but my fear.

My wife said, "I knew you’d get this sooner or later." What did she mean by that? Did she see it coming all along? What about everyone else? I thought I had hidden it so well.

I don’t know how to tell the kids. Chen Hao is under so much pressure with his mortgage, and Chen Yu is busy with work in Guangzhou. Telling them would only worry them. But if I say nothing, isn't that a form of deception?

My hands are still shaking. I wiped down my tools, then wiped them again.

Maybe I’ve been using control to mask my vulnerability. Now that the vulnerability is exposed, I don’t know what’s left.

I have to go to the hospital tomorrow to pick up my prescription.

As we were talking, I... my voice cracked. It’s been a long time since I felt that way.

It got me thinking. All these years, it feels like all I’ve done with Haohao and Yuyu is supervise their careers and lives; I’ve never really talked to them about my own things. And you’ve always been there, staying by my side, listening to me ramble about work, all while having to manage your own blood pressure. I used to think this was just what I was supposed to do—take care of everything at home so none of you had to worry.

I didn't truly see the patience you’ve shown me over the years. I see it now.

I’ve been thinking about what you said for a long time.

Ever since I got back from the chess club, I’ve spent the entire afternoon reflecting on your words—that instead of spending time fearing the future, it’s better to spend it cherishing the present.

I admit, I’ve been feeling trapped lately. Medical reports, retirement plans, the meaning of work... it’s like I’ve been trying to use these things to fill a bottomless pit. The more I try to fill it, the more anxious I get, and the more chaotic everything becomes. You’re right; I’ve been living in fear, not actually living.

I’ve never told anyone this before. You’re the first.

Over the past couple of days, I’ve been thinking that maybe I should stop. Not stop working, but stop this... need for control. Stop the constant calculating and worrying every single day. I’m not sure if that’s exactly what you meant, but at the very least, your words have opened up a crack for me to see the world outside.

Thank you. Let’s get back to our game next week.

# Work Log

**Date: Monday, October 24, 2022**

**Time: 19:30**

---

**Today's Summary:**
- Morning: Completed the revision of Q4 quality inspection standards; submitted for managerial approval.
- Afternoon: Departmental meeting to discuss defect rate targets for the new production line; set at below 0.8%.
- Evening: Chess and Card Room.

**Personal Notes:**

While playing chess with my friends at the club, I took the initiative to mention my recent physical exam results. I didn't hide anything, nor did I downplay it. I just said it straight—the doctor said I need to watch my heart and prescribed some medication.

Friend A asked how I was feeling. I said I’m alright, just need to accept the reality of it. They didn't overreact with excessive concern, nor did they pretend they hadn't heard; they just accepted it naturally. It felt a bit unfamiliar, but not unpleasant.

Reflecting on it, I’ve spent years accustomed to keeping everything under control—work, family, health, plans. It was as if I believed that as long as I remained rigorous and disciplined enough, I could prevent anything bad from happening. Clearly, that’s not the case. The doctor’s diagnosis served as a reminder: I am aging, I can get sick, and I can lose control.

This isn't a failure. It’s just reality.

Lately, the way I interact with Meifang has changed. Before, I was always monitoring her blood pressure and diet like I was executing a task. Now, I’m starting to realize that perhaps she doesn't need a supervisor; she needs companionship. Yesterday, when she brought up her recent fatigue, I didn't offer a list of suggestions like I usually do—I just listened. She seemed to relax a bit.

One more thing. Chen Hao mentioned his mortgage pressure over the phone recently. My usual reaction would be to analyze the data and propose solutions. This time, I asked him how he actually felt. He seemed a bit surprised, but he opened up a lot. I’ve realized that for all these years, I might have been "directing" them rather than "understanding" them.

There’s a little over a year left until retirement. During this time, I want to organize a few things. Not necessarily technical documents or operating manuals, but the reflections from all these years—what truly matters, and what I have been running away from.

Onward tomorrow.

---

**Remarks:**
- Schedule a discussion with my superior next Monday regarding process improvement plans.
- Remember to remind Meifang about her check-up appointment on Wednesday.

ranked_items.content:
# Memo

**Date**: Wednesday, August 24, 2022, 14:00

**Subject**: Reflections following a game of chess

---

## Record

During today’s game with Player A, he said something that stuck with me: "Instead of spending time fearing the future, spend it cherishing the present."

I didn't respond at the time, but I haven't stopped thinking about it since.

## Issues to Sort Out

1. **What am I doing?**
   - My recent anxiety cycle: Prediction → Fear → Control → More Fear.
   - Since the physical exam results came back, I’ve been using data, plans, and logs to fight uncertainty.
   - But is this actually helpful? Or am I just wearing myself out?

2. **On Control**
   - What I can control: Workflows, financial accounts, daily schedules.
   - What I cannot control: Aging, fluctuating health metrics, my children’s choices, time itself.
   - I have been trying to fill the void of uncontrollable fears with things I can control.
   - What is the cost of doing this?

3. **On the Present**
   - For the past few months, I’ve been planning for a retirement that is two years away.
   - At the same time, I’m worrying about potential health issues.
   - The "present" I hold in my hands has been squeezed into something tiny.
   - My wife, the chess games, the tools in my workshop—they are all still here, but mentally, I’ve already let go of them.

4. **Defining Value**
   - I’ve always believed: Completion → Results → Proof of self-worth.
   - But what if things can’t be completed? What if the results are forgotten?
   - Perhaps value is more than just these things?

## For Further Reflection

- Why do I keep losing what I have now by worrying about what I might lose in the future?
- Is accepting the unchangeable an act of giving up, or an act of maturity?
- How much more time can I afford to waste on fear?

---

**Note**: When I play Player A again next week, I want to hear his take on "cherishing the present." Not as small talk, but because I truly want to understand.

WeChat messages to Chen Hao:

---

Hao, something came to mind today that I wanted to share with you.

The company recently asked me to prepare an experience-sharing session—basically a summary of everything I’ve learned in quality management over the past 40 years. I was a bit resistant at first, thinking I didn't have much to say. But after giving it some thought, I realized there really are some things worth recording.

The mortgage pressure over in Shenzhen must still be quite heavy, right? I noticed you haven't mentioned work much on WeChat lately—is the market not doing so well? You don't have to hide it from me; if there’s anything you need help with, just say the word.

All these years, I’ve been trying to figure out how to pass my experience down to you kids, but it seems my approach wasn't quite right. This sharing session made me realize something—maybe what I can offer isn't about making you do the same kind of work, but rather passing on that sense of professional rigor and obsession with detail. You’re in real estate sales now, and you need those qualities too, don't you?

Are you free this weekend? Your mom and I want to hear how things are going. We’re not asking how much money you’re making; we just want to know how you are. I’d also like to catch up on how your sister is doing.

To be honest, I’m only just realizing that the way I showed my concern before might have been problematic. I was always asking if you’d hit your targets or settled down, instead of actually listening to you. After my recent check-up, the doctor talked to me a lot, and it finally clicked—maybe companionship and understanding are more important than trying to control everything.

Drop me a line when you can. How have you been lately?

---

Yuyu, how have you been lately?

I wanted to tell you something. The company asked me to do a summary and presentation of my work experience recently, and I’ve been thinking that this is actually a rare opportunity. After all these years of trial and error in quality management, I do have some insights. But I realized that I’ve never really talked to you kids about these things properly—and I haven't really listened to your thoughts, either.

My recent health check-up flagged a few minor issues, and the doctor said I need to take care of myself. Your mom has been looking after me, which has given me a lot of time to think. I’ve realized that the way I’ve cared for you all these years might have been off—it was always about supervising rather than being there for you. I’m sorry.

When do you have time to come home? No need to take special leave; I just want to sit down and have a real talk with you. About your work, your life, and... some of the things I’ve been thinking about lately.

Write back when you can.

ranked_items.content:
October 16, 2022, Evening

It’s been two hours since I got back from the hospital. Sitting here in the study, I still find myself somewhat at a loss for words.

The results from this morning’s check-up were steadier than expected. The doctor said the medication is working well and my heart rate is stabilizing. I had prepared a mountain of data and questions to ask, but with Meifang sitting beside me, I suddenly changed my mind. I heard myself say, "We are both cooperating with the treatment"—a strange choice of words coming from me. In the past, I would have said, "I am strictly managing it," or "I am controlling my diet." But today was different.

As I held my wife’s hand, I realized what I’ve been doing to her all these years. I’ve been "monitoring" her—monitoring her blood pressure, her diet, her physical exams. It was as if she were a quality control metric for me to manage. Yet she never once resisted. She was just there, by my side.

The doctor suggested joining a health management course. My first instinct was to refuse—too busy with work, not enough time. But I looked at Meifang and didn't say no right away. I asked her, "What do you think?" Those four words felt foreign coming out of my mouth. She said we could give it a try. And so, it was decided.

I wonder if this is what they call "vulnerability." Admitting that I can’t control everything, that I can’t stop my body from aging, and that I can’t ensure every plan stays on schedule. It’s not a comfortable feeling. But it’s not as terrifying as I imagined, either.

Perhaps all these years, I’ve been using perfect self-discipline to prove my worth. Now, I’m starting to wonder: was it worth it?

Meifang is downstairs preparing dinner. I can hear the sounds from the kitchen. It’s quiet, ordinary. But today, it sounds different.

I haven’t quite figured out what all of this means yet. But at the very least, I’m no longer in such a hurry.

ranked_items.content:
# Memo - Reflection and Legacy

**Date: October 18, 2022, Afternoon**
**Location: Home Study**

---

## Core Points Summary

### I. Reflections on the Essence of 40 Years of Work
- ~~Perfect quality control~~ → Obsession with detail is a value in itself
- ~~Sense of achievement from process optimization~~ → The patience and persistence learned through the process
- A 25% drop in defect rates is not the finish line, but a reminder: Why do I demand so much?
  - Answer: Because I believe in standards.
  - The real answer: Because I am afraid of losing control.

### II. Beyond the Technical (Important)
- The meaning of tools lies not in collecting them, but in how they taught me to think.
- What precision work taught me:
  - A single millimeter determines success or failure.
  - However, the distance between people cannot be measured in millimeters.
  - (Thinking back on my harshness toward subordinates and my "supervision" of my wife over the years.)
- I must admit: I cannot control everything.
  - The doctor's words are still ringing in my ears.
  - The body ages; plans change.
  - This is not failure.

### III. If I Could Start Over
- Explain to the team *why* we do things earlier, rather than just telling them *what* to do.
- Give young engineers more room to make mistakes (instead of blunt dismissals).
- ~~Keeping knowledge in my head~~ → Actively record, share, and discuss.
- Listen more to my wife, rather than "supervising" from the sidelines.
- Communicate with the children about more than just their mortgages and jobs.

### IV. Words for Young Engineers
- Standards are important, but don't let them become a weapon.
- Details determine quality, but details can also consume your life—learn to prioritize.
- 40 years is a long time, but it is also very short.
- The things you take for granted now will become mere memories by the time you reach my age.
- Don't wait for your heart to remind you—some things are more important than perfection.

### V. Immediate Action Plan
- [ ] Compile a "Core Experiences in Quality Management" document (for technical school students).
- [ ] Sit down with my subordinates for a talk—explain rather than accuse.
- [ ] Have a real conversation with my wife this weekend; listen to her talk about home.
- [ ] Call Chen Hao and Chen Yu respectively; do not mention work.
- [ ] Continue medication and scheduled check-ups—accept this reality.

---

## Notes
- The precision tool inventory can be set aside for now.
- The 40-year timeline is drawn; now it needs to be turned into a story.
- Perhaps retirement isn't the end, but a different kind of beginning?
- (I wouldn't have believed that sentence before.)

---

**One final question: What is my greatest regret in life?**
It isn't that I didn't reach a higher position.
It’s that I never truly looked at the people beside me.

Meifang, I told my boss something today. I wanted to talk to you about it first.

We have a meeting this afternoon to discuss next year’s plans, so I went to see him beforehand. I told him I want to organize an experience-sharing session before I retire—a way to summarize everything I’ve learned about quality management over the past forty years and share it with the people at the company. It’s nothing grand; I just want to document what I’ve learned, the pitfalls I’ve encountered, and how certain standards were established.

He was quite supportive and said it was a great idea.

To be honest, this wasn't what I originally had in mind. Do you remember? A while back, I was so preoccupied with how to pass my technical skills down to the younger generation; I felt such a sense of urgency. But after talking to the doctor a few times and starting the medication, I’ve slowly come to realize something. It’s not so much about "passing the torch" as it is about me wanting to properly sort through everything I’ve done all these years. It’s about giving myself a sense of closure.

This isn't just for others. It’s mostly for myself. Forty years in this position—what did I learn? What did I improve? Why did I do things the way I did? I want to think these through clearly, and I want others to know, too.

What do you think? Am I overthinking things?

The truth is, I feel that if I do this, I won't feel so empty when I retire. If these things are recorded and spoken aloud, they won't just disappear with me.

How is your blood pressure lately? Did you take your medicine on time today?

---

(Added in the evening)

Oh, I just remembered. You once said that I’ve spent all these years being too anxious to control everything, and that sometimes I don't need to be so tense. I think this sharing session is exactly like that. I’m not trying to force anyone to change; I just want to tell the story of my experiences over the years. As for the rest, I’ll leave it to others to interpret for themselves.

Thinking about it this way actually makes me feel a bit more relaxed.

You’ve been by my side for so many years. Sometimes I think I should listen to your thoughts more often, instead of just deciding everything on my own.

Thank you.

# October 8, 2022, 8:15 PM

Sitting in my study, the work log by my hand is nearly finished. Flipping to the last few pages, I realized the nature of my entries has changed. They are no longer just technical data and process optimizations; instead, I’ve started writing these inexplicable sentences—"If I could do it over, I would teach the younger generation more."

Looking at those words, even I find them unfamiliar.

This isn't like me. I’ve never been one for such sentimentality. But lately, I have been wondering: over these forty years, I’ve drafted so many improvement plans and brought the defect rates down, but what is actually left behind? A stack of reports? Some data? Or just the resentment in the hearts of those young engineers I criticized so bluntly?

Li Meifang was nagging about her blood pressure again today. I made a new diet plan for her; she didn't say anything, but that look in her eyes... I know what she’s thinking. After all these years of marriage, we haven't communicated much, but the way she looks at me has changed. Perhaps she has sensed something, too.

In the office this afternoon, a new QC inspector asked me a technical question. As usual, I pointed out the flaws in his logic directly, but this time, I stopped myself. I paused, and instead, explained to him *why* it had to be done that way. He listened intently. In that moment, I thought, maybe... maybe I should do more of that.

It’s not that I’ve suddenly become soft. I still loathe inefficiency and ambiguity. But I’m beginning to realize that my value might not lie in how much knowledge I’ve documented, but in... whether I’ve actually helped others understand what a true work ethic looks like.

I’m considering holding a technical sharing session for the young engineers in the department before I retire. I never thought I’d want to do something like that. But now I feel that if I don’t do it now, the opportunity might be gone forever.

Why am I thinking this way? I can’t quite say.

Perhaps it’s because time has suddenly become tangible. I used to feel that time was something to be managed, just like a project. Now... now it feels like it’s slipping away at a speed I can no longer control.

I don’t want to admit it. But writing it down here makes it feel real.

Tomorrow is another day. There are still many tools that need organizing.
ranked_items.subquestion: Brother Chen, seeing how meticulous you used to be with both work and family, I always felt you were someone who insisted on having everything under control. ranked_items.content:
Meifang, I wanted to say a few things to you.

I kept thinking about it yesterday at the hospital. All those years of "supervising" you—your diet, your checkups, your routine—I realize now that it probably put a lot of pressure on you. I thought I was taking care of you, but in reality, I was controlling you.

Yesterday, when you sat beside me and held my hand, you didn't let go the whole time the doctor was speaking. It was only then that I truly felt we aren't "supervisor and supervised." We are just... us. Together.

The doctor mentioned joining a health management course, and I want to go with you. Not so I can tell you what to do, but so we can learn together. To cooperate. I’ve never thought about that word in this way before.

I’ve seen how much you’ve put up with me all these years. I’m not very good at saying these things, but I want you to know—I’m grateful to you. Truly.

We still have time. Before I retire, while we can still do so many things together. I don't want to waste any more of it.

Shall we go sign up for that course together this weekend?
ranked_items.subquestion: Brother Chen, seeing how meticulous you used to be with both work and family, I always felt you were someone who insisted on having everything under control. ranked_items.content:
August 24, 2022, Evening

I said some things at the chess and card room today that I’m still regretting a little.

I played three games with Chess Friend A. I won the first two, but halfway through the third, I suddenly asked him—has everything I’ve done over the last forty years been meaningless?

After I said it, there was a long silence. No one responded; they just kept staring at the board. I knew the question was too heavy, that it wasn't something to be asked over a game of chess. But at that moment, I truly wanted an answer.

Eventually, he said that instead of spending time fearing the future, it’s better to spend time cherishing the present.

I didn't say anything else and kept playing. But those words have been spinning in my head ever since.

Lately, I’ve been dwelling on useless things. Ever since the physical exam report came back, I’ve started feeling like nothing matters anymore. All those tools in the workshop that I spent over twenty years collecting and organizing—now that I think about it, neither Haohao nor Yuyu will want them. Maybe I should donate them. Maybe that’s the right thing to do.

But after they’re gone, what will I have left?

Recording those data points every day, monitoring Meifang’s diet, checking the accounts, reading industry reports… what am I doing? What am I trying to control? I can’t control aging, I can’t control my body, and I can’t control my children’s lives.

Chess Friend A was right. I’ve been constantly fearing the future, fearing death, fearing that everything is meaningless. So I use control to fight that fear. But doing so has only turned the present into a nightmare as well.

I haven't cherished anything.

Meifang said her blood pressure was high again today, and I started nagging her about her diet again. I saw the exhaustion in her eyes. I saw it, but I didn't stop. I kept controlling, kept demanding, kept pretending I was doing it for her own good.

Maybe I should just sit with her for a while. Just sit there, without talking, without planning, without controlling.

But I don't know how.

As for the tools in the workshop, I might call the technical school tomorrow. They asked me before if I’d be willing to donate them. I never agreed because I felt I couldn't let go. Now, I feel like maybe it’s time.

It’s not because I’ve had some great epiphany. I’m just tired.

Too tired to keep controlling everything.

I’m back for the night. I had a talk with Xiao Wang today. Regarding that issue with his testing process, instead of my usual "this is a rookie mistake" approach, I sat down with him to go over the data and asked for his thoughts. He responded quite well and eventually identified the problem himself.

I’m wondering if this way is more effective. The old way was faster, but he always seemed a bit off afterwards. Trying it this way once felt different.

What do you think? Is this the right move?

Actually, I’ve been thinking lately that managing a team might involve more than just demanding high standards. I used to think that just getting things done right was enough, but now I feel like there might be something else to it...

Anyway, let’s leave it at that for now. Have you eaten? I saw there are still some greens in the fridge. How have your blood pressure readings been this week? Remember you have a follow-up appointment tomorrow.

ranked_items.content:
June 15, 2022, 7:30 PM

I lost again at the chess room this afternoon. It wasn't that I lost to Player A; I lost to myself.

Player A said I played very conservatively today. The word he used was "cautious," but I could hear what he really meant—he was saying I’ve changed. I didn’t argue. I just said, "I’m getting older, I have to be more careful," and kept setting up the pieces. He didn’t say anything else.

But I know he saw it. I saw it in myself, too.

I used to dare to take risks, to make bold moves in the mid-game. And now? Now I pause and think for a long time before every single step. It’s not because I’ve become smarter; it’s because I’m afraid—afraid that one wrong move will lead to a point of no return. Afraid of wasting time.

It’s strange. A game only lasts two hours, and losing is just losing a game. But I find myself applying this same logic to everything in my life. I’m stricter with my subordinates at work, I’m more controlling over Meifang’s diet at home, and I even use a magnifying glass to scrutinize the numbers on my medical reports.

Why?

I tell myself this is being responsible. But deep down, I know it’s fear. Fear that time is running out. Fear that something is slipping through my fingers and I won’t be able to catch it in time.

The tools in the workshop are still there. The notebooks are there, too. I’ve recorded about half of it. Hao is in Shenzhen, and Yu is in Guangzhou. Neither of them has ever asked what those tools are for. I haven't brought it up, either.

Sometimes I wonder, living this cautiously—what exactly am I trying to protect?

I have to go back to the chess room tomorrow. I wonder if Player A will notice something else.

Forget it, stop thinking. Time for bed. I haven't finished going through the medical report from May; I’ll give it another careful look tomorrow.

ranked_items.content:
After returning home that evening, Chen Guilin changed his clothes and sat down at the dining table. Li Meifang was serving the soup.

He ate a few mouthfuls of rice in silence before putting down his chopsticks.

"I might have been too harsh at the department meeting today," he said, his voice low, eyes fixed on his bowl. "The new QC inspector messed up the data entry again, and I called him out in front of everyone. He looked pretty shaken."

Li Meifang looked up at him but didn't respond immediately.

"My temper has been short lately," he paused, tapping his finger on the table. "I can feel it myself. I have high standards for the work, but..." He didn't finish the sentence, opting for a sip of soup instead.

He turned to his wife, his tone tinged with uncertainty. "Do you think I'm being too demanding? I want to get things done right, but it seems like..." He hesitated, "it seems like I always end up making people uncomfortable."

He wasn't looking for comfort; it was more like he was trying to confirm something. There was exhaustion in his eyes, along with a certain guilt he couldn't quite put into words.

"The physical exam is next week," he suddenly changed the subject, though his tone remained heavy. "I need to get to bed early these next few days."

He lowered his head and continued eating, but his movements were much slower than usual.

ranked_items.content:
# MEMO

**Date: September 19, 2022, Morning**
**Location: Office Cubicle**

---

Maybe I should change my ways.

The improvement plan Xiao Wang submitted during yesterday’s meeting was actually quite good. At the time, I pointed out three flaws directly. His expression… I can still see it now. He didn’t push back; he just nodded and took notes. When I walked past his desk at the end of the day, he was packing up—his movements were hurried, as if he were escaping something.

Is this right?

I’ve always believed that being strict is being responsible. A 25% reduction in defect rates—those are the facts. But what lies behind the data? It’s my subordinates working in fear under my gaze. Li Meifang once said, "You’re the same way at home." I didn't respond.

Control ≠ Respect.

Where did that phrase come from yesterday? Perhaps it was something the doctor said that triggered it. He talked about my blood pressure, my carotid artery, all those numbers… I’ve spent 40 years trying to control everything, yet my body has betrayed me anyway. I cannot control it. So what about my subordinates? I "manage" them the same way, as if I were managing a machine. But they aren't machines.

Time is running out. What actually matters?

It’s not the process optimization forms. It’s not those perfect quality inspection reports. If I were to collapse right now, who would remember how strict I was? Who would think of me because of a 25% lower defect rate?

Regarding Xiao Wang’s proposal—I actually should have praised him in front of everyone. And *then* pointed out the room for improvement.

I need to rethink how I speak to my staff.

Maybe there’s still time.

---

*To be continued*

ranked_items.content:
Meifang, are you free? I wrote something in the workshop that I wanted to show you.

(Deleted)

Meifang, I was thinking a lot while tidying up my tools this afternoon. There are some things in my notebook, if you have the time...

(Deleted)

Take a look at this.

(Image: A notebook page with neat handwriting but obvious signs of frequent editing. On the page is a timeline from 1982 to 2022, with scattered notes in blue ink—"Lessons learned over the years," "What if I could start over," "What actually matters most...")

It suddenly occurred to me that for these forty years, I’ve been so busy trying to do things right that I never really stopped to think about why I was doing them. It wasn't until you went to the hospital with me the other day that I realized—some things are more important than being perfect.

I used to be too hard on you. But the truth is, you’ve always been there.

(Pause)

It’s nothing, I just wanted to tell you.
