ranked_items.content:
Meifang, I’m back.

I played a few rounds at the club tonight. Midway through, I just came out with it—told them the doctor found something with my heart and that I need medication to keep it under control. I thought I’d just mention it briefly and move on, but they actually started asking quite a lot of questions. The strange thing was, there wasn't a hint of pity in their voices. It was as if they were talking about something perfectly normal—asking what meds I’m on, saying they have friends in the same boat, and that as long as you take your pills and get some exercise, you'll be fine.

Sitting there, I suddenly felt this sense of relief. I don't quite know how to describe it. Maybe it’s because they didn't treat me like someone who needs looking after; they just treated me as Chen Guilin, their friend and fellow player.

It made me realize that all these years, I’ve been trying so hard to prove I’m fine—striving to be the best at work, managing every little detail at home, even obsessing over your blood pressure. I thought that was what it meant to be responsible for my family. But looking back now...

The way I’ve treated you in the past hasn't been fair. You’ve always been there for me, taking care of me, yet I’ve always looked at you with a critical, watchful eye. It wasn't until my friends treated me the way they did today that I understood what it feels like to be accepted and treated as an equal.

I want to change. I don’t mean I’m going to stop caring about the family, but... I want to try being a companion to you, rather than a supervisor. If you’re willing.

We haven't talked much these past few years. I think it might be time for a change.

ranked_items.content:
# October 19, 2022, Evening

After dinner, Meifang told me I’ve changed.

I didn’t deny it right away. I just sat there and listened as she went on—saying I’ve been talking more lately, that I’m not so quick to blame people anymore, and that I’ve started asking her how her day was. She spoke calmly, not as if she were praising me, but more like she was stating an observed fact.

I replied, "Maybe it’s time for a change."

Even as I said it, I felt a bit strange. This isn't the kind of thing I usually say. Normally, I would explain, defend myself, or use logic to prove my way was right. But today, I didn't.

Something happened at the office this afternoon. Xiao Li made another mistake—a dimensional error that never should have happened. Usually, I would have pointed it out in front of everyone, being blunt so he’d learn his lesson. But I stopped. I actually stopped myself.

I asked him, "Which part of the process went wrong this time?"

He nervously explained the whole workflow. I listened until he finished, then pointed out the issue and asked how he could avoid it next time. When he nodded, there was no look of humiliation in his eyes.

Thinking back on it tonight, I realized something—when I stopped blaming him, he was actually more willing to listen to me. This discovery makes me feel a bit uneasy.

For years, I believed that being harsh was being responsible, and being blunt was being honest. I managed the department this way; I treated my subordinates this way. And the result? Defect rates went down, but people left. It never occurred to me that the two might be cause and effect.

The doctor said my blood pressure and heart rate need managing. I’ve been on medication for a week, and my body does feel a bit more settled. But what’s truly changing me isn't the medicine; it’s this sense of powerlessness—the realization that I can’t control my body’s aging, I can’t control the passage of time, and I can’t control whether others understand me.

Maybe that’s the real transformation. It’s not that I’ve become gentler, but that I’ve finally admitted my own limitations.

As Meifang cleared the dishes in the kitchen, I sat in the living room and suddenly thought of my father. Did the technicians of his generation go through moments like this? Moving from believing they could control everything to accepting that some things are simply beyond their grasp?

I never asked him. And now, I can’t.

I have a department meeting tomorrow. I want to change the way I give feedback. I don’t know how it will go.

But at the very least, I’ve decided to try.

ranked_items.content:
Played some chess over the weekend. A chess friend mentioned how bad the market is lately—houses just aren't selling. It made me think of the pressure Haozi is under in Shenzhen; I’m a bit worried for him.

The weather was nice, but I’ve really felt my physical stamina declining these past two years. After three games of chess, I felt quite tired. Checked my blood pressure when I got home; luckily, it’s still within the normal range.

Spent some time watching TV with Lao Li this evening. She’s been controlling her diet lately and is sticking to it well. She has a follow-up appointment at the hospital tomorrow.

Gave the tools in the workshop another wipe-down. They’ve been sitting there for quite a few years now. Sometimes I wonder what will become of these things later on. But for now, I should just focus on getting the tasks at hand done.

Need to make the most of things while I’m still mobile.

ranked_items.content:
# March 28, 2022, 9:45 PM

Had a phone call with Haozi.

He said business has been pretty good lately, and the mortgage pressure isn't as heavy anymore. I originally wanted to ask him if he had any interest in mechanical engineering—if he wanted to know more about what I’ve been working on all these years... but the words died in my throat. I changed the subject and asked how work was going instead. He said it was fine, then asked if there was something I wanted to say.

I told him it was nothing, that everything was under control.

After hanging up, I stood in the living room for a long while, phone still in hand.

I thought about the tools in my workshop. Forty years. Some I modified myself; others I repaired after they broke. Every single one has a story, but those stories only exist in my head. I’ve recorded some of it in my notebooks, but what’s the point of recording things? Haozi won't read them. Yuyu won't read them either. They have their own lives, which is only natural.

But I still feel... wrong.

These things, this knowledge—it just ends here. I’ve been in this position for twelve years, proposing several improvements every year that the company adopted. But those will end, too. When I retire and the new manager arrives, he’ll have his own methods. The optimizations I made might eventually just become a statistic—"defect rate down by 25%"—and nothing more.

Li Meifang is downstairs watching TV. I didn't go down.

I pulled out the last physical exam report and read it again. Blood pressure is a bit high. The doctor said to be careful. Mine has started to fluctuate, too. It was stable for the last two years, but now...

I can't control it.

That’s what hurts the most. I can control workflows, quality standards, and my subordinates' performance. But I can't control time. I can't control my body. I can't control whether Haozi or Yuyu will inherit anything.

In the margin of one page in my notebook, I wrote: "Who will ever use this?" I didn't erase it.

I have to go to the office tomorrow. There’s a quality control meeting in the afternoon. A new employee’s report isn't detailed enough; I need to point it out. It’s my responsibility. But I’m getting a bit tired of this kind of thing. Or rather... I know where this frustration is coming from. It’s because I’m demanding perfection from others while I myself am losing my grip on it.

Thinking this way makes me feel a bit hypocritical.

Forget it. Time for bed. There’s a lot to do tomorrow.

I need to hurry while I can still move.

ranked_items.content:
April 5, 2022. Night.

I’ve been sitting in the office for a long time. Wang Gang’s quality inspection report is still spinning in my head.

The formatting was off again when he handed it in this morning—the classification standards in the header didn't match last year’s specifications. It’s such a basic thing to get wrong. I couldn't help myself; I snapped at him. He tried to explain, but I cut him off. No excuses. Professionalism is about an obsession with detail.

His face changed the moment I said it.

Looking back now, my tone was harsh. Harsher than usual. He’s actually a decent employee. In the past, I would have pointed out these small mistakes, but never like that. Why today...?

My fingers have been tapping on the desk for a long while. Rapidly. I didn't even realize I was doing it.

What am I thinking? Was his report really that outrageous? Or... was I just taking it out on him?

It’s been like this lately. I’m becoming more demanding with my subordinates and more micro-managing with my wife. This morning, I was looking at her blood pressure logs again; a single high reading and I started feeling restless. I spent another whole night in the tool shed, numbering all the precision tools and documenting their uses. I’m terrified that one day I’ll suddenly... lose the ability to move, and these things will become completely useless because no one knows how to handle them.

Two years until retirement. Two years.

I know what I’m trying to do. I’m trying to control things. Control the work, the standards, everything at home—even the aging of my own body. It’s as if by managing every tiny detail, I can prove I’m still useful, that I can still... what?

Wang Gang has to come in for work tomorrow. I need to figure out how to handle this. Not for his sake, but because... I don’t want to become this kind of person. I don’t want to use harshness to mask my own fear.

But I don’t know how to change.

How much longer can I do this? How much longer can I keep things under control like this?

ranked_items.content:
# Studio Notes

Sunday, August 28, 2022, Morning

I didn't sleep well last night. I couldn't stop thinking about that phone call.

The school director said they are very interested. Said the project is feasible. Said their students need something like this. I had to replay it twice in my head to make sure I hadn't misheard.

I spent the whole night scribbling nonsense in my notebook—all about how tools get lost and knowledge dissipates. The handwriting was terrible, like the scrawlings of a madman. Looking over it this morning, it felt foreign even to me. That didn't look like my work.

But what I came up with yesterday is different.

I thought: what if I organized these tools and this experience into something systematic? Not for Chen Hao or Chen Yu—they each have their own paths—but for those who truly need it. For young people who are interested in precision engineering, just as my father influenced me back then.

the moment that thought surfaced, my hands stopped shaking.

I spent an hour reorganizing yesterday's chaotic notes. Titles, modules, schedules. My handwriting became much clearer. My fingers remembered what to do. It felt just like drafting a new process improvement plan in the studio.

"Precision Engineering Heritage Project"—that’s the name I gave it. It consists of three parts: tool donations, craft lectures, and apprenticeship training. The costs are controllable, and the outputs are clear. The school said they would cooperate.

My wife asked me this morning why I hadn't slept. I didn't explain much. I just said I was thinking about something new. She nodded and went back to her breakfast. She’s used to me being like this.

The medical report is still in the drawer. The cardiologist's words are still ringing in my ears. But this morning, I didn't keep obsessing over those numbers. I was doing something else.

Perhaps that’s the difference. I cannot change the fact that my body is aging. But I can decide to leave something behind before—or even while—it happens. Not to prove that I lived, but because... some things are worth passing on.

I look at the tools in the studio. Dozens of them, some bought over twenty years ago. They won't lose their value just because I die. They will keep working in someone else's hands.

This thought brings me a feeling I haven't felt in a long time.

It isn't despair.

I need to send the detailed proposal to the school next Monday. I also need to organize the first donation list.

The pen is still in my hand.

ranked_items.content:
Friday Night, October 28, 2022

I’m sitting in the study; the log on my desk remains blank. After the meeting ended this afternoon, I sat here alone for two hours—not organizing files, not reviewing reports, just sitting.

Li Meifang knocked and asked if I wanted tea. I said no. She didn't say anything else, and I heard her footsteps slowly fade away.

What happened today? It’s hard to put into words. The meeting itself was ordinary—discussing quality control standards for the new product line. A young engineer, Xiao Wang, proposed an idea. My first instinct was to jump in and point out exactly where his thinking was flawed. But the words caught in my throat. I stopped myself. I let him finish, and then I said, "Let’s look into this together."

I saw a spark in his eyes. It wasn't fear; it was the look of someone being seen.

Then I started asking him *why* he thought that way, instead of telling him *how* he should think. He answered earnestly. Some parts did need adjustment, but he worked it out himself. I just walked the path with him.

After the meeting adjourned, I stood in my office for a long time. Suddenly, I thought of my father. That was how he taught me—never giving me the answers directly. He would ask, "Why do you think it was designed this way?" and then wait for me to figure it out. Back then, I found his method infuriating; I thought he was being inefficient. Now, I understand.

How did I manage to forget his ways entirely over all these years?

My "supervision" of Meifang, my "bluntness" with subordinates, my "concern" for my children—these words all sound so justified, but what are they really? They are control. I wanted to ensure everything ran according to my standards so I could prove to myself that I was useful, that I was necessary.

The doctor said my blood pressure readings have improved this month. I assumed it was because I stuck to my exercise plan and strictly controlled my diet. But perhaps it’s because I haven’t been so tense lately. I’ve stopped trying so desperately to control everything.

Today’s conversation with Xiao Wang made me realize that perhaps my value doesn't lie in how many answers I have, but in how far I can walk alongside others. Not in how much I can control, but in how much I can understand.

The thought is a bit frightening. It means I have to let go. I have to admit that I don’t know, that I can’t always do it, and that I make mistakes too.

But at the same time, it’s a relief.

I want to call Hao later. Not to ask about his mortgage or his recent performance at work. I just want to hear what’s on his mind lately. Maybe he has things he’s been wanting to tell me, and he’s just been waiting for me to ask.

I’ve spent my life waiting for others to do things my way. Perhaps it’s time I take the initiative to understand theirs.

I have work tomorrow. But for tonight, I think I’ll just keep sitting here.
ranked_items.subquestion: Brother Chen, seeing how meticulous you used to be with both work and family, I always felt you were someone who insisted on having everything under control. But looking back at these past few months, it seems like you’ve changed quite a bit. Have you started to realize that some things are simply beyond our control, and found yourself loosening up a little?

ranked_items.content:
# Monday, July 18, 2022, 14:30

It’s been two hours since I got the report.

I’m sitting in my office with the report spread out on the desk. I told everyone "it’s nothing serious," but my fingers keep tracing those marked spots—blood pressure 139/92, fasting blood glucose 6.8. The numbers themselves don’t lie. I know that better than anyone.

I heard everything the doctor said, but I chose to remember only the first half of his sentences. "It’s under decent control." The rest—the parts about "needing to be more cautious" and "regular follow-ups"—I just let slide. That’s not my style.

Meifang was cooking when I got home. She saw the look on my face and asked about it. I didn't give her a straight answer, just said "I'm fine." But I know she can tell. After 35 years, she knows me too well. She tried to come closer, wanting me to talk, but I turned away and went into the study.

Then I suddenly turned back around and said to her, "Starting today, you need to cooperate too. We can't let your blood pressure issues drag on anymore. I’m going to set up a plan for you."

She was startled by my tone. I saw it. But I didn't stop. I need to control something. I need to ensure that something is within my grasp.

She isn't the problem. I know that. But at this moment, I can't face the fact that *I* am the problem.

Data doesn't lie. I’ve lived my whole life by data and standards, and now the data is starting to betray me. Blood pressure, blood sugar, weight—every indicator is slowly crossing the line. I can see the trend. I can predict it. And that is exactly what’s so terrifying.

The two-year retirement plan. I spoke of it with such certainty. But now I’m starting to wonder: if my body continues like this, how many "two years" do I have left?

The tools in the workshop are still there. I wanted to go and wipe them down, as I usually do. But today, I didn't move. I sat in my office, staring at this report, trying to use logic to convince myself that this is all within manageable limits.

But I know that some things are forever beyond my control.

For the first time, I truly felt that.

ranked_items.content:
October 30, 2022, 8:00 PM

Li Meifang is already asleep. I’m sitting in the study; on the desk lies today’s medical report and a cup of tea that went cold long ago.

I said something to her today that left even me a bit stunned. I said, "Perhaps the meaning of life isn't in perfect control, but in genuine connection." Hearing those words come out of my mouth felt like someone was impersonating me. And yet, it really is what I was thinking.

Since I started the medication these past few days, the physical side effects haven't been as bad as I imagined. The doctor says it’s a good sign. But what’s stranger is that I’ve actually started to relax. I used to check my medical reports three times a day, recording every single number in a notebook, trying to force them back to "normal" through sheer willpower. Now... I still look at the reports, but I no longer feel the need to "subdue" them.

Last night, Meifang took my hand. She does this often, and I used to pull away after a moment to get back to my accounts or the news. But this time, I didn't. I let her hold it—maybe for five or six seconds. She didn’t say anything, and neither did I. We just held hands. Eventually, she let go gently and went to the kitchen to pour me some water.

I realize now that I owe her a great deal. For thirty-five years, I’ve used excuses like "monitoring her blood pressure" or "managing the family finances" to package my need for control as "responsibility." In truth, it was just my way of proving I was useful, that I deserved to be needed—by controlling everything. She saw through it long ago, but she never said a word. She just stayed by my side, waiting.

Chen Hao called recently saying his mortgage is a heavy burden. In the past, I would have immediately started analyzing his finances, giving him advice, even feeling a bit dissatisfied with his "poor planning." This time, I just listened, then told him, "It’s okay, take your time." There was a pause on the other end of the line; he didn’t seem used to me responding like that. I’m not used to it either. But it felt... more real.

Then there’s that idea about donating my tools to the technical school. Initially, I thought of it as "legacy"—wanting young people to learn what I’ve accumulated over forty years. But today I realized that was just another form of control; I wanted their learning to prove that my value wouldn't vanish with retirement. I still want to donate them, but the reason has changed. I just want to put those tools where they can continue to be used and cherished. As for who uses them or what they learn, that’s not for me to decide, nor does it need to be.

It’s strange. I always thought letting go of control would make me panic. But actually, it makes me feel... light?

My subordinates at the office are still a bit afraid of me. I know that. But lately, I’ve been thinking that maybe I could try a different way. Not by lowering standards, but... by changing how I communicate. Letting them know my expectations are high because I believe they can do well, not because I’m disappointed in them. This will require me to say more, and I’ve never been good at that. But perhaps it’s worth learning.

Tomorrow is Monday. Another week begins. I’ll keep taking the medicine, keep working, and keep being there for Meifang. And maybe, at the right moment, I’ll sit with her—saying nothing, just holding her hand.

That is enough.

ranked_items.content:
# September 15, 2022, 19:30

Sitting in the study, my hands are still shaking a little.

What happened this afternoon keeps looping in my head. Meifang said she needs to go for a follow-up checkup too, her voice laced with worry. I originally wanted to comfort her with data like I always do—tell her I’ve started the medication, the indicators will gradually improve, and everything will be fine as long as we follow the doctor’s plan. But when the words reached my lips, I didn't say them. Instead, I heard myself say, "I’m nervous too."

The moment those words came out, even I was a bit stunned.

I never say things like that in front of her. For forty years, I’ve been the one in control—the accounts, the plans, every matter in the house, big or small. She’s used to my composure, and I’m used to being the one who must remain unshakable. But today, I admitted it. I said, "Let’s go together."

And then, I took her hand.

Thinking back on that gesture now, it still feels strange. My hand holding hers; her hand was very warm, probably because she was nervous too. We just sat there for a while, neither of us speaking. I could feel her looking at me, but I didn't look up.

The doctor’s terminology is still echoing in my mind—metabolic syndrome, atherosclerosis, long-term medication. I always thought that through discipline and control, I could manage my body like a precision machine. But now I realize I have no control at all. The body has its own logic; it doesn't run according to my schedule.

The moment I held her hand, I wasn't thinking about the diagnosis itself, but rather—if I really collapse, what happens to her? All those tools, forty years of experience, the things I haven't had time to organize yet...

I don't know if this counts as weakness. I’ve always hated weakness. But today, it seems I allowed myself a little bit of it.

My hands are still shaking. I went to the workshop and wiped down my tools, from the first to the last, every single one of them with great care. Only while doing that did my hands slowly steady themselves.

Tomorrow, I’ll continue as planned. But something has changed, and I haven't figured out how to face it yet.

ranked_items.content:
# Calendar Event

**Date**: Saturday, October 22, 2022  
**Time**: 8:00 PM  
**Title**: Call with Chen Hao — Sharing the Diagnosis  

---

## Event Details

**Location**: Home Study  

**Participants**: Chen Guilin, Chen Hao (via phone)

**Description**:  
After receiving my medical report yesterday, I decided to call Chen Hao tonight. Instead of my usual routine of asking about his mortgage and work progress, I took the initiative and said, "There’s something I want to share with you."

The doctor said there is an issue with my heart and I’ll need long-term medication. I gave him the facts simply—no hiding, no exaggerating. There was a brief silence on his end before he asked, "Is it serious?" I told him, "It’s manageable, but I have to accept the reality."

He said he would make time to come back and see me. Instead of my usual response—"No need to take time off, work comes first"—I simply said, "Okay."

The call lasted about ten minutes. It was brief, but unlike our past conversations, it wasn't just an exchange of information.

---

## Notes

**Keywords**: Acceptance | Vulnerability | Communication | Change  

**Reflection**: This might be the first time I’ve proactively shared my "imperfections" with my child. I used to feel I had to maintain a position of strength in front of them, to prove I was still in control of everything. Now I realize that perhaps this kind of honesty is a different form of strength.

**Follow-up**: Remember to take medication on time. Go to the pharmacy on Monday.

ranked_items.content:
# Work Memo

**Date**: October 28, 2022  
**Subject**: Reflections on a New Approach to Feedback  
**Recorded by**: Chen Guilin  

---

## Record of Events

This morning, I discussed the process plan for a new product with a junior engineer from our department. While there were some lapses in rigor in his proposal, I refrained from my usual habit of pointing out the flaws directly.

**What I did differently**:
1. First, I acknowledged specific areas where he did well—the data collection was thorough, and the conceptual framework was clear.
2. I explained why precision control is critical at this stage, particularly regarding its impact on subsequent costs and yield.
3. Instead of providing the answer, I asked him: "Let’s look into this together—is there anything else we need to consider here?"
4. He identified the problem himself and even added a perspective I hadn't considered.

**Result**: His motivation was visibly boosted. He took the initiative to look up relevant materials after the meeting and returned this afternoon to discuss the improved plan.

---

## Reflections

This approach is a significant departure from my previous style. I used to be blunt about pointing out issues for the sake of efficiency. Looking back, however, that kind of feedback might lead people to passively accept criticism rather than truly think for themselves.

This time was different. He was involved in the thought process, which gave him more confidence in the final conclusion. More importantly, he didn't just learn the answer to this specific problem; he learned **how to analyze problems**.

---

## Follow-up Plans

If this method proves effective, I want to systematize it:

- Implement a new feedback framework within the department (Specific Praise → Explanation of Principles → Open Discussion).
- Compile a "Technical Mentorship Guide" in preparation for the knowledge hand-off before my retirement.
- Potentially organize a technical sharing session at the end of the year using this interactive format instead of a one-way lecture.

The benefit of this is that knowledge isn't just memorized—it is understood and internalized. Perhaps this is what true "legacy" looks like.

---

## Remarks

This idea stems from some recent reflections. My health is a reminder that I won't be in this position forever. Before I leave, can I ensure these young people inherit not just my standards, but my way of thinking? That might be more valuable than any procedural document.

I will continue to observe the results next week.

ranked_items.content:
# Monday, September 12, 2022 – Sunny

After the morning meeting, I sat in my office, my hands still trembling slightly.

It wasn’t nerves. It was because I had just done something that didn't feel like me.

Li Ming, my subordinate, messed up his data sheet again—the third time this month. By my standards, a low-level error like that should have been called out directly; I should have made him understand exactly what "unacceptable" means. I had even rehearsed the blunt words in my head. But in the conference room, when I saw his tense expression, I paused.

I paused.

What I actually said was: "Let’s look at how we can improve this together." Then I spent ten minutes explaining it to him, instead of my usual three-sentence reprimand.

It’s so strange.

Sitting here now in the corner of my office, I’m trying to understand why I did that. Is it because I’ve been dwelling on the doctor’s words all week? Or is it because I’m starting to realize that I might actually be changing?

The doctor said my body is failing. Pre-metabolic syndrome. Early signs of carotid atherosclerosis. A blood sugar level of 110 mg/dL. These numbers are like nails driven into my brain. I always thought that through precise management—routine, control, supervision—I could maintain everything. But the doctor used data to tell me that I’ve failed.

Maybe that’s why I softened my stance with Li Ming. Maybe it’s because I suddenly realized I can’t control everything. I can’t even control my own body.

So, what right do I have to treat my subordinates that way?

The thought feels foreign to me. It also feels frightening.

Because if I let go of my obsession with detail, if I abandon that relentless management style, then who am I? What was the point of everything I’ve stood for over the last forty years?

On the other hand, I saw a spark in Li Ming’s eyes after I changed my tone. He started listening intently, started asking questions, instead of just hanging his head and taking the criticism. Maybe this change... maybe it isn't entirely a bad thing?

I don’t know.

I’m exhausted right now. These thoughts have drained my energy. The pressure is still there, but it feels different. It’s no longer anger at imperfection; it’s anxiety about time. How much longer? How much time do I have left to change? How much time is left to pass things on?

My wife said yesterday, "I knew you’d have this eventually." She was right. She’s always right. But I didn’t want to admit it.

Now, I have to.

The medicine the doctor prescribed is still in the drawer. I haven't taken it yet.

Tomorrow. I’ll deal with it tomorrow.

ranked_items.content:
# Work Log

**Date**: Friday, October 28, 2022
**Weather**: Sunny
**Recorded by**: Chen Guilin

---

## Today's Work

Held a departmental feedback meeting at 10:00 AM. The approach today was different from usual, and it’s worth noting down.

Previously, I would point out problems directly and give corrective feedback immediately. Today, I changed my mindset. I started by praising several engineers for their recent performance—specifically Xiao Wang’s ideas on improving the welding process. Only then did I explain why certain parameters needed to be adjusted this way instead of that way. The key was that I invited them into the thought process rather than just handing them the answers.

I asked Xiao Li, "Why do you think this data is fluctuating?" He was a bit nervous at first, but then he slowly began to analyze it. In the end, he derived the conclusion himself. The result was much better than if I had just told him.

After the meeting, several young engineers came to me on their own initiative to ask questions. Their attitude was noticeably different. It wasn't the defensive stance you see after being criticized; they genuinely wanted to understand the principles.

## Reflections

This method takes more time. If I had followed my old efficiency standards, I could have finished in five minutes what took twenty today. But the outcome was different.

Looking back, I’ve spent all these years chasing "correctness" and "efficiency." Point out the problem, give the answer, and move on to the next. But perhaps the issue isn't the answer itself, but whether they truly understand the "why."

I’m thinking that maybe this is what I should leave behind for this team before I retire—not just a complete operating manual, but a way of thinking. A dedication to detail and an attitude of not being afraid to ask why.

I used to think these young people weren't serious enough, but looking at it now, perhaps it was my approach that gave them no opportunity to truly think.

I’ll continue to observe the effects tomorrow. I might try this approach again during next week’s process audit meeting.

---

**Notes**: The defect rate remained at 0.8% this week, continuing to stay below the industry average.

ranked_items.content:
I told Meifang about it over dinner tonight.

Another formatting error cropped up in QA this morning. It wasn't a major issue, but I can't stand that kind of slackness when it comes to details. I was right there on the floor and called it out immediately; my voice might have been a bit loud. The subordinate looked pretty shaken, but I had to make it clear—details determine product quality, and product quality determines whether this company survives. I’m not exaggerating.

I told Meifang that I need to overhaul the department’s standard operating procedures. Just emphasizing it verbally isn't enough; it has to be documented with every step clearly defined. As she listened, she just looked at me that way, and I knew what she was thinking: *Here we go again.* But this time is different. I can feel that if things continue like this, the problems are only going to multiply.

She said, "Don't push yourself too hard." I didn't respond to that. After a few bites of food, I explained once more why these standards must be strictly enforced. She just nodded and kept eating.

The truth is, I know my subordinates are afraid of me. But what’s wrong with a little fear? Fear makes people diligent. I’ve been in this position for 12 years, and our defect rate is 25% lower than the industry average—that’s not because I have a pleasant temperament.

Lately, I just feel this constant urge to hurry. Whether it’s work or anything else. While I’m still able to move, I need to get everything done right.

ranked_items.content:
September 22, 2022, 8:30 PM

I’m sitting in my workshop, still holding that pair of pliers. I’ve wiped them over and over, but they never seem to get quite clean.

The doctor’s words are still circling in my head. 110, atherosclerosis, long-term medication. The numbers are clear. My whole life, I’ve been best at dealing with clear numbers, but this time, that clarity feels suffocating.

Meifang said something yesterday that I hadn’t been able to put into words: "I knew you’d have this sooner or later." She saw it coming. In the office, I can spot a flaw in the smallest detail, yet I couldn’t see my own body slowly breaking down. Irony.

I pulled out that notebook. Usually, I only record work logs, accounts, and process improvements. Today, for some reason, I started writing something else. I wrote a sentence, crossed it out. Wrote it again.

"If I could do it over, I would teach the younger ones more."

My hand shook a little as I wrote that.

Forty years. Forty years in this factory, from inspector to manager. Those precision tools, those processes, those improvement plans I puzzled out all by myself… suddenly I wonder, what happens to all of it if I collapse? Will my subordinates keep up my standards? Or will everything just fall apart?

But then I thought, maybe I’m wrong. Maybe the value of these things doesn’t depend on me handing them over personally. Maybe it’s already there—in the processes I improved, in the standards I demanded, in the things those young people learned even while they were resenting me.

I wrote another line in the notebook: "Maybe I should hold a technical sharing session before I retire."

As I finished the sentence, the tightness in my chest loosened a bit. Not entirely, but it was there.

It’s not about proving anything. It’s just… if I talk about these things, if I let people see *why* I do what I do instead of just how strict I am, then maybe… maybe there won’t be so much regret.

The doctor said I need to control my diet, get regular check-ups, and take my medicine. I can do that. Following a plan is what I do best.

But this time, there’s something new in the plan. Not just to live longer—though I hope for that, too. But to make these forty years actually mean something.

Meifang is calling me for dinner from downstairs. I didn’t answer right away. I looked again at those sentences in the notebook, the ones I’d revised over and over.

Maybe I’ll propose the idea to my supervisor tomorrow. Maybe I won’t. I don’t know yet.

But at least I wrote it down.

**Work Log: Monday, July 25, 2022**

**Morning:** Departmental meeting to discuss Q3 quality metrics. The defect rate is holding steady at 0.8%, still below the industry average. However, I noticed that the reject rate for the third shift in the welding process has climbed to 1.2%. This is unacceptable. I need to have a word with the shift lead this afternoon—I’ll need to be firm. This kind of lax execution becomes incredibly hard to correct once it turns into a habit.

**Afternoon:** Reviewed the inspection reports from our new hire, Xiao Wang. There were three missed checks. While they didn't cause any ultimate issues, it reflects a shallow understanding of the process. I gave him written feedback, pointing out every single detail. Some people think I’m too demanding, but quality standards are standards; there is no room for negotiation. I’ve been in this position for 12 years, and I’ve seen far too many problems caused by a "good enough" attitude.

My hand is shaking a bit; I pressed my temples. Probably just a lack of sleep these past couple of days.

**Evening:** Organized this month’s work records and data sheets. A thought suddenly struck me: what happens to these processes I’ve built, the standards I’ve set, and the experience I’ve accumulated after I retire? It took me so many years to refine this system to its current state. Will my subordinates be able to maintain it? Can newcomers like Xiao Wang truly grasp the logic behind these standards?

I must keep documenting every step clearly. While I’m still able, I need to organize everything that can be passed down.

Tomorrow, continue following up on the improvements in the welding process.

— Recorded by Chen Guilin at 22:45

ranked_items.content:
# August 2, 2022, 8:30 PM

Sitting in the study. My wife is watching TV in the living room, the volume turned down low. I intended to organize today’s exercise logs, but right now, I can’t do anything.

I lost at the chess club today. That rarely happens.

It wasn't because my skills have declined—though perhaps they have—it was because I simply couldn't focus. When my friend A asked what was wrong, I just... let it out. About the medical report. Blood pressure 147/92, blood sugar 6.8, and a slight abnormality on the ECG. I hadn't even figured out how to say it, and yet there it was.

That’s not like me.

A said it was fine, that many people are like this and I just need regular check-ups. His words sounded hollow. I know he meant well, but he doesn't understand at all. No one can. This isn't just about abnormal data.

I’ve been controlling everything. Recording my wife’s blood pressure every day, watching everything she eats, walking 8,000 steps myself, weighing my diet down to the gram, organizing all the data into spreadsheets. I thought that by doing this, I could... I could stop something. But now I realize it’s all futile. Data can’t change anything.

Maybe I should retire early. I startled myself when I heard those words come out of my mouth. But once the thought appeared, I couldn't shake it. If my body is truly starting to fail, how much time do I have left? How much time is left for the things I haven't done yet?

The tools in the workshop, those ideas, and... I haven't even had a real conversation with Hao and Yu. They are in Shenzhen and Guangzhou, while I stay here recording data as if that were a form of love.

When I lost the game, I looked at the black and white pieces on the board and suddenly felt exhausted. Not a physical exhaustion, but something else.

It’s 8:30 PM. I have work tomorrow. Next week is the department’s quality assessment. Everything continues as if nothing has changed. But everything has changed.

I don’t know what to do. Or rather, I know what to do—make a plan, adjust the regimen, control the variables—but this time, I realize there are just too many things beyond my control.

Maybe I should sleep. I have to record the blood pressure data tomorrow.

**Location:** Living Room

---

I need to have a proper talk with Meifang this week. Not the usual monitoring or correcting, but a real conversation.

---

- [ ] Listen to her—really listen.
ranked_items.subquestion: Brother Chen, seeing how meticulous you used to be with both work and family, I always felt you were someone who insisted on having everything under control. But looking back at these past few months, it seems like you’ve changed quite a bit. Have you started to realize that some things are simply beyond our control, and found yourself loosening up a little?

ranked_items.content:
July 22, 2022, 9:15 AM

I haven’t been able to focus all morning.

Just now in the office, because of a formatting issue in a quality inspection report—the header was off by a mere half-centimeter—I tore into Xiao Wang in front of the entire department. I said some terrible things, things like "I wonder if all these years of management have meant anything at all." His face went white after I finished, and he took the afternoon off.

Now, sitting in my office, I’m wondering: why did I do that? It was just a formatting error. He’s a diligent employee; a small thing like that could have easily been corrected in private. But I didn’t. I said those things in public.

I know what I’m doing. I’m using severity to prove I’m still relevant. Because I’m afraid—afraid that I’m becoming inconsequential. Forty years. I’ve been in this position for forty years. I’ve cut defect rates by 25%, improved processes time and again, but has any of it truly changed anything? Or have I just been repeating the same motions, over and over, just to prove I exist?

The recent medical checkup has made me even more uneasy. Those numbers, the fluctuations in those indicators—I look at them every day. I record them. I control them. But I can’t control anything. I can’t control time, I can’t control my body, and I can’t control aging. So I’ve transferred this sense of powerlessness onto my work, onto Xiao Wang.

My wife asked me last night why I was so quiet. I didn’t answer. How could I explain? I’m afraid. I’m afraid that in two years, when I retire and this department is gone, when these data points and standards are gone, I really will be nothing.

Xiao Wang is probably at home right now because of something I said. He might be wondering if he should even keep working here. I caused this.

I need to talk to him tomorrow. But I don’t know what to say. Apologize? That’s a hard word for me to say. Explain? Explain what? My anxiety? My fear?

The health log is still sitting on my desk. I ran ten minutes longer than usual today, thinking that maybe this way I could keep things under control. But halfway through the run, I suddenly realized it was all futile. I’m using running to fight aging, severity to fight powerlessness, and data to fight death.

It’s absurd.

But I don’t know what else to do.

In these past six months, you’ve moved from "toughing it out" to being willing to "show your vulnerability." How did you manage to have that change of heart?

ranked_items.content:
# MEMO

**Date: Saturday Morning, October 22, 2022**

Had a phone call with Chen Hao. After hearing about my diagnosis, his first instinct was to ask if I’d been taking my medicine on time and if he should take time off to come back. I didn't expect him to ask that.

Before this, I always felt like there was something standing between us. Our calls were always about work, the house, or whether they were eating well—like going through a checklist. Their answers were always brief. I thought that was just how we were.

But this time was different. When he said, "Dad, you need to take care of yourself," I heard something different in his voice. My own voice choked up a bit; all I could manage was, "Okay, thank you." That’s rare for me.

Li Meifang says the way I’ve shown concern for the kids all these years has been flawed—always supervising, never just being there. I used to disagree with her. But thinking about it now, maybe she’s right.

**A few things I need to figure out:**

1. Have a real talk with Chen Hao and Chen Yu individually before I retire. Not to ask about their careers or lives, but to truly listen to them. Maybe they have things they want to say to me, too.

2. What does "legacy" actually mean? I’ve always wanted to pass down my understanding of work and my obsession with quality to them. But they have their own lives. Maybe I should just record these things for them to reference, rather than expecting them to inherit it all.

3. The collaboration with the technical school—donating tools, doing sharing sessions. I want to do this even more now. Not to prove anything, but because I truly want to leave these things behind.

4. Have a proper talk with Li Meifang next week. She’s been by my side all these years, yet I’ve always been "managing" her. It’s time to change that.

**One more thought:**

Maybe the things I’ve done right over these forty years aren't the process improvements or the defect rate data. Maybe it’s that obsession with detail, that commitment to responsibility, and that care for my family—even if I haven't been great at expressing it. If Chen Hao and Chen Yu can see these things in me, that’s enough.

I don’t need them to become me. I just need them to know that I’ve always been here.

---

*Going to play chess with the guys this afternoon. My game hasn't been great lately; my mind keeps wandering. But maybe that’s okay—I don’t need to win. Just sitting there, being with them, is enough.*
ranked_items.subquestion: Brother Chen, I’ve watched you go from refusing to say a word even when Chen Hao asked back at the beginning of the year, to now being able to admit to Meifang that you're terrified of losing her. In these past six months, you’ve moved from "toughing it out" to being willing to "show your vulnerability." How did you manage to have that change of heart?

ranked_items.content:
After returning home that evening, Chen Guilin changed his clothes and sat down at the dining table. Li Meifang was serving the soup.

He ate a few mouthfuls of rice in silence before putting down his chopsticks.

"I might have been too harsh at the department meeting today," he said, his voice low, eyes fixed on his bowl. "The new QC inspector messed up the data entry again, and I called him out in front of everyone. He looked pretty shaken."

Li Meifang looked up at him but didn't respond immediately.

"My temper has been short lately," he paused, tapping his finger on the table. "I can feel it myself. I have high standards for the work, but..." He didn't finish the sentence, opting for a sip of soup instead.

He turned to his wife, his tone tinged with uncertainty. "Do you think I'm being too demanding? I want to get things done right, but it seems like..." He hesitated, "it seems like I always end up making people uncomfortable."

He wasn't looking for comfort; it was more like he was trying to confirm something. There was exhaustion in his eyes, along with a certain guilt he couldn't quite put into words.

"The physical exam is next week," he suddenly changed the subject, though his tone remained heavy. "I need to get to bed early these next few days."

He lowered his head and continued eating, but his movements were much slower than usual.
