May 18, 2022, 8:45 PM

I had another minor disagreement with Meifang today over the salt. She added an extra spoonful while simmering the soup; I caught the scent and went over immediately to check. She said, "You can't even taste the difference," and I didn't say much back, but I was thinking—her blood pressure is already at 140/92. I saw the last physical exam report clearly. Just one extra spoonful of salt can cause a five-point spike; it’s not a small matter.

I poured her a glass of water and told her to drink it first. Then I told her I’d be putting together a low-sodium meal plan for her this weekend. She didn’t object; she just gave me a smile—that look of someone who’s grown used to my ways.

While sitting in the workshop, I flipped through the tool log again. I’ve already recorded over thirty items this year, each marked with its purpose and maintenance method. Sometimes I write "Who will use this?" in the margins—a question for myself. Hao is selling real estate in Shenzhen, and Yu is teaching in Guangzhou; neither of them will ever touch these things. I know that.

But I keep recording anyway. While I can still move.

The May physical is coming up. The cardiologist mentioned last time that I need regular follow-ups. I haven't told the family exactly what it's for, just that it's a routine check-up. Meifang asked once, and I said, "Nothing major, just some fluctuations in the data." Honestly, I don't really want to dwell on it myself.

Tonight, I glanced at a WeChat message from Hao. He said the mortgage pressure is a bit heavy this month and asked if I could support him a little more. I transferred five thousand yuan to him without saying much. He replied with a "Thanks, Dad." That was it. I tried to remember the last time he came home—was it three months ago? Or four? I couldn't recall.

I wonder, should I keep doing all this after I retire? Should I keep keeping the accounts? Should I keep polishing the tools? Should I keep managing everything in this house?

I have to get everything done that needs doing, while I still can.

I have to hurry.

ranked_items.content:
September 17, 2022 Afternoon

My hands are still shaking.

It’s been three hours since I left the hospital. I’m sitting in my workshop, and my hands are still shaking. 110 mg/dL. Grade 2 hypertension. Carotid atherosclerosis. The doctor spoke these words with a calm tone, as if he were talking about the weather. But I heard him loud and clear. Every number felt like a nail being driven in.

I tell myself these are just data points. I’ve organized forty years of production reports; I know how to read data. But this time, the data is telling me that my body is failing.

My wife said, "I knew this would happen to you sooner or later." That hurt more than the diagnosis itself. When did she notice? I always thought I had everything under control—diet, routine, checkups. I was even secretly monitoring her blood pressure, terrified something might happen to her. And the result? I’m the one who collapsed first.

It’s ridiculous.

My wife urged me to adjust my pace of life. I told her, "I know. That’s exactly what I’m doing." But what am I doing right now? I’m in the workshop, repeatedly wiping down these tools. I’ve wiped the same screwdriver over a dozen times. My movements are forceful, as if I’m trying to scrub something off. I know it’s absurd. I’m using this to mask my anxiety. But I can’t stop.

The doctor said I’ll need long-term medication. "Long-term." That’s a heavy word.

I’m thinking about those precision tools—the ones I’ve collected for over twenty years. And those forty years of experience—the quality control standards, the process optimizations, the things I’ve never explicitly said but are etched into my brain. If I fall, what happens to all of it? I don’t have time to think about that, because the moment I do, I start to feel afraid.

I don’t want to tell Chen Hao and Chen Yu. Not yet. They’ll ask too many questions, and I don’t have the energy to answer them. Besides, I don’t know what I’d even say. "Your father is sick"? That sounds too dramatic. But if I don’t tell them, they’ll hear it from their mother sooner or later.

I need to make a plan. The doctor gave me a medication list; I’ve already noted it down. Regular follow-ups. Dietary adjustments. And... I don’t know what else. I listened to everything the doctor said, but right now, only one thought is stuck in my head: I’m running out of time.

I originally planned to retire in two years. Now that plan might have to change. Or maybe it can’t be changed at all. I don’t even know how much longer I can keep working.

It’s ironic. I’ve spent my whole life pursuing control. Precise management, clear plans, every step taken by the book. But my body won’t listen. The body has its own logic, and I can’t change it with willpower alone.

I put down the screwdriver. My hands are still shaking.

I have work tomorrow.

ranked_items.content:
It was just after 5:00 AM. Chen Guilin had been sitting in the bedroom for a long time. Outside the window, the sky was still dark, save for the occasional sound of a passing car below. He hadn't slept—or rather, he had given up on trying.

Beside him, Li Meifang rolled over. He could hear her breathing, very faint.

He opened the notes app on his phone. Inside were the figures he’d jotted down yesterday: 110, 140/90, pre-metabolic syndrome, long-term medication. His finger hovered over the screen for a long time before he finally deleted the line: "How much longer can I work?"

He put the phone down, then picked it up again.

Finally, he sent Li Meifang a message on WeChat. She wouldn't be awake yet, but he needed to get these words out.

---

**Chen Guilin**: I’ve been thinking all night about what the doctor said yesterday. It’s not a small thing.

**Chen Guilin**: Long-term medication—he was very clear about that. It means... we might have to change our plans.

**Chen Guilin**: I’m thinking about the retirement timeline. Two years might be too tight. Or rather, I need to run the numbers again.

**Chen Guilin**: And the insurance. Our current coverage might not be enough. I’m going to look into it today.

**Chen Guilin**: The other day you said, "I knew you’d have this sooner or later." When did you start worrying?

**Chen Guilin**: ...Never mind. We’ll talk in the morning. I just keep thinking—all these years, I thought I was managing things well. Turns out...

**Chen Guilin**: Keep sleeping. I’m fine. Just thinking through some of the accounts.

---

He put the phone down and sat for a while longer. The sky was gradually turning gray.

He got up, walked to the living room, and switched on the light in his workshop. His tools were arranged neatly on the shelves. He picked up a pair of vernier calipers and began to wipe them with a soft cloth. His movements were slow and heavy.

He wiped them once, then wiped them again.

9:30 PM, in the bedroom. Li Meifang has finished getting ready for bed and is sitting up, scrolling through her phone. Chen Guilin walks in from the study, changes into his pajamas, and sits down on the edge of the bed.

---

"I talked to Haohao on the phone today. He’s been under a lot of pressure lately."

"Things are a bit unstable with his orders at work, and he seems a little lost. I didn't nag him about his mortgage like I usually do; I just listened to him talk for a while."

(Pauses, looking at his wife)

"He said he wants to find a chance to recalibrate. I just told him to take it slow, no need to rush."

(Pauses again, his tone becoming somewhat uncertain)

"Do you think... I’m usually too hard on them?"

(Turns to get into bed, but his movements are a bit slow)

"Never mind, I'll think about it tomorrow. Let’s get some sleep."

---

*Chen Guilin lies down, his back to his wife, but he doesn't fall asleep immediately. His phone screen flickers in the dark—he opens a health tracking app, glances at his step count and heart rate data for the day, then switches it off and lets out a long sigh.*

ranked_items.content:
Meifang, I wanted to say a few things to you.

I kept thinking about it yesterday at the hospital. All those years of "supervising" you—your diet, your checkups, your routine—I realize now that it probably put a lot of pressure on you. I thought I was taking care of you, but in reality, I was controlling you.

Yesterday, when you sat beside me and held my hand, you didn't let go the whole time the doctor was speaking. It was only then that I truly felt we aren't "supervisor and supervised." We are just... us. Together.

The doctor mentioned joining a health management course, and I want to go with you. Not so I can tell you what to do, but so we can learn together. To cooperate. I’ve never thought about that word in this way before.

I’ve seen how much you’ve put up with me all these years. I’m not very good at saying these things, but I want you to know—I’m grateful to you. Truly.

We still have time. Before I retire, while we can still do so many things together. I don't want to waste any more of it.

Shall we go sign up for that course together this weekend?

ranked_items.content:
After returning home that evening, Chen Guilin changed his clothes and sat down at the dining table. Li Meifang was serving the soup.

He ate a few mouthfuls of rice in silence before putting down his chopsticks.

"I might have been too harsh at the department meeting today," he said, his voice low, eyes fixed on his bowl. "The new QC inspector messed up the data entry again, and I called him out in front of everyone. He looked pretty shaken."

Li Meifang looked up at him but didn't respond immediately.

"My temper has been short lately," he paused, tapping his finger on the table. "I can feel it myself. I have high standards for the work, but..." He didn't finish the sentence, opting for a sip of soup instead.

He turned to his wife, his tone tinged with uncertainty. "Do you think I'm being too demanding? I want to get things done right, but it seems like..." He hesitated, "it seems like I always end up making people uncomfortable."

He wasn't looking for comfort; it was more like he was trying to confirm something. There was exhaustion in his eyes, along with a certain guilt he couldn't quite put into words.

"The physical exam is next week," he suddenly changed the subject, though his tone remained heavy. "I need to get to bed early these next few days."

He lowered his head and continued eating, but his movements were much slower than usual.

I’ve been thinking about what you said for a long time.

Ever since I got back from the chess club, I’ve spent the entire afternoon reflecting on your words—that instead of spending time fearing the future, it’s better to spend it cherishing the present.

I admit, I’ve been feeling trapped lately. Medical reports, retirement plans, the meaning of work... it’s like I’ve been trying to use these things to fill a bottomless pit. The more I try to fill it, the more anxious I get, and the more chaotic everything becomes. You’re right; I’ve been living in fear, not actually living.

I’ve never told anyone this before. You’re the first.

Over the past couple of days, I’ve been thinking that maybe I should stop. Not stop working, but stop this... need for control. Stop the constant calculating and worrying every single day. I’m not sure if that’s exactly what you meant, but at the very least, your words have opened up a crack for me to see the world outside.

# August 2, 2022, 8:30 PM

Sitting in the study. My wife is watching TV in the living room, the volume turned down low. I intended to organize today’s exercise logs, but right now, I can’t do anything.

I lost at the chess club today. That rarely happens.

It wasn't because my skills have declined—though perhaps they have—it was because I simply couldn't focus. When my friend A asked what was wrong, I just... let it out. About the medical report. Blood pressure 147/92, blood sugar 6.8, and a slight abnormality on the ECG. I hadn't even figured out how to say it, and yet there it was.

That’s not like me.

A said it was fine, that many people are like this and I just need regular check-ups. His words sounded hollow. I know he meant well, but he doesn't understand at all. No one can. This isn't just about abnormal data.

I’ve been controlling everything. Recording my wife’s blood pressure every day, watching everything she eats, walking 8,000 steps myself, weighing my diet down to the gram, organizing all the data into spreadsheets. I thought that by doing this, I could... I could stop something. But now I realize it’s all futile. Data can’t change anything.

Maybe I should retire early. I startled myself when I heard those words come out of my mouth. But once the thought appeared, I couldn't shake it. If my body is truly starting to fail, how much time do I have left? How much time is left for the things I haven't done yet?

The tools in the workshop, those ideas, and... I haven't even had a real conversation with Hao and Yu. They are in Shenzhen and Guangzhou, while I stay here recording data as if that were a form of love.

When I lost the game, I looked at the black and white pieces on the board and suddenly felt exhausted. Not a physical exhaustion, but something else.

It’s 8:30 PM. I have work tomorrow. Next week is the department’s quality assessment. Everything continues as if nothing has changed. But everything has changed.

I don’t know what to do. Or rather, I know what to do—make a plan, adjust the regimen, control the variables—but this time, I realize there are just too many things beyond my control.

Maybe I should sleep. I have to record the blood pressure data tomorrow.

ranked_items.content:
September 10, 2022, 8:15 PM

I’ve been sitting in the workshop for two hours. I’m wiping down that 1985 vernier caliper, my movements slow. My wife is watching TV in the living room; I can hear the sound, but I don’t want to go out.

110 mg/dL. Stage 2 hypertension. Early signs of carotid atherosclerosis.

The doctor spoke those words calmly, as if reading a product inspection report. But I heard them clearly. Every word felt like a nail being driven in. I’ve always done my checkups, always logged the data, always believed I was in control. Wake up at 6:00 AM, sleep at 10:00 PM, a light diet, regular exercise. For forty years, that’s how I’ve lived.

My wife said, "I knew you’d get this sooner or later."

That sentence stung more than the diagnosis itself. When did she see through me? I thought I’d hidden it well. I thought that through precise management, I could stop aging, stop my body from betraying me. But she knew all along. What does that mean? Does it mean my efforts look like a joke to everyone else?

Long-term medication. The doctor said I need long-term medication.

I don’t know how long "long-term" is. One year? Five? Ten? Until I die? I’ve never taken medicine long-term. I rarely even catch a cold. Now I have to take pills for the rest of my life just to maintain a number—110. The number itself is meaningless, but what it represents matters. It represents my loss of control over my own body.

My wife said she’d go with me to get the prescription filled tomorrow. I didn’t respond, just nodded, and turned to walk into this workshop. I didn’t want her to see my hands shaking.

The tools are still here. Dozens of them. I spent forty years collecting them. Each one represents a stage, a decision, a moment I thought was important. Now I’m wondering, if I collapse, what happens to these things? Chen Hao doesn't understand mechanics. Neither does Chen Yu. Will they be treated as scrap metal?

I donated some tools to the industrial technical school. Was that the right decision? Or was it just a disguised way of settling my estate?

I don’t want to think like this. But I can’t help it.

The doctor said I need to change my lifestyle. Reduce stress. Maintain a routine. I’m already doing those things. Does that mean I haven’t done enough? Or does it mean none of it matters?

My hands are still shaking. I put the caliper down.

I have to get the medicine tomorrow. I need to make a list—drug names, usage, side effects, prices, insurance coverage. I need to turn this into data, into something manageable. That’s the only way I’ll feel at peace.

But I know that some things simply cannot be managed.

# March 28, 2022, 9:45 PM

Had a phone call with Haozi.

He said business has been pretty good lately, and the mortgage pressure isn't as heavy anymore. I originally wanted to ask him if he had any interest in mechanical engineering—if he wanted to know more about what I’ve been working on all these years... but the words died in my throat. I changed the subject and asked how work was going instead. He said it was fine, then asked if there was something I wanted to say.

I told him it was nothing, that everything was under control.

After hanging up, I stood in the living room for a long while, phone still in hand.

I thought about the tools in my workshop. Forty years. Some I modified myself; others I repaired after they broke. Every single one has a story, but those stories only exist in my head. I’ve recorded some of it in my notebooks, but what’s the point of recording things? Haozi won't read them. Yuyu won't read them either. They have their own lives, which is only natural.

But I still feel... wrong.

These things, this knowledge—it just ends here. I’ve been in this position for twelve years, proposing several improvements every year that the company adopted. But those will end, too. When I retire and the new manager arrives, he’ll have his own methods. The optimizations I made might eventually just become a statistic—"defect rate down by 25%"—and nothing more.

Li Meifang is downstairs watching TV. I didn't go down.

I pulled out the last physical exam report and read it again. Blood pressure is a bit high. The doctor said to be careful. Mine has started to fluctuate, too. It was stable for the last two years, but now...

I can't control it.

That’s what hurts the most. I can control workflows, quality standards, and my subordinates' performance. But I can't control time. I can't control my body. I can't control whether Haozi or Yuyu will inherit anything.

In the margin of one page in my notebook, I wrote: "Who will ever use this?" I didn't erase it.

I have to go to the office tomorrow. There’s a quality control meeting in the afternoon. A new employee’s report isn't detailed enough; I need to point it out. It’s my responsibility. But I’m getting a bit tired of this kind of thing. Or rather... I know where this frustration is coming from. It’s because I’m demanding perfection from others while I myself am losing my grip on it.

Thinking this way makes me feel a bit hypocritical.

Forget it. Time for bed. There’s a lot to do tomorrow.

I need to hurry while I can still move.

ranked_items.content:
Meifang, I’m back.

I played a few rounds at the club tonight. Midway through, I just came out with it—told them the doctor found something with my heart and that I need medication to keep it under control. I thought I’d just mention it briefly and move on, but they actually started asking quite a lot of questions. The strange thing was, there wasn't a hint of pity in their voices. It was as if they were talking about something perfectly normal—asking what meds I’m on, saying they have friends in the same boat, and that as long as you take your pills and get some exercise, you'll be fine.

Sitting there, I suddenly felt this sense of relief. I don't quite know how to describe it. Maybe it’s because they didn't treat me like someone who needs looking after; they just treated me as Chen Guilin, their friend and fellow player.

It made me realize that all these years, I’ve been trying so hard to prove I’m fine—striving to be the best at work, managing every little detail at home, even obsessing over your blood pressure. I thought that was what it meant to be responsible for my family. But looking back now...

The way I’ve treated you in the past hasn't been fair. You’ve always been there for me, taking care of me, yet I’ve always looked at you with a critical, watchful eye. It wasn't until my friends treated me the way they did today that I understood what it feels like to be accepted and treated as an equal.

I want to change. I don’t mean I’m going to stop caring about the family, but... I want to try being a companion to you, rather than a supervisor. If you’re willing.

We haven't talked much these past few years. I think it might be time for a change.

ranked_items.content:
October 12, 2022, 7:30 PM

Had a phone call with Chen Hao.

In the past, I’d always ask him if the mortgage was a burden, how the orders were coming along, or if his job was stable. I don’t know what came over me today, but the first thing out of my mouth was, "How have you been lately? Is there anything I can help with?" He seemed taken aback; there was a two-second silence before he answered.

He said work is exhausting, that sales is high-pressure, and sometimes he wonders if he’s doing the right thing. He’s lost. He was very candid, and I could hear the fatigue in his voice.

Before, I would have jumped straight in with advice, telling him exactly what he should do. But this time, I just said, "I understand. I’ve been through a stage like that, too."

Even I was a bit surprised by my own words. In forty years, I’ve never spoken to him like that.

He asked me when that was. I told him that when I was his age, I wasn't sure if I was making the right choices either. Back then, I was a quality inspector at the grassroots level, doing the same repetitive work every day, unable to see a way out. What I didn’t tell him was that I just kept going down that same path ever since, never changing course again.

He didn’t respond immediately, but I could sense something had shifted. It wasn't that superior-subordinate dynamic anymore; it felt more like... two people who had walked the same road.

After hanging up, I sat at my desk and looked at that medical report. The doctor said I’ll need long-term medication and a lifestyle change. I’ve been thinking about how to reverse this outcome through stricter management, just like I do at work—precise control, eliminating variables.

But while talking to Chen Hao, it suddenly occurred to me that maybe some things can’t be controlled. Some paths, once walked, are simply walked.

I need to rethink how I interact with them. Not supervising, not guiding. Maybe just... being there?

The thought makes me a little uneasy. I don't know if I still know how to do it.

More tomorrow.

ranked_items.content:
Haozi, how have you been lately?

I’ve been thinking, and I wanted to share a few words with you. This recent health scare has given me a lot to reflect on. The doctor said I need to stay on medication and slow down. With your mom here by my side, I’ve realized that all these years, I’ve been constantly rushing—rushing to keep everything under control, to make everything perfect.

Regarding your mortgage in Shenzhen, I know I used to pester you with questions, almost like I was auditing your books. Looking back, that wasn't right. You’re an adult now, and you have your own plans. I thought I was "helping" you in my own way, but in reality, I was just putting pressure on you.

I’ve only recently come to understand that maybe life doesn't need to be perfectly controlled. In my forty years of work, I always pursued "zero defects," but life isn't an assembly line. There are things you just can't control, and there’s pressure you have to learn to let go of. Otherwise, you’ll end up like me—waiting until your sixties to regret what you’ve missed out on.

I’m not telling you this so you’ll follow in my footsteps. On the contrary, I hope you can live a more relaxed life than I did. A mortgage is just a mortgage, and a job is just a job; don't keep yourself wound so tight. Taking time for family and getting proper rest—those things matter, too.

Your mom says I’ve become quite talkative lately, ha. Maybe I really am changing.

If you’re free this weekend, give me a call. Let’s have a good chat.

— Dad

ranked_items.content:
WeChat messages to Chen Hao:

---

Hao, something came to mind today that I wanted to share with you.

The company recently asked me to prepare an experience-sharing session—basically a summary of everything I’ve learned in quality management over the past 40 years. I was a bit resistant at first, thinking I didn't have much to say. But after giving it some thought, I realized there really are some things worth recording.

The mortgage pressure over in Shenzhen must still be quite heavy, right? I noticed you haven't mentioned work much on WeChat lately—is the market not doing so well? You don't have to hide it from me; if there’s anything you need help with, just say the word.

All these years, I’ve been trying to figure out how to pass my experience down to you kids, but it seems my approach wasn't quite right. This sharing session made me realize something—maybe what I can offer isn't about making you do the same kind of work, but rather passing on that sense of professional rigor and obsession with detail. You’re in real estate sales now, and you need those qualities too, don't you?

Are you free this weekend? Your mom and I want to hear how things are going. We’re not asking how much money you’re making; we just want to know how you are. I’d also like to catch up on how your sister is doing.

To be honest, I’m only just realizing that the way I showed my concern before might have been problematic. I was always asking if you’d hit your targets or settled down, instead of actually listening to you. After my recent check-up, the doctor talked to me a lot, and it finally clicked—maybe companionship and understanding are more important than trying to control everything.

Drop me a line when you can. How have you been lately?

---

WeChat messages to Chen Yu:

---

Yuyu, how have you been lately?

I wanted to tell you something. The company asked me to do a summary and presentation of my work experience recently, and I’ve been thinking that this is actually a rare opportunity. After all these years of trial and error in quality management, I do have some insights. But I realized that I’ve never really talked to you kids about these things properly—and I haven't really listened to your thoughts, either.

In your work in education, you probably also need that same dedication to detail and adherence to standards. It seems like what I’ve learned over the years could be useful for your work, too. But more importantly, I want to hear your perspective—what kind of education do you think is the right way?

My recent health check-up flagged a few minor issues, and the doctor said I need to take care of myself. Your mom has been looking after me, which has given me a lot of time to think. I’ve realized that the way I’ve cared for you all these years might have been off—it was always about supervising rather than being there for you. I’m sorry.

When do you have time to come home? No need to take special leave; I just want to sit down and have a real talk with you. About your work, your life, and... some of the things I’ve been thinking about lately.

Write back when you can.

ranked_items.content:
[Phone Memo]

09-12 Mon 09:30

---

110 mg/dL
Stage 2 Hypertension
Pre-metabolic syndrome
Early signs of carotid atherosclerosis

—The doctor’s exact words. I’ve noted them down.

Long-term medication. That phrase has been circling in my head all night. Long-term. What does that even mean?

---

In today’s meeting, Wang Lei’s data was wrong again. I was going to say, "This is unacceptable," but the words came out as, "Let’s look at how we can improve this together."

He froze for a second. So did I.

Why did I say that?

---

Talked to him privately for ten minutes after work. Explaining, not accusing. He said, "Manager Chen, you seem different today."

I didn't answer.

---

How much longer?

I find myself asking this every day now.

It’s been 40 years. From 1982 until now. How many working years are left? How many years left to pass these things—the tools, the experience, the standards—on to someone?

My wife said, "I knew you’d have this sooner or later."

She’s right. But I didn't want to hear it.

---

The precision tools room. Need to organize it this weekend. Not sure why that suddenly came to mind.

Maybe I should take photos to document them. Maybe I should make a list.

Maybe I should...

I don't know.

---

Going back for a follow-up checkup this afternoon. The doctor wants me to log my blood pressure.

Once every morning and evening.

Yet another new routine to maintain.

Weren't the old routines enough?

---

Chess buddy Jia said we’re playing Friday as usual. I said okay.

But I wonder, when I’m sitting at the chessboard, what will I be thinking about?

---

My hands are shaking. I noticed it just now while wiping down the tools.

I didn't notice when it started.

---

Need to monitor Li Meifang’s blood pressure too. She’s been sneaking pickles again lately.

I need to be stricter. Or...

No. It is what it is.

---

110 mg/dL

Is this number going to be here forever?

---

[End of Memo]

ranked_items.content:
# MEMO

**Date: September 19, 2022, Morning**
**Location: Office Cubicle**

---

Maybe I should change my ways.

The improvement plan Xiao Wang submitted during yesterday’s meeting was actually quite good. At the time, I pointed out three flaws directly. His expression… I can still see it now. He didn’t push back; he just nodded and took notes. When I walked past his desk at the end of the day, he was packing up—his movements were hurried, as if he were escaping something.

Is this right?

I’ve always believed that being strict is being responsible. A 25% reduction in defect rates—those are the facts. But what lies behind the data? It’s my subordinates working in fear under my gaze. Li Meifang once said, "You’re the same way at home." I didn't respond.

Control ≠ Respect.

Where did that phrase come from yesterday? Perhaps it was something the doctor said that triggered it. He talked about my blood pressure, my carotid artery, all those numbers… I’ve spent 40 years trying to control everything, yet my body has betrayed me anyway. I cannot control it. So what about my subordinates? I "manage" them the same way, as if I were managing a machine. But they aren't machines.

Time is running out. What actually matters?

It’s not the process optimization forms. It’s not those perfect quality inspection reports. If I were to collapse right now, who would remember how strict I was? Who would think of me because of a 25% lower defect rate?

Regarding Xiao Wang’s proposal—I actually should have praised him in front of everyone. And *then* pointed out the room for improvement.

I need to rethink how I speak to my staff.

Maybe there’s still time.

---

*To be continued*

b1607a3d-4353-46c7-8813-6e545e5e0660.question: I’ve noticed that whenever you’re stressed or anxious lately, your fingers start making these subtle movements—like tapping rapidly on the desk or repeatedly rubbing those paper reports. What exactly is going through your mind in those moments?

ranked_items.content:
April 28, 2022, 11:15 PM

I’m sitting in the study. The lights are off. It’s pitch black outside the window.

I’ve been sitting here in the dark for a long time. My hand is pressed against my chest; I can feel my heartbeat. The rhythm is irregular. My breathing is a bit shallow, but I haven't moved. I just sit here, feeling it all.

The physical exam report is still on the desk. Blood pressure 139/88, fasting blood sugar 6.8. There’s a waveform on the ECG that the doctor said needs observation. He used the phrase "requires attention." I heard him loud and clear.

I’m thinking: this is how it begins.

I press my fingers against my pulse and count—76 beats per minute. Within the normal range. But I know it was racing just a moment ago. Why did it speed up? No exercise, nothing special happened. I was just sitting here when I suddenly felt... something I can't put into words.

Hao called today. He said the mortgage pressure is heavy and he might have to pay back a little less this month. I didn't say much; I just noted it down in my ledger. The mortgage, his sales commissions, the fluctuations of Shenzhen’s housing prices. I track all these data points. But what’s the use of tracking? Can I change anything?

The tools in the workshop are still on the shelves. Lately, I’ve been numbering them, writing down their uses and the years they were purchased. Some were bought in 1995, others in 2008. I wonder, if I were suddenly gone, who would know how to use these things? Not Hao. Not Yu. And certainly not Meifang.

They would just become scrap metal. Or be tossed out as junk.

I’ve been sitting in the dark for a long time, without turning on the light.

Tomorrow, I need to schedule a more detailed check-up. Cardiology. I need to know exactly what that abnormal waveform means. I need to establish a long-term monitoring plan. Every month’s data must be recorded so I can see the trends.

I also need to keep supervising Meifang’s blood pressure. She’s started eating salty foods again; I have to remind her once more.

My breathing has settled down a bit now.

But that sense of unease remains.

It’s as if something is slowly coming loose, and I can’t reach out to catch it.
ranked_items.subquestion: I’ve noticed that whenever you’re stressed or anxious lately, your fingers start making these subtle movements—like tapping rapidly on the desk or repeatedly rubbing those paper reports. What exactly is going through your mind in those moments?

ranked_items.content:
# August 15, 2022, 3:20 AM

Can’t sleep. I’ve been awake for two hours now.

My chest still feels a bit tight; I can feel my heartbeat when I press down on it. My rational mind tells me this is anxiety, not a heart condition. But my fingers still instinctively press down several times, trying to be sure. My breathing is a bit shallow. I tell myself to take deep breaths—one, two, three, exhale—but the more I try to control it, the more labored it feels. It’s ridiculous.

I turned off my phone. Meifang knocked on the door and asked if I wanted some warm water. I didn't respond. She didn't come in eventually; I heard her footsteps slowly fading away. She’s used to me being like this. After 35 years, she knows all too well that I don’t like to be disturbed, especially at times like this. But I could sense her lingering outside the door for a few seconds. I feel a twinge of guilt, but I don't have the energy to deal with that right now.

I checked the Excel sheet again this afternoon. Meifang’s blood pressure readings for this week: 150/95, 148/92, 152/98… I marked three of them in red pen. I wrote "follow-up appointment needed" next to them, and then "dietary control." But I know these numbers don’t change anything. I can record them, I can mark them, I can make plans, but I cannot change the fact that her body is aging.

Last night, I searched for information on hypertension complications again. Stroke, myocardial infarction, kidney failure… page after page. I told myself to stop, that searching like this is pointless. But my fingers kept clicking. It’s as if something is driving me to confirm the worst-case scenarios, as if knowing in advance would somehow make me prepared. It’s stupid. I know perfectly well it’s stupid.

My own data sheets are there, too. Daily exercise time, step count, heart rate, blood sugar levels… all neatly arranged. It’s as if as long as these numbers stay in the green zone, I can guarantee that nothing will go wrong with me. But I know I’m lying to myself. I can’t stop aging by keeping records. I can’t guarantee my heart won’t fail through a strict diet. This realization terrifies me more than any abnormal data point ever could.

The physical exam is in two weeks. I’m both looking forward to it and dreading it. I’m looking for a definitive answer, yet I’m afraid that answer will confirm all the premonitions I’ve had over the past few months.

The tools in the workshop haven't been wiped down yet. I was supposed to organize those precision instruments today, but I just couldn't find the motivation. I sat there, looking at them, thinking that I might not have much time left. Time seems to be accelerating. Last year, retirement still felt far off; now, it feels like two years have passed in the blink of an eye.

No good news from the kids either. Hao is still under a lot of pressure with his mortgage, and Yu hasn't said much lately. I want to give them a call, but I don't know what to say. Ask them how their health is? That would be too strange. Or just listen to them talk about work in silence? There doesn't seem to be any other way for us to communicate anymore.

It’s not dawn yet. The study is very quiet. It’s just me and these data points, these records—these things I try to use to fight against this sense of powerlessness.

I think I need to sleep. But I know I won’t be able to.
ranked_items.subquestion: I’ve noticed that whenever you’re stressed or anxious lately, your fingers start making these subtle movements—like tapping rapidly on the desk or repeatedly rubbing those paper reports. What exactly is going through your mind in those moments?

ranked_items.content:
# September 15, 2022, 19:30

Sitting in the study, my hands are still shaking a little.

What happened this afternoon keeps looping in my head. Meifang said she needs to go for a follow-up checkup too, her voice laced with worry. I originally wanted to comfort her with data like I always do—tell her I’ve started the medication, the indicators will gradually improve, and everything will be fine as long as we follow the doctor’s plan. But when the words reached my lips, I didn't say them. Instead, I heard myself say, "I’m nervous too."

The moment those words came out, even I was a bit stunned.

I never say things like that in front of her. For forty years, I’ve been the one in control—the accounts, the plans, every matter in the house, big or small. She’s used to my composure, and I’m used to being the one who must remain unshakable. But today, I admitted it. I said, "Let’s go together."

And then, I took her hand.

Thinking back on that gesture now, it still feels strange. My hand holding hers; her hand was very warm, probably because she was nervous too. We just sat there for a while, neither of us speaking. I could feel her looking at me, but I didn't look up.

The doctor’s terminology is still echoing in my mind—metabolic syndrome, atherosclerosis, long-term medication. I always thought that through discipline and control, I could manage my body like a precision machine. But now I realize I have no control at all. The body has its own logic; it doesn't run according to my schedule.

The moment I held her hand, I wasn't thinking about the diagnosis itself, but rather—if I really collapse, what happens to her? All those tools, forty years of experience, the things I haven't had time to organize yet...

I don't know if this counts as weakness. I’ve always hated weakness. But today, it seems I allowed myself a little bit of it.

My hands are still shaking. I went to the workshop and wiped down my tools, from the first to the last, every single one of them with great care. Only while doing that did my hands slowly steady themselves.

Tomorrow, I’ll continue as planned. But something has changed, and I haven't figured out how to face it yet.
ranked_items.subquestion: I’ve noticed that whenever you’re stressed or anxious lately, your fingers start making these subtle movements—like tapping rapidly on the desk or repeatedly rubbing those paper reports. What exactly is going through your mind in those moments?

ranked_items.content:
March 20, 2022, 11:47 PM

Sitting in the workshop. My wife is already asleep. The noises downstairs have all quieted down.

I finished page thirty today. Starting from the first page, my handwriting was neat; the purpose, specifications, and purchase date of every tool were recorded clearly. But toward the end, the pen started to shake. I’m not sure when it began—maybe around page twenty-five. The strokes became irregular, and some words looked rushed.

When I got to the entry for the wrench, I stopped for a long time. I bought this wrench in 1998. It’s a Japanese import with a precision of 0.02 mm. I’ve used it for over twenty years, and it shows very little wear. I wanted to record its use, but the pen just hovered there. Then I wrote—"Who will ever use this?"

After writing that sentence, I didn't cross it out. It’s strange. I’m not usually like this. The notebooks on my desk have always been spotless—no scribbles, no traces of hesitation. But this time, looking at that sentence, I just couldn't bring myself to erase it.

Hao is in Shenzhen; Yu is in Guangzhou. Neither of them will ever come to this workshop. Hao is in real estate sales, and Yu teaches. They have their own lives. I never expected them to inherit anything, but sitting here now, looking at these tools, I suddenly realized—these things are about to become useless. Not because they’re broken or lost, but because no one will know how to use them.

This morning, Meifang asked me what I was busy with. I only replied with a grunt. She didn't ask again. She’s used to my silence. After thirty-five years, she’s long known that I’m a man of few words. But when that "mm-hmm" came out today, even I could hear it—it wasn't silence; it was avoidance. I don’t know how to explain to her what I’m doing. I can’t even explain it to myself.

The physical exam report is still in the drawer. My blood sugar is up a bit again. The doctor said I need to be careful. I’m in my fifties now; my body is starting to disobey me. I used to think that as long as I kept things under control and followed the plan, nothing would go wrong. But the body doesn't follow a plan. Neither does time.

I’m a little over a year away from retirement. I’ve been waiting for this day, but now that it’s almost here, I’m starting to feel afraid. Afraid of what I’ll do after I retire. Afraid of being idle. Afraid that these tools will just keep sitting here, unused and misunderstood.

The notebook is still open. "Who will ever use this" is still there on page thirty. Tomorrow, I have to keep writing. There are still many tools left to record. I have to get them all down while I can still move.

But even if I record them, what then?
ranked_items.subquestion: I’ve noticed that whenever you’re stressed or anxious lately, your fingers start making these subtle movements—like tapping rapidly on the desk or repeatedly rubbing those paper reports. What exactly is going through your mind in those moments?

ranked_items.content:
# Studio Notes

Sunday, August 28, 2022, Morning

I didn't sleep well last night. I couldn't stop thinking about that phone call.

The school director said they are very interested. Said the project is feasible. Said their students need something like this. I had to replay it twice in my head to make sure I hadn't misheard.

I spent the whole night scribbling nonsense in my notebook—all about how tools get lost and knowledge dissipates. The handwriting was terrible, like the scrawlings of a madman. Looking over it this morning, it felt foreign even to me. That didn't look like my work.

But what I came up with yesterday is different.

I thought: what if I organized these tools and this experience into something systematic? Not for Chen Hao or Chen Yu—they each have their own paths—but for those who truly need it. For young people who are interested in precision engineering, just as my father influenced me back then.

the moment that thought surfaced, my hands stopped shaking.

I spent an hour reorganizing yesterday's chaotic notes. Titles, modules, schedules. My handwriting became much clearer. My fingers remembered what to do. It felt just like drafting a new process improvement plan in the studio.

"Precision Engineering Heritage Project"—that’s the name I gave it. It consists of three parts: tool donations, craft lectures, and apprenticeship training. The costs are controllable, and the outputs are clear. The school said they would cooperate.

My wife asked me this morning why I hadn't slept. I didn't explain much. I just said I was thinking about something new. She nodded and went back to her breakfast. She’s used to me being like this.

The medical report is still in the drawer. The cardiologist's words are still ringing in my ears. But this morning, I didn't keep obsessing over those numbers. I was doing something else.

Perhaps that’s the difference. I cannot change the fact that my body is aging. But I can decide to leave something behind before—or even while—it happens. Not to prove that I lived, but because... some things are worth passing on.

I look at the tools in the studio. Dozens of them, some bought over twenty years ago. They won't lose their value just because I die. They will keep working in someone else's hands.

This thought brings me a feeling I haven't felt in a long time.

It isn't despair.

I need to send the detailed proposal to the school next Monday. I also need to organize the first donation list.

The pen is still in my hand.

ranked_items.content:
# Office Memo

**April 5, 2022, 2:20 PM**

---

My fingers are tapping the desk again. I can’t stop.

I was too harsh with Wang Gang just now. His suggestion regarding the inspection process was actually fine; why did I have to insist on pointing out his lack of precision? His expression changed—I saw it—but I didn't apologize. I just kept talking about my standards.

Why is it always like this?

During the afternoon meeting, I lowered the third-quarter defect rate target again. From 0.8 to 0.6. No one dared to object, but I saw Li Qiang’s pen tear through the paper while he was taking notes. He’s afraid. Someone else in my department will quit. I know it.

But I can’t control myself.

The physical exam report is still in the drawer. Blood pressure 139/92. The doctor said I need to be careful. I’m monitoring Li Meifang’s salt intake, yet I—never mind.

I wiped down the tools in the workshop again today. Screwdrivers, calipers, micrometers. Every single one is recorded in the logbook. Use, specifications, year of purchase. Chen Hao called to say his mortgage went up again; I listened to him finish and then just said, "Cut back on your spending." He didn't say anything else. It occurred to me that he might have wanted my help, but I didn't ask.

What use are these tools, anyway?

Two years until retirement. What exactly am I planning for?

Li Meifang said she wants braised pork for dinner. I’m already thinking about how to convince her to eat something lighter. She’ll listen to me, as she always does. But the way she looks at me—I’ve noticed it—she’s avoiding my eyes.

When did I become this kind of person?

Fingers still tapping. Tap. Tap. Tap.

I need to write this down. While I still can.

---

*Check tomorrow’s quality inspection standards before leaving. Might need to adjust them one more time.*

ranked_items.content:
# Memo

**Date**: Wednesday, August 24, 2022, 14:00

**Subject**: Reflections following a game of chess

---

## Record

During today’s game with Player A, he said something that stuck with me: "Instead of spending time fearing the future, spend it cherishing the present."

I didn't respond at the time, but I haven't stopped thinking about it since.

## Issues to Sort Out

1. **What am I doing?**
   - My recent anxiety cycle: Prediction → Fear → Control → More Fear.
   - Since the physical exam results came back, I’ve been using data, plans, and logs to fight uncertainty.
   - But is this actually helpful? Or am I just wearing myself out?

2. **On Control**
   - What I can control: Workflows, financial accounts, daily schedules.
   - What I cannot control: Aging, fluctuating health metrics, my children’s choices, time itself.
   - I have been trying to fill the void of uncontrollable fears with things I can control.
   - What is the cost of doing this?

3. **On the Present**
   - For the past few months, I’ve been planning for a retirement that is two years away.
   - At the same time, I’m worrying about potential health issues.
   - The "present" I hold in my hands has been squeezed into something tiny.
   - My wife, the chess games, the tools in my workshop—they are all still here, but mentally, I’ve already let go of them.

4. **Defining Value**
   - I’ve always believed: Completion → Results → Proof of self-worth.
   - But what if things can’t be completed? What if the results are forgotten?
   - Perhaps value is more than just these things?

## For Further Reflection

- Why do I keep losing what I have now by worrying about what I might lose in the future?
- Is accepting the unchangeable an act of giving up, or an act of maturity?
- How much more time can I afford to waste on fear?

---

**Note**: When I play Player A again next week, I want to hear his take on "cherishing the present." Not as small talk, but because I truly want to understand.
ranked_items.subquestion: I’ve noticed that whenever you’re stressed or anxious lately, your fingers start making these subtle movements—like tapping rapidly on the desk or repeatedly rubbing those paper reports. What exactly is going through your mind in those moments?

ranked_items.content:
# Monday, September 12, 2022 – Sunny

After the morning meeting, I sat in my office, my hands still trembling slightly.

It wasn’t nerves. It was because I had just done something that didn't feel like me.

Li Ming, my subordinate, messed up his data sheet again—the third time this month. By my standards, a low-level error like that should have been called out directly; I should have made him understand exactly what "unacceptable" means. I had even rehearsed the blunt words in my head. But in the conference room, when I saw his tense expression, I paused.

I paused.

What I actually said was: "Let’s look at how we can improve this together." Then I spent ten minutes explaining it to him, instead of my usual three-sentence reprimand.

It’s so strange.

Sitting here now in the corner of my office, I’m trying to understand why I did that. Is it because I’ve been dwelling on the doctor’s words all week? Or is it because I’m starting to realize that I might actually be changing?

The doctor said my body is failing. Pre-metabolic syndrome. Early signs of carotid atherosclerosis. A blood sugar level of 110 mg/dL. These numbers are like nails driven into my brain. I always thought that through precise management—routine, control, supervision—I could maintain everything. But the doctor used data to tell me that I’ve failed.

Maybe that’s why I softened my stance with Li Ming. Maybe it’s because I suddenly realized I can’t control everything. I can’t even control my own body.

So, what right do I have to treat my subordinates that way?

The thought feels foreign to me. It also feels frightening.

Because if I let go of my obsession with detail, if I abandon that relentless management style, then who am I? What was the point of everything I’ve stood for over the last forty years?

On the other hand, I saw a spark in Li Ming’s eyes after I changed my tone. He started listening intently, started asking questions, instead of just hanging his head and taking the criticism. Maybe this change... maybe it isn't entirely a bad thing?

I don’t know.

I’m exhausted right now. These thoughts have drained my energy. The pressure is still there, but it feels different. It’s no longer anger at imperfection; it’s anxiety about time. How much longer? How much time do I have left to change? How much time is left to pass things on?

My wife said yesterday, "I knew you’d have this eventually." She was right. She’s always right. But I didn’t want to admit it.

Now, I have to.

The medicine the doctor prescribed is still in the drawer. I haven't taken it yet.

Tomorrow. I’ll deal with it tomorrow.

**[Sunday, May 8, 3:00 PM – Chess Game]**

Location: Recreation Room
Participants: Player A, Player B, Player C

---

**Log:**
Today’s afternoon game. During the second round, my reactions were noticeably slower, and my strategy felt muddled. Player A asked if I’d been overworked lately; I told him no, I was just having an off day. But it’s not about being tired.

I stared at the board for nearly two minutes before making a move—something that never used to happen. My fingers were resting on the piece, but my mind was elsewhere—thinking about those figures in last week’s physical exam report, thinking about the dizzy spells I’ve been having some mornings.

I left early during the third round, claiming I had something to take care of at home. I didn’t. I just didn't want to keep going.

**Flag/Reminder: Contact the doctor next Monday. Ask if this recent sluggishness requires a follow-up. Might need a neurocognitive assessment.**

*Note: Review chess performance once a month. If things continue to deteriorate, must take the initiative to go to the hospital.*

ranked_items.content:
October 30, 2022, 8:00 PM

Li Meifang is already asleep. I’m sitting in the study; on the desk lies today’s medical report and a cup of tea that went cold long ago.

I said something to her today that left even me a bit stunned. I said, "Perhaps the meaning of life isn't in perfect control, but in genuine connection." Hearing those words come out of my mouth felt like someone was impersonating me. And yet, it really is what I was thinking.

Since I started the medication these past few days, the physical side effects haven't been as bad as I imagined. The doctor says it’s a good sign. But what’s stranger is that I’ve actually started to relax. I used to check my medical reports three times a day, recording every single number in a notebook, trying to force them back to "normal" through sheer willpower. Now... I still look at the reports, but I no longer feel the need to "subdue" them.

Last night, Meifang took my hand. She does this often, and I used to pull away after a moment to get back to my accounts or the news. But this time, I didn't. I let her hold it—maybe for five or six seconds. She didn’t say anything, and neither did I. We just held hands. Eventually, she let go gently and went to the kitchen to pour me some water.

I realize now that I owe her a great deal. For thirty-five years, I’ve used excuses like "monitoring her blood pressure" or "managing the family finances" to package my need for control as "responsibility." In truth, it was just my way of proving I was useful, that I deserved to be needed—by controlling everything. She saw through it long ago, but she never said a word. She just stayed by my side, waiting.

Chen Hao called recently saying his mortgage is a heavy burden. In the past, I would have immediately started analyzing his finances, giving him advice, even feeling a bit dissatisfied with his "poor planning." This time, I just listened, then told him, "It’s okay, take your time." There was a pause on the other end of the line; he didn’t seem used to me responding like that. I’m not used to it either. But it felt... more real.

Then there’s that idea about donating my tools to the technical school. Initially, I thought of it as "legacy"—wanting young people to learn what I’ve accumulated over forty years. But today I realized that was just another form of control; I wanted their learning to prove that my value wouldn't vanish with retirement. I still want to donate them, but the reason has changed. I just want to put those tools where they can continue to be used and cherished. As for who uses them or what they learn, that’s not for me to decide, nor does it need to be.

It’s strange. I always thought letting go of control would make me panic. But actually, it makes me feel... light?

My subordinates at the office are still a bit afraid of me. I know that. But lately, I’ve been thinking that maybe I could try a different way. Not by lowering standards, but... by changing how I communicate. Letting them know my expectations are high because I believe they can do well, not because I’m disappointed in them. This will require me to say more, and I’ve never been good at that. But perhaps it’s worth learning.

Tomorrow is Monday. Another week begins. I’ll keep taking the medicine, keep working, and keep being there for Meifang. And maybe, at the right moment, I’ll sit with her—saying nothing, just holding her hand.

That is enough.

ranked_items.content:
# Monday, July 18, 2022, 14:30

It’s been two hours since I got the report.

I’m sitting in my office with the report spread out on the desk. I told everyone "it’s nothing serious," but my fingers keep tracing those marked spots—blood pressure 139/92, fasting blood glucose 6.8. The numbers themselves don’t lie. I know that better than anyone.

I heard everything the doctor said, but I chose to remember only the first half of his sentences. "It’s under decent control." The rest—the parts about "needing to be more cautious" and "regular follow-ups"—I just let slide. That’s not my style.

Meifang was cooking when I got home. She saw the look on my face and asked about it. I didn't give her a straight answer, just said "I'm fine." But I know she can tell. After 35 years, she knows me too well. She tried to come closer, wanting me to talk, but I turned away and went into the study.

Then I suddenly turned back around and said to her, "Starting today, you need to cooperate too. We can't let your blood pressure issues drag on anymore. I’m going to set up a plan for you."

She was startled by my tone. I saw it. But I didn't stop. I need to control something. I need to ensure that something is within my grasp.

She isn't the problem. I know that. But at this moment, I can't face the fact that *I* am the problem.

Data doesn't lie. I’ve lived my whole life by data and standards, and now the data is starting to betray me. Blood pressure, blood sugar, weight—every indicator is slowly crossing the line. I can see the trend. I can predict it. And that is exactly what’s so terrifying.

The two-year retirement plan. I spoke of it with such certainty. But now I’m starting to wonder: if my body continues like this, how many "two years" do I have left?

The tools in the workshop are still there. I wanted to go and wipe them down, as I usually do. But today, I didn't move. I sat in my office, staring at this report, trying to use logic to convince myself that this is all within manageable limits.

But I know that some things are forever beyond my control.

For the first time, I truly felt that.

ranked_items.content:
In the workshop, Chen Guilin sat on a simple wooden stool, a notebook open before him. On the tool rack, the precision instruments glinted coldly under the fluorescent lights. His phone screen lit up—a WeChat message from his wife, Li Meifang: "What do you want for dinner?"

He didn't reply immediately. Instead, he stared at the dense notes in his notebook and the words "Who would use this?" circled in red ink.

Five minutes later, he finally typed:

"Do you think... there's any point in me keeping these records?"

After sending it, he added another line:

"Just thinking about what happens after I retire. These tools will just be sitting there anyway."

He paused for a long time, stopping himself from typing more. His fingers hovered over the keyboard before he finally lowered them.

Then he added one last message:

"I’ve been feeling a bit tired lately. Not the work kind of tired."

---

*Chen Guilin put down his phone and turned back to wiping a precision vernier caliper on the rack. His movements were slow and meticulous, but his eyes were no longer on the tool.*

ranked_items.content:
April 15, 2022. Evening.

Another entry today. Li Meifang added too much salt to the food at dinner. I tapped my bowl with my chopsticks. Her hand gave a little tremor. I got up, walked to the kitchen, took out my notebook, and wrote: "Added salt at dinner; blood pressure management inadequate."

She said she was afraid.

I didn't comfort her. I told her that data doesn't lie, and that this was for her own good. She kept her head down and didn't say another word.

It’s 7:30 PM now. She’s in the living room watching TV; I’m in the study. My hand is still trembling slightly.

It’s not out of anger. I have to admit, it’s not.

I’m thinking: if I can’t even manage her blood pressure, what else can I manage? Process optimization, quality standards—I can do all of that at work. But at home? She’s been used to my coldness for thirty-five years, and now that I’m using data and records to "care" for her, she’s actually afraid. What does that say?

I flip open my medical report. My blood sugar levels are fluctuating again. The doctor says it’s nothing major, but I know it’s the beginning. My body is weakening; I can feel it. I need to control everything more strictly—diet, routine, data—otherwise, things will spiral out of control.

But I’ve realized I can’t control her. I can’t control time. I can’t control where those tools will eventually end up.

My eldest son is in Shenzhen, my daughter in Guangzhou. Neither of them will learn my craft. I’ve used this notebook to record the purpose of every tool, trying to turn the intangible into words, but halfway through, I stopped and asked myself—what’s the point? Who will read this? Who will care?

I’ll be retiring in two years. What then? No work, no processes to optimize, no data to manage. What will I have left?

She might be crying right now. I can’t hear it, but I know.

Should I go and apologize? But I was right. Her blood pressure *does* need managing. Data *is* objective.

Why does my rationality always hurt her? Why can’t I just say something soft like everyone else?

I keep flipping through the notebook. Page after page of entries. Neat, precise, and utterly cold.

Is this my life?

I put down the pen. My hand is still shaking.

To be continued tomorrow.

ranked_items.content:
September 22, 2022, 8:30 PM

I’m sitting in my workshop, still holding that pair of pliers. I’ve wiped them over and over, but they never seem to get quite clean.

The doctor’s words are still circling in my head. 110, atherosclerosis, long-term medication. The numbers are clear. My whole life, I’ve been best at dealing with clear numbers, but this time, that clarity feels suffocating.

Meifang said something yesterday that I hadn’t been able to put into words: "I knew you’d have this sooner or later." She saw it coming. In the office, I can spot a flaw in the smallest detail, yet I couldn’t see my own body slowly breaking down. Irony.

I pulled out that notebook. Usually, I only record work logs, accounts, and process improvements. Today, for some reason, I started writing something else. I wrote a sentence, crossed it out. Wrote it again.

"If I could do it over, I would teach the younger ones more."

My hand shook a little as I wrote that.

Forty years. Forty years in this factory, from inspector to manager. Those precision tools, those processes, those improvement plans I puzzled out all by myself… suddenly I wonder, what happens to all of it if I collapse? Will my subordinates keep up my standards? Or will everything just fall apart?

But then I thought, maybe I’m wrong. Maybe the value of these things doesn’t depend on me handing them over personally. Maybe it’s already there—in the processes I improved, in the standards I demanded, in the things those young people learned even while they were resenting me.

I wrote another line in the notebook: "Maybe I should hold a technical sharing session before I retire."

As I finished the sentence, the tightness in my chest loosened a bit. Not entirely, but it was there.

It’s not about proving anything. It’s just… if I talk about these things, if I let people see *why* I do what I do instead of just how strict I am, then maybe… maybe there won’t be so much regret.

The doctor said I need to control my diet, get regular check-ups, and take my medicine. I can do that. Following a plan is what I do best.

But this time, there’s something new in the plan. Not just to live longer—though I hope for that, too. But to make these forty years actually mean something.

Meifang is calling me for dinner from downstairs. I didn’t answer right away. I looked again at those sentences in the notebook, the ones I’d revised over and over.

Maybe I’ll propose the idea to my supervisor tomorrow. Maybe I won’t. I don’t know yet.

But at least I wrote it down.

ranked_items.content:
September 10, 2022, 9:45 PM

I’ve been in the workshop for nearly three hours. Li Meifang knocked twice; I didn’t answer.

The morning’s medical report is still spinning in my head. Blood sugar at 110 mg/dL, Stage 2 hypertension, and that phrase—"pre-metabolic syndrome." The doctor spoke calmly, as if reading a production report, but I caught the "you should be worried" undertone. Early signs of carotid atherosclerosis. Long-term medication.

I’m sitting at the workbench, holding a pair of pliers, wiping them over and over with a piece of flannel. How many times have I wiped them? I’ve lost count. Usually, I follow a system—precision measuring tools in one group, cutting tools in another, fasteners in a third. Not today. I just pick up whatever is at hand, wipe it, put it back. Pick up another. My movements are slow because my hands are shaking slightly.

I don’t want to admit that.

Forty years. For forty years, I’ve been in control—schedules, diet logs, regular check-ups. My desk is organized without a single stray wire. The household ledgers are accurate to the penny. I thought that was enough. I thought that with enough precision, enough discipline, I could keep everything in my grasp.

But the doctor said it’s not enough.

That sentence keeps ringing in my ears—the one Li Meifang said: "I knew you’d get this sooner or later." When did she know? How did she know? Was she pitying me, or was she saying, "I saw through you long ago"? I don’t want to ask, because I’m afraid of the answer.

110 mg/dL. That number is wrong. Something has gone wrong with the management of my life, but I can’t find where. Or rather, I can’t find a solution at all. Pills? Long-term medication? What does that mean? It means my body has begun to betray me, and I cannot reverse it through stricter management.

How much longer?

The question has suddenly become urgent. How much longer can I work? How much longer until I see Chen Hao’s mortgage paid off? How much longer until I pass these tools, these forty years of things, on to someone? I never gave it serious thought because I assumed there was plenty of time.

Now, I’m not sure.

The tools are still here, piece by piece. My hands are still shaking.

I have to go to work tomorrow, read production reports, point out mistakes to my subordinates. Everything must continue. But I don’t know how much longer I can keep going.

I need to look at that report again. Maybe I missed something. Maybe the numbers will change if I scrutinize them enough.

But I know they won’t.

ranked_items.content:
After returning home that evening, Chen Guilin changed his clothes and sat down at the dining table. Li Meifang was serving the soup.

He ate a few mouthfuls of rice in silence before putting down his chopsticks.

"I might have been too harsh at the department meeting today," he said, his voice low, eyes fixed on his bowl. "The new QC inspector messed up the data entry again, and I called him out in front of everyone. He looked pretty shaken."

Li Meifang looked up at him but didn't respond immediately.

"My temper has been short lately," he paused, tapping his finger on the table. "I can feel it myself. I have high standards for the work, but..." He didn't finish the sentence, opting for a sip of soup instead.

He turned to his wife, his tone tinged with uncertainty. "Do you think I'm being too demanding? I want to get things done right, but it seems like..." He hesitated, "it seems like I always end up making people uncomfortable."

He wasn't looking for comfort; it was more like he was trying to confirm something. There was exhaustion in his eyes, along with a certain guilt he couldn't quite put into words.

"The physical exam is next week," he suddenly changed the subject, though his tone remained heavy. "I need to get to bed early these next few days."

He lowered his head and continued eating, but his movements were much slower than usual.

ranked_items.content:
Meifang, I’m back.

I played a few rounds at the club tonight. Midway through, I just came out with it—told them the doctor found something with my heart and that I need medication to keep it under control. I thought I’d just mention it briefly and move on, but they actually started asking quite a lot of questions. The strange thing was, there wasn't a hint of pity in their voices. It was as if they were talking about something perfectly normal—asking what meds I’m on, saying they have friends in the same boat, and that as long as you take your pills and get some exercise, you'll be fine.

Sitting there, I suddenly felt this sense of relief. I don't quite know how to describe it. Maybe it’s because they didn't treat me like someone who needs looking after; they just treated me as Chen Guilin, their friend and fellow player.

It made me realize that all these years, I’ve been trying so hard to prove I’m fine—striving to be the best at work, managing every little detail at home, even obsessing over your blood pressure. I thought that was what it meant to be responsible for my family. But looking back now...

The way I’ve treated you in the past hasn't been fair. You’ve always been there for me, taking care of me, yet I’ve always looked at you with a critical, watchful eye. It wasn't until my friends treated me the way they did today that I understood what it feels like to be accepted and treated as an equal.

I want to change. I don’t mean I’m going to stop caring about the family, but... I want to try being a companion to you, rather than a supervisor. If you’re willing.

We haven't talked much these past few years. I think it might be time for a change.

ranked_items.content:
September 17, 2022 Afternoon

My hands are still shaking.

It’s been three hours since I left the hospital. I’m sitting in my workshop, and my hands are still shaking. 110 mg/dL. Grade 2 hypertension. Carotid atherosclerosis. The doctor spoke these words with a calm tone, as if he were talking about the weather. But I heard him loud and clear. Every number felt like a nail being driven in.

I tell myself these are just data points. I’ve organized forty years of production reports; I know how to read data. But this time, the data is telling me that my body is failing.

My wife said, "I knew this would happen to you sooner or later." That hurt more than the diagnosis itself. When did she notice? I always thought I had everything under control—diet, routine, checkups. I was even secretly monitoring her blood pressure, terrified something might happen to her. And the result? I’m the one who collapsed first.

It’s ridiculous.

My wife urged me to adjust my pace of life. I told her, "I know. That’s exactly what I’m doing." But what am I doing right now? I’m in the workshop, repeatedly wiping down these tools. I’ve wiped the same screwdriver over a dozen times. My movements are forceful, as if I’m trying to scrub something off. I know it’s absurd. I’m using this to mask my anxiety. But I can’t stop.

The doctor said I’ll need long-term medication. "Long-term." That’s a heavy word.

I’m thinking about those precision tools—the ones I’ve collected for over twenty years. And those forty years of experience—the quality control standards, the process optimizations, the things I’ve never explicitly said but are etched into my brain. If I fall, what happens to all of it? I don’t have time to think about that, because the moment I do, I start to feel afraid.

I don’t want to tell Chen Hao and Chen Yu. Not yet. They’ll ask too many questions, and I don’t have the energy to answer them. Besides, I don’t know what I’d even say. "Your father is sick"? That sounds too dramatic. But if I don’t tell them, they’ll hear it from their mother sooner or later.

I need to make a plan. The doctor gave me a medication list; I’ve already noted it down. Regular follow-ups. Dietary adjustments. And... I don’t know what else. I listened to everything the doctor said, but right now, only one thought is stuck in my head: I’m running out of time.

I originally planned to retire in two years. Now that plan might have to change. Or maybe it can’t be changed at all. I don’t even know how much longer I can keep working.

It’s ironic. I’ve spent my whole life pursuing control. Precise management, clear plans, every step taken by the book. But my body won’t listen. The body has its own logic, and I can’t change it with willpower alone.

I put down the screwdriver. My hands are still shaking.

I have work tomorrow.

9:30 PM, in the bedroom. Li Meifang has finished getting ready for bed and is sitting up, scrolling through her phone. Chen Guilin walks in from the study, changes into his pajamas, and sits down on the edge of the bed.

---

"I talked to Haohao on the phone today. He’s been under a lot of pressure lately."

"Things are a bit unstable with his orders at work, and he seems a little lost. I didn't nag him about his mortgage like I usually do; I just listened to him talk for a while."

(Pauses, looking at his wife)

"He said he wants to find a chance to recalibrate. I just told him to take it slow, no need to rush."

(Pauses again, his tone becoming somewhat uncertain)

"Do you think... I’m usually too hard on them?"

(Turns to get into bed, but his movements are a bit slow)

"Never mind, I'll think about it tomorrow. Let’s get some sleep."

---

*Chen Guilin lies down, his back to his wife, but he doesn't fall asleep immediately. His phone screen flickers in the dark—he opens a health tracking app, glances at his step count and heart rate data for the day, then switches it off and lets out a long sigh.*

ranked_items.content:
# MEMO

**Date: September 19, 2022, Morning**
**Location: Office Cubicle**

---

Maybe I should change my ways.

The improvement plan Xiao Wang submitted during yesterday’s meeting was actually quite good. At the time, I pointed out three flaws directly. His expression… I can still see it now. He didn’t push back; he just nodded and took notes. When I walked past his desk at the end of the day, he was packing up—his movements were hurried, as if he were escaping something.

Is this right?

I’ve always believed that being strict is being responsible. A 25% reduction in defect rates—those are the facts. But what lies behind the data? It’s my subordinates working in fear under my gaze. Li Meifang once said, "You’re the same way at home." I didn't respond.

Control ≠ Respect.

Where did that phrase come from yesterday? Perhaps it was something the doctor said that triggered it. He talked about my blood pressure, my carotid artery, all those numbers… I’ve spent 40 years trying to control everything, yet my body has betrayed me anyway. I cannot control it. So what about my subordinates? I "manage" them the same way, as if I were managing a machine. But they aren't machines.

Time is running out. What actually matters?

It’s not the process optimization forms. It’s not those perfect quality inspection reports. If I were to collapse right now, who would remember how strict I was? Who would think of me because of a 25% lower defect rate?

Regarding Xiao Wang’s proposal—I actually should have praised him in front of everyone. And *then* pointed out the room for improvement.

I need to rethink how I speak to my staff.

Maybe there’s still time.

---

*To be continued*
ranked_items.subquestion: I’ve noticed that whenever you’re stressed or anxious lately, your fingers start making these subtle movements—like tapping rapidly on the desk or repeatedly rubbing those paper reports. What exactly is going through your mind in those moments?

ranked_items.content:
Tuesday afternoon, October 18, 2022

I spent the entire afternoon sitting in my small workshop. I didn’t clean any tools or organize my inventory. I just flipped through my notebooks, page by page.

What was I recording back then? Screwdriver models, wrench specifications, the purchase year and maintenance cycle for every single tool. Everything was neat and tidy, precise to a fault. Looking at these records used to give me a sense of peace—as if I had mastered something.

But today, I’m wondering: what did these tools actually teach me?

It wasn't how to use them. That’s too simple. It was... how to approach a task. With seriousness. Without compromise. Knowing that every detail matters. For forty years, the products I made with these tools had a defect rate 25% lower than the industry average. That wasn't a coincidence. It was because I never allowed myself to slack off.

But—

I wrote that word on a new page, then crossed it out. Wrote it again. Crossed it out again.

But I treated people the same way. My subordinates, my family. I demanded perfection and left no room for error. I thought that was called being responsible. Now, I’m not so sure.

Li Meifang has been humming in the kitchen lately. I haven't heard her hum like that in a long time. She says it’s because I’ve finally "loosened up a bit." I can hear it in her voice—it wasn't a compliment. It was... relief? She feels relieved.

That hurts a little.

I turned to a blank page and drew a timeline with a pencil. From 1982 to now. Forty years. I marked the key years: technician, team leader, department manager. I did well at every stage. Defect rates, process optimizations, the proposals that were adopted. It’s a beautiful report card.

But so what?

Chen Hao is in Shenzhen, under a lot of pressure. I keep track of his mortgage and his performance, but we barely have anything to say to each other. When Chen Yu comes home occasionally, our conversations are like status reports—how’s work, is your marriage stable. I thought I was teaching them about perseverance and quality awareness, but I was never truly there for them.

This is my regret. Perhaps my greatest regret.

Next to the timeline, I wrote: "If I could do it over, what would I do?"

But I know there are no "ifs." I only have the now. And about two years left of work. Maybe in these two years, I should record some things. Not tool lists, not process documents. But... what I was thinking during these forty years. Why I did things the way I did. Young people might need that.

Or rather, I need to do this. To forgive myself.

Meifang is calling me for dinner. I hear her voice drifting up from downstairs—gentle, not rushing me. I close the notebook and stand up.

Maybe I should listen to her more. Really listen, instead of mentally calculating her blood pressure and diet.

Maybe that is what I really need to learn.
