# September 5, 2022, 8:15 PM

I deleted that spreadsheet today.

It took me a long time to finally hit the delete key. My finger hovered over the mouse for nearly five minutes. That spreadsheet held three months of Meifang’s blood pressure readings, diet logs, exercise durations... I had color-coded everything: green for 'target met,' yellow for 'needs improvement,' and red for 'danger.'

When I finally clicked it, I realized I was sweating.

I said those words to Meifang tonight. She was in the kitchen, and I stood in the doorway and said, "My over-supervision isn't protecting you; it's hurting you." She turned around and looked at me with something in her eyes I can't quite describe. She didn't say anything, she just nodded.

We were silent for a long time. Then she said, "We are living together, not just surviving in fear every day."

When I heard that, I felt something in my chest loosen.

Afterward, I sat in my workshop for two hours. I didn't organize my tools; I didn't do anything at all. I just sat there. I looked at those precision parts and instruments, wondering where they would eventually end up. Maybe I really should donate them to a school. Maybe that’s the right thing to do.

I’ve made a new plan. Not one of those extreme ones filled with numbers and color codes, but something... more like a plan for living:

Exercise three times a week—not for the data, but for the feeling of being alive.
Check medical data once a month, not every week.
Eat healthily, but not to extremes.
See a therapist next month. I need to deal with the reality of aging. The reality of death.

I can't figure these things out on my own.

The medical report is still on the desk. I haven't looked at it again. Maybe I’ll look tomorrow, maybe the day after. It’s okay not to look right now.

Back to work tomorrow. Back to chess next week. Life goes on.

But it feels different now.

ranked_items.content:
3:20 AM. The phone screen lit up. Chen Guilin stared at the WeChat chat box. His wife’s message was still there: "Are you okay? Do you want some warm water?"

His fingers hovered over the keyboard for a long time.

Finally, he turned off the screen.

---

Daylight broke.

The light from outside slowly filtered in. Chen Guilin had sat on the sofa all night, and the tightness in his chest had finally eased a bit. He touched his chest and took a few deep breaths. Reason told him it was just anxiety. But reason didn't seem to count for much in the early hours of the morning.

He picked up his phone. His wife’s message was still there. He checked the time—6:47 AM. Li Meifang would be waking up soon.

He started typing, then deleted it. Typed again, and deleted it again.

Finally, he sent:

"Good morning. I'm fine, just stayed up thinking about work and missed your message, sorry. How’s your blood pressure? Remember to eat breakfast."

After hitting send, he put the phone down and walked to the kitchen. There was some porridge left over from yesterday in the fridge. He scooped out a bowl and set it on the dining table.

Then he sat down and opened the thick health logbook. Turning to yesterday’s page, he noted down: "3:00 AM, chest tightness, shortness of breath, lasted about 20 minutes, then subsided."

His handwriting was neat, but he pressed down so hard that the tip of the pen nearly tore through the paper.

He looked at the line of text and added one more sentence: "Likely an anxiety response."

Then he closed the book and waited for his wife to wake up.

ranked_items.content:
# May 8, 2022, past 10 PM

Sitting in the study, it’s already very quiet outside. Meifang fell asleep a long while ago; I can hear her light snoring drifting in from the bedroom. Her blood pressure was a bit high again today. I saw the reading she took this afternoon, but I didn't tell her.

What happened at the chess room today keeps spinning in my head.

Midway through the game, I suddenly realized I was staring blankly at the board. My opponent asked what I was thinking about, and I said, "I'm getting older; my reactions aren't as sharp anymore." As soon as the words left my mouth, they felt foreign to me. I wouldn't have said that before. I would have said, "This is an interesting move," or I wouldn't have explained at all and just played the piece.

But today, I was just slow. The calculations in my head felt sluggish. I could feel it.

Player A tried to comfort me, saying my game has always been stable. I heard myself reply: "It’s stable because I’m afraid to change."

I regretted saying it immediately. Not because it wasn't true, but because it was *too* true. I’ve never been that blunt before. Stable... yes, I’ve always chased stability. Forty years, the same factory, the same way of working, the same rhythm of life. No job-hopping, no risks, every step planned out.

That’s not stability. That’s stagnation.

I left the chess room halfway through. That has never happened before. My friends were a bit surprised; I told them I was tired and needed to go home. As I turned to leave, I looked back at that place—the familiar boards, the familiar faces, everything so familiar. My eyes stung a little.

Why is this happening?

The tools in the workshop are still there. All these years, I’ve been wiping them down and organizing them, like I’m looking after something precious. But Hao is in real estate in Shenzhen, and Yu is teaching in Guangzhou. They’ve never asked what those tools are for. Once, I tried to explain the use of a specific drill bit to Hao, but before I could finish, he said, "Dad, I’ve got a client I need to call back."

I’m recording the purpose of every tool in a notebook. Writing them down, one by one. But halfway through, I asked myself—what’s the point? Who is ever going to look at this notebook?

The physical exam report is still sitting in the drawer. The doctor said those figures "require attention." Every two days, I take it out and look at it, staring at the numbers, trying to find some kind of pattern. As if by doing that, I could control something. But the truth is, I can’t control anything. Blood pressure rises, blood sugar fluctuates, reactions slow down. These things are happening, and all I can do is record them.

Meifang’s blood pressure is rising too. I’m monitoring her diet and pushing her to get checkups. But what if one day I can’t move either? In our thirty-five years together, we’ve said too little. Now, even if I want to make up for it, I can't.

How much time is left?

I don't know. But I can feel time accelerating. It’s as if I used to have plenty of time to do something, and now I suddenly realize it’s gone. Retirement in two years, and then what? Empty days, no work, no one wanting anything from me, no one inheriting anything I know.

I sit here, pen in hand, but I don’t know what to write.

Maybe it’s best to just keep working tomorrow. At least when I’m working, I still know what I’m doing.

April 5, 2022. Night.

I’ve been sitting in the office for a long time. Wang Gang’s quality inspection report is still spinning in my head.

The formatting was off again when he handed it in this morning—the classification standards in the header didn't match last year’s specifications. It’s such a basic thing to get wrong. I couldn't help myself; I snapped at him. He tried to explain, but I cut him off. No excuses. Professionalism is about an obsession with detail.

His face changed the moment I said it.

Looking back now, my tone was harsh. Harsher than usual. He’s actually a decent employee. In the past, I would have pointed out these small mistakes, but never like that. Why today...?

My fingers have been tapping on the desk for a long while. Rapidly. I didn't even realize I was doing it.

What am I thinking? Was his report really that outrageous? Or... was I just taking it out on him?

It’s been like this lately. I’m becoming more demanding with my subordinates and more micro-managing with my wife. This morning, I was looking at her blood pressure logs again; a single high reading and I started feeling restless. I spent another whole night in the tool shed, numbering all the precision tools and documenting their uses. I’m terrified that one day I’ll suddenly... lose the ability to move, and these things will become completely useless because no one knows how to handle them.

Two years until retirement. Two years.

I know what I’m trying to do. I’m trying to control things. Control the work, the standards, everything at home—even the aging of my own body. It’s as if by managing every tiny detail, I can prove I’m still useful, that I can still... what?

Wang Gang has to come in for work tomorrow. I need to figure out how to handle this. Not for his sake, but because... I don’t want to become this kind of person. I don’t want to use harshness to mask my own fear.

But I don’t know how to change.

How much longer can I do this? How much longer can I keep things under control like this?

Saturday, October 22, 2022 | Sunny

I had a phone call with Haohao last night.

I originally intended to just check in on him—ask how things were going, if he was feeling the pressure of his mortgage, if work was still smooth. The usual questions. But as we kept talking, I found myself telling him about the doctor’s diagnosis. I said, "I want to share something with you"—the moment those words left my mouth, I even surprised myself. For years, I’ve never been one to initiate conversations like this with them.

He was silent for a while after hearing it. Then he asked, "Dad, how are you doing now? Are you taking your medicine properly?" There was something in his voice I hadn't heard in a long time. It caught me off guard, and my eyes began to sting. "I am, thank you," I said, my voice thick with emotion.

I sat there for a long time after hanging up.

For so many years, I’ve been the one supervising everyone—supervising Meifang’s diet and checkups, supervising the quality of my subordinates' work, supervising Haohao and Yuyu’s careers and marriages. I thought this was my responsibility, my value. I thought that as long as I maintained enough control and planned meticulously enough, I could protect them and prove that I was a good husband, a good father, and a good manager.

But in reality? I was just using "care" as a pretext for control. I never truly let them take care of me.

Haohao’s question last night—"Dad, how are you doing"—made me suddenly realize that perhaps being cared for is harder than caring for others. Perhaps admitting one's vulnerability requires more courage than maintaining a perfect image.

I am not omnipotent. My body will age, my plans will be disrupted, and I cannot control everything. I’ve actually known this for a long time, but it wasn't until last night that I truly accepted it.

My dedication to work over the years, my pursuit of quality, my responsibility to my family—these things are enough in themselves. I don’t need to prove myself through perfect control. Perhaps this is what I truly want to leave behind for them.

I’m retiring in two years. I think it’s time I had some real talks with Meifang, Haohao, and Yuyu. Real conversations, not just inquiries and instructions.

ranked_items.content:
# Work Memo

**Date**: October 28, 2022  
**Subject**: Reflections on a New Approach to Feedback  
**Recorded by**: Chen Guilin  

---

## Record of Events

This morning, I discussed the process plan for a new product with a junior engineer from our department. While there were some lapses in rigor in his proposal, I refrained from my usual habit of pointing out the flaws directly.

**What I did differently**:
1. First, I acknowledged specific areas where he did well—the data collection was thorough, and the conceptual framework was clear.
2. I explained why precision control is critical at this stage, particularly regarding its impact on subsequent costs and yield.
3. Instead of providing the answer, I asked him: "Let’s look into this together—is there anything else we need to consider here?"
4. He identified the problem himself and even added a perspective I hadn't considered.

**Result**: His motivation was visibly boosted. He took the initiative to look up relevant materials after the meeting and returned this afternoon to discuss the improved plan.

---

## Reflections

This approach is a significant departure from my previous style. I used to be blunt about pointing out issues for the sake of efficiency. Looking back, however, that kind of feedback might lead people to passively accept criticism rather than truly think for themselves.

This time was different. He was involved in the thought process, which gave him more confidence in the final conclusion. More importantly, he didn't just learn the answer to this specific problem; he learned **how to analyze problems**.

---

## Follow-up Plans

If this method proves effective, I want to systematize it:

- Implement a new feedback framework within the department (Specific Praise → Explanation of Principles → Open Discussion).
- Compile a "Technical Mentorship Guide" in preparation for the knowledge hand-off before my retirement.
- Potentially organize a technical sharing session at the end of the year using this interactive format instead of a one-way lecture.

The benefit of this is that knowledge isn't just memorized—it is understood and internalized. Perhaps this is what true "legacy" looks like.

---

## Remarks

This idea stems from some recent reflections. My health is a reminder that I won't be in this position forever. Before I leave, can I ensure these young people inherit not just my standards, but my way of thinking? That might be more valuable than any procedural document.

I will continue to observe the results next week.

- Morning: Completed the revision of Q4 quality inspection standards; submitted for managerial approval.
- Afternoon: Departmental meeting to discuss defect rate targets for the new production line; set at below 0.8%.
- Evening: Chess and Card Room.

**Personal Notes:**

While playing chess with my friends at the club, I took the initiative to mention my recent physical exam results. I didn't hide anything, nor did I downplay it. I just said it straight—the doctor said I need to watch my heart and prescribed some medication.

Friend A asked how I was feeling. I said I’m alright, just need to accept the reality of it. They didn't overreact with excessive concern, nor did they pretend they hadn't heard; they just accepted it naturally. It felt a bit unfamiliar, but not unpleasant.

Reflecting on it, I’ve spent years accustomed to keeping everything under control—work, family, health, plans. It was as if I believed that as long as I remained rigorous and disciplined enough, I could prevent anything bad from happening. Clearly, that’s not the case. The doctor’s diagnosis served as a reminder: I am aging, I can get sick, and I can lose control.

This isn't a failure. It’s just reality.

Lately, the way I interact with Meifang has changed. Before, I was always monitoring her blood pressure and diet like I was executing a task. Now, I’m starting to realize that perhaps she doesn't need a supervisor; she needs companionship. Yesterday, when she brought up her recent fatigue, I didn't offer a list of suggestions like I usually do—I just listened. She seemed to relax a bit.

One more thing. Chen Hao mentioned his mortgage pressure over the phone recently. My usual reaction would be to analyze the data and propose solutions. This time, I asked him how he actually felt. He seemed a bit surprised, but he opened up a lot. I’ve realized that for all these years, I might have been "directing" them rather than "understanding" them.

There’s a little over a year left until retirement. During this time, I want to organize a few things. Not necessarily technical documents or operating manuals, but the reflections from all these years—what truly matters, and what I have been running away from.

Onward tomorrow.

---

**Remarks:**
- Schedule a discussion with my superior next Monday regarding process improvement plans.
- Remember to remind Meifang about her check-up appointment on Wednesday.

July 5, 2022, 6:45 PM

Had a call with Hao. He said the real estate market has been a bit cold lately, but he’s holding on. I asked him if he’d ever thought about learning something about mechanics—maybe it could be a hobby later on. He laughed and said, "Dad, what are you talking about? I don’t have the brain for that." I didn't push it further.

After hanging up, I sat in the workshop for a long time.

Actually, that wasn't what I wanted to ask. What I wanted to ask was—has he ever thought about what will happen to all these tools, all these things I’ve spent forty years learning? But I couldn't get the words out. Just like I never can.

During our call this afternoon, I told him "everything is under control." Thinking back on that phrase now, it feels a bit ridiculous. What does "under control" even mean? What am I controlling? Hao is in Shenzhen worrying about his mortgage, Yu is busy with work in Guangzhou, and here I am, documenting the uses of these tools as if I’m in a race against time. But time isn't racing me—it doesn't care about me at all.

Li Meifang is downstairs watching TV. I can hear her coughing. It’s time to nag her about going to the hospital to check her blood pressure again. These are things I can control. But what’s the use of controlling them?

The tool records in my notebook are halfway finished. With every entry I write, I wonder—who is going to use this? Hao? No. Yu? Even less likely. So why am I writing all this?

Maybe it’s just to keep myself from being idle. To tell myself there’s still something left to do.

The physical exam report from May is still in the drawer. A few of the indicators fluctuated a bit. The doctor said it’s nothing serious, just needs regular follow-ups. But I keep taking it out, looking at those numbers over and over. As if I could find some hidden meaning in them.

I’ve never known how to talk to them. That’s the truth. It’s been that way since they were little. I thought it would get better as they grew up, but it seems to have gotten worse. Now, I even self-censor before asking a simple question, terrified of saying something that might cause awkwardness.

Maybe I should have started teaching them earlier. But back then, I was busy with work. Now that I want to teach, it’s already too late.

Tomorrow, I’ll continue documenting the tools. There are still over thirty items left to record.

I should do it while I can still move.

ranked_items.content:
# June 28, 2022, 10:15 PM

The light in the workshop is still on. Li Meifang has already gone to bed. Her blood pressure has been a bit high lately, and she’s still not really drinking the herbal tea I brewed for her. Forget it, I’ll deal with it tomorrow.

I just flipped through my notebook again. I’ve been documenting the tools since last year, and there are over thirty entries now. For each one, I’ve noted its purpose, specifications, and date of purchase. Some of the handwriting has started to fade, so I traced over it again in pencil.

A question suddenly crossed my mind—if my body starts to fail, what will I have left to do?

This question has been circling in my head for a long time. The May physical exam report is still in the drawer; I haven’t looked at it a second time. I remember all the metrics and indicators the doctor mentioned, but I chose not to dwell on them. And now? I still don't want to think too deeply about it, but it seems I can’t avoid it anymore.

My eldest son is in Shenzhen, preoccupied with his mortgage; my daughter is teaching in Guangzhou. I’ve never asked them if they’re interested in learning any of this. Perhaps they lost interest long ago. Perhaps I shouldn't have expected anything from the start.

I added another question mark under the one already in the notebook. Then, I placed the notebook in the most prominent spot on the workbench. I kept staring at it in the darkness. It’s a bit of a foolish gesture, but I just wanted to look at it. I wanted to see if those questions sitting there meant they truly existed, and truly needed to be resolved.

*While I can still move, I have to hurry.* This phrase keeps popping up now.

I proposed four more process improvement plans at work this year, and all of them were adopted. My subordinates are still afraid of me. A new QC inspector made three mistakes last week that I pointed out in front of everyone; now he’s all submissive whenever he sees me. I know it’s not right, but I can’t help myself. Maybe I’m using this as a way to fight back against something—against time, against powerlessness, against that growing realization: I am losing control over everything.

Li Meifang says I’ve become even quieter lately. She’s right. What else is there to say?

Tomorrow, I’ll continue documenting the tools. There are still seven or eight left to write down.

Must hurry.

Haozi called while I was in the workshop. He said business hasn't been great lately and he’s feeling the pressure. I asked if he’d thought about switching gears, but he said he has to stick with real estate. I didn't push it further.

After hanging up, I just sat there, looking at the tools on the wall. Some were left by my father; others I’ve picked up myself over the years. It took me nearly a month to number them and document what each one is for. I only finished the last page yesterday.

I’ve been wondering what will happen to all this stuff in the end. Haozi has no interest in them, and Yu’er wouldn't know how to use them. No matter how detailed my notes are, it doesn't seem like it’ll change anything.

I have to go back to the hospital for a follow-up tomorrow. The doctor said I need regular monitoring. I didn’t tell you because there wasn’t much to say—it’s just a routine check-up.

How is your blood pressure? Have you been taking your medicine on time lately? I’ll go to the hospital with you this weekend to pick up that test report.

ranked_items.content:
July 25, 2022, 10:45 PM

I’ve been sitting in the workshop for two hours. Haven’t moved.

While I was organizing my tools this afternoon, I came across that notebook. The one from last year. Tucked inside was a slip of paper with my own handwriting on it: "Who is ever going to use this?" I don’t remember writing it. When I saw those words, my hands started to shake.

I massaged my temples. I still haven’t quite recovered.

Forty years. From entering the factory in 1982 until now—forty years. Every single one of these tools has a story. That Swiss precision knife? Bought it in 1995 during a business trip to Beijing. That set of measuring gauges? I’ve used them for over thirty years, and the precision is still within 0.01 millimeters. I can tell you the origin, the purpose, and the maintenance routine for every piece here. Sometimes I spend my weekends here, wiping them down one by one, documenting them one by one.

But—who is going to use them?

Hao is selling real estate in Shenzhen. Yu is teaching in Guangzhou. They’ve never asked me about these things. I never expected them to. But sitting here now, I suddenly wonder: I spent all this time maintaining, organizing, and recording... for what? In the end, is it all just a pile of unwanted junk?

I donated some tools to the technical school. They were very happy to have them. But that’s different. That’s a donation, not a legacy. A legacy should be someone standing by your side, watching how you use the tools, asking why you do it that way, and then carrying it on. Right now, no one is asking.

Yesterday’s physical exam results aren't out yet. My blood pressure is high again. The doctor said I have to keep it under control. I know I do. I control everything every day—recording my wife’s diet, my own step count, every single data point. It’s as if by recording things meticulously enough, I can stop something from happening. But it’s a lie. I know that in my heart.

My hands are shaking again.

Forty years of work might end up as a pile of scrap metal. Or sold as secondhand junk. Or tossed into a recycling bin. When I picture this in my head, I find it a bit hard to breathe.

Maybe I should just donate it all. Let the students at the school use them. At least they’d be useful. It’s better than leaving them here for me to look at every weekend—a constant reminder of what I once did and how I can’t change anything now.

Li Meifang is downstairs watching TV. She doesn’t know what I’m thinking up here. She never asks. I’m used to not saying anything.

I need to hurry. While I can still move. But hurry for what?

—

The notebook is closed. I didn’t write any more.

ranked_items.content:
September 19, 2022, 10:47 PM

Something strange happened in the office today.

During this afternoon's meeting, Xiao Wang from Quality Control handed in a report with problematic data. Usually, I would have said bluntly, "This is unacceptable. Redo it." But in that moment, I paused. I looked at his nervous expression and a thought suddenly hit me—is he afraid of something, too?

Instead, I said, "Let’s take a look together and see where things went wrong."

There was a moment of silence in the conference room. I could feel everyone looking at me. Xiao Wang’s shoulders relaxed a little.

After the meeting, I wrote in my notebook: "Maybe I should change my approach."

But now, sitting here in my workshop looking at these tools, I’m not sure if this is progress or a retreat.

The doctor’s words are still ringing in my ears: "Long-term medication, regular check-ups." I always thought that as long as I was strict and cautious enough, I could control everything. My work, my health—everything. But clearly, I was wrong. My body doesn't follow my orders. I can’t protect myself through perfect management.

Maybe what my subordinates see isn't my high standards, but my fear.

My wife said, "I knew you’d get this sooner or later." What did she mean by that? Did she see it coming all along? What about everyone else? I thought I had hidden it so well.

I don’t know how to tell the kids. Chen Hao is under so much pressure with his mortgage, and Chen Yu is busy with work in Guangzhou. Telling them would only worry them. But if I say nothing, isn't that a form of deception?

My hands are still shaking. I wiped down my tools, then wiped them again.

Maybe I’ve been using control to mask my vulnerability. Now that the vulnerability is exposed, I don’t know what’s left.

I have to go to the hospital tomorrow to pick up my prescription.

ranked_items.content:
# Work Log: October 30, 2022

**Sunday, 20:00**

I’ve been reflecting on something over the past few days. This afternoon, while sitting with Meifang for a long time, she mentioned that I’ve been talking a bit more lately. I just smiled and didn’t deny it.

Last Friday’s medical report showed that things have stabilized. The doctor said to just keep taking the medication on schedule and come in for regular check-ups. Meifang was right there beside me taking notes, even more focused than I was. It made me think back on all the "supervising" I’ve done at home over the years—checking her blood pressure, monitoring her diet. My intentions might have been right, but my approach was probably too rigid. She never complained; instead, she’s now taking the initiative to accompany me to the hospital. It’s a strange feeling, but a grounding one.

Back to the office tomorrow. I’ve been thinking about how to organize everything I’ve accumulated over these forty years before I retire. It’s not necessarily about passing it on to someone specific, but I feel there should be a record. On Monday, I plan to talk to my supervisor about putting together a summary of my quality management experience in the first half of next year, perhaps as a formal document. Also, regarding that technical school—I want to move forward with that. I plan to systematically categorize and label the precision tools in the workshop and donate them. In my hands, they are just tools; in the hands of students, they might become something more.

Feedback from my subordinates has been better lately. I’ve noticed that when giving feedback, I’ve started asking for their thoughts first instead of just pointing out problems. The defect rate remains low, but the atmosphere seems less tense. I haven't fully processed this shift myself, but it feels right.

Haohao called last week saying his mortgage pressure is still quite heavy. I didn’t jump in with advice immediately; instead, I asked what his plans were. He said he might need to adjust his work schedule. I told him, "As long as you’ve thought it through. Let us know if you need help." I surprised myself a bit saying that, but in that moment, I was being sincere.

Yuyu’s work in Guangzhou is also going well. She said she wants to come back for a visit after National Day; Meifang and I are both very happy about it.

Perhaps the meaning of life isn't about perfect control, but about genuine connection. That thought surfaced while I was chatting with Meifang—I didn't expect to say it myself. But once it was out there, it felt like I had finally admitted something.

Onward to next week.

---
*Recorded by: Chen Guilin*  
*Date: October 30, 2022*

ranked_items.content:
June 15, 2022, 7:30 PM

I lost again at the chess room this afternoon. It wasn't that I lost to Player A; I lost to myself.

Player A said I played very conservatively today. The word he used was "cautious," but I could hear what he really meant—he was saying I’ve changed. I didn’t argue. I just said, "I’m getting older, I have to be more careful," and kept setting up the pieces. He didn’t say anything else.

But I know he saw it. I saw it in myself, too.

I used to dare to take risks, to make bold moves in the mid-game. And now? Now I pause and think for a long time before every single step. It’s not because I’ve become smarter; it’s because I’m afraid—afraid that one wrong move will lead to a point of no return. Afraid of wasting time.

It’s strange. A game only lasts two hours, and losing is just losing a game. But I find myself applying this same logic to everything in my life. I’m stricter with my subordinates at work, I’m more controlling over Meifang’s diet at home, and I even use a magnifying glass to scrutinize the numbers on my medical reports.

Why?

I tell myself this is being responsible. But deep down, I know it’s fear. Fear that time is running out. Fear that something is slipping through my fingers and I won’t be able to catch it in time.

The tools in the workshop are still there. The notebooks are there, too. I’ve recorded about half of it. Hao is in Shenzhen, and Yu is in Guangzhou. Neither of them has ever asked what those tools are for. I haven't brought it up, either.

Sometimes I wonder, living this cautiously—what exactly am I trying to protect?

I have to go back to the chess room tomorrow. I wonder if Player A will notice something else.

Forget it, stop thinking. Time for bed. I haven't finished going through the medical report from May; I’ll give it another careful look tomorrow.

ranked_items.content:
Haozi, how have you been lately?

I’ve been thinking, and I wanted to share a few words with you. This recent health scare has given me a lot to reflect on. The doctor said I need to stay on medication and slow down. With your mom here by my side, I’ve realized that all these years, I’ve been constantly rushing—rushing to keep everything under control, to make everything perfect.

Regarding your mortgage in Shenzhen, I know I used to pester you with questions, almost like I was auditing your books. Looking back, that wasn't right. You’re an adult now, and you have your own plans. I thought I was "helping" you in my own way, but in reality, I was just putting pressure on you.

I’ve only recently come to understand that maybe life doesn't need to be perfectly controlled. In my forty years of work, I always pursued "zero defects," but life isn't an assembly line. There are things you just can't control, and there’s pressure you have to learn to let go of. Otherwise, you’ll end up like me—waiting until your sixties to regret what you’ve missed out on.

I’m not telling you this so you’ll follow in my footsteps. On the contrary, I hope you can live a more relaxed life than I did. A mortgage is just a mortgage, and a job is just a job; don't keep yourself wound so tight. Taking time for family and getting proper rest—those things matter, too.

Your mom says I’ve become quite talkative lately, ha. Maybe I really am changing.

If you’re free this weekend, give me a call. Let’s have a good chat.

— Dad

ranked_items.content:
# Memo - Reflection and Legacy

**Date: October 18, 2022, Afternoon**
**Location: Home Study**

---

## Core Points Summary

### I. Reflections on the Essence of 40 Years of Work
- ~~Perfect quality control~~ → Obsession with detail is a value in itself
- ~~Sense of achievement from process optimization~~ → The patience and persistence learned through the process
- A 25% drop in defect rates is not the finish line, but a reminder: Why do I demand so much?
  - Answer: Because I believe in standards.
  - The real answer: Because I am afraid of losing control.

### II. Beyond the Technical (Important)
- The meaning of tools lies not in collecting them, but in how they taught me to think.
- What precision work taught me:
  - A single millimeter determines success or failure.
  - However, the distance between people cannot be measured in millimeters.
  - (Thinking back on my harshness toward subordinates and my "supervision" of my wife over the years.)
- I must admit: I cannot control everything.
  - The doctor's words are still ringing in my ears.
  - The body ages; plans change.
  - This is not failure.

### III. If I Could Start Over
- Explain to the team *why* we do things earlier, rather than just telling them *what* to do.
- Give young engineers more room to make mistakes (instead of blunt dismissals).
- ~~Keeping knowledge in my head~~ → Actively record, share, and discuss.
- Listen more to my wife, rather than "supervising" from the sidelines.
- Communicate with the children about more than just their mortgages and jobs.

### IV. Words for Young Engineers
- Standards are important, but don't let them become a weapon.
- Details determine quality, but details can also consume your life—learn to prioritize.
- 40 years is a long time, but it is also very short.
- The things you take for granted now will become mere memories by the time you reach my age.
- Don't wait for your heart to remind you—some things are more important than perfection.

### V. Immediate Action Plan
- [ ] Compile a "Core Experiences in Quality Management" document (for technical school students).
- [ ] Sit down with my subordinates for a talk—explain rather than accuse.
- [ ] Have a real conversation with my wife this weekend; listen to her talk about home.
- [ ] Call Chen Hao and Chen Yu respectively; do not mention work.
- [ ] Continue medication and scheduled check-ups—accept this reality.

---

## Notes
- The precision tool inventory can be set aside for now.
- The 40-year timeline is drawn; now it needs to be turned into a story.
- Perhaps retirement isn't the end, but a different kind of beginning?
- (I wouldn't have believed that sentence before.)

---

**One final question: What is my greatest regret in life?**
It isn't that I didn't reach a higher position.
It’s that I never truly looked at the people beside me.

# Work Log

**Date:** April 5, 2022  
**Recorded by:** Chen Guilin  
**Department:** Production Quality Management

---

## Morning Summary

At 9:30 AM, a departmental meeting was held to review quality inspection standards. The primary focus was checking the formatting compliance of recently submitted quality inspection reports.

**Issues Identified:** The third batch product inspection report submitted by inspector Wang Gang showed significant formatting non-compliance. Specific issues included:
- Missing mandatory timestamps in the data record columns.
- Vague descriptions in the "Abnormal Items" section, failing to meet the requirements of Article 4.2 of the *Quality Inspection Report Standardization Template v2.1*.
- Incomplete information in the signature fields.

**Actions Taken:**  
I pointed out these issues publicly and emphasized the necessity of standardized formatting. The core of quality management lies in an obsession with detail—this is not a trivial matter; it is a manifestation of professionalism. If we cannot even control basic report formatting, how can we ensure the credibility and traceability of our data?

Wang Gang attempted to explain the reasons, but that is beside the point. Standards are standards; there is no room for negotiation in execution. I have ordered him to resubmit a compliant version by 3:00 PM tomorrow.

---

## Afternoon Schedule

- 14:00-14:30: Coordinate this week's production capacity plan with the Production Department.
- 14:30-15:30: Audit report samples from other inspectors (spot check of 10 reports).
- 16:00: Compile the first draft of this month's process improvement plan, expected to be submitted to superiors next week.

---

## Remarks

The department's defect rate remains at 0.8% this month, continuing to stay below the industry average. However, personnel execution needs further strengthening. Professionalism is non-negotiable.

I told Meifang about it over dinner tonight.

Another formatting error cropped up in QA this morning. It wasn't a major issue, but I can't stand that kind of slackness when it comes to details. I was right there on the floor and called it out immediately; my voice might have been a bit loud. The subordinate looked pretty shaken, but I had to make it clear—details determine product quality, and product quality determines whether this company survives. I’m not exaggerating.

I told Meifang that I need to overhaul the department’s standard operating procedures. Just emphasizing it verbally isn't enough; it has to be documented with every step clearly defined. As she listened, she just looked at me that way, and I knew what she was thinking: *Here we go again.* But this time is different. I can feel that if things continue like this, the problems are only going to multiply.

She said, "Don't push yourself too hard." I didn't respond to that. After a few bites of food, I explained once more why these standards must be strictly enforced. She just nodded and kept eating.

The truth is, I know my subordinates are afraid of me. But what’s wrong with a little fear? Fear makes people diligent. I’ve been in this position for 12 years, and our defect rate is 25% lower than the industry average—that’s not because I have a pleasant temperament.

Lately, I just feel this constant urge to hurry. Whether it’s work or anything else. While I’m still able to move, I need to get everything done right.

ranked_items.content:
**July 10, 2022 Work Log**

**Weather: Sunny  Temperature: 32°C**

**Morning Work Record**

09:15 Quality Control Department weekly meeting. Discovered issues while reviewing yesterday’s production reports.

The periodic inspection sheet submitted by my subordinate, Li, was a mess—he used Chinese capitalized characters for the date column, which violates the company’s unified standards. This is no small matter. I pointed out the error in front of everyone. My tone might have been harsh, but it had to be. Details determine product quality, and product quality determines the company’s survival. This logical chain cannot be allowed to slacken. A formatting error can lead to data misinterpretation; misinterpretation can lead to missed inspections; and a missed inspection means defective products reaching the market. I cannot tolerate a failure in any link of this chain.

Li looked visibly upset at the time. I know he probably thinks I went overboard. But I don’t need him to like me; I need him to understand the importance of standardization.

**Developing New QC Standards**

09:45-11:20 Drafted the new "Detailed Rules for Quality Inspection of Precision Components (Revised Edition)" in my office.

Key adjustments:
- Table formats unified to Arabic numerals and 24-hour clock.
- Added a "Basis for Judgment" column for every inspection item; reference standard numbers must be cited.
- Added an "Auditor Review" step at the inspector’s signature line—a double-confirmation system.
- Data entry deadline moved up to 17:00 daily, leaving 2 hours for data auditing.
- Added a "Deviation Analysis" section to the monthly summary report; reasons and corrective measures for every out-of-spec item must be explained.

None of this is needless bureaucracy. Every single rule points toward the same goal: turning invisible risks into visible data, and turning potential errors into inevitable discoveries.

I plan to hold a departmental meeting next Monday to announce the new standards. Everyone will be given a 3-day transition period, with formal implementation starting July 15.

**Other**

Did not go out for lunch. Stayed in the office to review the draft of the new standards again and refined the wording in two places.

I am meeting with my superior this afternoon to report on this month’s improvement plan. This new set of standards should be adopted.

**Physical Status**

Sleep has been okay lately. No particular discomfort.

My wife mentioned yesterday that her blood pressure has risen again. Reminded her to take her medication on time and sent her a text at noon telling her to use less salt in dinner.

**Remarks**

I feel my demands for work have been increasing lately. It’s not because of new pressure from the company, but rather a feeling that I cannot allow for any slack. I have less than two years until retirement; I want to optimize all processes and set all standards in stone during this time. That way, even after I leave, the system will continue to function properly. This is important.

---

*Recorded by: Chen Guilin*
*Date: 2022-07-10*

ranked_items.content:
Tuesday afternoon, October 18, 2022

I spent the entire afternoon sitting in my small workshop. I didn’t clean any tools or organize my inventory. I just flipped through my notebooks, page by page.

What was I recording back then? Screwdriver models, wrench specifications, the purchase year and maintenance cycle for every single tool. Everything was neat and tidy, precise to a fault. Looking at these records used to give me a sense of peace—as if I had mastered something.

But today, I’m wondering: what did these tools actually teach me?

It wasn't how to use them. That’s too simple. It was... how to approach a task. With seriousness. Without compromise. Knowing that every detail matters. For forty years, the products I made with these tools had a defect rate 25% lower than the industry average. That wasn't a coincidence. It was because I never allowed myself to slack off.

But—

I wrote that word on a new page, then crossed it out. Wrote it again. Crossed it out again.

But I treated people the same way. My subordinates, my family. I demanded perfection and left no room for error. I thought that was called being responsible. Now, I’m not so sure.

Li Meifang has been humming in the kitchen lately. I haven't heard her hum like that in a long time. She says it’s because I’ve finally "loosened up a bit." I can hear it in her voice—it wasn't a compliment. It was... relief? She feels relieved.

That hurts a little.

I turned to a blank page and drew a timeline with a pencil. From 1982 to now. Forty years. I marked the key years: technician, team leader, department manager. I did well at every stage. Defect rates, process optimizations, the proposals that were adopted. It’s a beautiful report card.

But so what?

Chen Hao is in Shenzhen, under a lot of pressure. I keep track of his mortgage and his performance, but we barely have anything to say to each other. When Chen Yu comes home occasionally, our conversations are like status reports—how’s work, is your marriage stable. I thought I was teaching them about perseverance and quality awareness, but I was never truly there for them.

This is my regret. Perhaps my greatest regret.

Next to the timeline, I wrote: "If I could do it over, what would I do?"

But I know there are no "ifs." I only have the now. And about two years left of work. Maybe in these two years, I should record some things. Not tool lists, not process documents. But... what I was thinking during these forty years. Why I did things the way I did. Young people might need that.

Or rather, I need to do this. To forgive myself.

Meifang is calling me for dinner. I hear her voice drifting up from downstairs—gentle, not rushing me. I close the notebook and stand up.

Maybe I should listen to her more. Really listen, instead of mentally calculating her blood pressure and diet.

Maybe that is what I really need to learn.

# Work Memo

**Date**: October 28, 2022  
**Subject**: Reflections on a New Approach to Feedback  
**Recorded by**: Chen Guilin  

---

## Record of Events

This morning, I discussed the process plan for a new product with a junior engineer from our department. While there were some lapses in rigor in his proposal, I refrained from my usual habit of pointing out the flaws directly.

**What I did differently**:
1. First, I acknowledged specific areas where he did well—the data collection was thorough, and the conceptual framework was clear.
2. I explained why precision control is critical at this stage, particularly regarding its impact on subsequent costs and yield.
3. Instead of providing the answer, I asked him: "Let’s look into this together—is there anything else we need to consider here?"
4. He identified the problem himself and even added a perspective I hadn't considered.

**Result**: His motivation was visibly boosted. He took the initiative to look up relevant materials after the meeting and returned this afternoon to discuss the improved plan.

---

## Reflections

This approach is a significant departure from my previous style. I used to be blunt about pointing out issues for the sake of efficiency. Looking back, however, that kind of feedback might lead people to passively accept criticism rather than truly think for themselves.

This time was different. He was involved in the thought process, which gave him more confidence in the final conclusion. More importantly, he didn't just learn the answer to this specific problem; he learned **how to analyze problems**.

---

## Follow-up Plans

If this method proves effective, I want to systematize it:

- Implement a new feedback framework within the department (Specific Praise → Explanation of Principles → Open Discussion).
- Compile a "Technical Mentorship Guide" in preparation for the knowledge hand-off before my retirement.
- Potentially organize a technical sharing session at the end of the year using this interactive format instead of a one-way lecture.

The benefit of this is that knowledge isn't just memorized—it is understood and internalized. Perhaps this is what true "legacy" looks like.

---

## Remarks

This idea stems from some recent reflections. My health is a reminder that I won't be in this position forever. Before I leave, can I ensure these young people inherit not just my standards, but my way of thinking? That might be more valuable than any procedural document.

I will continue to observe the results next week.

ranked_items.content:
Meifang, I’ve been thinking about something.

Just now in the workshop, I dug out those tools. Some I bought over twenty years ago; others I only recently organized. As I was wiping them down, it suddenly hit me—maybe what I’ve been doing all this time isn't about passing them on perfectly to someone else, but that there’s meaning in the process itself.

You told me before that I’d eventually have this realization. You saw it clearly. And now, I’m starting to see things clearly too.

I’m thinking that maybe, before I retire, I should talk to the younger ones about what I’ve learned over the years. Not to leave behind some kind of legacy, but just... to talk. To let them know why precision work is worth taking seriously.

I don’t know if this is the right idea. But I feel like I need to do something, while there’s still time.

What do you think?

ranked_items.content:
# Preparation Checklist for Technical Sharing Session

**Date**: October 8, 2022, 8:15 PM
**Location**: Office (Tool Storage Room)

---

## I. Basic Meeting Information

**Topic**: Precision Quality Management: 40 Years of Experience and Reflections

**Tentative Time**: Mid-November (Wednesday, 2:00 PM – 4:00 PM)

**Venue**: Conference Room B

**Attendees**:
- Core Department Engineers (8-10 people)
- Quality Control Team Leads (3 people)
- Interested Junior Technicians

**Speaker**: Myself

---

## II. Content Outline

### Part 1: The Evolution of Quality Management (20 mins)
- A 40-year retrospective: From entering the factory in 1982 to the present
- The transition from manual inspection to data-driven management
- Changes in industry standards and the company’s adaptation process
- Key turning point case studies (List 3-5 examples)

### Part 2: The Essence of Precision Work (25 mins)
- Why details determine success or failure
- The management logic behind a 25% reduction in defect rates
- Common quality blind spots and how to avoid them
- Balancing the three dimensions: Tools, Processes, and People

### Part 3: Advice for Young Engineers (15 mins)
- Critical choices in career development
- How to establish your own professional standards
- The relationship between stability and innovation
- Methodologies for accumulating experience

---

## III. Materials Preparation Checklist

- [ ] Organize improvement proposal documents from the past 12 years (approx. 40 files)
- [ ] Compile defect rate statistical curves (in chart format)
- [ ] Prepare 5-8 specific case analyses (Success stories + Lessons from failure)
- [ ] Collect early handwritten inspection records (if any still exist)
- [ ] Create a simple PPT (no more than 20 slides)
- [ ] Prepare physical displays of certain precision tools

---

## IV. Execution Steps

**Week 1 (Oct 8–14)**:
- Complete initial organization of materials
- Finalize the attendee list
- Report the session plan to management

**Week 2 (Oct 15–21)**:
- Complete PPT production
- Write the detailed script
- Conduct a rehearsal (timed)

**Week 3 (Oct 22–28)**:
- Final review
- Prepare physical exhibits
- Confirm conference room and equipment

**Week 4 (Oct 29 – Nov 4)**:
- Send formal invitations to participants
- Final preparations

---

## V. Personal Reflections

This is more than just a technical sharing session; it is a summary of 40 years of work. The younger generation needs to understand that stability is not stagnation, and precision is not rigidity. I want them to see that there is profound meaning in dedicating oneself deeply to a single craft.

Perhaps this is exactly what I should be doing right now.

---

**Note**: Add more details while continuing to organize tools over the weekend.

# Work Notebook

**Date: Wednesday, October 26, 2022**
**Location: Office**

---

## Thoughts on Technical Sharing

Had a brief chat with my supervisor this morning about my pre-retirement arrangements. He asked if I’d considered doing some training or a knowledge-sharing session for the younger engineers. I didn’t give him a direct answer at the time, but the idea has been on my mind ever since.

I used to feel this heavy obligation to "pass down" everything I’ve learned over the years in its entirety—as if that were the only way to prove these 40 years weren't for nothing. Looking back now, I think I was being too stubborn about it. Maybe that’s not what matters most.

**What I actually want to record:**

1. **On Quality Standards**
   - It’s not about pursuing perfection for perfection’s sake.
   - It’s about being accountable at every step and being responsible to the user.
   - The logic behind setting a standard is more important than the standard itself.
   - Example: Last year’s drop in defect rates wasn't due to new regulations, but because everyone finally understood the "why."

2. **On Process Improvement**
   - The goal of improvement isn't to make life difficult for people; it’s to make the work clearer.
   - Over the years, the proposals I made that actually worked were all rooted in real-world problems, not abstract imagination.
   - Listen to the voices on the front line; they know best where the bottlenecks are.

3. **On Working with People**
   - I need to be honest here: my approach has had its flaws.
   - Being blunt can be hurtful, and I know it has contributed to staff turnover.
   - Perhaps I should mention this in the session—not as self-criticism, but to tell the younger generation that being rigorous and being respectful are not mutually exclusive.
   - Where I fell short, they can do better.

4. **On Perseverance**
   - Spending 40 years in one place—some people say I lacked ambition.
   - But what I see is the value of deeply understanding an industry, a company, and a role.
   - Not all change is progress; sometimes, stability itself is a skill.

---

## Why I want to do this now

After that check-up with the doctor, things started to click. I can’t control aging, and I can’t ensure everything goes according to plan. What I can do, however, is record my reflections from all these years—not as a "legacy," but to clarify for myself what I’ve actually learned.

Meifang has been accompanying me on walks lately, and she mentioned I’ve become more talkative. I feel it too; I’m no longer in such a rush to prove anything.

If I do a session before I retire, this is what I want to talk about. It doesn't need to be formal—just a clear account of the experiences and the lessons. I want the younger ones to know there are different ways to do a job: you can be rigorous, but you can also be more human.

---

## To-Do

- Organize key improvement cases from the last 12 years (3-5 typical ones).
- Think about how to talk about the failed attempts.
- Confirm the specific timing and format of the session with my supervisor.
- Ask Hao and Yu what they think about me doing this.

To be honest, I felt a bit nervous when this idea first surfaced. Now that I’ve written it down, I actually feel a sense of relief.

ranked_items.content:
September 22, 2022, 8:30 PM

I’m sitting in my workshop, still holding that pair of pliers. I’ve wiped them over and over, but they never seem to get quite clean.

The doctor’s words are still circling in my head. 110, atherosclerosis, long-term medication. The numbers are clear. My whole life, I’ve been best at dealing with clear numbers, but this time, that clarity feels suffocating.

Meifang said something yesterday that I hadn’t been able to put into words: "I knew you’d have this sooner or later." She saw it coming. In the office, I can spot a flaw in the smallest detail, yet I couldn’t see my own body slowly breaking down. Irony.

I pulled out that notebook. Usually, I only record work logs, accounts, and process improvements. Today, for some reason, I started writing something else. I wrote a sentence, crossed it out. Wrote it again.

"If I could do it over, I would teach the younger ones more."

My hand shook a little as I wrote that.

Forty years. Forty years in this factory, from inspector to manager. Those precision tools, those processes, those improvement plans I puzzled out all by myself… suddenly I wonder, what happens to all of it if I collapse? Will my subordinates keep up my standards? Or will everything just fall apart?

But then I thought, maybe I’m wrong. Maybe the value of these things doesn’t depend on me handing them over personally. Maybe it’s already there—in the processes I improved, in the standards I demanded, in the things those young people learned even while they were resenting me.

I wrote another line in the notebook: "Maybe I should hold a technical sharing session before I retire."

As I finished the sentence, the tightness in my chest loosened a bit. Not entirely, but it was there.

It’s not about proving anything. It’s just… if I talk about these things, if I let people see *why* I do what I do instead of just how strict I am, then maybe… maybe there won’t be so much regret.

The doctor said I need to control my diet, get regular check-ups, and take my medicine. I can do that. Following a plan is what I do best.

But this time, there’s something new in the plan. Not just to live longer—though I hope for that, too. But to make these forty years actually mean something.

Meifang is calling me for dinner from downstairs. I didn’t answer right away. I looked again at those sentences in the notebook, the ones I’d revised over and over.

Maybe I’ll propose the idea to my supervisor tomorrow. Maybe I won’t. I don’t know yet.

But at least I wrote it down.

# Work Log

**Date**: Friday, October 28, 2022
**Weather**: Sunny
**Recorded by**: Chen Guilin

---

## Today's Work

Held a departmental feedback meeting at 10:00 AM. The approach today was different from usual, and it’s worth noting down.

Previously, I would point out problems directly and give corrective feedback immediately. Today, I changed my mindset. I started by praising several engineers for their recent performance—specifically Xiao Wang’s ideas on improving the welding process. Only then did I explain why certain parameters needed to be adjusted this way instead of that way. The key was that I invited them into the thought process rather than just handing them the answers.

I asked Xiao Li, "Why do you think this data is fluctuating?" He was a bit nervous at first, but then he slowly began to analyze it. In the end, he derived the conclusion himself. The result was much better than if I had just told him.

After the meeting, several young engineers came to me on their own initiative to ask questions. Their attitude was noticeably different. It wasn't the defensive stance you see after being criticized; they genuinely wanted to understand the principles.

## Reflections

This method takes more time. If I had followed my old efficiency standards, I could have finished in five minutes what took twenty today. But the outcome was different.

Looking back, I’ve spent all these years chasing "correctness" and "efficiency." Point out the problem, give the answer, and move on to the next. But perhaps the issue isn't the answer itself, but whether they truly understand the "why."

I’m thinking that maybe this is what I should leave behind for this team before I retire—not just a complete operating manual, but a way of thinking. A dedication to detail and an attitude of not being afraid to ask why.

I used to think these young people weren't serious enough, but looking at it now, perhaps it was my approach that gave them no opportunity to truly think.

I’ll continue to observe the effects tomorrow. I might try this approach again during next week’s process audit meeting.

---

**Notes**: The defect rate remained at 0.8% this week, continuing to stay below the industry average.

ranked_items.content:
July 22, 2022, 9:15 AM

I haven’t been able to focus all morning.

Just now in the office, because of a formatting issue in a quality inspection report—the header was off by a mere half-centimeter—I tore into Xiao Wang in front of the entire department. I said some terrible things, things like "I wonder if all these years of management have meant anything at all." His face went white after I finished, and he took the afternoon off.

Now, sitting in my office, I’m wondering: why did I do that? It was just a formatting error. He’s a diligent employee; a small thing like that could have easily been corrected in private. But I didn’t. I said those things in public.

I know what I’m doing. I’m using severity to prove I’m still relevant. Because I’m afraid—afraid that I’m becoming inconsequential. Forty years. I’ve been in this position for forty years. I’ve cut defect rates by 25%, improved processes time and again, but has any of it truly changed anything? Or have I just been repeating the same motions, over and over, just to prove I exist?

The recent medical checkup has made me even more uneasy. Those numbers, the fluctuations in those indicators—I look at them every day. I record them. I control them. But I can’t control anything. I can’t control time, I can’t control my body, and I can’t control aging. So I’ve transferred this sense of powerlessness onto my work, onto Xiao Wang.

My wife asked me last night why I was so quiet. I didn’t answer. How could I explain? I’m afraid. I’m afraid that in two years, when I retire and this department is gone, when these data points and standards are gone, I really will be nothing.

Xiao Wang is probably at home right now because of something I said. He might be wondering if he should even keep working here. I caused this.

I need to talk to him tomorrow. But I don’t know what to say. Apologize? That’s a hard word for me to say. Explain? Explain what? My anxiety? My fear?

The health log is still sitting on my desk. I ran ten minutes longer than usual today, thinking that maybe this way I could keep things under control. But halfway through the run, I suddenly realized it was all futile. I’m using running to fight aging, severity to fight powerlessness, and data to fight death.

It’s absurd.

But I don’t know what else to do.

July 10, 2022, 10:47 PM

I pulled out this unused notebook, though I’m not sure why. Maybe it’s because what happened yesterday is still spinning in my head.

I lost my temper yesterday over a spreadsheet formatting issue. Xiao Wang had reversed the order of the data columns, and I pointed it out in front of everyone, using some pretty harsh words. His face went pale instantly. I saw it, but I didn’t stop. Instead, I kept going on and on about how "details determine product quality, and product quality determines the company's survival."

Thinking back now, what was I so angry about? A formatting error. It was that simple.

But it wasn’t, really.

I’ve noticed myself setting stricter standards lately. Stricter inspection processes. Stricter requirements. As if doing this could... could what? I can’t quite put my finger on it. Maybe I’m trying to prove something. Maybe I’m trying to control something.

Li Meifang asked me what was wrong last night, but I didn't answer. She’s long since used to it.

I’ve gone over my May medical report three times now. The doctor said there’s nothing majorly wrong, just that I need to "pay attention." Pay attention to what? Pay attention to time passing. Pay attention to my body changing. Pay attention to how many more years I can keep doing this job.

I reorganized the tools in the workshop again recently. I’m recording the purpose of each one, one by one. Chen Hao won’t use them. Neither will Chen Yu. They have their own lives. I know this, yet I’m still taking notes. Why? While I can still move?

How many years of movement do I have left?

I’m supposed to play chess with my friends next week. I don’t know what to say to them. Lately, when I’m with them, I’ve been pathetically quiet. I was always a man of few words, but now I feel like I can’t even focus on the game. My mind is always elsewhere.

I’m trying to fight something off by exerting more control. I know this. But I can’t stop.

Xiao Wang didn't look at me today. I should say something to him, but I don't know what.

Maybe I’ll delete these words tomorrow. There’s no need to leave this behind.

But for now, I’ll let them stay here.

ranked_items.content:
April 5, 2022. Night.

I’ve been sitting in the office for a long time. Wang Gang’s quality inspection report is still spinning in my head.

The formatting was off again when he handed it in this morning—the classification standards in the header didn't match last year’s specifications. It’s such a basic thing to get wrong. I couldn't help myself; I snapped at him. He tried to explain, but I cut him off. No excuses. Professionalism is about an obsession with detail.

His face changed the moment I said it.

Looking back now, my tone was harsh. Harsher than usual. He’s actually a decent employee. In the past, I would have pointed out these small mistakes, but never like that. Why today...?

My fingers have been tapping on the desk for a long while. Rapidly. I didn't even realize I was doing it.

What am I thinking? Was his report really that outrageous? Or... was I just taking it out on him?

It’s been like this lately. I’m becoming more demanding with my subordinates and more micro-managing with my wife. This morning, I was looking at her blood pressure logs again; a single high reading and I started feeling restless. I spent another whole night in the tool shed, numbering all the precision tools and documenting their uses. I’m terrified that one day I’ll suddenly... lose the ability to move, and these things will become completely useless because no one knows how to handle them.

Two years until retirement. Two years.

I know what I’m trying to do. I’m trying to control things. Control the work, the standards, everything at home—even the aging of my own body. It’s as if by managing every tiny detail, I can prove I’m still useful, that I can still... what?

Wang Gang has to come in for work tomorrow. I need to figure out how to handle this. Not for his sake, but because... I don’t want to become this kind of person. I don’t want to use harshness to mask my own fear.

But I don’t know how to change.

How much longer can I do this? How much longer can I keep things under control like this?

ranked_items.content:
Wife, I’ve been sitting here in my workspace, thinking.

I really took what you said at dinner to heart. Maybe over these years, I truly have been too... I don’t know how to put it. Too obsessed with those numbers and standards, and in the process, I lost sight of the things that actually matter.

I’m also processing what the doctor said. I know I can’t go on like this. Tomorrow, I’ll go get the prescription as he directed, and I’ll follow your lead regarding my diet. I won’t... argue with you like I used to.

And about work. Perhaps I have been too harsh on my subordinates. You’re right—being serious doesn’t have to mean being cold. I’ll try to see if I can improve.

Thank you for not saying "I told you so" tonight. You saw this coming all along, didn't you?

My head is still a bit of a mess, but I’m slowly figuring things out.

—Chen Guilin

**Date**: 2022.09.10  
**Weather**: Sunny  
**Status**: Exhausted  

---

I spent two hours longer in the office today than expected. The 3:00 PM quality control meeting was pushed back to 5:00 PM, which threw off my planned workflow audit. However, I did manage to finish the third-quarter defect rate statistics; the figures are 0.3 percentage points better than last month.

Back in the workshop tonight, I inspected that new set of precision measuring tools from Japan. They are in excellent condition, with all accuracy levels within standard ranges. I spent over an hour cleaning them and applying anti-rust treatment. If handled properly, these tools will last for many years.

Sitting in the workshop, a question suddenly crossed my mind—all these things I’ve accumulated over the years, these experiences and methods, is anyone actually using them? The younger people in the department seem to prefer blindly following the software system’s suggestions rather than understanding the principles behind them. How many people have actually read the process documentation I wrote?

I’m retiring in two years. I’ve thought about this plan for a long time and felt it was the right move. But today, I’m suddenly feeling a bit uncertain—how much work is still left unfinished? The things I wanted to pass on to others haven't truly been handed over yet.

Tomorrow, I’ll continue pushing the new inspection process improvement plan. I’ve been brewing this proposal for three months, and the data model is essentially finalized. If it gets approved, it should reduce the defect rate by another 2% or so.

I also need to remind my subordinates that the end-of-month equipment maintenance cannot be delayed again. Some people always want to procrastinate as much as possible, but it’s exactly in that kind of slackness where the details go wrong.

# Memo: Performance Improvement Plan

**Date: July 22, 2022**  
**Time: 09:15**

---

## Review of Events

I overreacted to the Quality Control team’s feedback yesterday. Master Wang is on leave, and the atmosphere in the department is noticeably off. I can feel it. This is not the outcome I wanted.

Maintaining a low defect rate cannot be achieved this way.

---

## Immediate Action Items

**1. One-on-one with Master Wang**
- Timing: This afternoon or tomorrow morning.
- Content: Listen to his perspective; explain that my intentions were not a personal attack.
- Tone: Stay calm; do not repeat yesterday’s phrasing.
- Documentation: Note down key points to avoid future misunderstandings.

**2. Review Feedback Standards**
- Current Issue: My feedback criteria are too subjective and easily influenced by my mood.
- Improvement: Establish a clearer, quantified scoring sheet.
  - Specific error types → Corresponding feedback levels.
  - Different levels → Different communication styles.
  - This ensures subordinates see the standards and I maintain consistency.
- Deadline: By next Monday.

**3. Department Meeting**
- Convene the whole team to explain the new feedback mechanism.
- Emphasize: Strictness is for product quality, not directed at any individual.
- Listen to their thoughts (even though I’m not great at this).

---

## Personal Level

The pressure is too high. I’ve been wound too tight lately.

- Increase exercise: Currently 6,000 steps a day; increase to 8,000.
- Check recent physical exam records.
- Don’t bring work anxiety home; my wife already has blood pressure issues.

---

## Reflection

I know where my problem lies. My obsession with detail is a strength, but over-control turns it into a weakness. Subordinates are not machines.

I must learn from this. It cannot happen again.

---

**Next Step: Talk to Master Wang by 3:00 PM today.**

# Work Log

**Date: Wednesday, October 26, 2022**
**Weather: Sunny**

---

This morning, I had a briefing with my supervisor to discuss the department's recent process optimization plans. As the meeting was drawing to a close, I brought up a new idea: I plan to host a "40 Years of Quality Management Experience" sharing session before I retire.

My supervisor listened intently. I laid out the concept clearly:

**Theme**: "From Frontline Inspector to Manager—Forty Years of Quality Management in Practice"

**Target Audience**: Mid-level management, key personnel from the Quality Control department, and interested technical staff.

**Core Modules**:
- The evolution and optimization of quality inspection processes
- Identification and solutions for common quality issues
- Balancing standardization and the human touch in team management
- Adaptation and innovation amidst industry shifts

He asked why I was proposing this now. I thought about it and told him the truth: "It’s not just about passing on knowledge. Everything I’ve learned over the years—if it just stays in my head, it’ll vanish once I retire. I want to document these things while I’m still on the job. It’s also a way for me to summarize and reflect on my own forty-year career."

He nodded and said it was a great idea, then asked me to put together an initial outline.

After returning to the office this afternoon, I jotted down a preliminary to-do list in my logbook:

- [ ] Compile department improvement cases from the past 12 years (data, results, lessons learned)
- [ ] Reflect on experiences from my time as a frontline inspector (comparisons with the present)
- [ ] Prepare detailed analyses for 3–5 typical case studies
- [ ] Reach out to potential participants (key subordinates, heads of other departments)
- [ ] Finalize the time and venue for the session
- [ ] Create the presentation outline and supporting materials

To be honest, I’ve been feeling anxious lately about the question, "What will I leave behind?" Now, I feel a bit more relaxed. Not everything has to be "passed down" to others. What matters more is that through this process, I can see clearly what I’ve done and what I’ve learned.

I mentioned this to Meifang during dinner. She was very supportive and even asked if I needed help organizing the materials. I said yes.

My physical condition has been okay these past two days after taking my medication. No particular discomfort. I’ll proceed with the scheduled medical checkup tomorrow.

---

**Notes**:
- Communication with my supervisor went smoothly; no resistance encountered.
- This plan gives me a clearer sense of purpose, replacing vague anxiety with a concrete goal.
- Need to complete the first draft of the outline by mid-November.

# Management Reflection Notes

**Date**: October 5, 2022, Afternoon
**Subject**: Findings from communication with the Quality Control (QC) team

---

While reviewing the Q3 defect rate data this afternoon, I asked Xiao Wang a question: "Do you think there’s another way to interpret this?"

The moment the question left my mouth, I even surprised myself.

Usually, I don’t ask things like that. My habit is to point out the problem, provide the solution, and demand execution. It’s the most efficient way. But for some reason today, instead of directly dismissing his line of reasoning, I felt like hearing what he had to say.

As it turned out, he offered a perspective I hadn't considered—that the fluctuations in Production Line B might be related to raw material batches, rather than just operational protocols. My previous analysis had completely overlooked this.

**Questions for Reflection:**
- Have I been managing the team with a "one right answer" mindset?
- Do diverse viewpoints actually improve decision quality, or do they just hinder efficiency?
- Are my subordinates withholding valuable ideas because they are afraid to contradict me?

**Potential Adjustments:**
1. Actively invite the team to offer differing perspectives before making key decisions.
2. Distinguish between "execution standards" and "problem analysis"—standards must be rigid, but the analytical process can be more open.
3. Provide explicit psychological safety to those who voice dissent (ensure they won't be dismissed just for having a different opinion).

**To Be Verified:**
- Will this approach impact the department's execution efficiency?
- Is there a need to establish "protocols for offering feedback" to prevent it from becoming a mere formality?
- Can improvements be observed in other similar scenarios?

---

*Note: This is not about relaxing quality requirements. Standards cannot be lowered. But perhaps I need to rethink—can rigorous results and an open process truly coexist?*

# Office Memo

**April 5, 2022, 2:20 PM**

---

My fingers are tapping the desk again. I can’t stop.

I was too harsh with Wang Gang just now. His suggestion regarding the inspection process was actually fine; why did I have to insist on pointing out his lack of precision? His expression changed—I saw it—but I didn't apologize. I just kept talking about my standards.

Why is it always like this?

During the afternoon meeting, I lowered the third-quarter defect rate target again. From 0.8 to 0.6. No one dared to object, but I saw Li Qiang’s pen tear through the paper while he was taking notes. He’s afraid. Someone else in my department will quit. I know it.

But I can’t control myself.

The physical exam report is still in the drawer. Blood pressure 139/92. The doctor said I need to be careful. I’m monitoring Li Meifang’s salt intake, yet I—never mind.

I wiped down the tools in the workshop again today. Screwdrivers, calipers, micrometers. Every single one is recorded in the logbook. Use, specifications, year of purchase. Chen Hao called to say his mortgage went up again; I listened to him finish and then just said, "Cut back on your spending." He didn't say anything else. It occurred to me that he might have wanted my help, but I didn't ask.

What use are these tools, anyway?

Two years until retirement. What exactly am I planning for?

Li Meifang said she wants braised pork for dinner. I’m already thinking about how to convince her to eat something lighter. She’ll listen to me, as she always does. But the way she looks at me—I’ve noticed it—she’s avoiding my eyes.

When did I become this kind of person?

Fingers still tapping. Tap. Tap. Tap.

I need to write this down. While I still can.

---

*Check tomorrow’s quality inspection standards before leaving. Might need to adjust them one more time.*

ranked_items.content:
# Wednesday, October 5, 2022, 14:20

Something slightly strange happened in the office this afternoon.

Xiao Wang made that same old mistake again—a discrepancy in the inspection report data entry. While it wasn't a fundamental error, by my standards, this kind of low-level oversight shouldn't happen. Sitting across from him, looking at that report, I already had my "straight-talking" critique ready in my head. But as the words reached the tip of my tongue, I stopped.

I asked him, "Do you think there’s another way to interpret this?"

He froze for a second, then started explaining his thought process. I waited until he finished before pointing out the issue. In the end, what I said was, "Let’s look at how we can improve this process together," instead of "This is unacceptable."

The look on his face visibly relaxed. I could tell.

What was I thinking? This isn't like me. I’ve always believed that strict standards drive better results—our defect rate being 25% lower than the industry average is proof of that. But today... it felt like I was conducting some kind of experiment. Seeing if gentle guidance could yield the same outcome.

Or rather, what am I trying to prove?

Before leaving work, I put together a "Quality Management Methodology Optimization Plan," documenting my observations on this change in feedback style. I used data to explain why this approach might be more efficient—reducing employee defensiveness and improving information retention, thereby lowering the rate of recurring errors. Writing it down that way made it feel more rational.

But I know that’s not the whole reason.

Lately, I feel like time is accelerating. Those numbers from my physical exam report are still spinning in my head. I find myself wondering: if one day I can no longer work the way I do now, how will people remember me? As a harsh quality control manager? Or... something else?

The thought is a bit absurd. I need to stay focused on management optimization, not these nebulous ideas.

I’ll continue to observe Xiao Wang’s performance tomorrow. Let’s see if this "experiment" actually works.
