October 12, 2022, 7:30 PM

Had a phone call with Chen Hao.

In the past, I’d always ask him if the mortgage was a burden, how the orders were coming along, or if his job was stable. I don’t know what came over me today, but the first thing out of my mouth was, "How have you been lately? Is there anything I can help with?" He seemed taken aback; there was a two-second silence before he answered.

He said work is exhausting, that sales is high-pressure, and sometimes he wonders if he’s doing the right thing. He’s lost. He was very candid, and I could hear the fatigue in his voice.

Before, I would have jumped straight in with advice, telling him exactly what he should do. But this time, I just said, "I understand. I’ve been through a stage like that, too."

Even I was a bit surprised by my own words. In forty years, I’ve never spoken to him like that.

He asked me when that was. I told him that when I was his age, I wasn't sure if I was making the right choices either. Back then, I was a quality inspector at the grassroots level, doing the same repetitive work every day, unable to see a way out. What I didn’t tell him was that I just kept going down that same path ever since, never changing course again.

He didn’t respond immediately, but I could sense something had shifted. It wasn't that superior-subordinate dynamic anymore; it felt more like... two people who had walked the same road.

After hanging up, I sat at my desk and looked at that medical report. The doctor said I’ll need long-term medication and a lifestyle change. I’ve been thinking about how to reverse this outcome through stricter management, just like I do at work—precise control, eliminating variables.

But while talking to Chen Hao, it suddenly occurred to me that maybe some things can’t be controlled. Some paths, once walked, are simply walked.

I need to rethink how I interact with them. Not supervising, not guiding. Maybe just... being there?

The thought makes me a little uneasy. I don't know if I still know how to do it.

More tomorrow.

ranked_items.content:
# August 15, 2022, 3:20 AM

Can’t sleep. I’ve been awake for two hours now.

My chest still feels a bit tight; I can feel my heartbeat when I press down on it. My rational mind tells me this is anxiety, not a heart condition. But my fingers still instinctively press down several times, trying to be sure. My breathing is a bit shallow. I tell myself to take deep breaths—one, two, three, exhale—but the more I try to control it, the more labored it feels. It’s ridiculous.

I turned off my phone. Meifang knocked on the door and asked if I wanted some warm water. I didn't respond. She didn't come in eventually; I heard her footsteps slowly fading away. She’s used to me being like this. After 35 years, she knows all too well that I don’t like to be disturbed, especially at times like this. But I could sense her lingering outside the door for a few seconds. I feel a twinge of guilt, but I don't have the energy to deal with that right now.

I checked the Excel sheet again this afternoon. Meifang’s blood pressure readings for this week: 150/95, 148/92, 152/98… I marked three of them in red pen. I wrote "follow-up appointment needed" next to them, and then "dietary control." But I know these numbers don’t change anything. I can record them, I can mark them, I can make plans, but I cannot change the fact that her body is aging.

Last night, I searched for information on hypertension complications again. Stroke, myocardial infarction, kidney failure… page after page. I told myself to stop, that searching like this is pointless. But my fingers kept clicking. It’s as if something is driving me to confirm the worst-case scenarios, as if knowing in advance would somehow make me prepared. It’s stupid. I know perfectly well it’s stupid.

My own data sheets are there, too. Daily exercise time, step count, heart rate, blood sugar levels… all neatly arranged. It’s as if as long as these numbers stay in the green zone, I can guarantee that nothing will go wrong with me. But I know I’m lying to myself. I can’t stop aging by keeping records. I can’t guarantee my heart won’t fail through a strict diet. This realization terrifies me more than any abnormal data point ever could.

The physical exam is in two weeks. I’m both looking forward to it and dreading it. I’m looking for a definitive answer, yet I’m afraid that answer will confirm all the premonitions I’ve had over the past few months.

The tools in the workshop haven't been wiped down yet. I was supposed to organize those precision instruments today, but I just couldn't find the motivation. I sat there, looking at them, thinking that I might not have much time left. Time seems to be accelerating. Last year, retirement still felt far off; now, it feels like two years have passed in the blink of an eye.

No good news from the kids either. Hao is still under a lot of pressure with his mortgage, and Yu hasn't said much lately. I want to give them a call, but I don't know what to say. Ask them how their health is? That would be too strange. Or just listen to them talk about work in silence? There doesn't seem to be any other way for us to communicate anymore.

It’s not dawn yet. The study is very quiet. It’s just me and these data points, these records—these things I try to use to fight against this sense of powerlessness.

I think I need to sleep. But I know I won’t be able to.

# Monday, July 18, 2022, 14:30

It’s been two hours since I got the report.

I’m sitting in my office with the report spread out on the desk. I told everyone "it’s nothing serious," but my fingers keep tracing those marked spots—blood pressure 139/92, fasting blood glucose 6.8. The numbers themselves don’t lie. I know that better than anyone.

I heard everything the doctor said, but I chose to remember only the first half of his sentences. "It’s under decent control." The rest—the parts about "needing to be more cautious" and "regular follow-ups"—I just let slide. That’s not my style.

Meifang was cooking when I got home. She saw the look on my face and asked about it. I didn't give her a straight answer, just said "I'm fine." But I know she can tell. After 35 years, she knows me too well. She tried to come closer, wanting me to talk, but I turned away and went into the study.

Then I suddenly turned back around and said to her, "Starting today, you need to cooperate too. We can't let your blood pressure issues drag on anymore. I’m going to set up a plan for you."

She was startled by my tone. I saw it. But I didn't stop. I need to control something. I need to ensure that something is within my grasp.

She isn't the problem. I know that. But at this moment, I can't face the fact that *I* am the problem.

Data doesn't lie. I’ve lived my whole life by data and standards, and now the data is starting to betray me. Blood pressure, blood sugar, weight—every indicator is slowly crossing the line. I can see the trend. I can predict it. And that is exactly what’s so terrifying.

The two-year retirement plan. I spoke of it with such certainty. But now I’m starting to wonder: if my body continues like this, how many "two years" do I have left?

The tools in the workshop are still there. I wanted to go and wipe them down, as I usually do. But today, I didn't move. I sat in my office, staring at this report, trying to use logic to convince myself that this is all within manageable limits.

But I know that some things are forever beyond my control.

For the first time, I truly felt that.

ranked_items.content:
July 5, 2022, 6:45 PM

Had a call with Hao. He said the real estate market has been a bit cold lately, but he’s holding on. I asked him if he’d ever thought about learning something about mechanics—maybe it could be a hobby later on. He laughed and said, "Dad, what are you talking about? I don’t have the brain for that." I didn't push it further.

After hanging up, I sat in the workshop for a long time.

Actually, that wasn't what I wanted to ask. What I wanted to ask was—has he ever thought about what will happen to all these tools, all these things I’ve spent forty years learning? But I couldn't get the words out. Just like I never can.

During our call this afternoon, I told him "everything is under control." Thinking back on that phrase now, it feels a bit ridiculous. What does "under control" even mean? What am I controlling? Hao is in Shenzhen worrying about his mortgage, Yu is busy with work in Guangzhou, and here I am, documenting the uses of these tools as if I’m in a race against time. But time isn't racing me—it doesn't care about me at all.

Li Meifang is downstairs watching TV. I can hear her coughing. It’s time to nag her about going to the hospital to check her blood pressure again. These are things I can control. But what’s the use of controlling them?

The tool records in my notebook are halfway finished. With every entry I write, I wonder—who is going to use this? Hao? No. Yu? Even less likely. So why am I writing all this?

Maybe it’s just to keep myself from being idle. To tell myself there’s still something left to do.

The physical exam report from May is still in the drawer. A few of the indicators fluctuated a bit. The doctor said it’s nothing serious, just needs regular follow-ups. But I keep taking it out, looking at those numbers over and over. As if I could find some hidden meaning in them.

I’ve never known how to talk to them. That’s the truth. It’s been that way since they were little. I thought it would get better as they grew up, but it seems to have gotten worse. Now, I even self-censor before asking a simple question, terrified of saying something that might cause awkwardness.

Maybe I should have started teaching them earlier. But back then, I was busy with work. Now that I want to teach, it’s already too late.

Tomorrow, I’ll continue documenting the tools. There are still over thirty items left to record.

I should do it while I can still move.

ranked_items.content:
# June 15, 2023, 6:45 PM

Sitting in the car, waiting for the light to change. My phone is on the passenger seat; all three messages have been sent.

I don’t know why I sent them. The entire afternoon was a blur of farewells—the handshakes, the photos, the looks in those young engineers' eyes, and that intern with her eyes red from crying. It all just sat heavy in my chest. It wasn't until I started driving that it hit me: I’ve never spoken to them like that before.

To Chen Hao, I wrote: "I haven't been there for you enough over the years because I was too busy with work. I hope we can talk more in the days ahead. Don't stress too much about the mortgage; we'll figure it out together."

To Chen Yu: "You are educating students, and I am manufacturing products; we are both pursuing excellence. I am proud of you. It’s my regret that I haven't truly listened to you talk about your work all these years."

And to Li Meifang: "Thank you for being by my side all this time. I know I’m not good with words, but you’ve always understood. From now on, I want to spend more time with you."

The moment I hit send, I regretted it. It felt a bit overly sentimental. But I didn't delete them.

Forty years. From that green kid fresh out of college to the man sitting here now. I always believed my value lay in the data—defect rates, process improvements, every perfect standard. This afternoon, a young engineer told me he decided to enter this industry because he read my paper, *Deviation Control in Precision Machining*. I didn't know how to react at the time.

Maybe... maybe what I’m leaving behind is more than just those things.

I don't know what to think right now. The light is still red. The phone hasn't budged. This feeling of waiting is unfamiliar—it’s not waiting for a work result, but waiting for... what? Their response? Or waiting for myself to adapt to this new identity?

I don't have to wake up at 5:30 tomorrow. That thought should make me happy, but right now, I mostly feel a sense of blankness. Like a precision part suddenly removed from a machine, unsure of what use it has left.

But... maybe that’s not a bad thing.

Maybe this is the beginning.

ranked_items.content:
July 10, 2023, 8:30 PM

Hao called while I was organizing my precision tools. My hands were still stained with engine oil, but I picked up.

He said there’s a new opportunity in Shenzhen—a sales director position for a major project. It sounds promising, but his voice was thick with hesitation. I could hear it.

In the past, I would have told him exactly what to do. I’d list the pros and cons and hand down a judgment. But today, I didn’t. I asked him: "Are you excited about this opportunity, or are you scared?"

There was a long silence. Then he said, "Dad, I’m both. I’m scared I won’t do a good job, and I’m scared of the mortgage pressure getting worse."

Listening to him, it suddenly hit me—I’ve always been supervising him, not accompanying him. Just like how I treat my subordinates at the department. I thought that was being responsible, but it was really just control.

I told him: "Then give it a try. I’ll be right behind you."

I surprised even myself when I said it. It’s not my style. But it was true. I really want to stand behind him, rather than standing in front of him telling him which way to go.

His tone changed. He sounded a bit more relaxed. We talked for a while longer; he shared some project details. I didn’t rush to judge; I just listened, occasionally asking a question.

Before hanging up, I told him: "Hao, I’m proud of you."

The moment the words left my mouth, I felt a pang of regret—I’ve said things like that far too rarely. But he said, "Thanks, Dad," and there was warmth in his voice.

After putting the phone down, I sat there for a long time. I was still holding that pair of pliers my father left me.

I think maybe retirement isn’t about losing anything, but finally having the time to do the things that truly matter. Not the achievements that can be quantified, but—being there. Listening. Saying the things I’ve kept bottled up inside.

My tools, my process improvements, my standards for perfect quality... those are all important. But Hao doesn’t need a perfect father; he needs a real one. A father willing to admit he isn’t omnipotent.

I’ll keep organizing the tools tomorrow. This time, it’s not just for the donation, but to remember—some things are more important than precision.

ranked_items.content:
# Work Log

**Date:** Monday, September 19, 2022
**Weather:** Sunny
**State of Mind:** Average

---

## Morning Work Record

**09:00-09:45 Department Meeting**

Called a meeting with the Quality Control team to discuss issues in last week's data report. In the weekly report submitted by Xiao Wang, the defect rate data showed a discrepancy of about 3.2% compared to the actual sampling records.

Following my usual practice, I should have pointed out directly that this was "unacceptable," but today I changed my wording. I said to Xiao Wang, "Let's look at this deviation together and see which part of the process went wrong."

His tense expression visibly softened. We went through the inspection sheets together and found it was a classification error during sample statistics—he had mixed up the count of reworked parts with those that failed the initial inspection. Once the problem was identified, he took the initiative to propose an improvement plan.

The whole process was smoother than usual. My subordinate's level of cooperation improved, and there wasn't that sense of defensiveness that usually follows a reprimand.

**Note:** This approach seems more effective. Although the result is the same, the process is different. Need to continue observing the effects.

---

**10:15-11:30 Revision of Process Documents**

Continued refining the Quality Inspection SOP for the fourth quarter. Added new data verification steps, which are expected to reduce manual entry errors.

Organized the revision comments and prepared to submit them for supervisor approval this Wednesday.

---

## Afternoon Plan

- 13:30 Coordinate production line adjustments with the Production Department
- 14:30 On-site spot check visit
- 16:00 Organize the draft for this week's improvement suggestions

---

## Other

No chess game tonight. Might organize the tool inventory in the workshop over the weekend; some precision measuring tools need calibration.

---

**Log Completion Time:** 11:50

ranked_items.content:
May 22, 2023, 10:47 PM

Sitting in the study, my journal is still open on the desk. It’s a habit now, writing every day. But tonight, the words are coming slowly.

The farewell party ended this afternoon. I stood there, looking at all those young faces. When they applauded, I didn’t feel much; it just felt a bit surreal—forty years, just like that, over.

But that intern’s tears... I’m still thinking about that. She said, "I’ve never heard a talk like that before." I don’t know if I said anything particularly special. I just spoke about some details from work, about how to handle a difficult quality issue. I also talked about... about how angry I was back then, and how I criticized a subordinate in the harshest way possible.

I paused for a long time before saying, "Maybe there was a better way."

It felt strange saying those words. Like I was admitting to something. In forty years, I’ve never said anything like that in front of a crowd. I always felt that admitting to imperfection was the same as admitting to failure. But today... maybe it’s not.

After the session, those young engineers came up to discuss technical details. They asked a lot of questions. I answered them. There wasn't that tension of hierarchy; it was just... discussing a problem. A real problem.

I’m thinking now that knowledge doesn’t necessarily have to be passed down in a specific way. It doesn’t have to be my son inheriting my craft, or forcing someone to learn something. Maybe it just happens naturally—I speak, they listen, they ask, I answer. Just like that.

Tomorrow, I leave that office for good. I’ve sat in that chair for twelve years. The folders, pens, tools on the desk... they all need to be organized and left behind. I wonder, what am I leaving behind?

Not objects. Something... else.

Maybe it’s just those stories. The ones I thought were nothing special.

My wife asked me how I felt today. I said I was fine. But honestly, I don’t know how to put it into words. Relief? Unease? A bit of both. There’s no work schedule for tomorrow. How am I going to arrange my days? I’ve thought about this for a long time, but I still don’t have an answer.

Maybe I need to stop chasing the perfect plan. Maybe... maybe I should just let things happen naturally.

Like the tears of those young people.

I’m still getting used to the idea.

I’ll think about it tomorrow.

ranked_items.content:
# May 11, Sunny

The physical exam report came back.

Blood pressure: 138/88. Higher than the 133/85 back in 2020. I did the math—systolic pressure is up by 3.7%. I wrote the numbers down on paper and calculated it again. It is indeed 3.7%.

Meifang has been taking blood pressure medication lately, the kind I bought for her. She says she doesn't feel much, just gets dizzy sometimes. I didn't say anything, but I made a mental note—I must urge her to go for another checkup next month. Her blood pressure is higher than mine.

Sitting in the office, I organized the last five years of my physical exam data into a table. 2018, 2020, 2022. The numbers lined up in a row on the paper. Looking at them, a strange question crossed my mind—why am I documenting my own aging with such precision?

During the meeting this afternoon, I proposed a process improvement plan. The new quality inspector hadn't been thorough enough, so I pointed it out in front of everyone. He turned red. I knew he was uncomfortable, but standards are standards; you can't lower requirements just to avoid hurting feelings. That has always been my principle.

But sitting in my office later, it suddenly occurred to me—how much longer can I keep demanding this level of rigor from others?

I wiped down the tools in the workshop once more. That vernier caliper I bought in 1985 is still as accurate as ever. In my notebook, I wrote down its purpose, precision, and range of application. I’ve already documented over thirty items. Hao is busy selling real estate in Shenzhen, and Yu is teaching in Guangzhou. Neither of them will ever use these things.

I asked myself—why keep recording?

But I kept recording anyway. I poked a small hole in the corner of the notebook with the tip of my pen. Not with force, just a gentle poke. I did it several times.

Li Meifang asked me what I wanted for dinner. I said "whatever." She said we’d have something light then. I nodded.

Two years until retirement. The plan hasn't changed. But I feel the plan becoming stricter—every item must be more detailed, more precise, more intolerant of error. It’s as if I’m using more control to fight against something. But it doesn't change anything.

Time is still slipping away.

3.7%.

That’s all.

ranked_items.content:
September 22, 2022, 8:30 PM

I’m sitting in my workshop, still holding that pair of pliers. I’ve wiped them over and over, but they never seem to get quite clean.

The doctor’s words are still circling in my head. 110, atherosclerosis, long-term medication. The numbers are clear. My whole life, I’ve been best at dealing with clear numbers, but this time, that clarity feels suffocating.

Meifang said something yesterday that I hadn’t been able to put into words: "I knew you’d have this sooner or later." She saw it coming. In the office, I can spot a flaw in the smallest detail, yet I couldn’t see my own body slowly breaking down. Irony.

I pulled out that notebook. Usually, I only record work logs, accounts, and process improvements. Today, for some reason, I started writing something else. I wrote a sentence, crossed it out. Wrote it again.

"If I could do it over, I would teach the younger ones more."

My hand shook a little as I wrote that.

Forty years. Forty years in this factory, from inspector to manager. Those precision tools, those processes, those improvement plans I puzzled out all by myself… suddenly I wonder, what happens to all of it if I collapse? Will my subordinates keep up my standards? Or will everything just fall apart?

But then I thought, maybe I’m wrong. Maybe the value of these things doesn’t depend on me handing them over personally. Maybe it’s already there—in the processes I improved, in the standards I demanded, in the things those young people learned even while they were resenting me.

I wrote another line in the notebook: "Maybe I should hold a technical sharing session before I retire."

As I finished the sentence, the tightness in my chest loosened a bit. Not entirely, but it was there.

It’s not about proving anything. It’s just… if I talk about these things, if I let people see *why* I do what I do instead of just how strict I am, then maybe… maybe there won’t be so much regret.

The doctor said I need to control my diet, get regular check-ups, and take my medicine. I can do that. Following a plan is what I do best.

But this time, there’s something new in the plan. Not just to live longer—though I hope for that, too. But to make these forty years actually mean something.

Meifang is calling me for dinner from downstairs. I didn’t answer right away. I looked again at those sentences in the notebook, the ones I’d revised over and over.

Maybe I’ll propose the idea to my supervisor tomorrow. Maybe I won’t. I don’t know yet.

But at least I wrote it down.
ranked_items.subquestion: You seem to have a habit of "digitizing" every detail of your life whenever you worry about your health—like precisely calculating your salt intake or tracking every uptick in your blood pressure. Does this kind of numerical management actually give you peace of mind?

ranked_items.content:
September 10, 2022, 9:45 PM

I’ve been in the workshop for nearly three hours. Li Meifang knocked twice; I didn’t answer.

The morning’s medical report is still spinning in my head. Blood sugar at 110 mg/dL, Stage 2 hypertension, and that phrase—"pre-metabolic syndrome." The doctor spoke calmly, as if reading a production report, but I caught the "you should be worried" undertone. Early signs of carotid atherosclerosis. Long-term medication.

I’m sitting at the workbench, holding a pair of pliers, wiping them over and over with a piece of flannel. How many times have I wiped them? I’ve lost count. Usually, I follow a system—precision measuring tools in one group, cutting tools in another, fasteners in a third. Not today. I just pick up whatever is at hand, wipe it, put it back. Pick up another. My movements are slow because my hands are shaking slightly.

I don’t want to admit that.

Forty years. For forty years, I’ve been in control—schedules, diet logs, regular check-ups. My desk is organized without a single stray wire. The household ledgers are accurate to the penny. I thought that was enough. I thought that with enough precision, enough discipline, I could keep everything in my grasp.

But the doctor said it’s not enough.

That sentence keeps ringing in my ears—the one Li Meifang said: "I knew you’d get this sooner or later." When did she know? How did she know? Was she pitying me, or was she saying, "I saw through you long ago"? I don’t want to ask, because I’m afraid of the answer.

110 mg/dL. That number is wrong. Something has gone wrong with the management of my life, but I can’t find where. Or rather, I can’t find a solution at all. Pills? Long-term medication? What does that mean? It means my body has begun to betray me, and I cannot reverse it through stricter management.

How much longer?

The question has suddenly become urgent. How much longer can I work? How much longer until I see Chen Hao’s mortgage paid off? How much longer until I pass these tools, these forty years of things, on to someone? I never gave it serious thought because I assumed there was plenty of time.

Now, I’m not sure.

The tools are still here, piece by piece. My hands are still shaking.

I have to go to work tomorrow, read production reports, point out mistakes to my subordinates. Everything must continue. But I don’t know how much longer I can keep going.

I need to look at that report again. Maybe I missed something. Maybe the numbers will change if I scrutinize them enough.

But I know they won’t.

# Office Memo

**April 5, 2022, 2:20 PM**

---

My fingers are tapping the desk again. I can’t stop.

I was too harsh with Wang Gang just now. His suggestion regarding the inspection process was actually fine; why did I have to insist on pointing out his lack of precision? His expression changed—I saw it—but I didn't apologize. I just kept talking about my standards.

Why is it always like this?

During the afternoon meeting, I lowered the third-quarter defect rate target again. From 0.8 to 0.6. No one dared to object, but I saw Li Qiang’s pen tear through the paper while he was taking notes. He’s afraid. Someone else in my department will quit. I know it.

But I can’t control myself.

The physical exam report is still in the drawer. Blood pressure 139/92. The doctor said I need to be careful. I’m monitoring Li Meifang’s salt intake, yet I—never mind.

I wiped down the tools in the workshop again today. Screwdrivers, calipers, micrometers. Every single one is recorded in the logbook. Use, specifications, year of purchase. Chen Hao called to say his mortgage went up again; I listened to him finish and then just said, "Cut back on your spending." He didn't say anything else. It occurred to me that he might have wanted my help, but I didn't ask.

What use are these tools, anyway?

Two years until retirement. What exactly am I planning for?

Li Meifang said she wants braised pork for dinner. I’m already thinking about how to convince her to eat something lighter. She’ll listen to me, as she always does. But the way she looks at me—I’ve noticed it—she’s avoiding my eyes.

When did I become this kind of person?

Fingers still tapping. Tap. Tap. Tap.

I need to write this down. While I still can.

---

*Check tomorrow’s quality inspection standards before leaving. Might need to adjust them one more time.*

ranked_items.content:
Sunday, September 18, 2022, 10:47 PM

I haven’t slept well since getting back from the hospital yesterday. I just lay in bed, turning those numbers over and over in my head. 110, Grade 2, atherosclerosis. Meifang was sound asleep; I didn’t disturb her.

I went out this morning. I didn’t tell her where I was going. I bought a blood pressure monitor, a glucose meter, and a new notebook—black cover, high-quality paper. The cashier asked if I was buying them for a family member. I didn’t answer.

After getting home, I spent two hours in the workshop. I organized everything. I wiped all my tools clean. Then, I started recording.

8:15 AM: BP 128/82.
12:30 PM: BP 125/79.
3:45 PM: Blood sugar 5.8.
7:20 PM: BP 132/85.

I downloaded three apps: "BP Manager," "Health Log," and one specifically for tracking diet. The interfaces are all very clear. I entered everything I ate today—plain congee with pickles for breakfast, steamed fish and greens for lunch, tomato and egg noodles for dinner. No oil. No salt.

The doctor said I need long-term medication. But I’m thinking, what if I can keep these numbers within the normal range? If I stick to the logs, stick to the diet, and stick to the exercise—I’m going to walk 8,000 steps a day now, I will do it—then maybe I won’t have to take those pills? Or at least I can delay it?

Meifang asked why I was so busy today. I told her it was nothing, just that I wanted to be more meticulous about managing my health. She gave me a look... that kind of look, as if she already knew what was coming. I didn’t want to look her in the eye.

On the first page of the notebook, I wrote a title: "Health Management Plan: September 2022 – September 2023." Below it are weekly logs and monthly summaries. Everything is clear and orderly.

As long as I persist, as long as I don’t slack off, I can definitely reverse this. What the doctor said... maybe he just saw me at my worst moment. I’m starting now, starting today.

Continue tomorrow. The blood pressure monitor is on the nightstand.

I cannot slack off.
ranked_items.subquestion: You seem to have a habit of "digitizing" every detail of your life whenever you worry about your health—like precisely calculating your salt intake or tracking every uptick in your blood pressure. Does this kind of numerical management actually give you peace of mind?

ranked_items.content:
3:30 AM. Awake again.

The photo of that prescription is still glowing on my phone. I don’t know why I keep looking at it over and over. The numbers are the same every time: blood pressure 140/110, fasting blood glucose 110 mg/dL, LDL-C 3.8 mmol/L, carotid intima-media thickness... The doctor used the term "pre-metabolic syndrome." And "long-term medication."

Long-term.

I shone a flashlight on the paper prescription, reading it word by word. The doctor’s handwriting is clear. I didn't misread it. I copied the data into my notebook again, just like I would verify a test report at work. Confirmed. It’s ironic.

How much longer can I keep working?

My hand was shaking as I wrote that sentence. Then I crossed it out. I wrote it again. Crossed it out again. In the end, I just sat there, staring at the blackened ink.

Forty years. From the day I entered the factory in 1982 until now, every day has gone according to plan. Every process precise to the millimeter. The defect rate in the department I manage is 25% lower than the industry average. I have never wasted a single minute. Regular diet, regular sleep, annual check-ups. I thought that was enough. I thought I was in control.

But the doctor said it wasn't enough.

Meifang’s words keep circling in my head: "I knew you’d have this sooner or later." She said it so calmly, like she was stating a fact she’d known all along. I didn’t argue. Because I realized I couldn't find the words to talk back. She saw right through me. She saw through forty years of self-deception.

I don’t believe this result. But I can’t deny it either.

There are four medications on the prescription. I haven't filled them yet. I sit in my office, looking at those precision tools, imagining myself taking these pills every day, my body slowly...

How many years are left?

I tried to draft a plan in my notebook. Retirement date, financial arrangements, things for the children. But I couldn't keep writing. Because suddenly, I don't know what the deadline for these plans is anymore.

Dawn is breaking. I should get up. But I don’t want to move.

I think I need to recalculate everything. But right now, I don’t have the strength.

— Chen Guilin
September 11, 2022, 3:30 AM

# Work Log: September 10, 2022

**Date**: 2022.09.10  
**Weather**: Sunny  
**Status**: Exhausted  

---

I spent two hours longer in the office today than expected. The 3:00 PM quality control meeting was pushed back to 5:00 PM, which threw off my planned workflow audit. However, I did manage to finish the third-quarter defect rate statistics; the figures are 0.3 percentage points better than last month.

Back in the workshop tonight, I inspected that new set of precision measuring tools from Japan. They are in excellent condition, with all accuracy levels within standard ranges. I spent over an hour cleaning them and applying anti-rust treatment. If handled properly, these tools will last for many years.

Sitting in the workshop, a question suddenly crossed my mind—all these things I’ve accumulated over the years, these experiences and methods, is anyone actually using them? The younger people in the department seem to prefer blindly following the software system’s suggestions rather than understanding the principles behind them. How many people have actually read the process documentation I wrote?

I’m retiring in two years. I’ve thought about this plan for a long time and felt it was the right move. But today, I’m suddenly feeling a bit uncertain—how much work is still left unfinished? The things I wanted to pass on to others haven't truly been handed over yet.

Tomorrow, I’ll continue pushing the new inspection process improvement plan. I’ve been brewing this proposal for three months, and the data model is essentially finalized. If it gets approved, it should reduce the defect rate by another 2% or so.

I also need to remind my subordinates that the end-of-month equipment maintenance cannot be delayed again. Some people always want to procrastinate as much as possible, but it’s exactly in that kind of slackness where the details go wrong.

---

*Recorded by: Chen Guilin*  
*21:47*

ranked_items.content:
# June 28, 2022, 10:15 PM

The light in the workshop is still on. Li Meifang has already gone to bed. Her blood pressure has been a bit high lately, and she’s still not really drinking the herbal tea I brewed for her. Forget it, I’ll deal with it tomorrow.

I just flipped through my notebook again. I’ve been documenting the tools since last year, and there are over thirty entries now. For each one, I’ve noted its purpose, specifications, and date of purchase. Some of the handwriting has started to fade, so I traced over it again in pencil.

A question suddenly crossed my mind—if my body starts to fail, what will I have left to do?

This question has been circling in my head for a long time. The May physical exam report is still in the drawer; I haven’t looked at it a second time. I remember all the metrics and indicators the doctor mentioned, but I chose not to dwell on them. And now? I still don't want to think too deeply about it, but it seems I can’t avoid it anymore.

My eldest son is in Shenzhen, preoccupied with his mortgage; my daughter is teaching in Guangzhou. I’ve never asked them if they’re interested in learning any of this. Perhaps they lost interest long ago. Perhaps I shouldn't have expected anything from the start.

I added another question mark under the one already in the notebook. Then, I placed the notebook in the most prominent spot on the workbench. I kept staring at it in the darkness. It’s a bit of a foolish gesture, but I just wanted to look at it. I wanted to see if those questions sitting there meant they truly existed, and truly needed to be resolved.

*While I can still move, I have to hurry.* This phrase keeps popping up now.

I proposed four more process improvement plans at work this year, and all of them were adopted. My subordinates are still afraid of me. A new QC inspector made three mistakes last week that I pointed out in front of everyone; now he’s all submissive whenever he sees me. I know it’s not right, but I can’t help myself. Maybe I’m using this as a way to fight back against something—against time, against powerlessness, against that growing realization: I am losing control over everything.

Li Meifang says I’ve become even quieter lately. She’s right. What else is there to say?

Tomorrow, I’ll continue documenting the tools. There are still seven or eight left to write down.

Must hurry.

ranked_items.content:
# Memo - Reflection and Legacy

**Date: October 18, 2022, Afternoon**
**Location: Home Study**

---

## Core Points Summary

### I. Reflections on the Essence of 40 Years of Work
- ~~Perfect quality control~~ → Obsession with detail is a value in itself
- ~~Sense of achievement from process optimization~~ → The patience and persistence learned through the process
- A 25% drop in defect rates is not the finish line, but a reminder: Why do I demand so much?
  - Answer: Because I believe in standards.
  - The real answer: Because I am afraid of losing control.

### II. Beyond the Technical (Important)
- The meaning of tools lies not in collecting them, but in how they taught me to think.
- What precision work taught me:
  - A single millimeter determines success or failure.
  - However, the distance between people cannot be measured in millimeters.
  - (Thinking back on my harshness toward subordinates and my "supervision" of my wife over the years.)
- I must admit: I cannot control everything.
  - The doctor's words are still ringing in my ears.
  - The body ages; plans change.
  - This is not failure.

### III. If I Could Start Over
- Explain to the team *why* we do things earlier, rather than just telling them *what* to do.
- Give young engineers more room to make mistakes (instead of blunt dismissals).
- ~~Keeping knowledge in my head~~ → Actively record, share, and discuss.
- Listen more to my wife, rather than "supervising" from the sidelines.
- Communicate with the children about more than just their mortgages and jobs.

### IV. Words for Young Engineers
- Standards are important, but don't let them become a weapon.
- Details determine quality, but details can also consume your life—learn to prioritize.
- 40 years is a long time, but it is also very short.
- The things you take for granted now will become mere memories by the time you reach my age.
- Don't wait for your heart to remind you—some things are more important than perfection.

### V. Immediate Action Plan
- [ ] Compile a "Core Experiences in Quality Management" document (for technical school students).
- [ ] Sit down with my subordinates for a talk—explain rather than accuse.
- [ ] Have a real conversation with my wife this weekend; listen to her talk about home.
- [ ] Call Chen Hao and Chen Yu respectively; do not mention work.
- [ ] Continue medication and scheduled check-ups—accept this reality.

---

## Notes
- The precision tool inventory can be set aside for now.
- The 40-year timeline is drawn; now it needs to be turned into a story.
- Perhaps retirement isn't the end, but a different kind of beginning?
- (I wouldn't have believed that sentence before.)

---

**One final question: What is my greatest regret in life?**
It isn't that I didn't reach a higher position.
It’s that I never truly looked at the people beside me.

ranked_items.content:
September 29, 2022 | Sunny | 7:30 AM

I hesitated for a long time when I picked up the pill.

Standing there by the kitchen sink, clutching that small white tablet, I saw that Li Meifang had already placed a glass of water next to me. She didn’t say anything; she just stood there, and I could feel her watching me. This feeling of being watched is unfamiliar. For all these years, I was the one watching her—making sure she took her blood pressure medication on time, watching her diet, checking her physical exam reports. Now, it’s reversed.

I know what swallowing this pill signifies. It’s not that I didn’t understand the doctor’s diagnosis before, but picking up the pill and actually taking it feel like two different things. One is data; the other is... reality.

I marked the medication time on the calendar. I used that 0.5mm black gel pen; the handwriting is small and neat. I also set three reminders on my phone—7:00 AM, 12:00 PM, and 7:00 PM. That way, I won't miss a dose. I can manage this. Through precise records, through making no mistakes, maybe...

But I’m lying to myself. I know that.

Only after I swallowed it did Li Meifang turn away to start the congee. She didn’t ask how I felt, nor did she say anything encouraging. She just went about her business quietly. For some reason, that made me feel worse. If she had said something, I would have known how to respond. But she said nothing, as if she has already accepted this, while I am still... still what?

I stared at the empty pill bottle for a long time. I’ve read the manual three times now. All the data, the dosage instructions, every possible side effect—I’ve committed them all to memory. This shouldn't be hard. I managed processes at the factory for twelve years; I’ve handled problems far more complex than this. But this is different.

This time, I can’t solve it by improving the process. I can’t reverse it through stricter management standards.

The office clock is about to strike 8:00. I should head out. But I sit here, looking at that marked calendar, thinking about how this single pill represents more than just blood pressure—it represents the fact that time is quietly slipping away.

How many more 7:00 AMs do I have left?

Forget it. Don’t think like that.

I stand up and organize the documents on my desk. Business as usual. Time for work.

# Health Management Action Plan

**Date: September 18, 2022**  
**Status: In Progress**

---

## I. Equipment Procurement List

- ✓ Digital Blood Pressure Monitor (Omron, Upper Arm) — Received
- ✓ Blood Glucose Meter (OneTouch Ultra) — Received
- ✓ Weight Scale (with Body Fat Measurement) — Pending Delivery
- □ Heart Rate Fitness Tracker — Under Consideration

**Recording Method**: A newly purchased A4 notebook, organized by date. Each page is divided into four columns: Time, Blood Pressure, Blood Glucose, and Remarks.

---

## II. App Download List

1. **BP Management Assistant** — Auto-syncs data; customizable reminders.
2. **Diabetes Management** — Records pre- and post-meal glucose levels; includes a food database.
3. **Heart Health** — Tracks heart rate and exercise data.

All apps have been installed. Data is synced to the cloud daily for backup.

---

## III. Daily Measurement Schedule

| Time | Item | Instructions |
|------|------|------|
| 6:30 AM | Blood Pressure | 30 minutes after waking up; rest for 5 minutes in a seated position. |
| 7:30 AM | Blood Glucose | Before breakfast. |
| 12:00 PM | Blood Pressure | Before lunch. |
| 5:30 PM | Blood Glucose | Before dinner. |
| 9:00 PM | Blood Pressure | Before bed. |

**Requirement**: No interruptions. To be followed on weekends as well.

---

## IV. Dietary Framework

**Prohibited**:
- Pickled/Cured foods (pickles, salted fish)
- High-fat foods
- Sugary drinks
- Alcohol

**Allowed**:
- Steamed fish, chicken breast
- Leafy greens (one handful per meal)
- Brown rice, oats
- Unsalted nuts (one small handful per day)

**Supervisor**: Li Meifang. Her cooperation is required to record daily food intake.

---

## V. Exercise Plan

- Monday to Friday: 30-minute brisk walk daily (after work or in the morning).
- Weekends: Maintain consistency; no breaks.
- Avoid strenuous exercise and sudden physical exertion.

---

## VI. Medication Reminders

Prescription Hypertension Medication: Take promptly every night at 8:00 PM. Keep the medicine next to the teacup; do not miss a dose.

---

## VII. Next Steps

- September 25: Blood pressure follow-up check.
- October 10: Blood glucose and lipid profile follow-up check.
- Regularly track weight trends.

**Goal**: Bring blood pressure below 130/80 within three months.

---

**Note**: This is not optional. This plan must be strictly executed.

ranked_items.content:
April 15, 2022. Evening.

Another entry today. Li Meifang added too much salt to the food at dinner. I tapped my bowl with my chopsticks. Her hand gave a little tremor. I got up, walked to the kitchen, took out my notebook, and wrote: "Added salt at dinner; blood pressure management inadequate."

She said she was afraid.

I didn't comfort her. I told her that data doesn't lie, and that this was for her own good. She kept her head down and didn't say another word.

It’s 7:30 PM now. She’s in the living room watching TV; I’m in the study. My hand is still trembling slightly.

It’s not out of anger. I have to admit, it’s not.

I’m thinking: if I can’t even manage her blood pressure, what else can I manage? Process optimization, quality standards—I can do all of that at work. But at home? She’s been used to my coldness for thirty-five years, and now that I’m using data and records to "care" for her, she’s actually afraid. What does that say?

I flip open my medical report. My blood sugar levels are fluctuating again. The doctor says it’s nothing major, but I know it’s the beginning. My body is weakening; I can feel it. I need to control everything more strictly—diet, routine, data—otherwise, things will spiral out of control.

But I’ve realized I can’t control her. I can’t control time. I can’t control where those tools will eventually end up.

My eldest son is in Shenzhen, my daughter in Guangzhou. Neither of them will learn my craft. I’ve used this notebook to record the purpose of every tool, trying to turn the intangible into words, but halfway through, I stopped and asked myself—what’s the point? Who will read this? Who will care?

I’ll be retiring in two years. What then? No work, no processes to optimize, no data to manage. What will I have left?

She might be crying right now. I can’t hear it, but I know.

Should I go and apologize? But I was right. Her blood pressure *does* need managing. Data *is* objective.

Why does my rationality always hurt her? Why can’t I just say something soft like everyone else?

I keep flipping through the notebook. Page after page of entries. Neat, precise, and utterly cold.

Is this my life?

I put down the pen. My hand is still shaking.

To be continued tomorrow.

Meifang, I bought a blood pressure monitor and a glucose meter yesterday and already took my readings this morning. My blood pressure was 130/85, which is a bit better than what the doctor said. I plan to test twice a day—once in the morning and once at night—and keep a log in my notebook.

You need to be careful lately, too. I noticed you’ve been eating pickled vegetables again; those are no good for your blood pressure. We need to use less salt in our dinners—I’ll be keeping an eye on that. Also, I looked over your physical exam report, and your blood pressure is indeed on the high side. We can’t put this off anymore. I’m going with you to the hospital for a follow-up next week.

You don't have to believe everything the doctors say; some of their data might be off. I’m using an app to record everything now, so the data is more accurate. As long as we stay on top of managing it, it won't be that serious. I’ve already downloaded three apps that can track all our vitals.

Don’t worry, I have a plan. We just need to adjust our routine at home—we must be in bed by 11:00 PM and up by 6:00 AM. We’ll do this together.

Oh, and stop buying those greasy foods. We need to keep our diet a bit lighter for a while.

ranked_items.content:
# August 2, 2022, 8:30 PM

Sitting in the study. My wife is watching TV in the living room, the volume turned down low. I intended to organize today’s exercise logs, but right now, I can’t do anything.

I lost at the chess club today. That rarely happens.

It wasn't because my skills have declined—though perhaps they have—it was because I simply couldn't focus. When my friend A asked what was wrong, I just... let it out. About the medical report. Blood pressure 147/92, blood sugar 6.8, and a slight abnormality on the ECG. I hadn't even figured out how to say it, and yet there it was.

That’s not like me.

A said it was fine, that many people are like this and I just need regular check-ups. His words sounded hollow. I know he meant well, but he doesn't understand at all. No one can. This isn't just about abnormal data.

I’ve been controlling everything. Recording my wife’s blood pressure every day, watching everything she eats, walking 8,000 steps myself, weighing my diet down to the gram, organizing all the data into spreadsheets. I thought that by doing this, I could... I could stop something. But now I realize it’s all futile. Data can’t change anything.

Maybe I should retire early. I startled myself when I heard those words come out of my mouth. But once the thought appeared, I couldn't shake it. If my body is truly starting to fail, how much time do I have left? How much time is left for the things I haven't done yet?

The tools in the workshop, those ideas, and... I haven't even had a real conversation with Hao and Yu. They are in Shenzhen and Guangzhou, while I stay here recording data as if that were a form of love.

When I lost the game, I looked at the black and white pieces on the board and suddenly felt exhausted. Not a physical exhaustion, but something else.

It’s 8:30 PM. I have work tomorrow. Next week is the department’s quality assessment. Everything continues as if nothing has changed. But everything has changed.

I don’t know what to do. Or rather, I know what to do—make a plan, adjust the regimen, control the variables—but this time, I realize there are just too many things beyond my control.

Maybe I should sleep. I have to record the blood pressure data tomorrow.

ranked_items.content:
# Memo

**Date**: Wednesday, August 24, 2022, 14:00

**Subject**: Reflections following a game of chess

---

## Record

During today’s game with Player A, he said something that stuck with me: "Instead of spending time fearing the future, spend it cherishing the present."

I didn't respond at the time, but I haven't stopped thinking about it since.

## Issues to Sort Out

1. **What am I doing?**
   - My recent anxiety cycle: Prediction → Fear → Control → More Fear.
   - Since the physical exam results came back, I’ve been using data, plans, and logs to fight uncertainty.
   - But is this actually helpful? Or am I just wearing myself out?

2. **On Control**
   - What I can control: Workflows, financial accounts, daily schedules.
   - What I cannot control: Aging, fluctuating health metrics, my children’s choices, time itself.
   - I have been trying to fill the void of uncontrollable fears with things I can control.
   - What is the cost of doing this?

3. **On the Present**
   - For the past few months, I’ve been planning for a retirement that is two years away.
   - At the same time, I’m worrying about potential health issues.
   - The "present" I hold in my hands has been squeezed into something tiny.
   - My wife, the chess games, the tools in my workshop—they are all still here, but mentally, I’ve already let go of them.

4. **Defining Value**
   - I’ve always believed: Completion → Results → Proof of self-worth.
   - But what if things can’t be completed? What if the results are forgotten?
   - Perhaps value is more than just these things?

## For Further Reflection

- Why do I keep losing what I have now by worrying about what I might lose in the future?
- Is accepting the unchangeable an act of giving up, or an act of maturity?
- How much more time can I afford to waste on fear?

---

**Note**: When I play Player A again next week, I want to hear his take on "cherishing the present." Not as small talk, but because I truly want to understand.

ranked_items.content:
# Monday, July 18, 2022, 14:30

It’s been two hours since I got the report.

I’m sitting in my office with the report spread out on the desk. I told everyone "it’s nothing serious," but my fingers keep tracing those marked spots—blood pressure 139/92, fasting blood glucose 6.8. The numbers themselves don’t lie. I know that better than anyone.

I heard everything the doctor said, but I chose to remember only the first half of his sentences. "It’s under decent control." The rest—the parts about "needing to be more cautious" and "regular follow-ups"—I just let slide. That’s not my style.

Meifang was cooking when I got home. She saw the look on my face and asked about it. I didn't give her a straight answer, just said "I'm fine." But I know she can tell. After 35 years, she knows me too well. She tried to come closer, wanting me to talk, but I turned away and went into the study.

Then I suddenly turned back around and said to her, "Starting today, you need to cooperate too. We can't let your blood pressure issues drag on anymore. I’m going to set up a plan for you."

She was startled by my tone. I saw it. But I didn't stop. I need to control something. I need to ensure that something is within my grasp.

She isn't the problem. I know that. But at this moment, I can't face the fact that *I* am the problem.

Data doesn't lie. I’ve lived my whole life by data and standards, and now the data is starting to betray me. Blood pressure, blood sugar, weight—every indicator is slowly crossing the line. I can see the trend. I can predict it. And that is exactly what’s so terrifying.

The two-year retirement plan. I spoke of it with such certainty. But now I’m starting to wonder: if my body continues like this, how many "two years" do I have left?

The tools in the workshop are still there. I wanted to go and wipe them down, as I usually do. But today, I didn't move. I sat in my office, staring at this report, trying to use logic to convince myself that this is all within manageable limits.

But I know that some things are forever beyond my control.

For the first time, I truly felt that.

**Recurrence**: Daily/Weekly

- Log in the table on page 3 of the notebook.
- Sync to the Health Kit (Jiankang Bao) app.

**7:30 AM - Blood Glucose Testing**
- Fasting blood glucose level.
- Record the data.
- Compare against the reference range provided by the doctor.

**12:00 PM - Lunch Log**
- Record food intake, oil, and salt levels.
- Check compliance with low-sodium diet standards.
- Remind Li Meifang not to add salt.

**7:00 PM - 7:30 PM - Evening Walk**
- Downstairs in the residential compound; walk slowly and calmly.
- Record step count and heart rate.
- Attendance is mandatory.

**9:00 PM - Evening Blood Pressure Measurement**
- Repeat the morning procedure.
- Compare data fluctuations.

**10:00 PM - Data Consolidation**
- Export data from the three apps to an Excel spreadsheet.
- Check for any abnormal fluctuations.
- Update the weekly report.

---

## Weekly Tasks

**Wednesday 14:00 - Exercise Plan Review**
- Check for any missed walks this week.
- Adjust next week’s schedule.

**Saturday Morning - Family Blood Pressure Monitoring**
- Measure Li Meifang’s blood pressure.
- Record her medication status.
- If readings are abnormal, contact the doctor on Monday.

## Periodic Follow-ups

**October 15 - Cardiology Follow-up**

- Questions for the doctor: Should medication be adjusted? Is a dosage increase necessary?
- Appointment time: 9:00 AM.

**End of Month - Comprehensive Health Data Benchmarking**

- Calculate the margin of improvement.
- Evaluate the effectiveness of the management plan.

---

## Notes

- Record all data in black ink; no corrections or white-out allowed.
- Keep the notebook in its fixed position on the left side of the desk.
- Set app notifications to "High Priority"; do not ignore them.
- In case of any abnormal data (Systolic >140 or <100), immediately record the time and current activity status.
- No interruptions; continuous recording is the prerequisite for validity.

We cannot afford to be as careless as we were last time.

# Memo - Abnormal Treadmill Heart Rate Data Log

**Date:** June 2, 2022  
**Time:** 10:15 AM  
**Location:** Home Gym

---

## Incident Log

Abnormal heart rate data was detected during this morning's treadmill workout.

**Machine Displayed Data:**
- Heart Rate: 135 bpm
- Duration: 8 minutes
- Speed: 6.5 km/h

**Manual Verification (Pulse Count Method):**
- Pulse count within 15 seconds: 19 beats
- Calculated Heart Rate: 76 bpm
- Variance: 59 bpm

**Exercise stopped immediately. Re-measured after 5 minutes of rest:**
- Machine Display: 118 bpm
- Manual Count: 72 bpm
- Variance: 46 bpm

---

## Analysis of Potential Causes

1. **Poor Sensor Contact** — Most Likely
   - Need to check if the electrode plates are making proper contact.
   - Consider cleaning the sensor surfaces.

2. **Machine Calibration Error** — Requires Investigation
   - Last maintenance: March 2022.
   - Suggest contacting the manufacturer for calibration.

3. **Measurement Error** — Low Probability
   - Manual counting may have a deviation of ±5 bpm.
   - However, a 59 bpm variance is well beyond the normal range.

---

## Follow-up Actions

- [ ] Inspect treadmill sensor contact condition.
- [ ] Record heart rate data comparisons for the coming week.
- [ ] Reference May medical checkup report: Resting heart rate 72 bpm (Normal range).
- [ ] Consult a doctor if abnormalities persist.
- [ ] Schedule a maintenance check for the treadmill.

---

**Notes:** Currently leaning towards a machine malfunction rather than a physical health issue. The data discrepancy is too large and does not align with normal physiological patterns. Will continue to monitor.

ranked_items.content:
October 30, 2022, 8:00 PM

Li Meifang is already asleep. I’m sitting in the study; on the desk lies today’s medical report and a cup of tea that went cold long ago.

I said something to her today that left even me a bit stunned. I said, "Perhaps the meaning of life isn't in perfect control, but in genuine connection." Hearing those words come out of my mouth felt like someone was impersonating me. And yet, it really is what I was thinking.

Since I started the medication these past few days, the physical side effects haven't been as bad as I imagined. The doctor says it’s a good sign. But what’s stranger is that I’ve actually started to relax. I used to check my medical reports three times a day, recording every single number in a notebook, trying to force them back to "normal" through sheer willpower. Now... I still look at the reports, but I no longer feel the need to "subdue" them.

Last night, Meifang took my hand. She does this often, and I used to pull away after a moment to get back to my accounts or the news. But this time, I didn't. I let her hold it—maybe for five or six seconds. She didn’t say anything, and neither did I. We just held hands. Eventually, she let go gently and went to the kitchen to pour me some water.

I realize now that I owe her a great deal. For thirty-five years, I’ve used excuses like "monitoring her blood pressure" or "managing the family finances" to package my need for control as "responsibility." In truth, it was just my way of proving I was useful, that I deserved to be needed—by controlling everything. She saw through it long ago, but she never said a word. She just stayed by my side, waiting.

Chen Hao called recently saying his mortgage is a heavy burden. In the past, I would have immediately started analyzing his finances, giving him advice, even feeling a bit dissatisfied with his "poor planning." This time, I just listened, then told him, "It’s okay, take your time." There was a pause on the other end of the line; he didn’t seem used to me responding like that. I’m not used to it either. But it felt... more real.

Then there’s that idea about donating my tools to the technical school. Initially, I thought of it as "legacy"—wanting young people to learn what I’ve accumulated over forty years. But today I realized that was just another form of control; I wanted their learning to prove that my value wouldn't vanish with retirement. I still want to donate them, but the reason has changed. I just want to put those tools where they can continue to be used and cherished. As for who uses them or what they learn, that’s not for me to decide, nor does it need to be.

It’s strange. I always thought letting go of control would make me panic. But actually, it makes me feel... light?

My subordinates at the office are still a bit afraid of me. I know that. But lately, I’ve been thinking that maybe I could try a different way. Not by lowering standards, but... by changing how I communicate. Letting them know my expectations are high because I believe they can do well, not because I’m disappointed in them. This will require me to say more, and I’ve never been good at that. But perhaps it’s worth learning.

Tomorrow is Monday. Another week begins. I’ll keep taking the medicine, keep working, and keep being there for Meifang. And maybe, at the right moment, I’ll sit with her—saying nothing, just holding her hand.

That is enough.

ranked_items.content:
July 7, 2023, 9:00 PM

The only light in the study comes from the desk lamp. My wife went to bed a while ago; it’s quiet downstairs.

Insomnia again tonight, but this time it’s different. It’s not the restless, agitated kind where you toss and turn. It’s... how should I put it? I’m captivated by something. I don’t want to go to sleep.

While I was tidying up my tools this afternoon, that quality crisis from 2010 suddenly crossed my mind. Remember that? The supplier cut corners, our inspectors missed it, and it nearly ruined the company’s reputation. I wrote the entire improvement plan back then—from source inspection and mid-process sampling to final acceptance. I added double-checks to every single step. Later, that system was adopted across the whole industry.

I sat down at my desk and started writing it all down. Not for anyone in particular—to be honest, I don’t know who would even read it. I just felt like these things shouldn't be allowed to simply vanish. Those experiences, those lessons—they deserve to be recorded.

As I wrote, I found myself asking strange questions. For instance: why did I think of that solution back then? What was it that kept me going until the plan was finished? I was only in my fifties then; I was still working, and I had clear goals. What about now?

But tonight is different. When I got to that case study, it suddenly hit me—maybe I could organize all of this into a proper document. Nothing fancy, just a clear explanation: when you face a problem like this, here is how you should think, and here is what you should do. For the young people. For the inspectors who just started at the factory.

I put down my pen and stared at the ceiling for a while.

The idea makes me feel... what’s the word? Fulfilled? It’s not the satisfaction you get from finishing a job; it’s something else. It feels like I’m doing something meaningful, but it’s no longer for a promotion or a year-end review. It’s just... a pure desire to pass on what I know.

It’s almost 11:00 PM now. I should be tired, but I want to keep writing. I want to record that process optimization from 2015. I want to include the failed attempts, too—those are just as important.

My wife is asleep upstairs. Her blood pressure has been a bit high lately; I need to remind her to take her medicine tomorrow. But for now, I want to stay right here.

There are still many blank pages in the notebook under the lamp.

I want to fill them.

ranked_items.content:
Tuesday, September 13, 2022. Sunny.

I didn’t sleep well last night. I couldn't stop thinking about those numbers.

110 mg/dL. Stage 2 hypertension. Pre-metabolic syndrome. Early signs of carotid atherosclerosis.

The doctor spoke calmly, as if reading a quality inspection report. I heard him clearly; I took in every word. And yet, it felt like I didn't truly hear it at all. I keep wondering how this is possible. I wake up at 6:30 every morning and go to bed at 11:00 PM. My diet is logged in my phone. I get regular check-ups every month. I have spent forty years maintaining order, maintaining control over everything.

My wife said, "I knew you’d have this sooner or later." I didn't answer her.

I spent the morning in the workshop, wiping down my tools one by one. The vernier caliper I bought in 1987, the set of Swiss precision screwdrivers, the vise... I wiped every single one clean. My movements were slow. I found myself wondering what would happen to these things if I collapsed.

My eldest son is in Shenzhen, struggling with his mortgage. My daughter is in Guangzhou, and her career isn't stable yet. I haven't told them about the diagnosis. What is there to say? That I need long-term medication? That my body has started to betray me?

I wrote a few things in my notebook. "What these tools have taught me." "Precision work has taught me more than just technique." I didn't keep writing.

I’m wondering if I should do a knowledge-sharing session before I retire. To systematically pass on everything from these forty years to the young people in the department. But I’m not sure how much time I have left. The thought makes me feel a sense of urgency, yet also a sense of powerlessness. I wrote "Sharing Session" on a sticky note, but the date box is still empty. I don’t know when would be appropriate. I don’t even know how much time I have left to be "appropriate."

The doctor prescribed some medicine. I have to take two kinds every day now. As I swallow them, I wonder if this is it—the beginning. The beginning of the decline.

I have always believed that precise management could prevent anything. But the medical report tells me that I cannot control everything.

I cannot control it.

How much longer?

Is it really too late?

ranked_items.content:
# MEMO

**Date: September 19, 2022, Morning**
**Location: Office Cubicle**

---

Maybe I should change my ways.

The improvement plan Xiao Wang submitted during yesterday’s meeting was actually quite good. At the time, I pointed out three flaws directly. His expression… I can still see it now. He didn’t push back; he just nodded and took notes. When I walked past his desk at the end of the day, he was packing up—his movements were hurried, as if he were escaping something.

Is this right?

I’ve always believed that being strict is being responsible. A 25% reduction in defect rates—those are the facts. But what lies behind the data? It’s my subordinates working in fear under my gaze. Li Meifang once said, "You’re the same way at home." I didn't respond.

Control ≠ Respect.

Where did that phrase come from yesterday? Perhaps it was something the doctor said that triggered it. He talked about my blood pressure, my carotid artery, all those numbers… I’ve spent 40 years trying to control everything, yet my body has betrayed me anyway. I cannot control it. So what about my subordinates? I "manage" them the same way, as if I were managing a machine. But they aren't machines.

Time is running out. What actually matters?

It’s not the process optimization forms. It’s not those perfect quality inspection reports. If I were to collapse right now, who would remember how strict I was? Who would think of me because of a 25% lower defect rate?

Regarding Xiao Wang’s proposal—I actually should have praised him in front of everyone. And *then* pointed out the room for improvement.

I need to rethink how I speak to my staff.

Maybe there’s still time.

---

*To be continued*

ranked_items.content:
September 17, 2022 Afternoon

My hands are still shaking.

It’s been three hours since I left the hospital. I’m sitting in my workshop, and my hands are still shaking. 110 mg/dL. Grade 2 hypertension. Carotid atherosclerosis. The doctor spoke these words with a calm tone, as if he were talking about the weather. But I heard him loud and clear. Every number felt like a nail being driven in.

I tell myself these are just data points. I’ve organized forty years of production reports; I know how to read data. But this time, the data is telling me that my body is failing.

My wife said, "I knew this would happen to you sooner or later." That hurt more than the diagnosis itself. When did she notice? I always thought I had everything under control—diet, routine, checkups. I was even secretly monitoring her blood pressure, terrified something might happen to her. And the result? I’m the one who collapsed first.

It’s ridiculous.

My wife urged me to adjust my pace of life. I told her, "I know. That’s exactly what I’m doing." But what am I doing right now? I’m in the workshop, repeatedly wiping down these tools. I’ve wiped the same screwdriver over a dozen times. My movements are forceful, as if I’m trying to scrub something off. I know it’s absurd. I’m using this to mask my anxiety. But I can’t stop.

The doctor said I’ll need long-term medication. "Long-term." That’s a heavy word.

I’m thinking about those precision tools—the ones I’ve collected for over twenty years. And those forty years of experience—the quality control standards, the process optimizations, the things I’ve never explicitly said but are etched into my brain. If I fall, what happens to all of it? I don’t have time to think about that, because the moment I do, I start to feel afraid.

I don’t want to tell Chen Hao and Chen Yu. Not yet. They’ll ask too many questions, and I don’t have the energy to answer them. Besides, I don’t know what I’d even say. "Your father is sick"? That sounds too dramatic. But if I don’t tell them, they’ll hear it from their mother sooner or later.

I need to make a plan. The doctor gave me a medication list; I’ve already noted it down. Regular follow-ups. Dietary adjustments. And... I don’t know what else. I listened to everything the doctor said, but right now, only one thought is stuck in my head: I’m running out of time.

I originally planned to retire in two years. Now that plan might have to change. Or maybe it can’t be changed at all. I don’t even know how much longer I can keep working.

It’s ironic. I’ve spent my whole life pursuing control. Precise management, clear plans, every step taken by the book. But my body won’t listen. The body has its own logic, and I can’t change it with willpower alone.

I put down the screwdriver. My hands are still shaking.

I have work tomorrow.

I was just thinking—we’ve been at this factory for almost 40 years. All these years, my focus has been on mastering every single step, setting strict standards, and making sure there’s no room for error. Thinking about it now... maybe these things should be carried on by someone.

Don’t laugh at me. I just feel like some things shouldn’t just stay locked in my head.

ranked_items.content:
September 10, 2022, 4:45 PM

Just got back from the hospital. My hands are still shaking.

I wrote down everything the doctor said. Blood sugar at 110 mg/dL, systolic 160, diastolic 100—Stage 2 hypertension. And then that phrase: pre-metabolic syndrome. Early signs of carotid atherosclerosis. I asked him if he was sure; he said he was. Then he wrote out a stack of prescriptions and said I’d need long-term medication.

Long-term medication.

I kept poking at those three words on the prescription slip for the longest time. The tip of my pen nearly tore through the paper. The doctor probably noticed, but he didn't say anything.

Forty years. I’ve worked at this plant for forty years. Up at 6:30 AM, in bed by 10:00 PM. Never missed a day in my work log. Portioned meals, regular exercise, timely check-ups. I thought that would be enough. I thought that through precise management—by keeping everything under my thumb—my body wouldn't betray me.

Now the doctor is telling me it wasn't enough.

I always felt I was different from the others. Everyone else is so careless, while I am precise down to the minute. I thought that was an advantage. And now? Now I realize there are far more things I can’t control than things I can. The things happening inside my body—I can’t see them, I can’t touch them, and I can’t change them.

My wife said, "I knew you'd end up with this sooner or later." Her words made me angry, but I didn't argue back. Because I couldn't. She probably saw it coming long ago, while I was still lying to myself.

I need to start the medication. Every day. Probably for a long time. Maybe for the rest of my life.

How much longer is left? I keep wondering. If my body is already like this, how much longer can I work? Can I hold on for two more years until retirement? Or should I leave early? But what does early retirement even mean? What do I have left besides work?

All those tools in the workshop. Collected over forty years. And those process improvement plans. If I collapse, who takes over? Hao and Yu aren't in this industry. They might not even know what any of it is worth.

My head is a mess right now. I should calm down and make a plan. But I find that I can't. Because I don't know what I'm supposed to be planning for.

My hands are still shaking.

I need to put these pills in a fixed spot. Record the dosage every day. I can do that. I must do that.

But as for everything else?

I don't know.

ranked_items.content:
September 10, 2022, 8:15 PM

I’ve been sitting in the workshop for two hours. I’m wiping down that 1985 vernier caliper, my movements slow. My wife is watching TV in the living room; I can hear the sound, but I don’t want to go out.

110 mg/dL. Stage 2 hypertension. Early signs of carotid atherosclerosis.

The doctor spoke those words calmly, as if reading a product inspection report. But I heard them clearly. Every word felt like a nail being driven in. I’ve always done my checkups, always logged the data, always believed I was in control. Wake up at 6:00 AM, sleep at 10:00 PM, a light diet, regular exercise. For forty years, that’s how I’ve lived.

My wife said, "I knew you’d get this sooner or later."

That sentence stung more than the diagnosis itself. When did she see through me? I thought I’d hidden it well. I thought that through precise management, I could stop aging, stop my body from betraying me. But she knew all along. What does that mean? Does it mean my efforts look like a joke to everyone else?

Long-term medication. The doctor said I need long-term medication.

I don’t know how long "long-term" is. One year? Five? Ten? Until I die? I’ve never taken medicine long-term. I rarely even catch a cold. Now I have to take pills for the rest of my life just to maintain a number—110. The number itself is meaningless, but what it represents matters. It represents my loss of control over my own body.

My wife said she’d go with me to get the prescription filled tomorrow. I didn’t respond, just nodded, and turned to walk into this workshop. I didn’t want her to see my hands shaking.

The tools are still here. Dozens of them. I spent forty years collecting them. Each one represents a stage, a decision, a moment I thought was important. Now I’m wondering, if I collapse, what happens to these things? Chen Hao doesn't understand mechanics. Neither does Chen Yu. Will they be treated as scrap metal?

I donated some tools to the industrial technical school. Was that the right decision? Or was it just a disguised way of settling my estate?

I don’t want to think like this. But I can’t help it.

The doctor said I need to change my lifestyle. Reduce stress. Maintain a routine. I’m already doing those things. Does that mean I haven’t done enough? Or does it mean none of it matters?

My hands are still shaking. I put the caliper down.

I have to get the medicine tomorrow. I need to make a list—drug names, usage, side effects, prices, insurance coverage. I need to turn this into data, into something manageable. That’s the only way I’ll feel at peace.

But I know that some things simply cannot be managed.

ranked_items.content:
April 28, 2022, 11:15 PM

I’m sitting in the study. The lights are off. It’s pitch black outside the window.

I’ve been sitting here in the dark for a long time. My hand is pressed against my chest; I can feel my heartbeat. The rhythm is irregular. My breathing is a bit shallow, but I haven't moved. I just sit here, feeling it all.

The physical exam report is still on the desk. Blood pressure 139/88, fasting blood sugar 6.8. There’s a waveform on the ECG that the doctor said needs observation. He used the phrase "requires attention." I heard him loud and clear.

I’m thinking: this is how it begins.

I press my fingers against my pulse and count—76 beats per minute. Within the normal range. But I know it was racing just a moment ago. Why did it speed up? No exercise, nothing special happened. I was just sitting here when I suddenly felt... something I can't put into words.

Hao called today. He said the mortgage pressure is heavy and he might have to pay back a little less this month. I didn't say much; I just noted it down in my ledger. The mortgage, his sales commissions, the fluctuations of Shenzhen’s housing prices. I track all these data points. But what’s the use of tracking? Can I change anything?

The tools in the workshop are still on the shelves. Lately, I’ve been numbering them, writing down their uses and the years they were purchased. Some were bought in 1995, others in 2008. I wonder, if I were suddenly gone, who would know how to use these things? Not Hao. Not Yu. And certainly not Meifang.

They would just become scrap metal. Or be tossed out as junk.

I’ve been sitting in the dark for a long time, without turning on the light.

Tomorrow, I need to schedule a more detailed check-up. Cardiology. I need to know exactly what that abnormal waveform means. I need to establish a long-term monitoring plan. Every month’s data must be recorded so I can see the trends.

I also need to keep supervising Meifang’s blood pressure. She’s started eating salty foods again; I have to remind her once more.

My breathing has settled down a bit now.

But that sense of unease remains.

It’s as if something is slowly coming loose, and I can’t reach out to catch it.
