ranked_items.content:
Meifang, I told my boss something today. I wanted to talk to you about it first.

We have a meeting this afternoon to discuss next year’s plans, so I went to see him beforehand. I told him I want to organize an experience-sharing session before I retire—a way to summarize everything I’ve learned about quality management over the past forty years and share it with the people at the company. It’s nothing grand; I just want to document what I’ve learned, the pitfalls I’ve encountered, and how certain standards were established.

He was quite supportive and said it was a great idea.

To be honest, this wasn't what I originally had in mind. Do you remember? A while back, I was so preoccupied with how to pass my technical skills down to the younger generation; I felt such a sense of urgency. But after talking to the doctor a few times and starting the medication, I’ve slowly come to realize something. It’s not so much about "passing the torch" as it is about me wanting to properly sort through everything I’ve done all these years. It’s about giving myself a sense of closure.

This isn't just for others. It’s mostly for myself. Forty years in this position—what did I learn? What did I improve? Why did I do things the way I did? I want to think these through clearly, and I want others to know, too.

What do you think? Am I overthinking things?

The truth is, I feel that if I do this, I won't feel so empty when I retire. If these things are recorded and spoken aloud, they won't just disappear with me.

How is your blood pressure lately? Did you take your medicine on time today?

---

(Added in the evening)

Oh, I just remembered. You once said that I’ve spent all these years being too anxious to control everything, and that sometimes I don't need to be so tense. I think this sharing session is exactly like that. I’m not trying to force anyone to change; I just want to tell the story of my experiences over the years. As for the rest, I’ll leave it to others to interpret for themselves.

Thinking about it this way actually makes me feel a bit more relaxed.

You’ve been by my side for so many years. Sometimes I think I should listen to your thoughts more often, instead of just deciding everything on my own.

Thank you.

An employee’s test report came back with errors this afternoon. In the past, I definitely would have told him straight out that it was unacceptable. Today, I changed my tone and suggested we look into where things went wrong together.

It worked out quite well. He ended up finding the cause on his own initiative, which was much faster than if I had just criticized him.

I might be back a bit late tonight as there are still some departmental adjustments to handle. Remember to eat something light, and don't worry too much about your blood pressure.

ranked_items.content:
July 7, 2023, 9:00 PM

The only light in the study comes from the desk lamp. My wife went to bed a while ago; it’s quiet downstairs.

Insomnia again tonight, but this time it’s different. It’s not the restless, agitated kind where you toss and turn. It’s... how should I put it? I’m captivated by something. I don’t want to go to sleep.

While I was tidying up my tools this afternoon, that quality crisis from 2010 suddenly crossed my mind. Remember that? The supplier cut corners, our inspectors missed it, and it nearly ruined the company’s reputation. I wrote the entire improvement plan back then—from source inspection and mid-process sampling to final acceptance. I added double-checks to every single step. Later, that system was adopted across the whole industry.

I sat down at my desk and started writing it all down. Not for anyone in particular—to be honest, I don’t know who would even read it. I just felt like these things shouldn't be allowed to simply vanish. Those experiences, those lessons—they deserve to be recorded.

As I wrote, I found myself asking strange questions. For instance: why did I think of that solution back then? What was it that kept me going until the plan was finished? I was only in my fifties then; I was still working, and I had clear goals. What about now?

But tonight is different. When I got to that case study, it suddenly hit me—maybe I could organize all of this into a proper document. Nothing fancy, just a clear explanation: when you face a problem like this, here is how you should think, and here is what you should do. For the young people. For the inspectors who just started at the factory.

I put down my pen and stared at the ceiling for a while.

The idea makes me feel... what’s the word? Fulfilled? It’s not the satisfaction you get from finishing a job; it’s something else. It feels like I’m doing something meaningful, but it’s no longer for a promotion or a year-end review. It’s just... a pure desire to pass on what I know.

It’s almost 11:00 PM now. I should be tired, but I want to keep writing. I want to record that process optimization from 2015. I want to include the failed attempts, too—those are just as important.

My wife is asleep upstairs. Her blood pressure has been a bit high lately; I need to remind her to take her medicine tomorrow. But for now, I want to stay right here.

There are still many blank pages in the notebook under the lamp.

I want to fill them.

ranked_items.content:
Meifang, go ahead and eat first. I’m just sorting through some things.

I was just thinking—we’ve been at this factory for almost 40 years. All these years, my focus has been on mastering every single step, setting strict standards, and making sure there’s no room for error. Thinking about it now... maybe these things should be carried on by someone.

I’m considering whether I should write something for the school while I’m still able-bodied. Just putting together the experience I’ve gathered over the years. It’s nothing fancy, just how to actually get the job done right. Those young people—they need to know what it means to be thorough.

Don’t laugh at me. I just feel like some things shouldn’t just stay locked in my head.

By the way, how’s your blood pressure lately? Last time, the doctor said to cut back on salt—have you been paying attention to that? I noticed you’ve been making soup again these past couple of days.

Wait a second, let me just finish organizing this spreadsheet first.

ranked_items.content:
June 14, 2023, 11:00 PM

The office lights are already out. I’m sitting in my study at home, a cup of tea sitting cold in front of me.

I still haven't figured out how to organize my thoughts from today.

I remember every detail of the 3:00 PM farewell party. But looking back now, it feels like someone else was up there speaking. I heard myself say, "Excellence is not just about perfect products, but perfect relationships"—where did that come from? I’ve never thought that way. Or rather, I’ve been avoiding thinking that way.

I felt it clearly when my voice choked up. That moment felt foreign. In forty years, I’ve never lost control in front of so many people. Meifang was sitting there, watching me with something in her eyes I can’t quite name. Maybe surprise. Maybe pity. I’m not sure.

The strangest part was the handshakes. After the speech, I took the initiative to walk down and shake hands with my colleagues one by one. That’s not like me. I know perfectly well it’s not like me. My hands were shaking; I could feel it. But I did it anyway. That intern—the boy who cried during the sharing session—his eyes turned red again when he shook my hand. He said, "Manager Chen, thank you." I didn't know what to say, so I just patted him on the shoulder.

Sitting here now, I wonder: who am I?

It’s a ridiculous question. I am Chen Guilin, 61 years old, Manager of the Production Quality Control Department. Starting tomorrow, that identity is gone. So, what about me?

Were these forty years worth it? I keep asking myself that. A 25% lower defect rate. Process optimization. Annual improvement plans. These are all numbers. They are measurable. But why did that boy cry? Because of some flowcharts? Because of my criticisms?

I don’t understand.

Meifang asked if I wanted something to eat. I said no. She’s sitting in the living room; I can hear the sound of the TV. She isn't rushing me. That’s rare.

I’m thinking about those young engineers. Their eyes lit up when they listened to my stories. I never noticed that before. Or rather, I noticed but didn't acknowledge it. I always felt that work was work, and didn't need any of that. But maybe... maybe I was wrong.

Maybe I’ve been using "perfection" to escape "relationships."

That thought hurts.

Tomorrow, I’m going to organize those tools. I’ve decided to donate them to the school. Maybe in doing so, I’ll understand something. Maybe those tools will have more meaning in someone else's hands than they did in mine.

But I’m still not sure.

I’m still not sure who I am.

Just now in the workshop, I dug out those tools. Some I bought over twenty years ago; others I only recently organized. As I was wiping them down, it suddenly hit me—maybe what I’ve been doing all this time isn't about passing them on perfectly to someone else, but that there’s meaning in the process itself.

You told me before that I’d eventually have this realization. You saw it clearly. And now, I’m starting to see things clearly too.

I’m thinking that maybe, before I retire, I should talk to the younger ones about what I’ve learned over the years. Not to leave behind some kind of legacy, but just... to talk. To let them know why precision work is worth taking seriously.

I don’t know if this is the right idea. But I feel like I need to do something, while there’s still time.

What do you think?

ranked_items.content:
July 14, 2022, 3:00 PM

Just got off the phone with the hospital. I moved my physical exam from July 25th up to tomorrow, July 15th.

The operator asked why I wanted to move it forward. I told her there was no special reason, just that I’m relatively free right now. That was a lie. I knew I was lying, but I couldn't explain the real reason. Maybe there isn't a reason at all, or maybe there are too many.

*The sooner it’s done, the better.* This sentence keeps looping in my head. It’s as if once I finish the check-up tomorrow and get the report, everything will be fine. But that logic doesn't hold up. I know that perfectly well.

My productivity has been low these past two days. I had a quality review meeting this morning and found myself repeating the same things; the expressions on my subordinates' faces told me they noticed it too. When I got back to the office this afternoon, I was supposed to organize some process documentation, but I sat there for half an hour without typing a single word. My appetite isn't great either; I only ate half of my lunch. Li Meifang asked if I was feeling unwell. I said no, I’m just not hungry.

My fingers have been tapping on the desk all afternoon. *Tap, tap, tap.* The rhythm is fast and restless. I can’t stand the sound of it myself, but I can’t stop. This isn't like me. I can usually control these things.

In the evening, I flipped through that tool logbook in the workshop. Looking at those dense notes, it suddenly felt a bit absurd. What am I recording? Who is going to use these tools? My eldest son is selling his house, and my daughter is teaching. Neither of them needs any of this. So why am I still keeping records?

I caught a glimpse of myself in the mirror. I look a bit pale.

The results will be out tomorrow afternoon. My heart is racing, but I tell myself it’s a normal physiological response. Just nerves. But why am I nervous? I should be feeling confident.

Whatever. Time for bed. Waking up at 6:00 AM tomorrow.

ranked_items.content:
June 26, 2023, 7:00 PM

I just got back from the chess and card room. My wife had already put dinner on the table. I didn't have much of an appetite and just had a few bites.

This is my third time at the club this week. What used to be just a weekend habit has now bled into Wednesdays and Fridays. One of my chess partners asked me, "How come you're here so often lately?" I didn't give him a straight answer; I just said, "Nothing better to do." But deep down, I know exactly what I’m doing—I’m trying to fill the gaps in my schedule.

It’s been nearly a month since I left my job. I still wake up on time every morning, but once I’m awake, I have no idea what to do with myself. It used to be work, but now? Playing chess, looking over financial statements, tidying up the tool shed... even all of that combined doesn't fill a whole day.

Another regular was talking today about how he went traveling in Yunnan after retiring and even took up photography. Someone else mentioned he’s teaching his grandson how to code. They all have things to do. I asked them, "What do you all do with yourselves now that you're retired?" The question came out awkward and stiff; I could feel it. Their advice was equally vague—"Just relax," "Find a hobby."

It’s not that I don’t understand the logic. The problem is, I’ve never had the habit of "relaxing." For forty years, every single day had a clear objective. Now that the goals are gone, I’m at a loss.

The days feel a bit empty.

That is my most honest feeling lately. It’s not melancholy, and it’s not depression; it’s just emptiness. It’s like a desk that’s been suddenly cleared off—all the files have been categorized and moved to the archives, leaving nothing but a bare, polished surface.

My wife asked me what TV show I wanted to watch tonight. I said, "Whatever." She sighed—a sigh I know well. She’s grown used to this version of me.

Maybe I need to do what she says and make a plan for myself. But what should that plan be? I’m starting to think it’s not a question of "what to do," but a question of "who I am." Without the title of manager, without the quality inspection reports, without the process optimizations... what is left of me?

Tomorrow, I’ll keep organizing the tool shed. At least the tools won't let me down.

# MEMORANDUM
**Date:** June 18, 2023, 2:00 PM  
**Ref No.:** 2023-06-18-001

---

## Issue Description
Had an unpleasant exchange with Li Meifang at noon today. When she brought out some high-fat snacks, I intervened as per the doctor’s advice. Her reaction was unexpected—not acceptance, but a gentle yet firm rejection. She said I am "always trying to control her."

I felt offended. I was acting out of concern.

## Key Details
- **Time:** Around 12:30 PM
- **Location:** Home kitchen
- **Dialogue:** I reminded her that the doctor said to limit fat intake → She said, "I know my own body" → I said nothing further.
- **My Reaction:** Felt a sense of losing control. Remained silent for a long time.

## Questions for Reflection
1. What am I doing? Why am I monitoring her diet?
   - Surface reason: She has high blood pressure and needs to manage it.
   - Actual reason: ? (Need to be honest with myself)

2. Is this related to recent changes?
   - My job is ending soon → I’ve lost control over my professional life.
   - Am I using "caring for my wife’s health" to fill that void?

3. What does my wife’s reaction indicate?
   - She has noticed my over-intervention.
   - She is setting boundaries.
   - I should respect these boundaries.

## Action Items
- [ ] Reflect: What is the root of this need for control? What am I afraid of?
- [ ] Research: Look into materials regarding communication and boundaries in marriage (perhaps ask my chess partners or look for books).
- [ ] Dialogue: Find the right time for a sincere conversation with Meifang, rather than giving her instructions.
- [ ] Observe: Over the next week, pay attention to whether I continue to exhibit similar interventionist behavior.

## Reflective Notes
Perhaps I need to admit it—I am losing control over many things. My work, my body, my children’s lives. It makes me feel uneasy. My coping mechanism has been to try and control whatever is within reach. But doing so will only cause me to lose what matters most.

Meifang is right. She is an adult. She knows her own body.

I need to learn to let go. But it is difficult.

---

**Next Review Date:** June 25, 2023

# Work Log: October 30, 2022

**Sunday, 20:00**

I’ve been reflecting on something over the past few days. This afternoon, while sitting with Meifang for a long time, she mentioned that I’ve been talking a bit more lately. I just smiled and didn’t deny it.

Last Friday’s medical report showed that things have stabilized. The doctor said to just keep taking the medication on schedule and come in for regular check-ups. Meifang was right there beside me taking notes, even more focused than I was. It made me think back on all the "supervising" I’ve done at home over the years—checking her blood pressure, monitoring her diet. My intentions might have been right, but my approach was probably too rigid. She never complained; instead, she’s now taking the initiative to accompany me to the hospital. It’s a strange feeling, but a grounding one.

Back to the office tomorrow. I’ve been thinking about how to organize everything I’ve accumulated over these forty years before I retire. It’s not necessarily about passing it on to someone specific, but I feel there should be a record. On Monday, I plan to talk to my supervisor about putting together a summary of my quality management experience in the first half of next year, perhaps as a formal document. Also, regarding that technical school—I want to move forward with that. I plan to systematically categorize and label the precision tools in the workshop and donate them. In my hands, they are just tools; in the hands of students, they might become something more.

Feedback from my subordinates has been better lately. I’ve noticed that when giving feedback, I’ve started asking for their thoughts first instead of just pointing out problems. The defect rate remains low, but the atmosphere seems less tense. I haven't fully processed this shift myself, but it feels right.

Haohao called last week saying his mortgage pressure is still quite heavy. I didn’t jump in with advice immediately; instead, I asked what his plans were. He said he might need to adjust his work schedule. I told him, "As long as you’ve thought it through. Let us know if you need help." I surprised myself a bit saying that, but in that moment, I was being sincere.

Yuyu’s work in Guangzhou is also going well. She said she wants to come back for a visit after National Day; Meifang and I are both very happy about it.

Perhaps the meaning of life isn't about perfect control, but about genuine connection. That thought surfaced while I was chatting with Meifang—I didn't expect to say it myself. But once it was out there, it felt like I had finally admitted something.

Onward to next week.

---

ranked_items.content:
**Lifestyle Adjustment Plan v2.0**
October 1, 2022 | Evening Entry

---

**Background:**
Went for a walk with Meifang this afternoon. I had originally planned to decline on the grounds of having work to handle, but I ended up going anyway. We walked for 40 minutes. No significant conversation—just walking.

A few things came to mind during the walk. I’ve organized my thoughts since returning.

---

**Core Adjustments:**

**1. Allocation of Quality Time**
- **Current Status:** Weekends are primarily dedicated to financial management and work reviews. Interaction with my wife has been compressed into "supervision" (diet, medical checkups, blood pressure management).
- **The Problem:** This "supervision" is essentially one-way control, not genuine communication.
- **New Plan:**
  - Fixed walks every Wednesday evening and Saturday afternoon (30–45 minutes).
  - No discussion of work, finances, or health data during walks.
  - Let my wife lead the conversation; I will shift from "instructing" to "listening."

**2. Redefining Supervision**
- **Old Mode:** "You must get your checkup on time," "Eat lighter"—these are commands.
- **New Mode:** Accompanying her to checkups, adjusting our diet together—this is participation.
- **Specific Action:** Book checkups together next week; I will also undergo a comprehensive examination.

**3. Communication of Information**
- **Regarding my diagnosis:** I still haven't told my wife the specifics.
- I now realize this concealment is another form of "supervision"—I am unilaterally deciding what she should and shouldn't know.
- **Plan:** Find a suitable time soon to share the doctor’s diagnosis and my management plan with her.
- The goal is not to seek sympathy, but to let her know the reality so we can develop a plan together.

---

**Data Tracking Adjustments:**
- **Retain:** Daily blood pressure, exercise volume, and diet logs (necessary for health management).
- **Add:** Frequency of interaction with my wife and an assessment of conversation quality (using a simple satisfaction log).
- **Remove:** The "supervision score" for my wife’s behavior (the practice itself was flawed).

---

**Expected Outcomes:**
- **Short-term:** Improve the quality of the relationship with my wife; reduce her feeling of being "managed."
- **Medium-term:** Gain better emotional support through more honest communication.
- **Long-term:** Lay the foundation for quality of life after retirement (if we can’t get along now, being together all day after retirement will be difficult).

---

**Notes:**
During the walk, my wife said something. She said, "You’re finally willing to walk with me for a while."

I didn't respond immediately, but those words have been stuck in my head.

I realize I’ve been using "work," "plans," and "management" to fill up my life, using the control of my wife to maintain a false sense of order. The doctor’s diagnosis broke that order. Now I need to rethink what truly matters.

Perhaps this isn't a loss of control, but a redefinition of its scope. I cannot control my body, but I can control how I treat those closest to me.

Implementation begins next week.

ranked_items.content:
September 20, 2022, 10:15 PM

Li Meifang said something tonight that I’m still thinking about.

Over dinner, I brought up work—the new QC inspector made another mistake, and I called him out on it directly. She put down her chopsticks, looked at me, and said, "You know, I knew you’d end up with this sooner or later." I didn't realize what she meant at first. Then she added, "Maybe your problem isn't physical; it’s that you never leave any room for error—not for others, and not for yourself."

I didn't answer. We kept eating, just like usual. But those words have been circling in my head ever since.

The doctor’s words from this afternoon are still ringing in my ears. 110, Grade 2, atherosclerosis. The numbers are clear. I always thought I had everything under control—diet, routine, exercise—but clearly, it wasn't enough. I don’t even know when it stopped being enough.

While I was wiping down my tools in the workshop, I thought that maybe Meifang is right. My attitude toward that new employee seems to be the same as my attitude toward my own body right now—demanding perfection, allowing no flaws. But perfection... I suddenly realized that maybe it doesn't even exist.

I told Meifang that being diligent might not be enough; maybe I need more patience. I was a bit surprised at myself for saying it. It felt strange coming out of my mouth. She just nodded and didn't say anything, but I could tell she was smiling.

I’m lying in bed now; my wife is already asleep. I’m wondering, if I can’t even fully control my own body, then what was the point of everything I’ve taken pride in for the last forty years—the precision management, the process optimizations?

Maybe the point isn't perfection itself, but... I haven't figured it out yet.

I have to go to the hospital tomorrow to pick up my prescription. The doctor said I’ll need long-term medication. I don’t like the word "long-term." But it seems I don't have a choice right now.

ranked_items.content:
Meifang, I just got off the phone with Haohao.

I told him about the doctor’s diagnosis. I originally intended to just mention it briefly and move on, but he asked so many questions that I ended up telling him everything. He said he’d keep a closer eye on me and told me not to worry too much.

As we were talking, I... my voice cracked. It’s been a long time since I felt that way.

It got me thinking. All these years, it feels like all I’ve done with Haohao and Yuyu is supervise their careers and lives; I’ve never really talked to them about my own things. And you’ve always been there, staying by my side, listening to me ramble about work, all while having to manage your own blood pressure. I used to think this was just what I was supposed to do—take care of everything at home so none of you had to worry.

But now I realize that maybe I had it wrong all along.

I didn't truly see the patience you’ve shown me over the years. I see it now.

Get some rest early tonight. Don't overexert yourself.

I told him, "I'm proud of you."

I was actually a bit surprised at myself when I said it. I don't think I've ever spoken to them like that all these years. After hanging up, I just sat there for a while.

You know, you’ve always been this way over the years—staying by my side, staying by theirs, never once complaining about my temper. Back then, I was always so obsessed with controlling everything and making sure they were the best, but it only pushed them further away. You saw things more clearly than I did.

I was thinking that maybe after I retire, we could spend more time with them. Not to check up on how they’re doing, but just to be there. What do you think?

Also, shall we go to the hospital together this weekend to recheck your blood pressure? I want to take good care of you.

It suddenly occurred to me that for these forty years, I’ve been so busy trying to do things right that I never really stopped to think about why I was doing them. It wasn't until you went to the hospital with me the other day that I realized—some things are more important than being perfect.

ranked_items.content:
# Memo

**Date:** July 12, 2023, 7:00 PM

**Subject:** A thought regarding our travel plans

---

Today, while discussing retirement travel arrangements with Meifang, I said something: "We don't need a frantic itinerary. We could stay in one place for two or three days and just take it slow."

After I said it, I paused, surprised at myself.

That doesn't sound like me. At least, not the old me.

I was always the one whose schedules had to be precise to the hour, whose trips had to maximize sightseeing, and whose efficiency had to be peak. Wasting two or three days in one spot? That sounded like a waste of one's life. That is how I have always thought.

But now I am wondering if I have misunderstood something.

Lately, I’ve been spending more time talking with Meifang. She talks about family matters, our daughter’s recent work, and the neighbor’s kids. In the past, I would listen while my mind drifted to the work at hand. Now, sometimes I put everything down and just sit there listening to her. Sometimes no response is needed; it’s just about being there. That feeling—I finally understand now—is not a waste of time.

It’s the same with chess. A weekly game with my friends can sometimes last three or four hours. There is small talk, there is silence, and there are those long pauses for thought. I used to think of it as my only form of relaxation. Now I think it might be life itself.

So why should travel be any different?

If we stay in a small town for two or three days—not rushing, not checking off landmarks, but just sitting somewhere having tea, watching how the locals live, or watching nothing at all and just being by Meifang’s side—that isn't "low efficiency." This might be the very thing I’ve been chasing but never found.

I spent 40 years optimizing production processes and eliminating waste. That was right—it was right for the factory. But life is not an assembly line.

Perhaps quality is not about density, but about depth.

I need to remember this thought. After I retire, I might forget, or I might start planning the old way again. So, I am writing it down.

---

*Note: Tomorrow, look for some suitable places with Meifang. Let her choose.*

ranked_items.content:
After returning home that evening, Chen Guilin changed his clothes and sat down at the dining table. Li Meifang was serving the soup.

He ate a few mouthfuls of rice in silence before putting down his chopsticks.

"I might have been too harsh at the department meeting today," he said, his voice low, eyes fixed on his bowl. "The new QC inspector messed up the data entry again, and I called him out in front of everyone. He looked pretty shaken."

Li Meifang looked up at him but didn't respond immediately.

"My temper has been short lately," he paused, tapping his finger on the table. "I can feel it myself. I have high standards for the work, but..." He didn't finish the sentence, opting for a sip of soup instead.

He turned to his wife, his tone tinged with uncertainty. "Do you think I'm being too demanding? I want to get things done right, but it seems like..." He hesitated, "it seems like I always end up making people uncomfortable."

He wasn't looking for comfort; it was more like he was trying to confirm something. There was exhaustion in his eyes, along with a certain guilt he couldn't quite put into words.

"The physical exam is next week," he suddenly changed the subject, though his tone remained heavy. "I need to get to bed early these next few days."

He lowered his head and continued eating, but his movements were much slower than usual.

ranked_items.content:
June 15, 2022, 7:30 PM

I lost again at the chess room this afternoon. It wasn't that I lost to Player A; I lost to myself.

Player A said I played very conservatively today. The word he used was "cautious," but I could hear what he really meant—he was saying I’ve changed. I didn’t argue. I just said, "I’m getting older, I have to be more careful," and kept setting up the pieces. He didn’t say anything else.

But I know he saw it. I saw it in myself, too.

I used to dare to take risks, to make bold moves in the mid-game. And now? Now I pause and think for a long time before every single step. It’s not because I’ve become smarter; it’s because I’m afraid—afraid that one wrong move will lead to a point of no return. Afraid of wasting time.

It’s strange. A game only lasts two hours, and losing is just losing a game. But I find myself applying this same logic to everything in my life. I’m stricter with my subordinates at work, I’m more controlling over Meifang’s diet at home, and I even use a magnifying glass to scrutinize the numbers on my medical reports.

Why?

I tell myself this is being responsible. But deep down, I know it’s fear. Fear that time is running out. Fear that something is slipping through my fingers and I won’t be able to catch it in time.

The tools in the workshop are still there. The notebooks are there, too. I’ve recorded about half of it. Hao is in Shenzhen, and Yu is in Guangzhou. Neither of them has ever asked what those tools are for. I haven't brought it up, either.

Sometimes I wonder, living this cautiously—what exactly am I trying to protect?

I have to go back to the chess room tomorrow. I wonder if Player A will notice something else.

Forget it, stop thinking. Time for bed. I haven't finished going through the medical report from May; I’ll give it another careful look tomorrow.

ranked_items.content:
# October 8, 2022, 8:15 PM

Sitting in my study, the work log by my hand is nearly finished. Flipping to the last few pages, I realized the nature of my entries has changed. They are no longer just technical data and process optimizations; instead, I’ve started writing these inexplicable sentences—"If I could do it over, I would teach the younger generation more."

Looking at those words, even I find them unfamiliar.

This isn't like me. I’ve never been one for such sentimentality. But lately, I have been wondering: over these forty years, I’ve drafted so many improvement plans and brought the defect rates down, but what is actually left behind? A stack of reports? Some data? Or just the resentment in the hearts of those young engineers I criticized so bluntly?

Li Meifang was nagging about her blood pressure again today. I made a new diet plan for her; she didn't say anything, but that look in her eyes... I know what she’s thinking. After all these years of marriage, we haven't communicated much, but the way she looks at me has changed. Perhaps she has sensed something, too.

In the office this afternoon, a new QC inspector asked me a technical question. As usual, I pointed out the flaws in his logic directly, but this time, I stopped myself. I paused, and instead, explained to him *why* it had to be done that way. He listened intently. In that moment, I thought, maybe... maybe I should do more of that.

It’s not that I’ve suddenly become soft. I still loathe inefficiency and ambiguity. But I’m beginning to realize that my value might not lie in how much knowledge I’ve documented, but in... whether I’ve actually helped others understand what a true work ethic looks like.

I’m considering holding a technical sharing session for the young engineers in the department before I retire. I never thought I’d want to do something like that. But now I feel that if I don’t do it now, the opportunity might be gone forever.

Why am I thinking this way? I can’t quite say.

Perhaps it’s because time has suddenly become tangible. I used to feel that time was something to be managed, just like a project. Now... now it feels like it’s slipping away at a speed I can no longer control.

I don’t want to admit it. But writing it down here makes it feel real.

Tomorrow is another day. There are still many tools that need organizing.

Meifang, are you asleep?

I just sent Chen Hao a message asking how he’s handling his mortgage pressure lately and if he’s considered adjusting his investment strategy. He just replied with a "Yeah, it’s fine for now," and that was it.

I’ve been thinking... maybe I’m falling into my old habits again. Insisting on giving him guidance. Do you think I’m always trying to "manage" other people’s business, and in doing so, I’m actually pushing them away?

Now that I’ve been retired for a couple of days, it’s starting to sink in—I think I’ve gotten used to interacting with people through "advice" and "plans." I was like that with my subordinates, and I’m the same with Chen Hao. Even you’ve told me that sometimes it feels like I’m giving you orders.

I’m not trying to be hard on myself. It’s just... now that work is gone, I’m suddenly seeing things more clearly. I always thought I was helping them, but in reality, maybe I was... how should I put it... trying to control things in my own way.

Chen Hao and his peers are all grown up now; they have their own ideas. I should be asking them how they’re doing, instead of jumping straight in with "this is what you should do."

Am I right?

ranked_items.content:
I told Meifang about it over dinner tonight.

Another formatting error cropped up in QA this morning. It wasn't a major issue, but I can't stand that kind of slackness when it comes to details. I was right there on the floor and called it out immediately; my voice might have been a bit loud. The subordinate looked pretty shaken, but I had to make it clear—details determine product quality, and product quality determines whether this company survives. I’m not exaggerating.

I told Meifang that I need to overhaul the department’s standard operating procedures. Just emphasizing it verbally isn't enough; it has to be documented with every step clearly defined. As she listened, she just looked at me that way, and I knew what she was thinking: *Here we go again.* But this time is different. I can feel that if things continue like this, the problems are only going to multiply.

She said, "Don't push yourself too hard." I didn't respond to that. After a few bites of food, I explained once more why these standards must be strictly enforced. She just nodded and kept eating.

The truth is, I know my subordinates are afraid of me. But what’s wrong with a little fear? Fear makes people diligent. I’ve been in this position for 12 years, and our defect rate is 25% lower than the industry average—that’s not because I have a pleasant temperament.

Lately, I just feel this constant urge to hurry. Whether it’s work or anything else. While I’m still able to move, I need to get everything done right.

ranked_items.content:
# Workshop Notebook

**August 21, 2022 – Evening**

I’m sitting in the workshop, still holding that pair of pliers. Meifang is downstairs; she’s probably clearing the dinner table. I didn’t go down to help her, just like always.

But today is different.

During dinner, she said something. I can still hear her voice—"We are living together, not so we can spend every day in fear." Her eyes were red, but she didn't cry; she just looked at me.

I didn’t know how to respond. I never do.

For these past 35 years, I thought I was protecting her. Monitoring her diet, her blood pressure, her medical checkups... I thought that was love. But sitting beside her, I suddenly understood that I’ve actually been torturing her. My fear—my fear of loss—I’ve been crushing her under the weight of all of it.

I am afraid of losing her. For the first time, I’ve admitted it this bluntly. Not through actions, not through control, but by actually saying the words: "I’m just afraid of losing you."

She cried. We sat there for a long, long time, saying nothing.

I don’t know what this has changed. Or if it changed anything at all. My body is still aging, the medical reports are still lying there, I’m still retiring in two years, and I still don’t know what I’ll do after I retire. None of that has changed.

But something has shattered. That wall I spent 40 years building. The thing I built out of indifference, control, precise data, and plans. It’s broken now, and I’m standing among the shards, feeling naked and powerless.

I cannot stop time by recording it. I cannot guarantee safety through control. I cannot prove my worth through perfection.

I’m starting to wonder what I’ve been doing all these years.

The tools in the workshop are still there. Dozens of them, arranged neatly. No one will ever use them. My children won't. Maybe I should donate them. Maybe that’s where they belong.

I don’t know where I belong.

It’s 7:30 PM. Meifang is probably downstairs waiting for me. I should go down. But I’m sitting here, gripping these pliers, not wanting to move.

I’m afraid that if I go down, I’ll see her face. Afraid that I’ll retreat into silence again. Afraid that everything will go back to the way it was.

But I’m also afraid that it won’t.

I don’t know which is more terrifying.

371f19b5-6a8a-422b-ab77-bcee941d1a29.question: Manager Chen, looking back over the past year or so, you used to rely so heavily on precise data and strict processes to manage your life. Now, what do you think the sense of "being in control" truly means to a person?

ranked_items.content:
July 5, 2022, 6:45 PM

Had a call with Hao. He said the real estate market has been a bit cold lately, but he’s holding on. I asked him if he’d ever thought about learning something about mechanics—maybe it could be a hobby later on. He laughed and said, "Dad, what are you talking about? I don’t have the brain for that." I didn't push it further.

After hanging up, I sat in the workshop for a long time.

Actually, that wasn't what I wanted to ask. What I wanted to ask was—has he ever thought about what will happen to all these tools, all these things I’ve spent forty years learning? But I couldn't get the words out. Just like I never can.

During our call this afternoon, I told him "everything is under control." Thinking back on that phrase now, it feels a bit ridiculous. What does "under control" even mean? What am I controlling? Hao is in Shenzhen worrying about his mortgage, Yu is busy with work in Guangzhou, and here I am, documenting the uses of these tools as if I’m in a race against time. But time isn't racing me—it doesn't care about me at all.

Li Meifang is downstairs watching TV. I can hear her coughing. It’s time to nag her about going to the hospital to check her blood pressure again. These are things I can control. But what’s the use of controlling them?

The tool records in my notebook are halfway finished. With every entry I write, I wonder—who is going to use this? Hao? No. Yu? Even less likely. So why am I writing all this?

Maybe it’s just to keep myself from being idle. To tell myself there’s still something left to do.

The physical exam report from May is still in the drawer. A few of the indicators fluctuated a bit. The doctor said it’s nothing serious, just needs regular follow-ups. But I keep taking it out, looking at those numbers over and over. As if I could find some hidden meaning in them.

I’ve never known how to talk to them. That’s the truth. It’s been that way since they were little. I thought it would get better as they grew up, but it seems to have gotten worse. Now, I even self-censor before asking a simple question, terrified of saying something that might cause awkwardness.

Maybe I should have started teaching them earlier. But back then, I was busy with work. Now that I want to teach, it’s already too late.

Tomorrow, I’ll continue documenting the tools. There are still over thirty items left to record.

I should do it while I can still move.

ranked_items.content:
# MEMO

**Date:** July 14, 2023, Afternoon
**Location:** Chess and Card Room
**Recorded by:** Chen Guilin

---

## Key Dialogue Record

**Chess Friend A:** "Old Chen, you’ve been talking more lately. What’s on your mind?"

**My Answer:** "I’ve been thinking about retirement. But not about how to idle away the time—rather, how to live with more meaning."

**Chess Friend B:** "So, do you have an answer yet?"

**My Thoughts (didn't say it all at the time):** 
I can’t control everything. But I can choose how I live.

---

## The Turning Point of This Conversation

I realized I did something in the card room I wouldn't normally do—I opened up. It wasn't just small talk; I truly spoke from the heart. My friends listened intently, without interrupting.

It got me thinking: perhaps all these years I’ve spent too much energy on control—controlling quality, controlling processes, controlling risks. But life itself cannot be controlled. All I can control is my attitude and my choices.

---

## Key Points for the Lecture

- Precision tools are more than just tools; they represent the pursuit of excellence.
- This pursuit can be passed down to the next generation, and it doesn’t have to be through bloodlines.
- My daughter teaches students, my son builds cities, and I manufacture products—everyone is passing something on.
- That is enough.

---

## To-Do List

- [ ] Organize the list of tools to be donated to the technical school.
- [ ] Think about the opening for the lecture—maybe start with "I can't control everything."
- [ ] Talk to Meifang about these thoughts this weekend.
- [ ] Call Chen Hao and Chen Yu to see how they are doing lately.

---

**Notes:** 
My energy was good this afternoon; I didn't feel tired. I haven't felt this way in a long time. Perhaps it’s because I finally have a clear direction, rather than just planning for the void of retirement.

ranked_items.content:
Meifang, I’ve been thinking about what you said.

I’ve been thinking about it ever since we got back from our walk.

You’re right. I’ve been in denial. I’ve used management, data, and schedules to deny it. Just like I do at the factory—as if as long as the process is rigorous enough, everything can be controlled. But the body isn't a machine. I know that.

I monitor your diet and push you to get checkups, but I’m actually just deflecting. Deflecting my own fear. I’ve been this way for years—replacing words with actions, and replacing companionship with control. I’ve done it to you, and I’ve done it to the kids.

I owe you an apology. I owe you so much more than that.

Shall we go for a walk together next week? Not to supervise you, but just... to be with you. The way you’ve always been there for me.

I still have a lot to learn. From you.

— Chen Guilin

ranked_items.content:
# Memo - Reflection and Legacy

**Date: October 18, 2022, Afternoon**
**Location: Home Study**

---

## Core Points Summary

### I. Reflections on the Essence of 40 Years of Work
- ~~Perfect quality control~~ → Obsession with detail is a value in itself
- ~~Sense of achievement from process optimization~~ → The patience and persistence learned through the process
- A 25% drop in defect rates is not the finish line, but a reminder: Why do I demand so much?
  - Answer: Because I believe in standards.
  - The real answer: Because I am afraid of losing control.

### II. Beyond the Technical (Important)
- The meaning of tools lies not in collecting them, but in how they taught me to think.
- What precision work taught me:
  - A single millimeter determines success or failure.
  - However, the distance between people cannot be measured in millimeters.
  - (Thinking back on my harshness toward subordinates and my "supervision" of my wife over the years.)
- I must admit: I cannot control everything.
  - The doctor's words are still ringing in my ears.
  - The body ages; plans change.
  - This is not failure.

### III. If I Could Start Over
- Explain to the team *why* we do things earlier, rather than just telling them *what* to do.
- Give young engineers more room to make mistakes (instead of blunt dismissals).
- ~~Keeping knowledge in my head~~ → Actively record, share, and discuss.
- Listen more to my wife, rather than "supervising" from the sidelines.
- Communicate with the children about more than just their mortgages and jobs.

### IV. Words for Young Engineers
- Standards are important, but don't let them become a weapon.
- Details determine quality, but details can also consume your life—learn to prioritize.
- 40 years is a long time, but it is also very short.
- The things you take for granted now will become mere memories by the time you reach my age.
- Don't wait for your heart to remind you—some things are more important than perfection.

### V. Immediate Action Plan
- [ ] Compile a "Core Experiences in Quality Management" document (for technical school students).
- [ ] Sit down with my subordinates for a talk—explain rather than accuse.
- [ ] Have a real conversation with my wife this weekend; listen to her talk about home.
- [ ] Call Chen Hao and Chen Yu respectively; do not mention work.
- [ ] Continue medication and scheduled check-ups—accept this reality.

---

## Notes
- The precision tool inventory can be set aside for now.
- The 40-year timeline is drawn; now it needs to be turned into a story.
- Perhaps retirement isn't the end, but a different kind of beginning?
- (I wouldn't have believed that sentence before.)

---

**One final question: What is my greatest regret in life?**
It isn't that I didn't reach a higher position.
It’s that I never truly looked at the people beside me.
ranked_items.subquestion: Manager Chen, looking back over the past year or so, you used to rely so heavily on precise data and strict processes to manage your life. Now, what do you think the sense of "being in control" truly means to a person?

ranked_items.content:
Are you asleep?

I was just in the study tidying up my tools, thinking about what you said. You’re right—I really have changed.

For years, I always felt that being responsible meant keeping everything under control—with you, the kids, and my work. I only realized later that it wasn't responsibility; it was a lack of trust. I didn't trust that you could take care of yourself, or that they could make their own decisions.

It wasn't until that incident with the doctor that I truly understood—there are some things I simply cannot control. Through it all, you were the one who stayed, never once looking down on me.

Thank you for being by my side.

Let’s take things slow, together.

ranked_items.content:
# MEMO

**Date: September 19, 2022, Morning**
**Location: Office Cubicle**

---

Maybe I should change my ways.

The improvement plan Xiao Wang submitted during yesterday’s meeting was actually quite good. At the time, I pointed out three flaws directly. His expression… I can still see it now. He didn’t push back; he just nodded and took notes. When I walked past his desk at the end of the day, he was packing up—his movements were hurried, as if he were escaping something.

Is this right?

I’ve always believed that being strict is being responsible. A 25% reduction in defect rates—those are the facts. But what lies behind the data? It’s my subordinates working in fear under my gaze. Li Meifang once said, "You’re the same way at home." I didn't respond.

Control ≠ Respect.

Where did that phrase come from yesterday? Perhaps it was something the doctor said that triggered it. He talked about my blood pressure, my carotid artery, all those numbers… I’ve spent 40 years trying to control everything, yet my body has betrayed me anyway. I cannot control it. So what about my subordinates? I "manage" them the same way, as if I were managing a machine. But they aren't machines.

Time is running out. What actually matters?

It’s not the process optimization forms. It’s not those perfect quality inspection reports. If I were to collapse right now, who would remember how strict I was? Who would think of me because of a 25% lower defect rate?

Regarding Xiao Wang’s proposal—I actually should have praised him in front of everyone. And *then* pointed out the room for improvement.

I need to rethink how I speak to my staff.

Maybe there’s still time.

---

*To be continued*

ranked_items.content:
Meifang, are you there?

I just stepped out of the meeting room. I handled things a bit differently today. There was an issue with the data from one of my subordinates, and instead of my usual "this is unacceptable," I sat down and went through it with him, asking how he thought we could improve it. He looked a bit surprised. I talked with him privately for a while afterward, too.

I’ve been thinking about what you said. "I knew you’d have this sooner or later"—when did you first notice?

I’ve been wondering lately if maybe I’ve had it wrong all along. Managing work, managing my health, managing everything... it feels like no matter how precise I am, it doesn't really protect me from anything. Have you noticed anything different about me lately?

No need to rush to reply. Just wanted to ask.

ranked_items.content:
Haozi, how have you been lately?

I’ve been thinking, and I wanted to share a few words with you. This recent health scare has given me a lot to reflect on. The doctor said I need to stay on medication and slow down. With your mom here by my side, I’ve realized that all these years, I’ve been constantly rushing—rushing to keep everything under control, to make everything perfect.

Regarding your mortgage in Shenzhen, I know I used to pester you with questions, almost like I was auditing your books. Looking back, that wasn't right. You’re an adult now, and you have your own plans. I thought I was "helping" you in my own way, but in reality, I was just putting pressure on you.

I’ve only recently come to understand that maybe life doesn't need to be perfectly controlled. In my forty years of work, I always pursued "zero defects," but life isn't an assembly line. There are things you just can't control, and there’s pressure you have to learn to let go of. Otherwise, you’ll end up like me—waiting until your sixties to regret what you’ve missed out on.

I’m not telling you this so you’ll follow in my footsteps. On the contrary, I hope you can live a more relaxed life than I did. A mortgage is just a mortgage, and a job is just a job; don't keep yourself wound so tight. Taking time for family and getting proper rest—those things matter, too.

Your mom says I’ve become quite talkative lately, ha. Maybe I really am changing.

If you’re free this weekend, give me a call. Let’s have a good chat.

— Dad

ranked_items.content:
Tuesday afternoon, October 18, 2022

I spent the entire afternoon sitting in my small workshop. I didn’t clean any tools or organize my inventory. I just flipped through my notebooks, page by page.

What was I recording back then? Screwdriver models, wrench specifications, the purchase year and maintenance cycle for every single tool. Everything was neat and tidy, precise to a fault. Looking at these records used to give me a sense of peace—as if I had mastered something.

But today, I’m wondering: what did these tools actually teach me?

It wasn't how to use them. That’s too simple. It was... how to approach a task. With seriousness. Without compromise. Knowing that every detail matters. For forty years, the products I made with these tools had a defect rate 25% lower than the industry average. That wasn't a coincidence. It was because I never allowed myself to slack off.

But—

I wrote that word on a new page, then crossed it out. Wrote it again. Crossed it out again.

But I treated people the same way. My subordinates, my family. I demanded perfection and left no room for error. I thought that was called being responsible. Now, I’m not so sure.

Li Meifang has been humming in the kitchen lately. I haven't heard her hum like that in a long time. She says it’s because I’ve finally "loosened up a bit." I can hear it in her voice—it wasn't a compliment. It was... relief? She feels relieved.

That hurts a little.

I turned to a blank page and drew a timeline with a pencil. From 1982 to now. Forty years. I marked the key years: technician, team leader, department manager. I did well at every stage. Defect rates, process optimizations, the proposals that were adopted. It’s a beautiful report card.

But so what?

Chen Hao is in Shenzhen, under a lot of pressure. I keep track of his mortgage and his performance, but we barely have anything to say to each other. When Chen Yu comes home occasionally, our conversations are like status reports—how’s work, is your marriage stable. I thought I was teaching them about perseverance and quality awareness, but I was never truly there for them.

This is my regret. Perhaps my greatest regret.

Next to the timeline, I wrote: "If I could do it over, what would I do?"

But I know there are no "ifs." I only have the now. And about two years left of work. Maybe in these two years, I should record some things. Not tool lists, not process documents. But... what I was thinking during these forty years. Why I did things the way I did. Young people might need that.

Or rather, I need to do this. To forgive myself.

Meifang is calling me for dinner. I hear her voice drifting up from downstairs—gentle, not rushing me. I close the notebook and stand up.

Maybe I should listen to her more. Really listen, instead of mentally calculating her blood pressure and diet.

Maybe that is what I really need to learn.
ranked_items.subquestion: Manager Chen, looking back over the past year or so, you used to rely so heavily on precise data and strict processes to manage your life. Now, what do you think the sense of "being in control" truly means to a person?

ranked_items.content:
September 19, 2022, 10:47 PM

Something strange happened in the office today.

During this afternoon's meeting, Xiao Wang from Quality Control handed in a report with problematic data. Usually, I would have said bluntly, "This is unacceptable. Redo it." But in that moment, I paused. I looked at his nervous expression and a thought suddenly hit me—is he afraid of something, too?

Instead, I said, "Let’s take a look together and see where things went wrong."

There was a moment of silence in the conference room. I could feel everyone looking at me. Xiao Wang’s shoulders relaxed a little.

After the meeting, I wrote in my notebook: "Maybe I should change my approach."

But now, sitting here in my workshop looking at these tools, I’m not sure if this is progress or a retreat.

The doctor’s words are still ringing in my ears: "Long-term medication, regular check-ups." I always thought that as long as I was strict and cautious enough, I could control everything. My work, my health—everything. But clearly, I was wrong. My body doesn't follow my orders. I can’t protect myself through perfect management.

Maybe what my subordinates see isn't my high standards, but my fear.

My wife said, "I knew you’d get this sooner or later." What did she mean by that? Did she see it coming all along? What about everyone else? I thought I had hidden it so well.

I don’t know how to tell the kids. Chen Hao is under so much pressure with his mortgage, and Chen Yu is busy with work in Guangzhou. Telling them would only worry them. But if I say nothing, isn't that a form of deception?

My hands are still shaking. I wiped down my tools, then wiped them again.

Maybe I’ve been using control to mask my vulnerability. Now that the vulnerability is exposed, I don’t know what’s left.

I have to go to the hospital tomorrow to pick up my prescription.

ranked_items.content:
# Memo

**Date**: Wednesday, August 24, 2022, 14:00

**Subject**: Reflections following a game of chess

---

## Record

During today’s game with Player A, he said something that stuck with me: "Instead of spending time fearing the future, spend it cherishing the present."

I didn't respond at the time, but I haven't stopped thinking about it since.

## Issues to Sort Out

1. **What am I doing?**
   - My recent anxiety cycle: Prediction → Fear → Control → More Fear.
   - Since the physical exam results came back, I’ve been using data, plans, and logs to fight uncertainty.
   - But is this actually helpful? Or am I just wearing myself out?

2. **On Control**
   - What I can control: Workflows, financial accounts, daily schedules.
   - What I cannot control: Aging, fluctuating health metrics, my children’s choices, time itself.
   - I have been trying to fill the void of uncontrollable fears with things I can control.
   - What is the cost of doing this?

3. **On the Present**
   - For the past few months, I’ve been planning for a retirement that is two years away.
   - At the same time, I’m worrying about potential health issues.
   - The "present" I hold in my hands has been squeezed into something tiny.
   - My wife, the chess games, the tools in my workshop—they are all still here, but mentally, I’ve already let go of them.

4. **Defining Value**
   - I’ve always believed: Completion → Results → Proof of self-worth.
   - But what if things can’t be completed? What if the results are forgotten?
   - Perhaps value is more than just these things?

## For Further Reflection

- Why do I keep losing what I have now by worrying about what I might lose in the future?
- Is accepting the unchangeable an act of giving up, or an act of maturity?
- How much more time can I afford to waste on fear?

---

**Note**: When I play Player A again next week, I want to hear his take on "cherishing the present." Not as small talk, but because I truly want to understand.
ranked_items.subquestion: Manager Chen, looking back over the past year or so, you used to rely so heavily on precise data and strict processes to manage your life. Now, what do you think the sense of "being in control" truly means to a person?

ranked_items.content:
October 12, 2022, 7:30 PM

Had a phone call with Chen Hao.

In the past, I’d always ask him if the mortgage was a burden, how the orders were coming along, or if his job was stable. I don’t know what came over me today, but the first thing out of my mouth was, "How have you been lately? Is there anything I can help with?" He seemed taken aback; there was a two-second silence before he answered.

He said work is exhausting, that sales is high-pressure, and sometimes he wonders if he’s doing the right thing. He’s lost. He was very candid, and I could hear the fatigue in his voice.

Before, I would have jumped straight in with advice, telling him exactly what he should do. But this time, I just said, "I understand. I’ve been through a stage like that, too."

Even I was a bit surprised by my own words. In forty years, I’ve never spoken to him like that.

He asked me when that was. I told him that when I was his age, I wasn't sure if I was making the right choices either. Back then, I was a quality inspector at the grassroots level, doing the same repetitive work every day, unable to see a way out. What I didn’t tell him was that I just kept going down that same path ever since, never changing course again.

He didn’t respond immediately, but I could sense something had shifted. It wasn't that superior-subordinate dynamic anymore; it felt more like... two people who had walked the same road.

After hanging up, I sat at my desk and looked at that medical report. The doctor said I’ll need long-term medication and a lifestyle change. I’ve been thinking about how to reverse this outcome through stricter management, just like I do at work—precise control, eliminating variables.

But while talking to Chen Hao, it suddenly occurred to me that maybe some things can’t be controlled. Some paths, once walked, are simply walked.

I need to rethink how I interact with them. Not supervising, not guiding. Maybe just... being there?

The thought makes me a little uneasy. I don't know if I still know how to do it.

More tomorrow.

ranked_items.content:
# MEMO

**Date:** June 15, 2023, 6:45 PM  
**Subject:** Reimagining Continuity and Legacy

---

The farewell party ended this afternoon. Sitting in my office, looking at the empty desk, I found myself wondering: after forty years, what have I actually left behind?

I used to think the answer was clear: a defect rate 25% lower than the industry average, annual process improvement plans, and all those adopted proposals. These figures and achievements were proof enough of my value. But this afternoon, when that intern said with teary eyes, "Manager Chen, you taught me so much more than just technical skills," I suddenly realized I might have misunderstood everything.

Perhaps the act of passing things on is the purpose itself.

In the message I sent to Chen Yu, I wrote: "You are educating students, I am manufacturing products, and we are both pursuing excellence. I am proud of you." As I wrote those words, it finally clicked—I don't need her to inherit my job, nor do I need her to become another quality inspector. The fact that she pursues excellence in her own field is a form of continuity in itself. It’s not about bloodlines or obligation; it’s about everyone finding their own way to carry forward that obsession with precision and responsibility.

The tools will be donated to the school. This decision now feels like more than just disposing of old belongings; it’s another form of dialogue—letting those tools continue to serve a purpose in the hands of the young. I don’t need to see them become me; I only need to know that when they use those tools, they will remember that someone once spent an entire lifetime just to get things right.

The stories from the sharing session—how to spot a 0.02mm deviation, how to uphold standards on the assembly line, how to stay calm under pressure—these are not my intellectual property. They now belong to those who heard them. They will understand, improve, and spread them in their own way. That is enough.

I’m starting to feel that perhaps I’ve been defining "legacy" the wrong way. I thought a legacy had to be something visible, quantifiable, something that could be inherited in its entirety. But true continuity likely happens in those seemingly tiny moments—a sentence, a demonstration, a tool, a bit of persistence.

Starting tomorrow, I will no longer be the department manager. But that doesn't mean it's over. It’s just continuing in a different way.

This requires long-term reflection.

---

*Note: Think through the specific details of the donation while sorting through the tools with Li Meifang this weekend.*

ranked_items.content:
# MEMO

**Date: Saturday Morning, October 22, 2022**

Had a phone call with Chen Hao. After hearing about my diagnosis, his first instinct was to ask if I’d been taking my medicine on time and if he should take time off to come back. I didn't expect him to ask that.

Before this, I always felt like there was something standing between us. Our calls were always about work, the house, or whether they were eating well—like going through a checklist. Their answers were always brief. I thought that was just how we were.

But this time was different. When he said, "Dad, you need to take care of yourself," I heard something different in his voice. My own voice choked up a bit; all I could manage was, "Okay, thank you." That’s rare for me.

Li Meifang says the way I’ve shown concern for the kids all these years has been flawed—always supervising, never just being there. I used to disagree with her. But thinking about it now, maybe she’s right.

**A few things I need to figure out:**

1. Have a real talk with Chen Hao and Chen Yu individually before I retire. Not to ask about their careers or lives, but to truly listen to them. Maybe they have things they want to say to me, too.

2. What does "legacy" actually mean? I’ve always wanted to pass down my understanding of work and my obsession with quality to them. But they have their own lives. Maybe I should just record these things for them to reference, rather than expecting them to inherit it all.

3. The collaboration with the technical school—donating tools, doing sharing sessions. I want to do this even more now. Not to prove anything, but because I truly want to leave these things behind.

4. Have a proper talk with Li Meifang next week. She’s been by my side all these years, yet I’ve always been "managing" her. It’s time to change that.

**One more thought:**

Maybe the things I’ve done right over these forty years aren't the process improvements or the defect rate data. Maybe it’s that obsession with detail, that commitment to responsibility, and that care for my family—even if I haven't been great at expressing it. If Chen Hao and Chen Yu can see these things in me, that’s enough.

I don’t need them to become me. I just need them to know that I’ve always been here.

---

*Going to play chess with the guys this afternoon. My game hasn't been great lately; my mind keeps wandering. But maybe that’s okay—I don’t need to win. Just sitting there, being with them, is enough.*

ranked_items.content:
After returning home that evening, Chen Guilin changed his clothes and sat down at the dining table. Li Meifang was serving the soup.

He ate a few mouthfuls of rice in silence before putting down his chopsticks.

"I might have been too harsh at the department meeting today," he said, his voice low, eyes fixed on his bowl. "The new QC inspector messed up the data entry again, and I called him out in front of everyone. He looked pretty shaken."

Li Meifang looked up at him but didn't respond immediately.

"My temper has been short lately," he paused, tapping his finger on the table. "I can feel it myself. I have high standards for the work, but..." He didn't finish the sentence, opting for a sip of soup instead.

He turned to his wife, his tone tinged with uncertainty. "Do you think I'm being too demanding? I want to get things done right, but it seems like..." He hesitated, "it seems like I always end up making people uncomfortable."

He wasn't looking for comfort; it was more like he was trying to confirm something. There was exhaustion in his eyes, along with a certain guilt he couldn't quite put into words.

"The physical exam is next week," he suddenly changed the subject, though his tone remained heavy. "I need to get to bed early these next few days."

He lowered his head and continued eating, but his movements were much slower than usual.

ranked_items.content:
# Management Reflection Notes

**Date**: October 5, 2022, Afternoon
**Subject**: Findings from communication with the Quality Control (QC) team

---

While reviewing the Q3 defect rate data this afternoon, I asked Xiao Wang a question: "Do you think there’s another way to interpret this?"

The moment the question left my mouth, I even surprised myself.

Usually, I don’t ask things like that. My habit is to point out the problem, provide the solution, and demand execution. It’s the most efficient way. But for some reason today, instead of directly dismissing his line of reasoning, I felt like hearing what he had to say.

As it turned out, he offered a perspective I hadn't considered—that the fluctuations in Production Line B might be related to raw material batches, rather than just operational protocols. My previous analysis had completely overlooked this.

**Questions for Reflection:**
- Have I been managing the team with a "one right answer" mindset?
- Do diverse viewpoints actually improve decision quality, or do they just hinder efficiency?
- Are my subordinates withholding valuable ideas because they are afraid to contradict me?

**Potential Adjustments:**
1. Actively invite the team to offer differing perspectives before making key decisions.
2. Distinguish between "execution standards" and "problem analysis"—standards must be rigid, but the analytical process can be more open.
3. Provide explicit psychological safety to those who voice dissent (ensure they won't be dismissed just for having a different opinion).

**To Be Verified:**
- Will this approach impact the department's execution efficiency?
- Is there a need to establish "protocols for offering feedback" to prevent it from becoming a mere formality?
- Can improvements be observed in other similar scenarios?

---

*Note: This is not about relaxing quality requirements. Standards cannot be lowered. But perhaps I need to rethink—can rigorous results and an open process truly coexist?*
ranked_items.subquestion: Manager Chen, looking back over the past year or so, you used to rely so heavily on precise data and strict processes to manage your life. Now, what do you think the sense of "being in control" truly means to a person?

ranked_items.content:
October 16, 2022, Evening

It’s been two hours since I got back from the hospital. Sitting here in the study, I still find myself somewhat at a loss for words.

The results from this morning’s check-up were steadier than expected. The doctor said the medication is working well and my heart rate is stabilizing. I had prepared a mountain of data and questions to ask, but with Meifang sitting beside me, I suddenly changed my mind. I heard myself say, "We are both cooperating with the treatment"—a strange choice of words coming from me. In the past, I would have said, "I am strictly managing it," or "I am controlling my diet." But today was different.

As I held my wife’s hand, I realized what I’ve been doing to her all these years. I’ve been "monitoring" her—monitoring her blood pressure, her diet, her physical exams. It was as if she were a quality control metric for me to manage. Yet she never once resisted. She was just there, by my side.

The doctor suggested joining a health management course. My first instinct was to refuse—too busy with work, not enough time. But I looked at Meifang and didn't say no right away. I asked her, "What do you think?" Those four words felt foreign coming out of my mouth. She said we could give it a try. And so, it was decided.

I wonder if this is what they call "vulnerability." Admitting that I can’t control everything, that I can’t stop my body from aging, and that I can’t ensure every plan stays on schedule. It’s not a comfortable feeling. But it’s not as terrifying as I imagined, either.

Perhaps all these years, I’ve been using perfect self-discipline to prove my worth. Now, I’m starting to wonder: was it worth it?

Meifang is downstairs preparing dinner. I can hear the sounds from the kitchen. It’s quiet, ordinary. But today, it sounds different.

I haven’t quite figured out what all of this means yet. But at the very least, I’m no longer in such a hurry.
ranked_items.subquestion: Manager Chen, looking back over the past year or so, you used to rely so heavily on precise data and strict processes to manage your life. Now, what do you think the sense of "being in control" truly means to a person?

ranked_items.content:
Wife, I’ve been sitting here in my workspace, thinking.

I really took what you said at dinner to heart. Maybe over these years, I truly have been too... I don’t know how to put it. Too obsessed with those numbers and standards, and in the process, I lost sight of the things that actually matter.

I’m also processing what the doctor said. I know I can’t go on like this. Tomorrow, I’ll go get the prescription as he directed, and I’ll follow your lead regarding my diet. I won’t... argue with you like I used to.

And about work. Perhaps I have been too harsh on my subordinates. You’re right—being serious doesn’t have to mean being cold. I’ll try to see if I can improve.

Thank you for not saying "I told you so" tonight. You saw this coming all along, didn't you?

My head is still a bit of a mess, but I’m slowly figuring things out.

—Chen Guilin

ranked_items.content:
July 10, 2023, 8:30 PM

Hao called while I was organizing my precision tools. My hands were still stained with engine oil, but I picked up.

He said there’s a new opportunity in Shenzhen—a sales director position for a major project. It sounds promising, but his voice was thick with hesitation. I could hear it.

In the past, I would have told him exactly what to do. I’d list the pros and cons and hand down a judgment. But today, I didn’t. I asked him: "Are you excited about this opportunity, or are you scared?"

There was a long silence. Then he said, "Dad, I’m both. I’m scared I won’t do a good job, and I’m scared of the mortgage pressure getting worse."

Listening to him, it suddenly hit me—I’ve always been supervising him, not accompanying him. Just like how I treat my subordinates at the department. I thought that was being responsible, but it was really just control.

I told him: "Then give it a try. I’ll be right behind you."

I surprised even myself when I said it. It’s not my style. But it was true. I really want to stand behind him, rather than standing in front of him telling him which way to go.

His tone changed. He sounded a bit more relaxed. We talked for a while longer; he shared some project details. I didn’t rush to judge; I just listened, occasionally asking a question.

Before hanging up, I told him: "Hao, I’m proud of you."

The moment the words left my mouth, I felt a pang of regret—I’ve said things like that far too rarely. But he said, "Thanks, Dad," and there was warmth in his voice.

After putting the phone down, I sat there for a long time. I was still holding that pair of pliers my father left me.

I think maybe retirement isn’t about losing anything, but finally having the time to do the things that truly matter. Not the achievements that can be quantified, but—being there. Listening. Saying the things I’ve kept bottled up inside.

My tools, my process improvements, my standards for perfect quality... those are all important. But Hao doesn’t need a perfect father; he needs a real one. A father willing to admit he isn’t omnipotent.

I’ll keep organizing the tools tomorrow. This time, it’s not just for the donation, but to remember—some things are more important than precision.

---

Hao, something came to mind today that I wanted to share with you.

The company recently asked me to prepare an experience-sharing session—basically a summary of everything I’ve learned in quality management over the past 40 years. I was a bit resistant at first, thinking I didn't have much to say. But after giving it some thought, I realized there really are some things worth recording.

The mortgage pressure over in Shenzhen must still be quite heavy, right? I noticed you haven't mentioned work much on WeChat lately—is the market not doing so well? You don't have to hide it from me; if there’s anything you need help with, just say the word.

All these years, I’ve been trying to figure out how to pass my experience down to you kids, but it seems my approach wasn't quite right. This sharing session made me realize something—maybe what I can offer isn't about making you do the same kind of work, but rather passing on that sense of professional rigor and obsession with detail. You’re in real estate sales now, and you need those qualities too, don't you?

Are you free this weekend? Your mom and I want to hear how things are going. We’re not asking how much money you’re making; we just want to know how you are. I’d also like to catch up on how your sister is doing.

To be honest, I’m only just realizing that the way I showed my concern before might have been problematic. I was always asking if you’d hit your targets or settled down, instead of actually listening to you. After my recent check-up, the doctor talked to me a lot, and it finally clicked—maybe companionship and understanding are more important than trying to control everything.

Drop me a line when you can. How have you been lately?

---

WeChat messages to Chen Yu:

---

Yuyu, how have you been lately?

I wanted to tell you something. The company asked me to do a summary and presentation of my work experience recently, and I’ve been thinking that this is actually a rare opportunity. After all these years of trial and error in quality management, I do have some insights. But I realized that I’ve never really talked to you kids about these things properly—and I haven't really listened to your thoughts, either.

In your work in education, you probably also need that same dedication to detail and adherence to standards. It seems like what I’ve learned over the years could be useful for your work, too. But more importantly, I want to hear your perspective—what kind of education do you think is the right way?

My recent health check-up flagged a few minor issues, and the doctor said I need to take care of myself. Your mom has been looking after me, which has given me a lot of time to think. I’ve realized that the way I’ve cared for you all these years might have been off—it was always about supervising rather than being there for you. I’m sorry.

When do you have time to come home? No need to take special leave; I just want to sit down and have a real talk with you. About your work, your life, and... some of the things I’ve been thinking about lately.

Write back when you can.
