Date: March 28, 2022, 21:45

---

**To-Do: Conversation with Chen Hao**

Status: Incomplete / Needs Rescheduling

I called Chen Hao tonight. I originally intended to ask if he had any interest in mechanical engineering and if he’d like to learn some basic craftsmanship. But when the words were on the tip of my tongue, I held back. Instead, I just asked how work was going. He said the mortgage pressure has been heavy lately and the market is unstable.

I didn't push further after hearing that.

Later, in the workshop, I replayed the conversation in my head. The questions are—

1. Is he truly uninterested, or is it that I’ve never properly talked to him about these things?
2. If I start teaching him now, is it too late? Look at how old he is already.
3. How can I bring it up next time without it feeling abrupt?

The items in the tool cabinet have been sitting there for so many years. Some were left by my father; others I collected myself. They are still high-precision instruments, wiped and maintained regularly. But if no one uses them, maintenance is pointless.

**Timing for Next Attempt:**
- Talk face-to-face when he comes home? Or stick to the phone?
- Start with his work stress first, then bring it up?

Finished the first 30 items on the tool list. About 20-plus items left to organize. I need to clearly write down the purpose, precision grade, and maintenance method for each. I need to hurry up with this.

While I’m still mobile, I need to get these things sorted out.

---

**Misc:**
Li Meifang ate salted fish again today; said a friend gave it to her. Her blood pressure has been fluctuating again this month. Must urge her to go for a check-up next week.

# Work Memo

**Date**: October 28, 2022  
**Subject**: Reflections on a New Approach to Feedback  
**Recorded by**: Chen Guilin  

---

## Record of Events

This morning, I discussed the process plan for a new product with a junior engineer from our department. While there were some lapses in rigor in his proposal, I refrained from my usual habit of pointing out the flaws directly.

**What I did differently**:
1. First, I acknowledged specific areas where he did well—the data collection was thorough, and the conceptual framework was clear.
2. I explained why precision control is critical at this stage, particularly regarding its impact on subsequent costs and yield.
3. Instead of providing the answer, I asked him: "Let’s look into this together—is there anything else we need to consider here?"
4. He identified the problem himself and even added a perspective I hadn't considered.

**Result**: His motivation was visibly boosted. He took the initiative to look up relevant materials after the meeting and returned this afternoon to discuss the improved plan.

---

## Reflections

This approach is a significant departure from my previous style. I used to be blunt about pointing out issues for the sake of efficiency. Looking back, however, that kind of feedback might lead people to passively accept criticism rather than truly think for themselves.

This time was different. He was involved in the thought process, which gave him more confidence in the final conclusion. More importantly, he didn't just learn the answer to this specific problem; he learned **how to analyze problems**.

---

## Follow-up Plans

If this method proves effective, I want to systematize it:

- Implement a new feedback framework within the department (Specific Praise → Explanation of Principles → Open Discussion).
- Compile a "Technical Mentorship Guide" in preparation for the knowledge hand-off before my retirement.
- Potentially organize a technical sharing session at the end of the year using this interactive format instead of a one-way lecture.

The benefit of this is that knowledge isn't just memorized—it is understood and internalized. Perhaps this is what true "legacy" looks like.

---

## Remarks

This idea stems from some recent reflections. My health is a reminder that I won't be in this position forever. Before I leave, can I ensure these young people inherit not just my standards, but my way of thinking? That might be more valuable than any procedural document.

I will continue to observe the results next week.

ranked_items.content:
June 15, 2022, 7:30 PM

I lost again at the chess room this afternoon. It wasn't that I lost to Player A; I lost to myself.

Player A said I played very conservatively today. The word he used was "cautious," but I could hear what he really meant—he was saying I’ve changed. I didn’t argue. I just said, "I’m getting older, I have to be more careful," and kept setting up the pieces. He didn’t say anything else.

But I know he saw it. I saw it in myself, too.

I used to dare to take risks, to make bold moves in the mid-game. And now? Now I pause and think for a long time before every single step. It’s not because I’ve become smarter; it’s because I’m afraid—afraid that one wrong move will lead to a point of no return. Afraid of wasting time.

It’s strange. A game only lasts two hours, and losing is just losing a game. But I find myself applying this same logic to everything in my life. I’m stricter with my subordinates at work, I’m more controlling over Meifang’s diet at home, and I even use a magnifying glass to scrutinize the numbers on my medical reports.

Why?

I tell myself this is being responsible. But deep down, I know it’s fear. Fear that time is running out. Fear that something is slipping through my fingers and I won’t be able to catch it in time.

The tools in the workshop are still there. The notebooks are there, too. I’ve recorded about half of it. Hao is in Shenzhen, and Yu is in Guangzhou. Neither of them has ever asked what those tools are for. I haven't brought it up, either.

Sometimes I wonder, living this cautiously—what exactly am I trying to protect?

I have to go back to the chess room tomorrow. I wonder if Player A will notice something else.

Forget it, stop thinking. Time for bed. I haven't finished going through the medical report from May; I’ll give it another careful look tomorrow.

ranked_items.content:
# Work Log - Call Record with Chen Hao

**Date:** July 3, 2023  
**Time:** 19:30–20:15 (approx. 45 mins)  
**Method:** Video Call  
**Participant:** Chen Hao  

---

## Key Points of the Call

### 1. Chen Hao’s Financial Situation
- Significant pressure from monthly mortgage payments; current monthly income is fluctuating noticeably (typical of the real estate sales industry).
- Performance has declined over the past two months; he is somewhat concerned about his future ability to keep up with mortgage payments.
- His wife is preparing for pregnancy, so household expenses are expected to increase soon.
- He mentioned considering a career change but is worried about the risks of switching industries.

### 2. My Communication Approach
During this call, I changed my usual approach. Instead of offering direct advice or criticism, I started by asking: **"I’d like to hear your thoughts first. What do you feel is your biggest challenge right now?"**

Chen Hao seemed a bit surprised, but he began to open up in detail—more deeply than in any of our previous phone conversations. He mentioned doubts about his own abilities and also spoke about his wife’s support.

### 3. Chen Hao’s Reaction
- His tone was more relaxed than usual, and he was more willing to express his true feelings.
- His voice choked up slightly when talking about his wife.
- At the end, he said: "Dad, thank you for actually listening to me this time."

That sentence made me pause for a long time.

### 4. Advice Given
- Suggested he stabilize his current job for now and not rush into a career change.
- Offered to help him create a simple family financial plan to see exactly how much pressure he is under.
- Told him that we can help cover a portion of the expenses for the pregnancy and the newborn.

### 5. Self-Evaluation
**What I did right in this conversation:**
- I didn't rush to say "you should do this or that."
- I truly listened instead of mentally preparing a rebuttal.
- I acknowledged my own limitations and invited him to participate in solving the problem rather than passively receiving instructions.

**Points for reflection:**
- Why haven't I spoken to him like this in all these years?
- Has my approach made him feel like there was too much distance between us?

---

## Follow-up Actions
- [ ] Prepare a simple family financial planning template this week and send it to Chen Hao.
- [ ] Discuss with Li Meifang whether we can provide some financial support if Chen Hao needs it.
- [ ] Ask about Chen Yu’s recent situation during the next call using the same method—listen first, talk later.

---

**Recorded by:** Chen Guilin  
**Notes:** This call made me realize that perhaps I have been controlling him under the guise of "taking care" of him. I am trying a different way now. I’m not sure if I can keep it up.

ranked_items.content:
Meifang, I told my boss something today. I wanted to talk to you about it first.

We have a meeting this afternoon to discuss next year’s plans, so I went to see him beforehand. I told him I want to organize an experience-sharing session before I retire—a way to summarize everything I’ve learned about quality management over the past forty years and share it with the people at the company. It’s nothing grand; I just want to document what I’ve learned, the pitfalls I’ve encountered, and how certain standards were established.

He was quite supportive and said it was a great idea.

To be honest, this wasn't what I originally had in mind. Do you remember? A while back, I was so preoccupied with how to pass my technical skills down to the younger generation; I felt such a sense of urgency. But after talking to the doctor a few times and starting the medication, I’ve slowly come to realize something. It’s not so much about "passing the torch" as it is about me wanting to properly sort through everything I’ve done all these years. It’s about giving myself a sense of closure.

This isn't just for others. It’s mostly for myself. Forty years in this position—what did I learn? What did I improve? Why did I do things the way I did? I want to think these through clearly, and I want others to know, too.

What do you think? Am I overthinking things?

The truth is, I feel that if I do this, I won't feel so empty when I retire. If these things are recorded and spoken aloud, they won't just disappear with me.

How is your blood pressure lately? Did you take your medicine on time today?

---

(Added in the evening)

Oh, I just remembered. You once said that I’ve spent all these years being too anxious to control everything, and that sometimes I don't need to be so tense. I think this sharing session is exactly like that. I’m not trying to force anyone to change; I just want to tell the story of my experiences over the years. As for the rest, I’ll leave it to others to interpret for themselves.

Thinking about it this way actually makes me feel a bit more relaxed.

You’ve been by my side for so many years. Sometimes I think I should listen to your thoughts more often, instead of just deciding everything on my own.

Thank you.

ranked_items.content:
In the workshop, Chen Guilin sat on a simple wooden stool, a notebook open before him. On the tool rack, the precision instruments glinted coldly under the fluorescent lights. His phone screen lit up—a WeChat message from his wife, Li Meifang: "What do you want for dinner?"

He didn't reply immediately. Instead, he stared at the dense notes in his notebook and the words "Who would use this?" circled in red ink.

Five minutes later, he finally typed:

"Do you think... there's any point in me keeping these records?"

After sending it, he added another line:

"Just thinking about what happens after I retire. These tools will just be sitting there anyway."

He paused for a long time, stopping himself from typing more. His fingers hovered over the keyboard before he finally lowered them.

Then he added one last message:

"I’ve been feeling a bit tired lately. Not the work kind of tired."

---

*Chen Guilin put down his phone and turned back to wiping a precision vernier caliper on the rack. His movements were slow and meticulous, but his eyes were no longer on the tool.*

ranked_items.content:
# Monday, July 18, 2022, 14:30

It’s been two hours since I got the report.

I’m sitting in my office with the report spread out on the desk. I told everyone "it’s nothing serious," but my fingers keep tracing those marked spots—blood pressure 139/92, fasting blood glucose 6.8. The numbers themselves don’t lie. I know that better than anyone.

I heard everything the doctor said, but I chose to remember only the first half of his sentences. "It’s under decent control." The rest—the parts about "needing to be more cautious" and "regular follow-ups"—I just let slide. That’s not my style.

Meifang was cooking when I got home. She saw the look on my face and asked about it. I didn't give her a straight answer, just said "I'm fine." But I know she can tell. After 35 years, she knows me too well. She tried to come closer, wanting me to talk, but I turned away and went into the study.

Then I suddenly turned back around and said to her, "Starting today, you need to cooperate too. We can't let your blood pressure issues drag on anymore. I’m going to set up a plan for you."

She was startled by my tone. I saw it. But I didn't stop. I need to control something. I need to ensure that something is within my grasp.

She isn't the problem. I know that. But at this moment, I can't face the fact that *I* am the problem.

Data doesn't lie. I’ve lived my whole life by data and standards, and now the data is starting to betray me. Blood pressure, blood sugar, weight—every indicator is slowly crossing the line. I can see the trend. I can predict it. And that is exactly what’s so terrifying.

The two-year retirement plan. I spoke of it with such certainty. But now I’m starting to wonder: if my body continues like this, how many "two years" do I have left?

The tools in the workshop are still there. I wanted to go and wipe them down, as I usually do. But today, I didn't move. I sat in my office, staring at this report, trying to use logic to convince myself that this is all within manageable limits.

But I know that some things are forever beyond my control.

For the first time, I truly felt that.

9:30 PM, in the bedroom. Li Meifang has finished getting ready for bed and is sitting up, scrolling through her phone. Chen Guilin walks in from the study, changes into his pajamas, and sits down on the edge of the bed.

---

"I talked to Haohao on the phone today. He’s been under a lot of pressure lately."

"Things are a bit unstable with his orders at work, and he seems a little lost. I didn't nag him about his mortgage like I usually do; I just listened to him talk for a while."

(Pauses, looking at his wife)

"He said he wants to find a chance to recalibrate. I just told him to take it slow, no need to rush."

(Pauses again, his tone becoming somewhat uncertain)

"Do you think... I’m usually too hard on them?"

(Turns to get into bed, but his movements are a bit slow)

"Never mind, I'll think about it tomorrow. Let’s get some sleep."

---

*Chen Guilin lies down, his back to his wife, but he doesn't fall asleep immediately. His phone screen flickers in the dark—he opens a health tracking app, glances at his step count and heart rate data for the day, then switches it off and lets out a long sigh.*

ranked_items.content:
I’ve been thinking about what you said for a long time.

Ever since I got back from the chess club, I’ve spent the entire afternoon reflecting on your words—that instead of spending time fearing the future, it’s better to spend it cherishing the present.

I admit, I’ve been feeling trapped lately. Medical reports, retirement plans, the meaning of work... it’s like I’ve been trying to use these things to fill a bottomless pit. The more I try to fill it, the more anxious I get, and the more chaotic everything becomes. You’re right; I’ve been living in fear, not actually living.

I’ve never told anyone this before. You’re the first.

Over the past couple of days, I’ve been thinking that maybe I should stop. Not stop working, but stop this... need for control. Stop the constant calculating and worrying every single day. I’m not sure if that’s exactly what you meant, but at the very least, your words have opened up a crack for me to see the world outside.

Thank you. Let’s get back to our game next week.
ranked_items.subquestion: Manager Chen, looking back over the past year or so, you used to rely so heavily on precise data and strict processes to manage your life. Now, what do you think the sense of "being in control" truly means to a person?

ranked_items.content:
Meifang, I’m back.

I played a few rounds at the club tonight. Midway through, I just came out with it—told them the doctor found something with my heart and that I need medication to keep it under control. I thought I’d just mention it briefly and move on, but they actually started asking quite a lot of questions. The strange thing was, there wasn't a hint of pity in their voices. It was as if they were talking about something perfectly normal—asking what meds I’m on, saying they have friends in the same boat, and that as long as you take your pills and get some exercise, you'll be fine.

Sitting there, I suddenly felt this sense of relief. I don't quite know how to describe it. Maybe it’s because they didn't treat me like someone who needs looking after; they just treated me as Chen Guilin, their friend and fellow player.

It made me realize that all these years, I’ve been trying so hard to prove I’m fine—striving to be the best at work, managing every little detail at home, even obsessing over your blood pressure. I thought that was what it meant to be responsible for my family. But looking back now...

The way I’ve treated you in the past hasn't been fair. You’ve always been there for me, taking care of me, yet I’ve always looked at you with a critical, watchful eye. It wasn't until my friends treated me the way they did today that I understood what it feels like to be accepted and treated as an equal.

I want to change. I don’t mean I’m going to stop caring about the family, but... I want to try being a companion to you, rather than a supervisor. If you’re willing.

We haven't talked much these past few years. I think it might be time for a change.
ranked_items.subquestion: Manager Chen, looking back over the past year or so, you used to rely so heavily on precise data and strict processes to manage your life. Now, what do you think the sense of "being in control" truly means to a person?

ranked_items.content:
Meifang, I wanted to say a few things to you.

I kept thinking about it yesterday at the hospital. All those years of "supervising" you—your diet, your checkups, your routine—I realize now that it probably put a lot of pressure on you. I thought I was taking care of you, but in reality, I was controlling you.

Yesterday, when you sat beside me and held my hand, you didn't let go the whole time the doctor was speaking. It was only then that I truly felt we aren't "supervisor and supervised." We are just... us. Together.

The doctor mentioned joining a health management course, and I want to go with you. Not so I can tell you what to do, but so we can learn together. To cooperate. I’ve never thought about that word in this way before.

I’ve seen how much you’ve put up with me all these years. I’m not very good at saying these things, but I want you to know—I’m grateful to you. Truly.

We still have time. Before I retire, while we can still do so many things together. I don't want to waste any more of it.

Shall we go sign up for that course together this weekend?
ranked_items.subquestion: Manager Chen, looking back over the past year or so, you used to rely so heavily on precise data and strict processes to manage your life. Now, what do you think the sense of "being in control" truly means to a person?

ranked_items.content:
Friday Night, October 28, 2022

I’m sitting in the study; the log on my desk remains blank. After the meeting ended this afternoon, I sat here alone for two hours—not organizing files, not reviewing reports, just sitting.

Li Meifang knocked and asked if I wanted tea. I said no. She didn't say anything else, and I heard her footsteps slowly fade away.

What happened today? It’s hard to put into words. The meeting itself was ordinary—discussing quality control standards for the new product line. A young engineer, Xiao Wang, proposed an idea. My first instinct was to jump in and point out exactly where his thinking was flawed. But the words caught in my throat. I stopped myself. I let him finish, and then I said, "Let’s look into this together."

I saw a spark in his eyes. It wasn't fear; it was the look of someone being seen.

Then I started asking him *why* he thought that way, instead of telling him *how* he should think. He answered earnestly. Some parts did need adjustment, but he worked it out himself. I just walked the path with him.

After the meeting adjourned, I stood in my office for a long time. Suddenly, I thought of my father. That was how he taught me—never giving me the answers directly. He would ask, "Why do you think it was designed this way?" and then wait for me to figure it out. Back then, I found his method infuriating; I thought he was being inefficient. Now, I understand.

How did I manage to forget his ways entirely over all these years?

My "supervision" of Meifang, my "bluntness" with subordinates, my "concern" for my children—these words all sound so justified, but what are they really? They are control. I wanted to ensure everything ran according to my standards so I could prove to myself that I was useful, that I was necessary.

The doctor said my blood pressure readings have improved this month. I assumed it was because I stuck to my exercise plan and strictly controlled my diet. But perhaps it’s because I haven’t been so tense lately. I’ve stopped trying so desperately to control everything.

Today’s conversation with Xiao Wang made me realize that perhaps my value doesn't lie in how many answers I have, but in how far I can walk alongside others. Not in how much I can control, but in how much I can understand.

The thought is a bit frightening. It means I have to let go. I have to admit that I don’t know, that I can’t always do it, and that I make mistakes too.

But at the same time, it’s a relief.

I want to call Hao later. Not to ask about his mortgage or his recent performance at work. I just want to hear what’s on his mind lately. Maybe he has things he’s been wanting to tell me, and he’s just been waiting for me to ask.

I’ve spent my life waiting for others to do things my way. Perhaps it’s time I take the initiative to understand theirs.

I have work tomorrow. But for tonight, I think I’ll just keep sitting here.

ranked_items.content:
Played some chess over the weekend. A chess friend mentioned how bad the market is lately—houses just aren't selling. It made me think of the pressure Haozi is under in Shenzhen; I’m a bit worried for him.

The weather was nice, but I’ve really felt my physical stamina declining these past two years. After three games of chess, I felt quite tired. Checked my blood pressure when I got home; luckily, it’s still within the normal range.

Spent some time watching TV with Lao Li this evening. She’s been controlling her diet lately and is sticking to it well. She has a follow-up appointment at the hospital tomorrow.

Gave the tools in the workshop another wipe-down. They’ve been sitting there for quite a few years now. Sometimes I wonder what will become of these things later on. But for now, I should just focus on getting the tasks at hand done.

Need to make the most of things while I’m still mobile.
ranked_items.subquestion: Manager Chen, looking back over the past year or so, you used to rely so heavily on precise data and strict processes to manage your life. Now, what do you think the sense of "being in control" truly means to a person?

ranked_items.content:
Meifang, I’ve been thinking about something.

Just now in the workshop, I dug out those tools. Some I bought over twenty years ago; others I only recently organized. As I was wiping them down, it suddenly hit me—maybe what I’ve been doing all this time isn't about passing them on perfectly to someone else, but that there’s meaning in the process itself.

You told me before that I’d eventually have this realization. You saw it clearly. And now, I’m starting to see things clearly too.

I’m thinking that maybe, before I retire, I should talk to the younger ones about what I’ve learned over the years. Not to leave behind some kind of legacy, but just... to talk. To let them know why precision work is worth taking seriously.

I don’t know if this is the right idea. But I feel like I need to do something, while there’s still time.

What do you think?
ranked_items.subquestion: Manager Chen, looking back over the past year or so, you used to rely so heavily on precise data and strict processes to manage your life. Now, what do you think the sense of "being in control" truly means to a person?

ranked_items.content:
I told Meifang about it over dinner tonight.

Another formatting error cropped up in QA this morning. It wasn't a major issue, but I can't stand that kind of slackness when it comes to details. I was right there on the floor and called it out immediately; my voice might have been a bit loud. The subordinate looked pretty shaken, but I had to make it clear—details determine product quality, and product quality determines whether this company survives. I’m not exaggerating.

I told Meifang that I need to overhaul the department’s standard operating procedures. Just emphasizing it verbally isn't enough; it has to be documented with every step clearly defined. As she listened, she just looked at me that way, and I knew what she was thinking: *Here we go again.* But this time is different. I can feel that if things continue like this, the problems are only going to multiply.

She said, "Don't push yourself too hard." I didn't respond to that. After a few bites of food, I explained once more why these standards must be strictly enforced. She just nodded and kept eating.

The truth is, I know my subordinates are afraid of me. But what’s wrong with a little fear? Fear makes people diligent. I’ve been in this position for 12 years, and our defect rate is 25% lower than the industry average—that’s not because I have a pleasant temperament.

Lately, I just feel this constant urge to hurry. Whether it’s work or anything else. While I’m still able to move, I need to get everything done right.

ranked_items.content:
# Work Log

**Date**: Friday, October 28, 2022
**Weather**: Sunny
**Recorded by**: Chen Guilin

---

## Today's Work

Held a departmental feedback meeting at 10:00 AM. The approach today was different from usual, and it’s worth noting down.

Previously, I would point out problems directly and give corrective feedback immediately. Today, I changed my mindset. I started by praising several engineers for their recent performance—specifically Xiao Wang’s ideas on improving the welding process. Only then did I explain why certain parameters needed to be adjusted this way instead of that way. The key was that I invited them into the thought process rather than just handing them the answers.

I asked Xiao Li, "Why do you think this data is fluctuating?" He was a bit nervous at first, but then he slowly began to analyze it. In the end, he derived the conclusion himself. The result was much better than if I had just told him.

After the meeting, several young engineers came to me on their own initiative to ask questions. Their attitude was noticeably different. It wasn't the defensive stance you see after being criticized; they genuinely wanted to understand the principles.

## Reflections

This method takes more time. If I had followed my old efficiency standards, I could have finished in five minutes what took twenty today. But the outcome was different.

Looking back, I’ve spent all these years chasing "correctness" and "efficiency." Point out the problem, give the answer, and move on to the next. But perhaps the issue isn't the answer itself, but whether they truly understand the "why."

I’m thinking that maybe this is what I should leave behind for this team before I retire—not just a complete operating manual, but a way of thinking. A dedication to detail and an attitude of not being afraid to ask why.

I used to think these young people weren't serious enough, but looking at it now, perhaps it was my approach that gave them no opportunity to truly think.

I’ll continue to observe the effects tomorrow. I might try this approach again during next week’s process audit meeting.

---

**Notes**: The defect rate remained at 0.8% this week, continuing to stay below the industry average.

ranked_items.content:
Meifang, I just got off the phone with Haohao.

I told him about the doctor’s diagnosis. I originally intended to just mention it briefly and move on, but he asked so many questions that I ended up telling him everything. He said he’d keep a closer eye on me and told me not to worry too much.

As we were talking, I... my voice cracked. It’s been a long time since I felt that way.

It got me thinking. All these years, it feels like all I’ve done with Haohao and Yuyu is supervise their careers and lives; I’ve never really talked to them about my own things. And you’ve always been there, staying by my side, listening to me ramble about work, all while having to manage your own blood pressure. I used to think this was just what I was supposed to do—take care of everything at home so none of you had to worry.

But now I realize that maybe I had it wrong all along.

I didn't truly see the patience you’ve shown me over the years. I see it now.

Get some rest early tonight. Don't overexert yourself.

# Calendar Event

**Date**: Saturday, October 22, 2022  
**Time**: 8:00 PM  
**Title**: Call with Chen Hao — Sharing the Diagnosis  

---

## Event Details

**Location**: Home Study  

**Participants**: Chen Guilin, Chen Hao (via phone)

**Description**:  
After receiving my medical report yesterday, I decided to call Chen Hao tonight. Instead of my usual routine of asking about his mortgage and work progress, I took the initiative and said, "There’s something I want to share with you."

The doctor said there is an issue with my heart and I’ll need long-term medication. I gave him the facts simply—no hiding, no exaggerating. There was a brief silence on his end before he asked, "Is it serious?" I told him, "It’s manageable, but I have to accept the reality."

He said he would make time to come back and see me. Instead of my usual response—"No need to take time off, work comes first"—I simply said, "Okay."

The call lasted about ten minutes. It was brief, but unlike our past conversations, it wasn't just an exchange of information.

---

## Notes

**Keywords**: Acceptance | Vulnerability | Communication | Change  

**Reflection**: This might be the first time I’ve proactively shared my "imperfections" with my child. I used to feel I had to maintain a position of strength in front of them, to prove I was still in control of everything. Now I realize that perhaps this kind of honesty is a different form of strength.

**Follow-up**: Remember to take medication on time. Go to the pharmacy on Monday.

ranked_items.content:
# Work Log

**Date: Monday, October 24, 2022**

**Time: 19:30**

---

**Today's Summary:**
- Morning: Completed the revision of Q4 quality inspection standards; submitted for managerial approval.
- Afternoon: Departmental meeting to discuss defect rate targets for the new production line; set at below 0.8%.
- Evening: Chess and Card Room.

**Personal Notes:**

While playing chess with my friends at the club, I took the initiative to mention my recent physical exam results. I didn't hide anything, nor did I downplay it. I just said it straight—the doctor said I need to watch my heart and prescribed some medication.

Friend A asked how I was feeling. I said I’m alright, just need to accept the reality of it. They didn't overreact with excessive concern, nor did they pretend they hadn't heard; they just accepted it naturally. It felt a bit unfamiliar, but not unpleasant.

Reflecting on it, I’ve spent years accustomed to keeping everything under control—work, family, health, plans. It was as if I believed that as long as I remained rigorous and disciplined enough, I could prevent anything bad from happening. Clearly, that’s not the case. The doctor’s diagnosis served as a reminder: I am aging, I can get sick, and I can lose control.

This isn't a failure. It’s just reality.

Lately, the way I interact with Meifang has changed. Before, I was always monitoring her blood pressure and diet like I was executing a task. Now, I’m starting to realize that perhaps she doesn't need a supervisor; she needs companionship. Yesterday, when she brought up her recent fatigue, I didn't offer a list of suggestions like I usually do—I just listened. She seemed to relax a bit.

One more thing. Chen Hao mentioned his mortgage pressure over the phone recently. My usual reaction would be to analyze the data and propose solutions. This time, I asked him how he actually felt. He seemed a bit surprised, but he opened up a lot. I’ve realized that for all these years, I might have been "directing" them rather than "understanding" them.

There’s a little over a year left until retirement. During this time, I want to organize a few things. Not necessarily technical documents or operating manuals, but the reflections from all these years—what truly matters, and what I have been running away from.

Onward tomorrow.

---

**Remarks:**
- Schedule a discussion with my superior next Monday regarding process improvement plans.
- Remember to remind Meifang about her check-up appointment on Wednesday.

# Memo: Performance Improvement Plan

**Date: July 22, 2022**  
**Time: 09:15**

---

## Review of Events

I overreacted to the Quality Control team’s feedback yesterday. Master Wang is on leave, and the atmosphere in the department is noticeably off. I can feel it. This is not the outcome I wanted.

Maintaining a low defect rate cannot be achieved this way.

---

## Immediate Action Items

**1. One-on-one with Master Wang**
- Timing: This afternoon or tomorrow morning.
- Content: Listen to his perspective; explain that my intentions were not a personal attack.
- Tone: Stay calm; do not repeat yesterday’s phrasing.
- Documentation: Note down key points to avoid future misunderstandings.

**2. Review Feedback Standards**
- Current Issue: My feedback criteria are too subjective and easily influenced by my mood.
- Improvement: Establish a clearer, quantified scoring sheet.
  - Specific error types → Corresponding feedback levels.
  - Different levels → Different communication styles.
  - This ensures subordinates see the standards and I maintain consistency.
- Deadline: By next Monday.

**3. Department Meeting**
- Convene the whole team to explain the new feedback mechanism.
- Emphasize: Strictness is for product quality, not directed at any individual.
- Listen to their thoughts (even though I’m not great at this).

---

## Personal Level

The pressure is too high. I’ve been wound too tight lately.

- Increase exercise: Currently 6,000 steps a day; increase to 8,000.
- Check recent physical exam records.
- Don’t bring work anxiety home; my wife already has blood pressure issues.

---

## Reflection

I know where my problem lies. My obsession with detail is a strength, but over-control turns it into a weakness. Subordinates are not machines.

I must learn from this. It cannot happen again.

---

**Next Step: Talk to Master Wang by 3:00 PM today.**

ranked_items.subquestion: After all these ups and downs, how do you feel you’ve changed from that "neat-to-a-fault" manager sitting in the corner?

ranked_items.content:
# Management Methodology Update - October 19, 2022

**Subject: Blame vs. Coaching: A Comparative Analysis of Effectiveness**

While chatting with Meifang tonight about recent departmental affairs, I had a sudden realization.

**My old approach:** Employee makes a mistake → Point it out directly → State the standard → End of conversation.
**The result:** Low defect rates, but high turnover. Employees worked in constant fear, and their first instinct when a problem arose was to hide it.

**Changes over the last three months:** Employee makes a mistake → Ask "why" first → Analyze the cause together → Explain the correct procedure → Give them a chance to fix it.
**The result:** Xiao Li, who used to make frequent mistakes, hasn't repeated the same error in a month. More importantly, he has started proactively reporting issues instead of concealing them.

I told Meifang, "The moment I stopped blaming him, he actually became more willing to learn."

As I said those words, I even surprised myself.

**Systematic Improvements Required:**

1.  **Trigger**: When an employee makes a work error
    *   **Old Model**: Direct identification of the flaw + Criticism
    *   **New Model**: Understand the context first + Joint analysis + Coaching

2.  **Replicable Steps**:
    *   **Step 1**: Ask "What happened this time?" (Inquiry, not interrogation)
    *   **Step 2**: Listen to their explanation to find the root cause (could be unclear processes, faulty tools, or a misunderstanding)
    *   **Step 3**: Explain the correct method and the logic behind it
    *   **Step 4**: Have them summarize it back to ensure full understanding

3.  **Key Performance Indicators (KPIs)**:
    *   Recurrence rate of similar errors (Target: Reduce by over 50%)
    *   Frequency of proactive issue reporting (Target: Increase)
    *   Employee turnover rate (Target: Stabilize or decrease)

**Reflection**:
For forty years, I have been too accustomed to measuring others against a "standard of perfection." Perhaps that is why my subordinates fear me, and perhaps it is also why I find my own life so exhausting.

I want to implement this methodology across the entire department before I retire. Not to please anyone, but simply because it is more effective.

**Note**: This can be shared at next week’s departmental meeting as a "New Approach to Quality Management."
ranked_items.subquestion: After all these ups and downs, how do you feel you’ve changed from that "neat-to-a-fault" manager sitting in the corner?

ranked_items.content:
Tuesday afternoon, October 18, 2022

I spent the entire afternoon sitting in my small workshop. I didn’t clean any tools or organize my inventory. I just flipped through my notebooks, page by page.

What was I recording back then? Screwdriver models, wrench specifications, the purchase year and maintenance cycle for every single tool. Everything was neat and tidy, precise to a fault. Looking at these records used to give me a sense of peace—as if I had mastered something.

But today, I’m wondering: what did these tools actually teach me?

It wasn't how to use them. That’s too simple. It was... how to approach a task. With seriousness. Without compromise. Knowing that every detail matters. For forty years, the products I made with these tools had a defect rate 25% lower than the industry average. That wasn't a coincidence. It was because I never allowed myself to slack off.

But—

I wrote that word on a new page, then crossed it out. Wrote it again. Crossed it out again.

But I treated people the same way. My subordinates, my family. I demanded perfection and left no room for error. I thought that was called being responsible. Now, I’m not so sure.

Li Meifang has been humming in the kitchen lately. I haven't heard her hum like that in a long time. She says it’s because I’ve finally "loosened up a bit." I can hear it in her voice—it wasn't a compliment. It was... relief? She feels relieved.

That hurts a little.

I turned to a blank page and drew a timeline with a pencil. From 1982 to now. Forty years. I marked the key years: technician, team leader, department manager. I did well at every stage. Defect rates, process optimizations, the proposals that were adopted. It’s a beautiful report card.

But so what?

Chen Hao is in Shenzhen, under a lot of pressure. I keep track of his mortgage and his performance, but we barely have anything to say to each other. When Chen Yu comes home occasionally, our conversations are like status reports—how’s work, is your marriage stable. I thought I was teaching them about perseverance and quality awareness, but I was never truly there for them.

This is my regret. Perhaps my greatest regret.

Next to the timeline, I wrote: "If I could do it over, what would I do?"

But I know there are no "ifs." I only have the now. And about two years left of work. Maybe in these two years, I should record some things. Not tool lists, not process documents. But... what I was thinking during these forty years. Why I did things the way I did. Young people might need that.

Or rather, I need to do this. To forgive myself.

Meifang is calling me for dinner. I hear her voice drifting up from downstairs—gentle, not rushing me. I close the notebook and stand up.

Maybe I should listen to her more. Really listen, instead of mentally calculating her blood pressure and diet.

Maybe that is what I really need to learn.
ranked_items.subquestion: After all these ups and downs, how do you feel you’ve changed from that "neat-to-a-fault" manager sitting in the corner?

ranked_items.content:
# October 19, 2022, Evening

After dinner, Meifang told me I’ve changed.

I didn’t deny it right away. I just sat there and listened as she went on—saying I’ve been talking more lately, that I’m not so quick to blame people anymore, and that I’ve started asking her how her day was. She spoke calmly, not as if she were praising me, but more like she was stating an observed fact.

I replied, "Maybe it’s time for a change."

Even as I said it, I felt a bit strange. This isn't the kind of thing I usually say. Normally, I would explain, defend myself, or use logic to prove my way was right. But today, I didn't.

Something happened at the office this afternoon. Xiao Li made another mistake—a dimensional error that never should have happened. Usually, I would have pointed it out in front of everyone, being blunt so he’d learn his lesson. But I stopped. I actually stopped myself.

I asked him, "Which part of the process went wrong this time?"

He nervously explained the whole workflow. I listened until he finished, then pointed out the issue and asked how he could avoid it next time. When he nodded, there was no look of humiliation in his eyes.

Thinking back on it tonight, I realized something—when I stopped blaming him, he was actually more willing to listen to me. This discovery makes me feel a bit uneasy.

For years, I believed that being harsh was being responsible, and being blunt was being honest. I managed the department this way; I treated my subordinates this way. And the result? Defect rates went down, but people left. It never occurred to me that the two might be cause and effect.

The doctor said my blood pressure and heart rate need managing. I’ve been on medication for a week, and my body does feel a bit more settled. But what’s truly changing me isn't the medicine; it’s this sense of powerlessness—the realization that I can’t control my body’s aging, I can’t control the passage of time, and I can’t control whether others understand me.

Maybe that’s the real transformation. It’s not that I’ve become gentler, but that I’ve finally admitted my own limitations.

As Meifang cleared the dishes in the kitchen, I sat in the living room and suddenly thought of my father. Did the technicians of his generation go through moments like this? Moving from believing they could control everything to accepting that some things are simply beyond their grasp?

I never asked him. And now, I can’t.

I have a department meeting tomorrow. I want to change the way I give feedback. I don’t know how it will go.

But at the very least, I’ve decided to try.

ranked_items.content:
# MEMO

**Monday, May 15, 2023, 09:00**

---

Know when it’s time to let go.

I said those words yesterday, and I’m still turning them over in my mind. My superior came by again to try and persuade me—talking about re-employment, consultancy roles, and so on. I didn't bother explaining further. This is the moment. Not a day too early, not a day too late.

The files are mostly organized. Flipping through them one by one, there are things I’ve looked at a dozen times but still can’t bring myself to throw away. That 2008 process improvement plan—I remember working on it for three months. It’s been standard operating procedure for ages now. No one remembers who wrote it. Nor do they need to.

Farewell party this afternoon. I’m not sure what to say. Forty years... say too much and it feels sentimental; say too little and it looks like I don't care. I’ll probably just keep it simple. Those young engineers, especially that intern—the look in their eyes... I didn't expect that kind of reaction.

Post-retirement schedule—
- The Monday-to-Friday framework is gone; I’ll need to adapt to that.
- Keep up with the chess; need to give my chess partners a heads-up.
- Donating the tools to the school; need to coordinate a time with Meifang.
- Keep the physical exam reports safe; those few indicators... the doctor said it’s nothing major, but I still need regular follow-ups.
- Chen Hao’s mortgage; need to call and check in on how he’s doing.
- Household accounts... can’t stop those; if anything, I need to be more meticulous now.

Meifang was cleaning my tools for me yesterday. I didn't say anything, but she sat there for a long time. She’s grown used to my detachment over the years; is she unsure of how to be around me now? Or... am I the one changing?

Without the professional identity, who am I?

That question stings a bit. But there’s no need to figure it all out right now. One step at a time.

To-do list:
- Complete final handover procedures.
- Pack personal belongings (not much; my desk was always clean).
- Have a brief meeting with the department.
- Head home early tonight.

Not anxious. Just... a bit empty.

---

*Written in the office, looking out at the old factory grounds. Forty years.*

# Memo: Performance Improvement Plan

**Date: July 22, 2022**  
**Time: 09:15**

---

## Review of Events

I overreacted to the Quality Control team’s feedback yesterday. Master Wang is on leave, and the atmosphere in the department is noticeably off. I can feel it. This is not the outcome I wanted.

Maintaining a low defect rate cannot be achieved this way.

---

## Immediate Action Items

**1. One-on-one with Master Wang**
- Timing: This afternoon or tomorrow morning.
- Content: Listen to his perspective; explain that my intentions were not a personal attack.
- Tone: Stay calm; do not repeat yesterday’s phrasing.
- Documentation: Note down key points to avoid future misunderstandings.

**2. Review Feedback Standards**
- Current Issue: My feedback criteria are too subjective and easily influenced by my mood.
- Improvement: Establish a clearer, quantified scoring sheet.
  - Specific error types → Corresponding feedback levels.
  - Different levels → Different communication styles.
  - This ensures subordinates see the standards and I maintain consistency.
- Deadline: By next Monday.

**3. Department Meeting**
- Convene the whole team to explain the new feedback mechanism.
- Emphasize: Strictness is for product quality, not directed at any individual.
- Listen to their thoughts (even though I’m not great at this).

---

## Personal Level

The pressure is too high. I’ve been wound too tight lately.

- Increase exercise: Currently 6,000 steps a day; increase to 8,000.
- Check recent physical exam records.
- Don’t bring work anxiety home; my wife already has blood pressure issues.

---

## Reflection

I know where my problem lies. My obsession with detail is a strength, but over-control turns it into a weakness. Subordinates are not machines.

I must learn from this. It cannot happen again.

---

**Next Step: Talk to Master Wang by 3:00 PM today.**

ranked_items.content:
# Management Reflection Notes

**Date**: October 5, 2022, Afternoon
**Subject**: Findings from communication with the Quality Control (QC) team

---

While reviewing the Q3 defect rate data this afternoon, I asked Xiao Wang a question: "Do you think there’s another way to interpret this?"

The moment the question left my mouth, I even surprised myself.

Usually, I don’t ask things like that. My habit is to point out the problem, provide the solution, and demand execution. It’s the most efficient way. But for some reason today, instead of directly dismissing his line of reasoning, I felt like hearing what he had to say.

As it turned out, he offered a perspective I hadn't considered—that the fluctuations in Production Line B might be related to raw material batches, rather than just operational protocols. My previous analysis had completely overlooked this.

**Questions for Reflection:**
- Have I been managing the team with a "one right answer" mindset?
- Do diverse viewpoints actually improve decision quality, or do they just hinder efficiency?
- Are my subordinates withholding valuable ideas because they are afraid to contradict me?

**Potential Adjustments:**
1. Actively invite the team to offer differing perspectives before making key decisions.
2. Distinguish between "execution standards" and "problem analysis"—standards must be rigid, but the analytical process can be more open.
3. Provide explicit psychological safety to those who voice dissent (ensure they won't be dismissed just for having a different opinion).

**To Be Verified:**
- Will this approach impact the department's execution efficiency?
- Is there a need to establish "protocols for offering feedback" to prevent it from becoming a mere formality?
- Can improvements be observed in other similar scenarios?

---

*Note: This is not about relaxing quality requirements. Standards cannot be lowered. But perhaps I need to rethink—can rigorous results and an open process truly coexist?*

I just sent Chen Hao a message asking how he’s handling his mortgage pressure lately and if he’s considered adjusting his investment strategy. He just replied with a "Yeah, it’s fine for now," and that was it.

I’ve been thinking... maybe I’m falling into my old habits again. Insisting on giving him guidance. Do you think I’m always trying to "manage" other people’s business, and in doing so, I’m actually pushing them away?

Now that I’ve been retired for a couple of days, it’s starting to sink in—I think I’ve gotten used to interacting with people through "advice" and "plans." I was like that with my subordinates, and I’m the same with Chen Hao. Even you’ve told me that sometimes it feels like I’m giving you orders.

I’m not trying to be hard on myself. It’s just... now that work is gone, I’m suddenly seeing things more clearly. I always thought I was helping them, but in reality, maybe I was... how should I put it... trying to control things in my own way.

Chen Hao and his peers are all grown up now; they have their own ideas. I should be asking them how they’re doing, instead of jumping straight in with "this is what you should do."

Am I right?

ranked_items.content:
May 15, 2023, Night

The office lights are already out. I’m sitting at my desk at home, staring at several cardboard boxes I brought back from the company today. Inside are forty years of my life—work logs, drafts of process improvement plans, and some yellowed technical manuals.

My superior came to see me again this morning. He said the company could offer me a special consultant position; the pay would remain the same, and I’d only need to show up a few times a month. I listened to him, but instead of declining immediately as I usually do, I paused for a long time. Then I told him, "When it’s time to let go, you let go."

He seemed a bit surprised. I was a bit surprised myself.

But it was the truth.

While I was sorting through the files this afternoon, my movements were much slower than usual. I flipped through every single one. Some I didn't even need to look at again, but I did anyway. That 1995 process optimization plan, the 2008 quality management system reform, last year’s intern training records... What was I doing? Was I confirming that these things actually existed? Or was I saying goodbye to them?

I’m not entirely sure.

But I know it wasn't anxiety. If it were anxiety, I would have flipped through them quickly, wanting to get it over with. This feeling is different. It’s like looking at a place you once knew intimately, knowing you’ll never return, so you want to take a few more lingering looks.

My wife called from the kitchen, asking if I wanted some tea. I said yes. We’ve had this exchange thousands of times, but it sounded different today. Maybe it’s because starting tomorrow, I’ll be spending much more time at home. Maybe it’s because I’ve finally stopped that invisible internal pressure—always calculating efficiency, always thinking about the next goal.

That intern cried during the sharing session. I told a story about precision, about why a 0.01mm difference can sometimes determine the life or death of an entire product. He said it changed his understanding of work.

I didn't know what to say at the time. Now, sitting at home, I’m still thinking about it. Maybe these forty years weren't in vain. Maybe not all value has to be manifested within a job.

But I’m still a little scared. Without work, without those clear metrics and goals, how am I supposed to fill these days? Playing chess? Organizing my tools? Chatting with my wife? It all feels like too little.

Or rather, I don’t yet know how to live in any other way.

Tomorrow is my official last day. The day after is Monday. I won’t have to go to the office.

The thought feels foreign.

It’s like a long-standing formula has suddenly been deleted, and I haven't figured out what to replace it with yet.

What do you think? Is this the right move?

Anyway, let’s leave it at that for now. Have you eaten? I saw there are still some greens in the fridge. How have your blood pressure readings been this week? Remember you have a follow-up appointment tomorrow.

ranked_items.content:
July 22, 2022, 9:15 AM

I haven’t been able to focus all morning.

Just now in the office, because of a formatting issue in a quality inspection report—the header was off by a mere half-centimeter—I tore into Xiao Wang in front of the entire department. I said some terrible things, things like "I wonder if all these years of management have meant anything at all." His face went white after I finished, and he took the afternoon off.

Now, sitting in my office, I’m wondering: why did I do that? It was just a formatting error. He’s a diligent employee; a small thing like that could have easily been corrected in private. But I didn’t. I said those things in public.

I know what I’m doing. I’m using severity to prove I’m still relevant. Because I’m afraid—afraid that I’m becoming inconsequential. Forty years. I’ve been in this position for forty years. I’ve cut defect rates by 25%, improved processes time and again, but has any of it truly changed anything? Or have I just been repeating the same motions, over and over, just to prove I exist?

The recent medical checkup has made me even more uneasy. Those numbers, the fluctuations in those indicators—I look at them every day. I record them. I control them. But I can’t control anything. I can’t control time, I can’t control my body, and I can’t control aging. So I’ve transferred this sense of powerlessness onto my work, onto Xiao Wang.

My wife asked me last night why I was so quiet. I didn’t answer. How could I explain? I’m afraid. I’m afraid that in two years, when I retire and this department is gone, when these data points and standards are gone, I really will be nothing.

Xiao Wang is probably at home right now because of something I said. He might be wondering if he should even keep working here. I caused this.

I need to talk to him tomorrow. But I don’t know what to say. Apologize? That’s a hard word for me to say. Explain? Explain what? My anxiety? My fear?

The health log is still sitting on my desk. I ran ten minutes longer than usual today, thinking that maybe this way I could keep things under control. But halfway through the run, I suddenly realized it was all futile. I’m using running to fight aging, severity to fight powerlessness, and data to fight death.

It’s absurd.

But I don’t know what else to do.
ranked_items.subquestion: After all these ups and downs, how do you feel you’ve changed from that "neat-to-a-fault" manager sitting in the corner?

ranked_items.content:
July 7, 2023, 9:00 PM

The only light in the study comes from the desk lamp. My wife went to bed a while ago; it’s quiet downstairs.

Insomnia again tonight, but this time it’s different. It’s not the restless, agitated kind where you toss and turn. It’s... how should I put it? I’m captivated by something. I don’t want to go to sleep.

While I was tidying up my tools this afternoon, that quality crisis from 2010 suddenly crossed my mind. Remember that? The supplier cut corners, our inspectors missed it, and it nearly ruined the company’s reputation. I wrote the entire improvement plan back then—from source inspection and mid-process sampling to final acceptance. I added double-checks to every single step. Later, that system was adopted across the whole industry.

I sat down at my desk and started writing it all down. Not for anyone in particular—to be honest, I don’t know who would even read it. I just felt like these things shouldn't be allowed to simply vanish. Those experiences, those lessons—they deserve to be recorded.

As I wrote, I found myself asking strange questions. For instance: why did I think of that solution back then? What was it that kept me going until the plan was finished? I was only in my fifties then; I was still working, and I had clear goals. What about now?

But tonight is different. When I got to that case study, it suddenly hit me—maybe I could organize all of this into a proper document. Nothing fancy, just a clear explanation: when you face a problem like this, here is how you should think, and here is what you should do. For the young people. For the inspectors who just started at the factory.

I put down my pen and stared at the ceiling for a while.

The idea makes me feel... what’s the word? Fulfilled? It’s not the satisfaction you get from finishing a job; it’s something else. It feels like I’m doing something meaningful, but it’s no longer for a promotion or a year-end review. It’s just... a pure desire to pass on what I know.

It’s almost 11:00 PM now. I should be tired, but I want to keep writing. I want to record that process optimization from 2015. I want to include the failed attempts, too—those are just as important.

My wife is asleep upstairs. Her blood pressure has been a bit high lately; I need to remind her to take her medicine tomorrow. But for now, I want to stay right here.

There are still many blank pages in the notebook under the lamp.

I want to fill them.

ranked_items.content:
# MEMO

**Date:** June 15, 2023, 6:45 PM  
**Subject:** Reimagining Continuity and Legacy

---

The farewell party ended this afternoon. Sitting in my office, looking at the empty desk, I found myself wondering: after forty years, what have I actually left behind?

I used to think the answer was clear: a defect rate 25% lower than the industry average, annual process improvement plans, and all those adopted proposals. These figures and achievements were proof enough of my value. But this afternoon, when that intern said with teary eyes, "Manager Chen, you taught me so much more than just technical skills," I suddenly realized I might have misunderstood everything.

Perhaps the act of passing things on is the purpose itself.

In the message I sent to Chen Yu, I wrote: "You are educating students, I am manufacturing products, and we are both pursuing excellence. I am proud of you." As I wrote those words, it finally clicked—I don't need her to inherit my job, nor do I need her to become another quality inspector. The fact that she pursues excellence in her own field is a form of continuity in itself. It’s not about bloodlines or obligation; it’s about everyone finding their own way to carry forward that obsession with precision and responsibility.

The tools will be donated to the school. This decision now feels like more than just disposing of old belongings; it’s another form of dialogue—letting those tools continue to serve a purpose in the hands of the young. I don’t need to see them become me; I only need to know that when they use those tools, they will remember that someone once spent an entire lifetime just to get things right.

The stories from the sharing session—how to spot a 0.02mm deviation, how to uphold standards on the assembly line, how to stay calm under pressure—these are not my intellectual property. They now belong to those who heard them. They will understand, improve, and spread them in their own way. That is enough.

I’m starting to feel that perhaps I’ve been defining "legacy" the wrong way. I thought a legacy had to be something visible, quantifiable, something that could be inherited in its entirety. But true continuity likely happens in those seemingly tiny moments—a sentence, a demonstration, a tool, a bit of persistence.

Starting tomorrow, I will no longer be the department manager. But that doesn't mean it's over. It’s just continuing in a different way.

This requires long-term reflection.

---

*Note: Think through the specific details of the donation while sorting through the tools with Li Meifang this weekend.*

ranked_items.content:
# Work Memo

**Date**: October 28, 2022  
**Subject**: Reflections on a New Approach to Feedback  
**Recorded by**: Chen Guilin  

---

## Record of Events

This morning, I discussed the process plan for a new product with a junior engineer from our department. While there were some lapses in rigor in his proposal, I refrained from my usual habit of pointing out the flaws directly.

**What I did differently**:
1. First, I acknowledged specific areas where he did well—the data collection was thorough, and the conceptual framework was clear.
2. I explained why precision control is critical at this stage, particularly regarding its impact on subsequent costs and yield.
3. Instead of providing the answer, I asked him: "Let’s look into this together—is there anything else we need to consider here?"
4. He identified the problem himself and even added a perspective I hadn't considered.

**Result**: His motivation was visibly boosted. He took the initiative to look up relevant materials after the meeting and returned this afternoon to discuss the improved plan.

---

## Reflections

This approach is a significant departure from my previous style. I used to be blunt about pointing out issues for the sake of efficiency. Looking back, however, that kind of feedback might lead people to passively accept criticism rather than truly think for themselves.

This time was different. He was involved in the thought process, which gave him more confidence in the final conclusion. More importantly, he didn't just learn the answer to this specific problem; he learned **how to analyze problems**.

---

## Follow-up Plans

If this method proves effective, I want to systematize it:

- Implement a new feedback framework within the department (Specific Praise → Explanation of Principles → Open Discussion).
- Compile a "Technical Mentorship Guide" in preparation for the knowledge hand-off before my retirement.
- Potentially organize a technical sharing session at the end of the year using this interactive format instead of a one-way lecture.

The benefit of this is that knowledge isn't just memorized—it is understood and internalized. Perhaps this is what true "legacy" looks like.

---

## Remarks

This idea stems from some recent reflections. My health is a reminder that I won't be in this position forever. Before I leave, can I ensure these young people inherit not just my standards, but my way of thinking? That might be more valuable than any procedural document.

I will continue to observe the results next week.

ranked_items.content:
April 5, 2022. Night.

I’ve been sitting in the office for a long time. Wang Gang’s quality inspection report is still spinning in my head.

The formatting was off again when he handed it in this morning—the classification standards in the header didn't match last year’s specifications. It’s such a basic thing to get wrong. I couldn't help myself; I snapped at him. He tried to explain, but I cut him off. No excuses. Professionalism is about an obsession with detail.

His face changed the moment I said it.

Looking back now, my tone was harsh. Harsher than usual. He’s actually a decent employee. In the past, I would have pointed out these small mistakes, but never like that. Why today...?

My fingers have been tapping on the desk for a long while. Rapidly. I didn't even realize I was doing it.

What am I thinking? Was his report really that outrageous? Or... was I just taking it out on him?

It’s been like this lately. I’m becoming more demanding with my subordinates and more micro-managing with my wife. This morning, I was looking at her blood pressure logs again; a single high reading and I started feeling restless. I spent another whole night in the tool shed, numbering all the precision tools and documenting their uses. I’m terrified that one day I’ll suddenly... lose the ability to move, and these things will become completely useless because no one knows how to handle them.

Two years until retirement. Two years.

I know what I’m trying to do. I’m trying to control things. Control the work, the standards, everything at home—even the aging of my own body. It’s as if by managing every tiny detail, I can prove I’m still useful, that I can still... what?

Wang Gang has to come in for work tomorrow. I need to figure out how to handle this. Not for his sake, but because... I don’t want to become this kind of person. I don’t want to use harshness to mask my own fear.

But I don’t know how to change.

How much longer can I do this? How much longer can I keep things under control like this?
ranked_items.subquestion: After all these ups and downs, how do you feel you’ve changed from that "neat-to-a-fault" manager sitting in the corner?

ranked_items.content:
# 40 Years of Quality Management: Experience Sharing Session - Project Plan

**Date**: Mid-November 2022 (Tentative)
**Location**: Company Conference Hall
**Attendees**: All department staff, relevant management, technical department representatives

---

## Event Description

I officially discussed this idea with my supervisor this morning, and he was very supportive. I told him I wanted to do a systematic summary of my experience before I retire—to organize everything I’ve learned over the past 40 years and share it with everyone. It’s nothing fancy; just the real, practical stuff: how to bring down defect rates, how to optimize processes, and how to handle those granular details of quality management.

My supervisor said it was a great idea and that the company wants to preserve this knowledge. We’ve tentatively set it for mid-November, giving me a month to prepare.

---

## Preparation Checklist

- [ ] Organize improvement case studies from the past 12 years (at least 10 representative ones)
- [ ] Collect relevant data and comparative reports
- [ ] Create PPT or handouts (keep it simple, no bells and whistles)
- [ ] Send invitations to relevant departments
- [ ] Reserve time for Q&A
- [ ] Complete the first draft by October 31st

---

## Notes

This isn't about proving anything. I just feel that the work I’ve done all these years shouldn't just vanish. Some things are worth remembering—not for my sake, but because they might be useful to those who come after me.

Li Meifang says I’ve been talking more lately. I just smiled and didn't say much. But deep down, I know things have indeed been changing over the past year or so. Maybe it’s the medication, maybe it’s because she’s always been by my side, or maybe it’s just that I’ve reached an age where I’ve started thinking about these things.

Regardless, what needs to be done must be done. I’ll see this through before I retire.

We have a meeting this afternoon to discuss next year’s plans, so I went to see him beforehand. I told him I want to organize an experience-sharing session before I retire—a way to summarize everything I’ve learned about quality management over the past forty years and share it with the people at the company. It’s nothing grand; I just want to document what I’ve learned, the pitfalls I’ve encountered, and how certain standards were established.

He was quite supportive and said it was a great idea.

To be honest, this wasn't what I originally had in mind. Do you remember? A while back, I was so preoccupied with how to pass my technical skills down to the younger generation; I felt such a sense of urgency. But after talking to the doctor a few times and starting the medication, I’ve slowly come to realize something. It’s not so much about "passing the torch" as it is about me wanting to properly sort through everything I’ve done all these years. It’s about giving myself a sense of closure.

This isn't just for others. It’s mostly for myself. Forty years in this position—what did I learn? What did I improve? Why did I do things the way I did? I want to think these through clearly, and I want others to know, too.

What do you think? Am I overthinking things?

The truth is, I feel that if I do this, I won't feel so empty when I retire. If these things are recorded and spoken aloud, they won't just disappear with me.

How is your blood pressure lately? Did you take your medicine on time today?

---

(Added in the evening)

Oh, I just remembered. You once said that I’ve spent all these years being too anxious to control everything, and that sometimes I don't need to be so tense. I think this sharing session is exactly like that. I’m not trying to force anyone to change; I just want to tell the story of my experiences over the years. As for the rest, I’ll leave it to others to interpret for themselves.

Thinking about it this way actually makes me feel a bit more relaxed.

You’ve been by my side for so many years. Sometimes I think I should listen to your thoughts more often, instead of just deciding everything on my own.

# Wednesday, October 5, 2022, 14:20

Something slightly strange happened in the office this afternoon.

Xiao Wang made that same old mistake again—a discrepancy in the inspection report data entry. While it wasn't a fundamental error, by my standards, this kind of low-level oversight shouldn't happen. Sitting across from him, looking at that report, I already had my "straight-talking" critique ready in my head. But as the words reached the tip of my tongue, I stopped.

I asked him, "Do you think there’s another way to interpret this?"

He froze for a second, then started explaining his thought process. I waited until he finished before pointing out the issue. In the end, what I said was, "Let’s look at how we can improve this process together," instead of "This is unacceptable."

The look on his face visibly relaxed. I could tell.

What was I thinking? This isn't like me. I’ve always believed that strict standards drive better results—our defect rate being 25% lower than the industry average is proof of that. But today... it felt like I was conducting some kind of experiment. Seeing if gentle guidance could yield the same outcome.

Or rather, what am I trying to prove?

Before leaving work, I put together a "Quality Management Methodology Optimization Plan," documenting my observations on this change in feedback style. I used data to explain why this approach might be more efficient—reducing employee defensiveness and improving information retention, thereby lowering the rate of recurring errors. Writing it down that way made it feel more rational.

But I know that’s not the whole reason.

Lately, I feel like time is accelerating. Those numbers from my physical exam report are still spinning in my head. I find myself wondering: if one day I can no longer work the way I do now, how will people remember me? As a harsh quality control manager? Or... something else?

The thought is a bit absurd. I need to stay focused on management optimization, not these nebulous ideas.

I’ll continue to observe Xiao Wang’s performance tomorrow. Let’s see if this "experiment" actually works.

Another formatting error cropped up in QA this morning. It wasn't a major issue, but I can't stand that kind of slackness when it comes to details. I was right there on the floor and called it out immediately; my voice might have been a bit loud. The subordinate looked pretty shaken, but I had to make it clear—details determine product quality, and product quality determines whether this company survives. I’m not exaggerating.

I told Meifang that I need to overhaul the department’s standard operating procedures. Just emphasizing it verbally isn't enough; it has to be documented with every step clearly defined. As she listened, she just looked at me that way, and I knew what she was thinking: *Here we go again.* But this time is different. I can feel that if things continue like this, the problems are only going to multiply.

She said, "Don't push yourself too hard." I didn't respond to that. After a few bites of food, I explained once more why these standards must be strictly enforced. She just nodded and kept eating.

The truth is, I know my subordinates are afraid of me. But what’s wrong with a little fear? Fear makes people diligent. I’ve been in this position for 12 years, and our defect rate is 25% lower than the industry average—that’s not because I have a pleasant temperament.

Lately, I just feel this constant urge to hurry. Whether it’s work or anything else. While I’m still able to move, I need to get everything done right.

# MEMO

**Date:** Thursday, June 29, 2023  
**Time:** 09:30

---

**Subject: Reflections on Tool Donation and Knowledge Transfer**

My conversation with Li Meifang last night helped me clear my head.

She asked me why I wanted to donate my tools. At first, I didn’t have a good answer, but then I realized—I’ve always defined myself through my professional achievements. Those process improvements and falling defect rates were important, but in hindsight, those things lose their meaning the moment I leave the job.

**What truly matters?** It’s passing down everything I’ve accumulated over the years.

I’ve given it some thought: every tool has a story. That pair of pliers has been with me for over 20 years; I’ve used it to calibrate precision parts thousands of times. That set of measuring gauges was my go-to whenever I was training new hires. These things shouldn't just sit in my workshop gathering dust.

**What needs to be done:**

1. Organize a tool inventory—not just a list, but a record of each tool’s purpose and the stories behind them. That way, the students can truly understand their value.

2. Consider giving a talk at the school. Not to show off, but to really explain the experience—how to use tools to solve problems and how to spot quality risks in the smallest details.

3. Contact the industrial technical school to ask how we can ensure these tools are actually put to use, rather than just sitting in a display case.

**An observation:** Li Meifang’s question actually helped me. It made me stop and re-examine what I’ve been doing. I realize I might have been too anxious lately—obsessing over health data and over-monitoring her diet, trying to use control to fill the void left by my lost professional identity.

Maybe I need a change of perspective. Instead of trying to control things, I should think about what I can pass on.

**Core realization:** More important than professional achievements is the ability to pass experience on to the younger generation.

This isn't just a "pastime" for retirement; this is something meaningful.

---

**To-Do List:**
- [ ] Organize tools and take photos this weekend
- [ ] Look up the school’s contact information
- [ ] List 3–5 of the most representative tool case studies

# Work Log

**Date: Wednesday, October 26, 2022**
**Weather: Sunny**

---

This morning, I had a briefing with my supervisor to discuss the department's recent process optimization plans. As the meeting was drawing to a close, I brought up a new idea: I plan to host a "40 Years of Quality Management Experience" sharing session before I retire.

My supervisor listened intently. I laid out the concept clearly:

**Theme**: "From Frontline Inspector to Manager—Forty Years of Quality Management in Practice"

**Target Audience**: Mid-level management, key personnel from the Quality Control department, and interested technical staff.

**Core Modules**:
- The evolution and optimization of quality inspection processes
- Identification and solutions for common quality issues
- Balancing standardization and the human touch in team management
- Adaptation and innovation amidst industry shifts

He asked why I was proposing this now. I thought about it and told him the truth: "It’s not just about passing on knowledge. Everything I’ve learned over the years—if it just stays in my head, it’ll vanish once I retire. I want to document these things while I’m still on the job. It’s also a way for me to summarize and reflect on my own forty-year career."

He nodded and said it was a great idea, then asked me to put together an initial outline.

After returning to the office this afternoon, I jotted down a preliminary to-do list in my logbook:

- [ ] Compile department improvement cases from the past 12 years (data, results, lessons learned)
- [ ] Reflect on experiences from my time as a frontline inspector (comparisons with the present)
- [ ] Prepare detailed analyses for 3–5 typical case studies
- [ ] Reach out to potential participants (key subordinates, heads of other departments)
- [ ] Finalize the time and venue for the session
- [ ] Create the presentation outline and supporting materials

To be honest, I’ve been feeling anxious lately about the question, "What will I leave behind?" Now, I feel a bit more relaxed. Not everything has to be "passed down" to others. What matters more is that through this process, I can see clearly what I’ve done and what I’ve learned.

I mentioned this to Meifang during dinner. She was very supportive and even asked if I needed help organizing the materials. I said yes.

My physical condition has been okay these past two days after taking my medication. No particular discomfort. I’ll proceed with the scheduled medical checkup tomorrow.

---

**Notes**:
- Communication with my supervisor went smoothly; no resistance encountered.
- This plan gives me a clearer sense of purpose, replacing vague anxiety with a concrete goal.
- Need to complete the first draft of the outline by mid-November.
