ranked_items.content:
July 10, 2023, 8:30 PM

Hao called while I was organizing my precision tools. My hands were still stained with engine oil, but I picked up.

He said there’s a new opportunity in Shenzhen—a sales director position for a major project. It sounds promising, but his voice was thick with hesitation. I could hear it.

In the past, I would have told him exactly what to do. I’d list the pros and cons and hand down a judgment. But today, I didn’t. I asked him: "Are you excited about this opportunity, or are you scared?"

There was a long silence. Then he said, "Dad, I’m both. I’m scared I won’t do a good job, and I’m scared of the mortgage pressure getting worse."

Listening to him, it suddenly hit me—I’ve always been supervising him, not accompanying him. Just like how I treat my subordinates at the department. I thought that was being responsible, but it was really just control.

I told him: "Then give it a try. I’ll be right behind you."

I surprised even myself when I said it. It’s not my style. But it was true. I really want to stand behind him, rather than standing in front of him telling him which way to go.

His tone changed. He sounded a bit more relaxed. We talked for a while longer; he shared some project details. I didn’t rush to judge; I just listened, occasionally asking a question.

Before hanging up, I told him: "Hao, I’m proud of you."

The moment the words left my mouth, I felt a pang of regret—I’ve said things like that far too rarely. But he said, "Thanks, Dad," and there was warmth in his voice.

After putting the phone down, I sat there for a long time. I was still holding that pair of pliers my father left me.

I think maybe retirement isn’t about losing anything, but finally having the time to do the things that truly matter. Not the achievements that can be quantified, but—being there. Listening. Saying the things I’ve kept bottled up inside.

My tools, my process improvements, my standards for perfect quality... those are all important. But Hao doesn’t need a perfect father; he needs a real one. A father willing to admit he isn’t omnipotent.

I’ll keep organizing the tools tomorrow. This time, it’s not just for the donation, but to remember—some things are more important than precision.
ranked_items.subquestion: After all these ups and downs, how do you feel you’ve changed from that "neat-to-a-fault" manager sitting in the corner?

ranked_items.content:
October 12, 2022, 7:30 PM

Had a phone call with Chen Hao.

In the past, I’d always ask him if the mortgage was a burden, how the orders were coming along, or if his job was stable. I don’t know what came over me today, but the first thing out of my mouth was, "How have you been lately? Is there anything I can help with?" He seemed taken aback; there was a two-second silence before he answered.

He said work is exhausting, that sales is high-pressure, and sometimes he wonders if he’s doing the right thing. He’s lost. He was very candid, and I could hear the fatigue in his voice.

Before, I would have jumped straight in with advice, telling him exactly what he should do. But this time, I just said, "I understand. I’ve been through a stage like that, too."

Even I was a bit surprised by my own words. In forty years, I’ve never spoken to him like that.

He asked me when that was. I told him that when I was his age, I wasn't sure if I was making the right choices either. Back then, I was a quality inspector at the grassroots level, doing the same repetitive work every day, unable to see a way out. What I didn’t tell him was that I just kept going down that same path ever since, never changing course again.

He didn’t respond immediately, but I could sense something had shifted. It wasn't that superior-subordinate dynamic anymore; it felt more like... two people who had walked the same road.

After hanging up, I sat at my desk and looked at that medical report. The doctor said I’ll need long-term medication and a lifestyle change. I’ve been thinking about how to reverse this outcome through stricter management, just like I do at work—precise control, eliminating variables.

But while talking to Chen Hao, it suddenly occurred to me that maybe some things can’t be controlled. Some paths, once walked, are simply walked.

I need to rethink how I interact with them. Not supervising, not guiding. Maybe just... being there?

The thought makes me a little uneasy. I don't know if I still know how to do it.

More tomorrow.

ranked_items.content:
July 14, 2022, 3:00 PM

Just got off the phone with the hospital. I moved my physical exam from July 25th up to tomorrow, July 15th.

The operator asked why I wanted to move it forward. I told her there was no special reason, just that I’m relatively free right now. That was a lie. I knew I was lying, but I couldn't explain the real reason. Maybe there isn't a reason at all, or maybe there are too many.

*The sooner it’s done, the better.* This sentence keeps looping in my head. It’s as if once I finish the check-up tomorrow and get the report, everything will be fine. But that logic doesn't hold up. I know that perfectly well.

My productivity has been low these past two days. I had a quality review meeting this morning and found myself repeating the same things; the expressions on my subordinates' faces told me they noticed it too. When I got back to the office this afternoon, I was supposed to organize some process documentation, but I sat there for half an hour without typing a single word. My appetite isn't great either; I only ate half of my lunch. Li Meifang asked if I was feeling unwell. I said no, I’m just not hungry.

My fingers have been tapping on the desk all afternoon. *Tap, tap, tap.* The rhythm is fast and restless. I can’t stand the sound of it myself, but I can’t stop. This isn't like me. I can usually control these things.

In the evening, I flipped through that tool logbook in the workshop. Looking at those dense notes, it suddenly felt a bit absurd. What am I recording? Who is going to use these tools? My eldest son is selling his house, and my daughter is teaching. Neither of them needs any of this. So why am I still keeping records?

I caught a glimpse of myself in the mirror. I look a bit pale.

The results will be out tomorrow afternoon. My heart is racing, but I tell myself it’s a normal physiological response. Just nerves. But why am I nervous? I should be feeling confident.

Whatever. Time for bed. Waking up at 6:00 AM tomorrow.

ranked_items.content:
# Memo - Reflection and Legacy

**Date: October 18, 2022, Afternoon**
**Location: Home Study**

---

## Core Points Summary

### I. Reflections on the Essence of 40 Years of Work
- ~~Perfect quality control~~ → Obsession with detail is a value in itself
- ~~Sense of achievement from process optimization~~ → The patience and persistence learned through the process
- A 25% drop in defect rates is not the finish line, but a reminder: Why do I demand so much?
  - Answer: Because I believe in standards.
  - The real answer: Because I am afraid of losing control.

### II. Beyond the Technical (Important)
- The meaning of tools lies not in collecting them, but in how they taught me to think.
- What precision work taught me:
  - A single millimeter determines success or failure.
  - However, the distance between people cannot be measured in millimeters.
  - (Thinking back on my harshness toward subordinates and my "supervision" of my wife over the years.)
- I must admit: I cannot control everything.
  - The doctor's words are still ringing in my ears.
  - The body ages; plans change.
  - This is not failure.

### III. If I Could Start Over
- Explain to the team *why* we do things earlier, rather than just telling them *what* to do.
- Give young engineers more room to make mistakes (instead of blunt dismissals).
- ~~Keeping knowledge in my head~~ → Actively record, share, and discuss.
- Listen more to my wife, rather than "supervising" from the sidelines.
- Communicate with the children about more than just their mortgages and jobs.

### IV. Words for Young Engineers
- Standards are important, but don't let them become a weapon.
- Details determine quality, but details can also consume your life—learn to prioritize.
- 40 years is a long time, but it is also very short.
- The things you take for granted now will become mere memories by the time you reach my age.
- Don't wait for your heart to remind you—some things are more important than perfection.

### V. Immediate Action Plan
- [ ] Compile a "Core Experiences in Quality Management" document (for technical school students).
- [ ] Sit down with my subordinates for a talk—explain rather than accuse.
- [ ] Have a real conversation with my wife this weekend; listen to her talk about home.
- [ ] Call Chen Hao and Chen Yu respectively; do not mention work.
- [ ] Continue medication and scheduled check-ups—accept this reality.

---

## Notes
- The precision tool inventory can be set aside for now.
- The 40-year timeline is drawn; now it needs to be turned into a story.
- Perhaps retirement isn't the end, but a different kind of beginning?
- (I wouldn't have believed that sentence before.)

---

**One final question: What is my greatest regret in life?**
It isn't that I didn't reach a higher position.
It’s that I never truly looked at the people beside me.

# Calendar Event

**Title:** Heart-to-heart with Meifang - Regarding recent issues

**Date:** June 18, 2023

**Time:** 19:30 - 20:30

**Location:** Living room at home

**Reminder:** 1 hour before

---

**Event Notes:**

I spent a long time thinking this afternoon. Meifang said I’ve been overmanaging lately—her blood pressure, her diet, her checkups... Her tone was calm, but I could hear the resentment.

I’ve realized something: I’m using "supervising her" as a way to fill the void left by losing my job. It’s not fair.

I need to have a real talk with her tonight. Not a routine report on household chores, but a genuine conversation. I need to listen to her instead of telling her what to do.

Maybe I’ve always been like this—using control as a way to show I care. But it’s wrong.

Topics to cover:
- Admit to my recent overstepping
- Ask her how she truly feels lately
- Listen to her; don't rush to offer solutions

This will be hard. But it must be done.

ranked_items.content:
July 1, 2023 Afternoon

I was tidying up the tool shed when the school called. They asked if I could give a lecture to the students about precision engineering and quality management.

I didn't say yes right away. There was a long pause.

The person on the other end was waiting for me to speak; I could feel it. What was I thinking? I was wondering if I’m even qualified to talk about these things anymore. Without the factory identity, without the title of Department Manager, does what I say still carry any weight? Or am I just using this to fill this growing void—the void called "me"?

But I said it anyway: "I can give it a try."

The moment I said it, I surprised myself. It’s not like me. Usually, I say "yes" or "no"; I rarely say "try." "Try" implies uncertainty; it implies the possibility of failure. I used to find that hard to accept.

After hanging up, I sat in the tool shed for a while. My fingers brushed against a pair of pliers I bought back in 1995—I’ve used them for nearly thirty years. It suddenly occurred to me that perhaps these tools shouldn't just stay in my hands. Maybe they should be seen, used, and learned from by more people.

That thought brought a feeling I haven't felt in a long time. It wasn't a sense of achievement—I got used to that long ago at work. It was something else. Maybe a sense of... being useful? No, more than just useful. A sense of being needed.

But I’m also doubting myself. Do I really want to help those students, or am I just lying to myself? Do I truly want to share knowledge, or am I just trying to prove I still have value? Where is the line between the two? I’m not entirely sure.

Maybe it doesn't matter. Maybe what matters is that I said, "I can give it a try."

I’ve decided to donate some tools to the school. Not all of them, but some. The ones I use most often, the ones that have the most to teach.

Li Meifang asked what I was doing sitting in the shed for so long. I told her nothing, just tidying up. She didn't ask further.

In the evening, I dug out some old work notes and lecture materials. I never imagined I’d be teaching. Now that I am, I need to figure out how to do it.

I feel a bit nervous. And also a bit... expectant?

It’s a strange feeling.

---

July 1, 2023 Evening Addendum

I admit, I still don't know if I can pull this lecture off. I don't even know what my true motive is.

But at least I’m doing something. I’m not just sitting at home, counting every single digit on my medical report.

# Work Log: Tuesday, July 5, 2022

**Weather: Sunny, 32°C**

Finished the quality assessment for the Q3 production plan this morning. The defect rate is holding steady at 0.8%, which is within expectations. In the afternoon, I met with the Procurement Department regarding inspection standards for the new equipment. I proposed three improvements, and they were accepted.

Spent the evening at home organizing the notebooks in the tool room. I realized that the descriptions I’d previously noted for dozens of precision tools aren't detailed enough. After some thought, I feel it’s necessary to establish a more systematic set of technical documentation. It’s not for any particular reason—I just feel that if these things are sitting here, anyone who wants to use them should know how.

Called Haozi to check in on him. He said the mortgage pressure is still there, but manageable. I didn't say much. As I hung up, it suddenly occurred to me—he has absolutely no interest in these tools, and neither does Yuyu. That’s normal; they have their own paths to follow. Still, I can't help but feel it's a bit of a pity.

Tomorrow, I’ll continue working on the framework for the employee training system. The current problem is that knowledge of many critical processes is held by only a few veteran employees; if they leave, that knowledge is lost. I have to find a way to document this tacit knowledge and turn it into something transferable. This, too, is a company asset.

Next week, I need to follow up on the May medical check-up reports. Li Meifang’s blood pressure has been fluctuating again this month; I must keep pushing her to watch her diet.

11:00 PM. Work log complete.

Proposed three process optimization suggestions, which have been forwarded to the superior department. Finished packing half of the office; document filing is largely complete.

- Reflected on a 40-year career.

- Briefly summarized my understanding of quality management.  

**Post-meeting:** Shook hands and said goodbye to every attendee. Received several commemorative gifts.  

---

## Summary  

40 years. From quality inspector to department manager. Defect rate reduced by 25%. An average of 3–5 process improvement proposals per year.  

The numbers are accurate. But there are other things that I saw more clearly today.

Perhaps I can start saying it now.

ranked_items.content:
# Work Log · Knowledge Repository

**Date:** July 7, 2023, 9:00 PM
**Recorded by:** Chen Guilin

---

## Case Study: Handling the 2010 Quality Crisis

I recently dug up some old work logs and came across the records from the 2010 crisis. Tonight, I want to systematically organize this case. It’s not about nostalgia; I just feel that these things shouldn't be left to rot in a drawer.

**Background:**
In March 2010, a major client reported batch-level weld cracking after two months of product use. The issue involved an order of approximately 8,000 units, with 6,500 already delivered. I was the Quality Control Manager at the time.

**The Decision-Making Process:**

Step 1—Immediate Damage Control (March 15–16)
- Halted all subsequent production and shipments for that batch.
- Initiated an emergency recall of delivered products (eventually recovered 4,200 units).
- Notified the client and explained the issue transparently rather than hiding it.
- Cost: A decline in client trust and a three-month freeze on orders.

Step 2—Root Cause Analysis (March 17–April 10)
- Determined it wasn't a design flaw, but a temperature control deviation in the welding process.
- Discovered that while the parameters on the newly replaced welding machines were set correctly, the actual power output fluctuated by ±3% from the standard.
- This fluctuation was overlooked during routine testing (our testing precision at the time was only ±5%).

Step 3—Systemic Improvements (April–June)
- Upgraded testing equipment, improving precision to ±1%.
- Fully standardized the welding process and established daily temperature logs.
- Retrained all QC inspectors, emphasizing a re-inspection mechanism for "borderline" products.
- Established an "Anomaly Early Warning" system—any time testing data approached the upper or lower limits, the process had to be traced.

**Results:**
- Production resumed in July, and supply was restored in August.
- The client eventually accepted our solution and compensation package.
- That client went on to become one of our most stable major accounts, and we continue to work together to this day.

**Distilled Principles:**

1. **Transparency over Concealment** — The decision to tell the truth back then was difficult. However, if we had hidden it and the client found out on their own, trust would have been gone forever. Honesty gave us a chance to make amends.

2. **Root Cause vs. Surface Cause** — It’s easy to blame "improper operator handling," but the real issue was the equipment's inherent fluctuation. If we had only punished the employees, the problem would have recurred.

3. **Testing Precision Must Lead the Process** — Our testing standard was ±5%, but the actual process fluctuation was ±3%. This meant our testing was essentially operating in a "blind spot." Since then, I’ve insisted that testing precision must be an order of magnitude higher than the process requirements.

4. **Early Warning vs. After-the-Fact Remediation** — Discovering a problem only after a crisis hits carries the highest cost. The core of quality management is establishing early warning mechanisms to catch issues in their infancy.

---

## Advice for Young Managers

If someone asked me how to handle quality management, I would say these few things:

**Don't just look at the numbers.** Low defect rates and high pass rates look great on paper. But what’s behind those numbers? Is the process truly stable, or are you just getting lucky? Frequently ask yourself: "If a link in this chain fails, how much lead time would I have to detect it?"

**Trust your subordinates, but don't trust the process.** My team has strong execution, but I never relax standards just because "they’ve never had a problem before." Process stability must be guaranteed by the system, not by individual conscientiousness.

**A crisis is worth its cost only if you gain systemic improvements.** In 2010, we spent three months and a significant amount of capital. But that early warning system has been in use ever since—how many potential crises has it prevented? It was worth it.

**Client relationships are forged in a crisis.** Routine cooperation is easy; how you handle things when they go wrong is what reveals a company's true character.

---

## Current Thoughts

It feels a bit strange sitting at home writing this. I’ve lived for forty years and never systematically organized these thoughts. When I was working, I always felt too busy; now, I finally have the time.

I’m wondering if I should compile these cases and reflections into a formal document. Not for fame or anything, but I just feel this experience shouldn't go to waste. If it can provide some reference for younger managers, it will have been of some use.

I mentioned donating tools to the school before; now I’m thinking, maybe I could also do a small knowledge-sharing session? It doesn't have to be formal—just talking through these cases.

I'll finish organizing these notes first.

---

**Next Steps:**
- [ ] Organize the 2015 supply chain optimization case.
- [ ] Organize the 2018 automation transformation experience.
- [ ] Consider whether to format these into a shareable document.

ranked_items.content:
# MEMO

**Date: Saturday Morning, October 22, 2022**

Had a phone call with Chen Hao. After hearing about my diagnosis, his first instinct was to ask if I’d been taking my medicine on time and if he should take time off to come back. I didn't expect him to ask that.

Before this, I always felt like there was something standing between us. Our calls were always about work, the house, or whether they were eating well—like going through a checklist. Their answers were always brief. I thought that was just how we were.

But this time was different. When he said, "Dad, you need to take care of yourself," I heard something different in his voice. My own voice choked up a bit; all I could manage was, "Okay, thank you." That’s rare for me.

Li Meifang says the way I’ve shown concern for the kids all these years has been flawed—always supervising, never just being there. I used to disagree with her. But thinking about it now, maybe she’s right.

**A few things I need to figure out:**

1. Have a real talk with Chen Hao and Chen Yu individually before I retire. Not to ask about their careers or lives, but to truly listen to them. Maybe they have things they want to say to me, too.

2. What does "legacy" actually mean? I’ve always wanted to pass down my understanding of work and my obsession with quality to them. But they have their own lives. Maybe I should just record these things for them to reference, rather than expecting them to inherit it all.

3. The collaboration with the technical school—donating tools, doing sharing sessions. I want to do this even more now. Not to prove anything, but because I truly want to leave these things behind.

4. Have a proper talk with Li Meifang next week. She’s been by my side all these years, yet I’ve always been "managing" her. It’s time to change that.

**One more thought:**

Maybe the things I’ve done right over these forty years aren't the process improvements or the defect rate data. Maybe it’s that obsession with detail, that commitment to responsibility, and that care for my family—even if I haven't been great at expressing it. If Chen Hao and Chen Yu can see these things in me, that’s enough.

I don’t need them to become me. I just need them to know that I’ve always been here.

---

*Going to play chess with the guys this afternoon. My game hasn't been great lately; my mind keeps wandering. But maybe that’s okay—I don’t need to win. Just sitting there, being with them, is enough.*

ranked_items.content:
# Work Notebook

**Date: Wednesday, October 26, 2022**
**Location: Office**

---

## Thoughts on Technical Sharing

Had a brief chat with my supervisor this morning about my pre-retirement arrangements. He asked if I’d considered doing some training or a knowledge-sharing session for the younger engineers. I didn’t give him a direct answer at the time, but the idea has been on my mind ever since.

I used to feel this heavy obligation to "pass down" everything I’ve learned over the years in its entirety—as if that were the only way to prove these 40 years weren't for nothing. Looking back now, I think I was being too stubborn about it. Maybe that’s not what matters most.

**What I actually want to record:**

1. **On Quality Standards**
   - It’s not about pursuing perfection for perfection’s sake.
   - It’s about being accountable at every step and being responsible to the user.
   - The logic behind setting a standard is more important than the standard itself.
   - Example: Last year’s drop in defect rates wasn't due to new regulations, but because everyone finally understood the "why."

2. **On Process Improvement**
   - The goal of improvement isn't to make life difficult for people; it’s to make the work clearer.
   - Over the years, the proposals I made that actually worked were all rooted in real-world problems, not abstract imagination.
   - Listen to the voices on the front line; they know best where the bottlenecks are.

3. **On Working with People**
   - I need to be honest here: my approach has had its flaws.
   - Being blunt can be hurtful, and I know it has contributed to staff turnover.
   - Perhaps I should mention this in the session—not as self-criticism, but to tell the younger generation that being rigorous and being respectful are not mutually exclusive.
   - Where I fell short, they can do better.

4. **On Perseverance**
   - Spending 40 years in one place—some people say I lacked ambition.
   - But what I see is the value of deeply understanding an industry, a company, and a role.
   - Not all change is progress; sometimes, stability itself is a skill.

---

## Why I want to do this now

After that check-up with the doctor, things started to click. I can’t control aging, and I can’t ensure everything goes according to plan. What I can do, however, is record my reflections from all these years—not as a "legacy," but to clarify for myself what I’ve actually learned.

Meifang has been accompanying me on walks lately, and she mentioned I’ve become more talkative. I feel it too; I’m no longer in such a rush to prove anything.

If I do a session before I retire, this is what I want to talk about. It doesn't need to be formal—just a clear account of the experiences and the lessons. I want the younger ones to know there are different ways to do a job: you can be rigorous, but you can also be more human.

---

## To-Do

- Organize key improvement cases from the last 12 years (3-5 typical ones).
- Think about how to talk about the failed attempts.
- Confirm the specific timing and format of the session with my supervisor.
- Ask Hao and Yu what they think about me doing this.

To be honest, I felt a bit nervous when this idea first surfaced. Now that I’ve written it down, I actually feel a sense of relief.

ranked_items.content:
**July 10, 2022 Work Log**

**Weather: Sunny  Temperature: 32°C**

**Morning Work Record**

09:15 Quality Control Department weekly meeting. Discovered issues while reviewing yesterday’s production reports.

The periodic inspection sheet submitted by my subordinate, Li, was a mess—he used Chinese capitalized characters for the date column, which violates the company’s unified standards. This is no small matter. I pointed out the error in front of everyone. My tone might have been harsh, but it had to be. Details determine product quality, and product quality determines the company’s survival. This logical chain cannot be allowed to slacken. A formatting error can lead to data misinterpretation; misinterpretation can lead to missed inspections; and a missed inspection means defective products reaching the market. I cannot tolerate a failure in any link of this chain.

Li looked visibly upset at the time. I know he probably thinks I went overboard. But I don’t need him to like me; I need him to understand the importance of standardization.

**Developing New QC Standards**

09:45-11:20 Drafted the new "Detailed Rules for Quality Inspection of Precision Components (Revised Edition)" in my office.

Key adjustments:
- Table formats unified to Arabic numerals and 24-hour clock.
- Added a "Basis for Judgment" column for every inspection item; reference standard numbers must be cited.
- Added an "Auditor Review" step at the inspector’s signature line—a double-confirmation system.
- Data entry deadline moved up to 17:00 daily, leaving 2 hours for data auditing.
- Added a "Deviation Analysis" section to the monthly summary report; reasons and corrective measures for every out-of-spec item must be explained.

None of this is needless bureaucracy. Every single rule points toward the same goal: turning invisible risks into visible data, and turning potential errors into inevitable discoveries.

I plan to hold a departmental meeting next Monday to announce the new standards. Everyone will be given a 3-day transition period, with formal implementation starting July 15.

**Other**

Did not go out for lunch. Stayed in the office to review the draft of the new standards again and refined the wording in two places.

I am meeting with my superior this afternoon to report on this month’s improvement plan. This new set of standards should be adopted.

**Physical Status**

Sleep has been okay lately. No particular discomfort.

My wife mentioned yesterday that her blood pressure has risen again. Reminded her to take her medication on time and sent her a text at noon telling her to use less salt in dinner.

**Remarks**

I feel my demands for work have been increasing lately. It’s not because of new pressure from the company, but rather a feeling that I cannot allow for any slack. I have less than two years until retirement; I want to optimize all processes and set all standards in stone during this time. That way, even after I leave, the system will continue to function properly. This is important.

---

*Recorded by: Chen Guilin*
*Date: 2022-07-10*

ranked_items.content:
# Work Log

**Date: Monday, September 12, 2022**

**Weather: Sunny**

---

During the morning workshop inspection, I noticed that the welding deviation at Station 3 on Line B exceeded the tolerance. That new technician—what’s his name again? I can’t recall—made the same mistake again. The welding parameters are clearly stated on page 12 of the operation manual, yet he still failed to follow the standards. The defect rate has ticked up by 0.3 percentage points.

I had a talk with him in the afternoon. As usual, I pointed out the issues—presenting the data, the causes, and the consequences clearly. He kept his head down and didn't say much. I could tell he was nervous. I used to think that pointing out the problem directly was enough—he should know how to fix it. But today, for some reason, looking at him like that, a thought suddenly struck me: Does he truly understand *why* it has to be done this way? Or is he just intimidated?

I mentioned this to Meifang tonight. She asked me, "Have you ever thought that maybe what he needs isn't just to be told he's wrong, but to understand why he can't afford to be wrong?" I didn't respond immediately, but her words have been playing over in my mind.

I’ve been in this position for 12 years. Those processes, standards, and details—they are all in my head, as natural as breathing. But what about for a newcomer? Perhaps I’m too quick to assume they should just know. Maybe I should take the time to explain the "why" instead of just the "what."

I’ll talk to him again tomorrow. This time, I want to ask him what his understanding of the welding parameters is. Maybe we’ll start from there.

Another thought: I’ve accumulated these experiences and methods over 40 years. If one day I’m no longer here, what happens to all of this? Perhaps it’s time to start thinking about how to truly pass these things down, rather than just leaving behind a pile of regulations and disciplinary records.

Tidied up the tool room before clocking out. The precision calipers on the third row were a bit rusty; gave them a wipe-down.

---

**Plan for Tomorrow:**
- Conduct a second follow-up with the Line B technician, focusing on understanding rather than criticism.
- Review last week’s quality inspection reports.
- Consider drafting a "Standard Operating Understanding Guide" for new employees.

# Work Log

**Date: Monday, March 21, 2022**

**Weather: Overcast**

---

**Morning Records:**

8:30 Arrived at the office. Reviewed last Friday’s QC reports and found that three products from Line B had dimensional deviations exceeding the standard by 0.2mm. This is unacceptable. Immediately called a meeting with the relevant personnel; the root cause must be identified today. There is no compromise on quality.

9:15 Discussed process issues with Master Li. His explanation was still too superficial. I pointed out the problem directly—it’s not a machine issue, but a failure to strictly follow operating procedures. This kind of low-level error should not occur in our department. Gave him a clear deadline: an improvement plan must be on my desk by this Friday. No room for negotiation.

10:00 Compiled the first draft of this month’s improvement proposals. I plan to submit four items to my superiors:
- Optimization of calibration cycles for testing equipment
- Upgrading classification standards for raw material warehousing
- Refining the skill assessment system for subordinates
- Preventive maintenance plan for the production lines

These are all necessary. We cannot slack off.

**Midday Notes:**

During lunch, it occurred to me that if we maintain current efficiency levels, we could reduce our defect rate by another 3-5%. But the prerequisite is that everyone must execute the standards to the letter. No one can be the weak link.

**Afternoon Plan:**

- 14:00 On-site production inspection
- 15:30 Monthly summary meeting with the QC team
- 16:30 Organize tool inventory (Need to document the specific uses of those precision tools in my home workshop, just in case—never mind, just get them recorded for now)

**Other:**

Lately, I feel the work pace needs to be tightened further. There can be no laxity. Time passes quickly; what can be done must be done early. Efficiency is paramount.

My wife mentioned yesterday that her blood pressure is a bit high again. Reminded her to take her medicine on time and stop eating so much salty food. These small matters must also be managed properly.

---

**Remarks:**

Focus on the improvement progress of Line B next week. A standard is a standard; there is no room for bargaining.

ranked_items.content:
After returning home that evening, Chen Guilin changed his clothes and sat down at the dining table. Li Meifang was serving the soup.

He ate a few mouthfuls of rice in silence before putting down his chopsticks.

"I might have been too harsh at the department meeting today," he said, his voice low, eyes fixed on his bowl. "The new QC inspector messed up the data entry again, and I called him out in front of everyone. He looked pretty shaken."

Li Meifang looked up at him but didn't respond immediately.

"My temper has been short lately," he paused, tapping his finger on the table. "I can feel it myself. I have high standards for the work, but..." He didn't finish the sentence, opting for a sip of soup instead.

He turned to his wife, his tone tinged with uncertainty. "Do you think I'm being too demanding? I want to get things done right, but it seems like..." He hesitated, "it seems like I always end up making people uncomfortable."

He wasn't looking for comfort; it was more like he was trying to confirm something. There was exhaustion in his eyes, along with a certain guilt he couldn't quite put into words.

"The physical exam is next week," he suddenly changed the subject, though his tone remained heavy. "I need to get to bed early these next few days."

He lowered his head and continued eating, but his movements were much slower than usual.
ranked_items.subquestion: After all these ups and downs, how do you feel you’ve changed from that "neat-to-a-fault" manager sitting in the corner?

ranked_items.content:
# October 8, 2022, 8:15 PM

Sitting in my study, the work log by my hand is nearly finished. Flipping to the last few pages, I realized the nature of my entries has changed. They are no longer just technical data and process optimizations; instead, I’ve started writing these inexplicable sentences—"If I could do it over, I would teach the younger generation more."

Looking at those words, even I find them unfamiliar.

This isn't like me. I’ve never been one for such sentimentality. But lately, I have been wondering: over these forty years, I’ve drafted so many improvement plans and brought the defect rates down, but what is actually left behind? A stack of reports? Some data? Or just the resentment in the hearts of those young engineers I criticized so bluntly?

Li Meifang was nagging about her blood pressure again today. I made a new diet plan for her; she didn't say anything, but that look in her eyes... I know what she’s thinking. After all these years of marriage, we haven't communicated much, but the way she looks at me has changed. Perhaps she has sensed something, too.

In the office this afternoon, a new QC inspector asked me a technical question. As usual, I pointed out the flaws in his logic directly, but this time, I stopped myself. I paused, and instead, explained to him *why* it had to be done that way. He listened intently. In that moment, I thought, maybe... maybe I should do more of that.

It’s not that I’ve suddenly become soft. I still loathe inefficiency and ambiguity. But I’m beginning to realize that my value might not lie in how much knowledge I’ve documented, but in... whether I’ve actually helped others understand what a true work ethic looks like.

I’m considering holding a technical sharing session for the young engineers in the department before I retire. I never thought I’d want to do something like that. But now I feel that if I don’t do it now, the opportunity might be gone forever.

Why am I thinking this way? I can’t quite say.

Perhaps it’s because time has suddenly become tangible. I used to feel that time was something to be managed, just like a project. Now... now it feels like it’s slipping away at a speed I can no longer control.

I don’t want to admit it. But writing it down here makes it feel real.

Tomorrow is another day. There are still many tools that need organizing.
ranked_items.subquestion: After all these ups and downs, how do you feel you’ve changed from that "neat-to-a-fault" manager sitting in the corner?

ranked_items.content:
# Work Log

**Date**: Friday, October 28, 2022
**Weather**: Sunny
**Recorded by**: Chen Guilin

---

## Today's Work

Held a departmental feedback meeting at 10:00 AM. The approach today was different from usual, and it’s worth noting down.

Previously, I would point out problems directly and give corrective feedback immediately. Today, I changed my mindset. I started by praising several engineers for their recent performance—specifically Xiao Wang’s ideas on improving the welding process. Only then did I explain why certain parameters needed to be adjusted this way instead of that way. The key was that I invited them into the thought process rather than just handing them the answers.

I asked Xiao Li, "Why do you think this data is fluctuating?" He was a bit nervous at first, but then he slowly began to analyze it. In the end, he derived the conclusion himself. The result was much better than if I had just told him.

After the meeting, several young engineers came to me on their own initiative to ask questions. Their attitude was noticeably different. It wasn't the defensive stance you see after being criticized; they genuinely wanted to understand the principles.

## Reflections

This method takes more time. If I had followed my old efficiency standards, I could have finished in five minutes what took twenty today. But the outcome was different.

Looking back, I’ve spent all these years chasing "correctness" and "efficiency." Point out the problem, give the answer, and move on to the next. But perhaps the issue isn't the answer itself, but whether they truly understand the "why."

I’m thinking that maybe this is what I should leave behind for this team before I retire—not just a complete operating manual, but a way of thinking. A dedication to detail and an attitude of not being afraid to ask why.

I used to think these young people weren't serious enough, but looking at it now, perhaps it was my approach that gave them no opportunity to truly think.

I’ll continue to observe the effects tomorrow. I might try this approach again during next week’s process audit meeting.

---

**Notes**: The defect rate remained at 0.8% this week, continuing to stay below the industry average.

ranked_items.content:
July 11, 2023, Night

Today, I finally did something I’ve been thinking about for a long time but never got around to—labeling my tools.

After returning from the office this afternoon, I spent two hours in my small workshop. I picked up every single tool, wiped it clean, and used a fine-tip pen to write down its origin and purpose on a label; for some, I even noted the year I bought them. That vernier caliper from 1995, that set of precision screwdrivers I brought back from a business trip to Japan in 2003... halfway through, I realized what I was actually doing—I was telling stories for these objects.

Meifang came in and asked what I was up to, so I told her. She didn't tease me; instead, she sat down beside me and listened to the story behind every tool. I had never shared the details of my work with her like this before, but today I just kept talking—slowly and meticulously.

When Chen Yu called, I happened to be talking about that pair of lineman’s pliers. I kept her on the line and continued. She listened intently, and later she said, "Dad, you are shaping a pursuit of excellence." I was a bit stunned when I heard that. She said she wanted to integrate these stories of mine into her teaching, to let her students know that work isn't just work—it’s a reflection of how a person thinks and gets things done.

I didn't know what to say, so I just said, "Okay."

When she came over later, we sat in the workshop for a while longer. She gave me a hug. My body was a bit stiff at first, but then I relaxed. I gently patted her back, just like... just like I should have done a long time ago but never did.

I understand now. I’ve been worried that my knowledge would have no heir, fearing that I’d lose my value after retirement. But that’s not the case. Chen Yu is teaching students in her classroom, Chen Hao is building things in Shenzhen, and I am here making quality products. Each of us, in our own place, is passing on this dedication to doing things right. That is enough.

That is enough.

I’m sitting at my desk now, looking at those labeled tools, and things feel a bit different. They are no longer just my collection; they are a conversation. A conversation between me and Chen Yu, and perhaps, a conversation between me and the students who will eventually receive these tools.

Maybe retirement isn't the end. Maybe it’s a different kind of beginning.

ranked_items.content:
# MEMO

**Date**: July 5, 2023, 7:00 PM
**Subject**: Reflections on the Passing of Values

---

I was on the phone with my daughter today when a thought suddenly struck me.

She was talking about student development, and I was listening. Then I realized—what she is pursuing seems to be the exact same thing I pursue in the factory.

I have always strived for product excellence. She strives for the growth of her students. My son upholds integrity in real estate sales.

On the surface, these paths look different. But at their core, we are all chasing "excellence."

Or rather, we are all striving to do things well. To do things right.

It’s a strange realization. I’ve never thought about it this way before. I used to think the value of my work existed only within the factory walls. Now it seems that this obsession with excellence is alive in them, just in different forms.

It’s not that I preached any grand philosophies to them. It’s that they saw how I worked. And now, they are pursuing that same thing in their own way.

This makes me feel that perhaps my value won’t simply vanish when I leave the factory. It already exists elsewhere, in other forms, continuing on.

The thought brings me a sense of peace.

---

**Postscript**:
The tool donation doesn’t seem like an empty gesture now. Perhaps that, too, is a way of passing it on—passing the pursuit of excellence to more people.

I need to think on this further.

# Work Notebook

**Date: Wednesday, October 26, 2022**
**Location: Office**

---

## Thoughts on Technical Sharing

Had a brief chat with my supervisor this morning about my pre-retirement arrangements. He asked if I’d considered doing some training or a knowledge-sharing session for the younger engineers. I didn’t give him a direct answer at the time, but the idea has been on my mind ever since.

I used to feel this heavy obligation to "pass down" everything I’ve learned over the years in its entirety—as if that were the only way to prove these 40 years weren't for nothing. Looking back now, I think I was being too stubborn about it. Maybe that’s not what matters most.

**What I actually want to record:**

1. **On Quality Standards**
   - It’s not about pursuing perfection for perfection’s sake.
   - It’s about being accountable at every step and being responsible to the user.
   - The logic behind setting a standard is more important than the standard itself.
   - Example: Last year’s drop in defect rates wasn't due to new regulations, but because everyone finally understood the "why."

2. **On Process Improvement**
   - The goal of improvement isn't to make life difficult for people; it’s to make the work clearer.
   - Over the years, the proposals I made that actually worked were all rooted in real-world problems, not abstract imagination.
   - Listen to the voices on the front line; they know best where the bottlenecks are.

3. **On Working with People**
   - I need to be honest here: my approach has had its flaws.
   - Being blunt can be hurtful, and I know it has contributed to staff turnover.
   - Perhaps I should mention this in the session—not as self-criticism, but to tell the younger generation that being rigorous and being respectful are not mutually exclusive.
   - Where I fell short, they can do better.

4. **On Perseverance**
   - Spending 40 years in one place—some people say I lacked ambition.
   - But what I see is the value of deeply understanding an industry, a company, and a role.
   - Not all change is progress; sometimes, stability itself is a skill.

---

## Why I want to do this now

After that check-up with the doctor, things started to click. I can’t control aging, and I can’t ensure everything goes according to plan. What I can do, however, is record my reflections from all these years—not as a "legacy," but to clarify for myself what I’ve actually learned.

Meifang has been accompanying me on walks lately, and she mentioned I’ve become more talkative. I feel it too; I’m no longer in such a rush to prove anything.

If I do a session before I retire, this is what I want to talk about. It doesn't need to be formal—just a clear account of the experiences and the lessons. I want the younger ones to know there are different ways to do a job: you can be rigorous, but you can also be more human.

---

## To-Do

- Organize key improvement cases from the last 12 years (3-5 typical ones).
- Think about how to talk about the failed attempts.
- Confirm the specific timing and format of the session with my supervisor.
- Ask Hao and Yu what they think about me doing this.

To be honest, I felt a bit nervous when this idea first surfaced. Now that I’ve written it down, I actually feel a sense of relief.

ranked_items.content:
July 8, 2023   Evening

After the game ended, I sat alone in the car for a long time before driving home.

What Player B said kept circling in my head: "From taking to giving." I didn't react immediately at the time; I just kept arranging the pieces. But thinking back now, those words were like a needle, pricking something deep inside.

I always thought my post-retirement anxiety came from losing my professional identity. But today I realized that might not be it. Maybe what I’m truly afraid of is that I have nothing left to give. When I was working, I had defect rate data, process improvement plans, and a relentless obsession with quality. Those things made me feel valuable. And now?

This afternoon, I took the initiative and said I’d bring out my tool collection to show them. The moment the words left my mouth, I was surprised at myself. It’s not like me. But Player A immediately said "Great," and Player B nodded too. They seemed like they actually wanted to see them. It made me feel… I don’t know how to put it, just a bit different.

I’m thinking that maybe my value didn't lie in those professional achievements. Maybe it lies in these tools, in the things I can share with others. I can tell them the purpose of every single tool, how to maintain them, and how to use them to make a piece of work more refined. That knowledge didn't vanish just because I left the job.

But at the same time, I’m doubting myself. Is this a real realization, or am I just lying to myself again? I’m trying to find a new identity, a new sense of worth. Maybe this is just my old habit—control. I can’t control work anymore, so now I’m trying to regain a sense of mastery by sharing tools, by "giving."

I’m a bit tired. My energy is low. But that moment this afternoon, when I invited them over, I felt something different. It wasn't that sense of accomplishment you get from work, but… a sense of being needed? Or rather, a sense of connection with others.

I need to think about it more. Maybe I can invite them over next week. I’ll need to tidy up the workshop.

Also, I’ve realized that my recent "supervision" of Meifang has probably gone too far. She gave me that look again today. I know she’s rejecting my concern. But I can't let go. I’m still trying to prove I’m useful through control. This habit is really hard to break.

Anyway, that’s enough for now. I’ll think more tomorrow.

ranked_items.content:
# Memo - Reflection and Legacy

**Date: October 18, 2022, Afternoon**
**Location: Home Study**

---

## Core Points Summary

### I. Reflections on the Essence of 40 Years of Work
- ~~Perfect quality control~~ → Obsession with detail is a value in itself
- ~~Sense of achievement from process optimization~~ → The patience and persistence learned through the process
- A 25% drop in defect rates is not the finish line, but a reminder: Why do I demand so much?
  - Answer: Because I believe in standards.
  - The real answer: Because I am afraid of losing control.

### II. Beyond the Technical (Important)
- The meaning of tools lies not in collecting them, but in how they taught me to think.
- What precision work taught me:
  - A single millimeter determines success or failure.
  - However, the distance between people cannot be measured in millimeters.
  - (Thinking back on my harshness toward subordinates and my "supervision" of my wife over the years.)
- I must admit: I cannot control everything.
  - The doctor's words are still ringing in my ears.
  - The body ages; plans change.
  - This is not failure.

### III. If I Could Start Over
- Explain to the team *why* we do things earlier, rather than just telling them *what* to do.
- Give young engineers more room to make mistakes (instead of blunt dismissals).
- ~~Keeping knowledge in my head~~ → Actively record, share, and discuss.
- Listen more to my wife, rather than "supervising" from the sidelines.
- Communicate with the children about more than just their mortgages and jobs.

### IV. Words for Young Engineers
- Standards are important, but don't let them become a weapon.
- Details determine quality, but details can also consume your life—learn to prioritize.
- 40 years is a long time, but it is also very short.
- The things you take for granted now will become mere memories by the time you reach my age.
- Don't wait for your heart to remind you—some things are more important than perfection.

### V. Immediate Action Plan
- [ ] Compile a "Core Experiences in Quality Management" document (for technical school students).
- [ ] Sit down with my subordinates for a talk—explain rather than accuse.
- [ ] Have a real conversation with my wife this weekend; listen to her talk about home.
- [ ] Call Chen Hao and Chen Yu respectively; do not mention work.
- [ ] Continue medication and scheduled check-ups—accept this reality.

---

## Notes
- The precision tool inventory can be set aside for now.
- The 40-year timeline is drawn; now it needs to be turned into a story.
- Perhaps retirement isn't the end, but a different kind of beginning?
- (I wouldn't have believed that sentence before.)

---

**One final question: What is my greatest regret in life?**
It isn't that I didn't reach a higher position.
It’s that I never truly looked at the people beside me.

That new young engineer—I used to correct him so bluntly that he seemed a bit intimidated by me. Today, I started by pointing out what he did right before explaining why certain changes were needed. Surprisingly, he started asking a lot of questions and eventually figured it out on his own.

ranked_items.content:
# Management Methodology Update - October 19, 2022

**Subject: Blame vs. Coaching: A Comparative Analysis of Effectiveness**

While chatting with Meifang tonight about recent departmental affairs, I had a sudden realization.

**My old approach:** Employee makes a mistake → Point it out directly → State the standard → End of conversation.
**The result:** Low defect rates, but high turnover. Employees worked in constant fear, and their first instinct when a problem arose was to hide it.

**Changes over the last three months:** Employee makes a mistake → Ask "why" first → Analyze the cause together → Explain the correct procedure → Give them a chance to fix it.
**The result:** Xiao Li, who used to make frequent mistakes, hasn't repeated the same error in a month. More importantly, he has started proactively reporting issues instead of concealing them.

I told Meifang, "The moment I stopped blaming him, he actually became more willing to learn."

As I said those words, I even surprised myself.

**Systematic Improvements Required:**

1.  **Trigger**: When an employee makes a work error
    *   **Old Model**: Direct identification of the flaw + Criticism
    *   **New Model**: Understand the context first + Joint analysis + Coaching

2.  **Replicable Steps**:
    *   **Step 1**: Ask "What happened this time?" (Inquiry, not interrogation)
    *   **Step 2**: Listen to their explanation to find the root cause (could be unclear processes, faulty tools, or a misunderstanding)
    *   **Step 3**: Explain the correct method and the logic behind it
    *   **Step 4**: Have them summarize it back to ensure full understanding

3.  **Key Performance Indicators (KPIs)**:
    *   Recurrence rate of similar errors (Target: Reduce by over 50%)
    *   Frequency of proactive issue reporting (Target: Increase)
    *   Employee turnover rate (Target: Stabilize or decrease)

**Reflection**:
For forty years, I have been too accustomed to measuring others against a "standard of perfection." Perhaps that is why my subordinates fear me, and perhaps it is also why I find my own life so exhausting.

I want to implement this methodology across the entire department before I retire. Not to please anyone, but simply because it is more effective.

**Note**: This can be shared at next week’s departmental meeting as a "New Approach to Quality Management."

ranked_items.content:
Tuesday afternoon, October 18, 2022

I spent the entire afternoon sitting in my small workshop. I didn’t clean any tools or organize my inventory. I just flipped through my notebooks, page by page.

What was I recording back then? Screwdriver models, wrench specifications, the purchase year and maintenance cycle for every single tool. Everything was neat and tidy, precise to a fault. Looking at these records used to give me a sense of peace—as if I had mastered something.

But today, I’m wondering: what did these tools actually teach me?

It wasn't how to use them. That’s too simple. It was... how to approach a task. With seriousness. Without compromise. Knowing that every detail matters. For forty years, the products I made with these tools had a defect rate 25% lower than the industry average. That wasn't a coincidence. It was because I never allowed myself to slack off.

But—

I wrote that word on a new page, then crossed it out. Wrote it again. Crossed it out again.

But I treated people the same way. My subordinates, my family. I demanded perfection and left no room for error. I thought that was called being responsible. Now, I’m not so sure.

Li Meifang has been humming in the kitchen lately. I haven't heard her hum like that in a long time. She says it’s because I’ve finally "loosened up a bit." I can hear it in her voice—it wasn't a compliment. It was... relief? She feels relieved.

That hurts a little.

I turned to a blank page and drew a timeline with a pencil. From 1982 to now. Forty years. I marked the key years: technician, team leader, department manager. I did well at every stage. Defect rates, process optimizations, the proposals that were adopted. It’s a beautiful report card.

But so what?

Chen Hao is in Shenzhen, under a lot of pressure. I keep track of his mortgage and his performance, but we barely have anything to say to each other. When Chen Yu comes home occasionally, our conversations are like status reports—how’s work, is your marriage stable. I thought I was teaching them about perseverance and quality awareness, but I was never truly there for them.

This is my regret. Perhaps my greatest regret.

Next to the timeline, I wrote: "If I could do it over, what would I do?"

But I know there are no "ifs." I only have the now. And about two years left of work. Maybe in these two years, I should record some things. Not tool lists, not process documents. But... what I was thinking during these forty years. Why I did things the way I did. Young people might need that.

Or rather, I need to do this. To forgive myself.

Meifang is calling me for dinner. I hear her voice drifting up from downstairs—gentle, not rushing me. I close the notebook and stand up.

Maybe I should listen to her more. Really listen, instead of mentally calculating her blood pressure and diet.

Maybe that is what I really need to learn.

# Work Log

**Date:** Wednesday, June 14, 2023  
**Weather:** Sunny  
**Working Hours:** 08:00–15:30  

---

## Record of Events

**Morning:** Final departmental meeting. Discussed Q3 quality inspection standard adjustments and the new employee training plan. Proposed three process optimization suggestions, which have been forwarded to the superior department. Finished packing half of the office; document filing is largely complete.

**Afternoon:** Farewell party, 14:00–15:30.  

**Attendees:** Company leadership, the entire department, and several colleagues from related departments—over 30 people in total.  

**Agenda:**  
- Opening remarks by company leadership (approx. 10 mins)  
- Employee video sharing (approx. 15 mins) — Several young engineers shared their work experiences; one intern mentioned the precision machining principles learned during a sharing session.  
- Personal speech (approx. 8 mins)  

**Key Points of Personal Speech:**  
- Reflected on a 40-year career.  
- Mentioned: Excellence is not just about perfect products, but also perfect relationships. I haven't done well enough in this regard.  
- Expressed gratitude for the team’s cooperation.  
- Briefly summarized my understanding of quality management.  

**Post-meeting:** Shook hands and said goodbye to every attendee. Received several commemorative gifts.  

---

## Summary  

40 years. From quality inspector to department manager. Defect rate reduced by 25%. An average of 3–5 process improvement proposals per year.  

The numbers are accurate. But there are other things that I saw more clearly today.  

That intern cried. I didn't expect that.  

---

## Next Steps  

- Complete offboarding procedures tomorrow.  
- Donate tools to the technical school after packing them up.  
- Official resignation date: June 30.  

---

## Remarks  

My wife came today. She sat in the back row. I saw her dabbing her eyes.  

I need to have a good talk with her when I get home. There is still so much left unsaid.  

Perhaps I can start saying it now.

ranked_items.content:
Meifang, I told my boss something today. I wanted to talk to you about it first.

We have a meeting this afternoon to discuss next year’s plans, so I went to see him beforehand. I told him I want to organize an experience-sharing session before I retire—a way to summarize everything I’ve learned about quality management over the past forty years and share it with the people at the company. It’s nothing grand; I just want to document what I’ve learned, the pitfalls I’ve encountered, and how certain standards were established.

He was quite supportive and said it was a great idea.

To be honest, this wasn't what I originally had in mind. Do you remember? A while back, I was so preoccupied with how to pass my technical skills down to the younger generation; I felt such a sense of urgency. But after talking to the doctor a few times and starting the medication, I’ve slowly come to realize something. It’s not so much about "passing the torch" as it is about me wanting to properly sort through everything I’ve done all these years. It’s about giving myself a sense of closure.

This isn't just for others. It’s mostly for myself. Forty years in this position—what did I learn? What did I improve? Why did I do things the way I did? I want to think these through clearly, and I want others to know, too.

What do you think? Am I overthinking things?

The truth is, I feel that if I do this, I won't feel so empty when I retire. If these things are recorded and spoken aloud, they won't just disappear with me.

How is your blood pressure lately? Did you take your medicine on time today?

---

(Added in the evening)

Oh, I just remembered. You once said that I’ve spent all these years being too anxious to control everything, and that sometimes I don't need to be so tense. I think this sharing session is exactly like that. I’m not trying to force anyone to change; I just want to tell the story of my experiences over the years. As for the rest, I’ll leave it to others to interpret for themselves.

Thinking about it this way actually makes me feel a bit more relaxed.

You’ve been by my side for so many years. Sometimes I think I should listen to your thoughts more often, instead of just deciding everything on my own.

Thank you.

ranked_items.content:
Meifang, go ahead and eat first. I’m just sorting through some things.

I was just thinking—we’ve been at this factory for almost 40 years. All these years, my focus has been on mastering every single step, setting strict standards, and making sure there’s no room for error. Thinking about it now... maybe these things should be carried on by someone.

I’m considering whether I should write something for the school while I’m still able-bodied. Just putting together the experience I’ve gathered over the years. It’s nothing fancy, just how to actually get the job done right. Those young people—they need to know what it means to be thorough.

Don’t laugh at me. I just feel like some things shouldn’t just stay locked in my head.

By the way, how’s your blood pressure lately? Last time, the doctor said to cut back on salt—have you been paying attention to that? I noticed you’ve been making soup again these past couple of days.

Wait a second, let me just finish organizing this spreadsheet first.

ranked_items.content:
Meifang, thank you for what you said yesterday.

I thought about it for a long time last night. When you asked me, "What do those things actually mean to you?" I didn't know how to answer at first. But then I realized that maybe I’ve been looking at it the wrong way all along.

All these years, I’ve felt this constant need to achieve something, to prove something. The work, the process improvements, those tools... it was as if those were the only things that gave me value. Now that the job is gone, I find myself not knowing what I’m even capable of anymore. But when you asked me that, it suddenly hit me: maybe the point isn't what I’ve done, but rather... that I want to pass these things on. Not to prove myself, but just so the people coming after me don't have to struggle as much.

I know it might be a bit late to say this. And I know I haven't been good enough to you or the kids over the years. I was too obsessed with things that could be measured.

Would you be willing to keep talking to me about this? I feel like I really need to think about how to live from here on out. Not like a schedule or a plan, but... just truly figuring it out.

# Memo: Performance Improvement Plan

**Date: July 22, 2022**  
**Time: 09:15**

---

## Review of Events

I overreacted to the Quality Control team’s feedback yesterday. Master Wang is on leave, and the atmosphere in the department is noticeably off. I can feel it. This is not the outcome I wanted.

Maintaining a low defect rate cannot be achieved this way.

---

## Immediate Action Items

**1. One-on-one with Master Wang**
- Timing: This afternoon or tomorrow morning.
- Content: Listen to his perspective; explain that my intentions were not a personal attack.
- Tone: Stay calm; do not repeat yesterday’s phrasing.
- Documentation: Note down key points to avoid future misunderstandings.

**2. Review Feedback Standards**
- Current Issue: My feedback criteria are too subjective and easily influenced by my mood.
- Improvement: Establish a clearer, quantified scoring sheet.
  - Specific error types → Corresponding feedback levels.
  - Different levels → Different communication styles.
  - This ensures subordinates see the standards and I maintain consistency.
- Deadline: By next Monday.

**3. Department Meeting**
- Convene the whole team to explain the new feedback mechanism.
- Emphasize: Strictness is for product quality, not directed at any individual.
- Listen to their thoughts (even though I’m not great at this).

---

## Personal Level

The pressure is too high. I’ve been wound too tight lately.

- Increase exercise: Currently 6,000 steps a day; increase to 8,000.
- Check recent physical exam records.
- Don’t bring work anxiety home; my wife already has blood pressure issues.

---

## Reflection

I know where my problem lies. My obsession with detail is a strength, but over-control turns it into a weakness. Subordinates are not machines.

I must learn from this. It cannot happen again.

---

**Next Step: Talk to Master Wang by 3:00 PM today.**

It wasn't until that incident with the doctor that I truly understood—there are some things I simply cannot control. Through it all, you were the one who stayed, never once looking down on me.

Every improvement I’ve made over the years hasn't truly been about pursuing perfection, though that’s what I’ve always told myself. Thinking back, it was more about proving my own worth. I realize now that this obsession has also made me too demanding of my subordinates.

# MEMO

**Date:** Thursday, June 29, 2023  
**Time:** 09:30

---

**Subject: Reflections on Tool Donation and Knowledge Transfer**

My conversation with Li Meifang last night helped me clear my head.

She asked me why I wanted to donate my tools. At first, I didn’t have a good answer, but then I realized—I’ve always defined myself through my professional achievements. Those process improvements and falling defect rates were important, but in hindsight, those things lose their meaning the moment I leave the job.

**What truly matters?** It’s passing down everything I’ve accumulated over the years.

I’ve given it some thought: every tool has a story. That pair of pliers has been with me for over 20 years; I’ve used it to calibrate precision parts thousands of times. That set of measuring gauges was my go-to whenever I was training new hires. These things shouldn't just sit in my workshop gathering dust.

**What needs to be done:**

1. Organize a tool inventory—not just a list, but a record of each tool’s purpose and the stories behind them. That way, the students can truly understand their value.

2. Consider giving a talk at the school. Not to show off, but to really explain the experience—how to use tools to solve problems and how to spot quality risks in the smallest details.

3. Contact the industrial technical school to ask how we can ensure these tools are actually put to use, rather than just sitting in a display case.

**An observation:** Li Meifang’s question actually helped me. It made me stop and re-examine what I’ve been doing. I realize I might have been too anxious lately—obsessing over health data and over-monitoring her diet, trying to use control to fill the void left by my lost professional identity.

Maybe I need a change of perspective. Instead of trying to control things, I should think about what I can pass on.

**Core realization:** More important than professional achievements is the ability to pass experience on to the younger generation.

This isn't just a "pastime" for retirement; this is something meaningful.

---

**To-Do List:**
- [ ] Organize tools and take photos this weekend
- [ ] Look up the school’s contact information
- [ ] List 3–5 of the most representative tool case studies

ranked_items.content:
# MEMO

**Date:** June 15, 2023, 6:45 PM  
**Subject:** Reimagining Continuity and Legacy

---

The farewell party ended this afternoon. Sitting in my office, looking at the empty desk, I found myself wondering: after forty years, what have I actually left behind?

I used to think the answer was clear: a defect rate 25% lower than the industry average, annual process improvement plans, and all those adopted proposals. These figures and achievements were proof enough of my value. But this afternoon, when that intern said with teary eyes, "Manager Chen, you taught me so much more than just technical skills," I suddenly realized I might have misunderstood everything.

Perhaps the act of passing things on is the purpose itself.

In the message I sent to Chen Yu, I wrote: "You are educating students, I am manufacturing products, and we are both pursuing excellence. I am proud of you." As I wrote those words, it finally clicked—I don't need her to inherit my job, nor do I need her to become another quality inspector. The fact that she pursues excellence in her own field is a form of continuity in itself. It’s not about bloodlines or obligation; it’s about everyone finding their own way to carry forward that obsession with precision and responsibility.

The tools will be donated to the school. This decision now feels like more than just disposing of old belongings; it’s another form of dialogue—letting those tools continue to serve a purpose in the hands of the young. I don’t need to see them become me; I only need to know that when they use those tools, they will remember that someone once spent an entire lifetime just to get things right.

The stories from the sharing session—how to spot a 0.02mm deviation, how to uphold standards on the assembly line, how to stay calm under pressure—these are not my intellectual property. They now belong to those who heard them. They will understand, improve, and spread them in their own way. That is enough.

I’m starting to feel that perhaps I’ve been defining "legacy" the wrong way. I thought a legacy had to be something visible, quantifiable, something that could be inherited in its entirety. But true continuity likely happens in those seemingly tiny moments—a sentence, a demonstration, a tool, a bit of persistence.

Starting tomorrow, I will no longer be the department manager. But that doesn't mean it's over. It’s just continuing in a different way.

This requires long-term reflection.

---

*Note: Think through the specific details of the donation while sorting through the tools with Li Meifang this weekend.*

ranked_items.content:
# October 8, 2022, 8:15 PM

Sitting in my study, the work log by my hand is nearly finished. Flipping to the last few pages, I realized the nature of my entries has changed. They are no longer just technical data and process optimizations; instead, I’ve started writing these inexplicable sentences—"If I could do it over, I would teach the younger generation more."

Looking at those words, even I find them unfamiliar.

This isn't like me. I’ve never been one for such sentimentality. But lately, I have been wondering: over these forty years, I’ve drafted so many improvement plans and brought the defect rates down, but what is actually left behind? A stack of reports? Some data? Or just the resentment in the hearts of those young engineers I criticized so bluntly?

Li Meifang was nagging about her blood pressure again today. I made a new diet plan for her; she didn't say anything, but that look in her eyes... I know what she’s thinking. After all these years of marriage, we haven't communicated much, but the way she looks at me has changed. Perhaps she has sensed something, too.

In the office this afternoon, a new QC inspector asked me a technical question. As usual, I pointed out the flaws in his logic directly, but this time, I stopped myself. I paused, and instead, explained to him *why* it had to be done that way. He listened intently. In that moment, I thought, maybe... maybe I should do more of that.

It’s not that I’ve suddenly become soft. I still loathe inefficiency and ambiguity. But I’m beginning to realize that my value might not lie in how much knowledge I’ve documented, but in... whether I’ve actually helped others understand what a true work ethic looks like.

I’m considering holding a technical sharing session for the young engineers in the department before I retire. I never thought I’d want to do something like that. But now I feel that if I don’t do it now, the opportunity might be gone forever.

Why am I thinking this way? I can’t quite say.

Perhaps it’s because time has suddenly become tangible. I used to feel that time was something to be managed, just like a project. Now... now it feels like it’s slipping away at a speed I can no longer control.

I don’t want to admit it. But writing it down here makes it feel real.

Tomorrow is another day. There are still many tools that need organizing.

ranked_items.content:
April 5, 2022. Night.

I’ve been sitting in the office for a long time. Wang Gang’s quality inspection report is still spinning in my head.

The formatting was off again when he handed it in this morning—the classification standards in the header didn't match last year’s specifications. It’s such a basic thing to get wrong. I couldn't help myself; I snapped at him. He tried to explain, but I cut him off. No excuses. Professionalism is about an obsession with detail.

His face changed the moment I said it.

Looking back now, my tone was harsh. Harsher than usual. He’s actually a decent employee. In the past, I would have pointed out these small mistakes, but never like that. Why today...?

My fingers have been tapping on the desk for a long while. Rapidly. I didn't even realize I was doing it.

What am I thinking? Was his report really that outrageous? Or... was I just taking it out on him?

It’s been like this lately. I’m becoming more demanding with my subordinates and more micro-managing with my wife. This morning, I was looking at her blood pressure logs again; a single high reading and I started feeling restless. I spent another whole night in the tool shed, numbering all the precision tools and documenting their uses. I’m terrified that one day I’ll suddenly... lose the ability to move, and these things will become completely useless because no one knows how to handle them.

Two years until retirement. Two years.

I know what I’m trying to do. I’m trying to control things. Control the work, the standards, everything at home—even the aging of my own body. It’s as if by managing every tiny detail, I can prove I’m still useful, that I can still... what?

Wang Gang has to come in for work tomorrow. I need to figure out how to handle this. Not for his sake, but because... I don’t want to become this kind of person. I don’t want to use harshness to mask my own fear.

But I don’t know how to change.

How much longer can I do this? How much longer can I keep things under control like this?

# Knowledge Transfer Project - Memo

**Date:** July 11, 2023, 14:00  
**Subject:** Chen Yu’s Proposal – Integrating Family Stories into Teaching

---

## Core Idea
Chen Yu suggested yesterday that some of my work experiences and professional principles could be integrated into her classroom teaching. This idea deserves serious consideration.

Previously, I felt my experience could only be passed down through tool donations, but I’ve now realized there are other dimensions. My daughter is teaching students, and through her, my stories reach the next generation. This is significant.

---

## List of Stories to Share with Students

- [ ] Case study on "Details Define Quality" – how a single small part’s inspection standard impacts the entire product.
- [ ] From Inspector to Manager – how 40 years of persistence and continuous learning led to progress.
- [ ] The Logic of Process Improvement – how to identify, analyze, and solve problems (using factory examples).
- [ ] "Doing the Right Thing" vs. "Doing Things Right" – the importance of setting standards.
- [ ] Stories of Tools – why choosing the right tool matters (can lead to reflections on teaching tools).

---

## Chen Yu’s Specific Suggestions
- She can use these stories in courses related to "Career Planning" or "Engineering Ethics."
- Students might be particularly interested in the topic of "how to stand by your principles at work."
- She suggested I write down short story snippets that would be easy for her to adapt.

---

## Next Steps

**Key Points for Discussion with Chen Yu:**
- [ ] Confirm what format she needs (length, level of detail).
- [ ] Clarify the students' grade level and course themes so I can provide more relevant examples.
- [ ] Discuss the possibility of a guest lecture at the school (if she deems it appropriate).
- [ ] Regular updates – this shouldn't be a one-off; it can be an ongoing accumulation.

**Content Preparation:**
- [ ] Organize key cases from my work logs (with over 20 years of records, there should be plenty of good examples).
- [ ] Write 3–5 "story snippets" as a trial, keeping each under 500 words.
- [ ] Think about how to tell these experiences in a language students can easily understand.

---

## Other Transfer Channels (To be considered)
- Writing articles for industry journals? (Might be too formal).
- Audio or video recordings? (Technically challenging, but worth a try if Chen Yu needs them).
- Sharing these stories with Chen Hao as well? He’s in real estate sales; there might be some useful takeaways for him.
- Attaching "usage stories" when donating tools.

---

## Personal Reflection
This idea made me realize that I don't have to wait until retirement to "pass something on." I can do it now. And it’s not just through one person, but through my daughter’s classroom, impacting dozens of students.

It’s a different kind of feeling.

---

**Note:** I can mention this idea to my chess friends during our game next Friday and see what they think.
